title: Support-Guessing Decoding Algorithms in the Sum-Rank Metric
authors: Thomas Jerkovits, Hannes Bartz, Antonia Wachter-Zeh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: The sum-rank metric generalizes the Hamming and rank metric by partitioning vectors into blocks and defining the total weight as the sum of the rank weights of these blocks, based on their matrix representation. In this work, we explore support-guessing algorithms for decoding sum-rank-metric codes. Support-guessing involves randomly selecting candidate supports and attempting to decode the error under the assumption that it is confined to these supports. While previous works have focused on worst-case scenarios, we analyze the average case and derive an optimal support-guessing distribution in the asymptotic regime. We show that this distribution also performs well for finite code lengths. Our analysis provides exact complexity estimates for unique decoding scenarios and establishes tighter bounds beyond the unique decoding radius. Additionally, we introduce a randomized decoding algorithm for linearized Reed–Solomon codes. This algorithm extends decoding capabilities beyond the unique decoding radius by leveraging an efficient error-and-erasure decoder. Instead of requiring the entire error support to be confined to the guessed support, the algorithm succeeds as long as there is sufficient overlap between the guessed support and the actual error support. As a result, the proposed method improves the success probability and reduces computational complexity compared to generic decoding algorithms. Our contributions offer more accurate complexity estimates than previous works and clarify how support-guessing methods behave under different assumptions on the code class, rank profile, and decoding radius. These estimates provide a codingtheoretic basis for future work on decoding algorithms and applications of the sum-rank metric.

title: A Minimalist CMOS Memelement-Based Neuromorphic Architecture for Energy-Efficient Spike-Driven Pattern Classification
authors: Sourav Maji, Basit Shafat Makhdoomi, Vedant Upadhyay, Rajeev Kumar Ranjan, Sung-Mo Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Artificial Intelligence
abstract: This paper presents a memelement-driven neuromorphic architecture for energy-efficient spike-based pattern classification. The proposed framework integrates a proposed 7T MOS-only meminductor emulator (MIE) with a memristorbased synaptic unit to enable event-driven mismatch detection within a crossbar structure. The compact seven-transistor MIE, implemented in UMC 65 nm CMOS technology, operates up to 1 GHz while consuming only 1.23 nW static power and occupying 18.6 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Leveraging its intrinsic memory dynamics, a meminductor-based neuron is realized, achieving 12.48 fJ/spike energy efficiency with 3.26 μW operating power and a layout area of 54.80 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Integrated with the memristive synapse, the proposed neuromorphic block performs spike-driven pattern recognition with a total inference power of 11.65 μW. Robustness is validated through Monte Carlo, temperature, and processcorner analyses. Experimental verification using SN7404 and CD4007 ICs confirms the feasibility of the proposed memelementbased architecture for scalable low-power neuromorphic AI systems, with spike-based encoding enabling correct classification in otherwise unfeasible cases.

title: Computing Scheme for Space-Air-Ground Integrated Edge Computing Networks: A Reputation Blockchain-Enabled FRL Approach
authors: Sicheng Liu, Wenxiao Shi, Xue Wang, Fengye Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Space-air-ground integrated edge computing (SAGI-EC) networks have emerged as a novel architecture due to seamless connectivity, flexible deployment, and scalable resources. However, existing studies mainly focus on computing strategies, while challenges such as low node cooperation willingness and poor device reliability hinder the adoption of SAGI-EC. To address these challenges, we first propose a heterogeneous SAGI-EC framework that incorporates a reputation blockchain. Then, a multi-weight subjective logic model and an adaptive interaction weight updating method are employed to achieve accurate reputation evaluation and dynamic management. In addition, a problem is formulated to minimize the weighted sum of total time delay and energy consumption. By modeling the computing process as a Markov decision process and solving it through a federated reinforcement learning (FRL) approach, we propose a space-air-ground integrated edge computing algorithm based on a reputation incentive mechanism and FRL (RFSIEC). Simulation results indicate that our proposed scheme can effectively suppress and filter unreliable devices, significantly reducing the average total time delay and energy consumption while improving the task success rate.

title: Image-Quality-Guided Consistency-Alignment Network for Fault Diagnosis
authors: Zhuowei Li, Jianxu Mao, Wenbin He, Yaonan Wang, Junfei Yi, Caiping Liu, Hui Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: Signal-to-image transformation has been widely used in mechanical fault diagnosis to provide unified visual representations for downstream diagnostic models. However, the resulting diagnostic images can exhibit substantial quality variations caused by measurement noise, sensor degradation, and partial signal loss. Most existing methods either discard low-quality samples or implicitly assume equal reliability across samples, leading to information loss or degraded robustness. This paper proposes an image-quality-guided consistency-alignment network that explicitly estimates sample quality and leverages degraded data during training. First, vibration signals are decomposed into sub-bands, and energy-ratio criteria are used to select informative components for reconstruction. The reconstructed signals are subsequently fused into fixed-layout two-dimensional images via a sector-allocation strategy. Next, a diagnosis-aware quality assessment module assigns a quality score to each image to guide training. Low-quality samples are further regularized via cross-quality feature alignment using a quality-weighted supervised pairwise loss, encouraging them to align with high-quality counterparts from the same class. Finally, a Swin Transformer backbone performs classification. Experiments on the proprietary RGFD dataset and the public SEUB dataset demonstrate consistent gains under controlled mixed-quality settings, with accuracies of up to 99.4% and 98.7%, respectively, while additional evaluations under reproducible degradations further confirm the robustness of the proposed quality-aware learning mechanism.

title: NeuroShield-Diabetes: A Hybrid Nature-Inspired Neural Cryptography and Machine Learning framework for Privacy-Preserving Diabetes Data Sharing in Consumer Health
authors: Jing Yang, Jialin Dai, Muhammad Attique Khan, Weiyu Chen, Zaffar Ahmed Shaikh, Vijay Govindarajan, Bayan Alabdullah, Lip Yee Por, Wangkai Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Consumer health applications for diabetes management create critical privacy vulnerabilities when sharing sensitive patient data across healthcare ecosystems, demanding lightweight cryptographic solutions for resource-constrained mobile environments. This paper presents NeuroShield-Diabetes, a hybrid framework integrating neural cryptography with machine learning for privacy-preserving diabetes data sharing on smartphones and wearables. The framework employs tree parity machines for neural key exchange, achieving cryptographic strength comparable to 2048-bit RSA with 91% reduced computational complexity measured as CPU cycles per key generation operation. Adaptive differential privacy with calibrated noise injection maintains formal guarantees (ε ≤ 0.8 for high-risk queries) while preserving 94.7% data utility for glycemic prediction. Evaluation across 5,600 diabetes patients using MIMIC-III and UCI Diabetes datasets demonstrates 73% reduction in data sharing latency versus conventional cryptographic solutions. The neural intrusion detection system achieves 92.6% accuracy in identifying malicious access attempts with sub-120ms overhead. Field deployment confirms HIPAA compliance and GDPR conformance while maintaining interoperability with existing healthcare infrastructures, establishing NeuroShield-Diabetes as a practical privacy-preserving solution for consumer diabetes applications.

title: Proceedings of the 8th ACM International Symposium on Blockchain and Secure Critical Infrastructure
authors: N/A
publish: N/A
abstract: None

title: Analysis and Design of Active Damping Strategy for LC-DAB System Based on Filter Inductor Current Single-Loop Control
authors: Xiao Huo, Xinchun Lin, Tianyu Fang, Chenyang Xie, Zijie Kang, Jing Zhou, Xiaoxiao Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Dual-Active-Bridge (DAB) converters have been extensively applied for power transmission owing to their advantages, such as bidirectional power flow and galvanic isolation. However, its input current contains significant high-frequency ripples, which degrade power quality and pose challenges to the stable operation of power systems. Although cascading an LC filter at the DAB input stage forming a LC-DAB converter system can effectively mitigate these high-frequency ripples, the interaction between the LC filter and the DAB converter often leads to resonance instability in the input current. Furthermore, when the DAB converter is deployed in energy storage unit (ESU) discharge scenarios, precise control of the discharge current is essential. To address these issues, this paper proposes an active damping strategy utilizing filter inductor current feedback within a single-loop inductor current control framework. A mathematical model incorporating control delay is established, revealing the specific impact of latency on the performance of the active damping scheme. Finally, experimental results are provided to verify the validity and effectiveness of the proposed theoretical approach.

title: An Integrated Structure of 5G and TSN Communication Network for Nuclear Power Plants
authors: Jiang Li, Long Zhao, ZiYue Jiao, HongZhi Sun, Tao Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In the context of the accelerated digital and intelligent transformation of the nuclear power industry, nuclear power plants have put forward strict requirements for the delay accuracy, reliability and security of wireless communication networks. 5G private networks, with their high bandwidth, low latency and independent deployment features, have become critical communication infrastructure for nuclear power plant. TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) compensates for the shortcomings of a single 5G technology in extremely real-time services with the advantages of high-precision Time synchronization, low latency jitter, and deterministic transmission. The combination of the two provides a dual guarantee of 'flexible access and deterministic transmission', perfectly meeting the demands of all scenarios such as nuclear power production control and intelligent operation and maintenance. This paper presents an integrated structure of two technologies in nuclear power plants communication network. It provides a reference for the digital transformation of nuclear power plants communication systems.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Resource Aggregation and Low-Carbon Dispatch Paradigms for Carbon-Aware Power Systems
authors: Yu Zong, Guang Hu, Lile Jiang, Xiaoyu Yu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Driven by the dual-carbon strategic mandates, the transition of power dispatch paradigms—from traditional economic-centric balance to carbon-power synergistic optimization—has become a pivotal research frontier. This paper provides a comprehensive taxonomy of the current state-of-the-art in distributed energy resource (DER) aggregation and low-carbon dispatch within the context of deep electricity-carbon coupling. We categorize the theoretical landscape into three pillars: high-fidelity carbon flow tracing, carbon-feature-aware aggregation, and resilient low-carbon dispatch strategies. Key technological breakthroughs, including distributionally robust optimization (DRO) and multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), are critically analyzed regarding their efficacy in addressing DER stochasticity and carbon-flow non-linearity. Finally, we delineate future trajectories, such as physics-informed edge intelligence and blockchain-facilitated carbon certification, providing a visionary roadmap for constructing next-generation carbon-aware power systems.

title: FedSiamese: Privacy-Preserving Face Verification using Federated Learning
authors: Arshia Akbari Aleagha, Athena Abdi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Face verification, as a key biometric technology, aims to confirm an individual’s identity from digital images. However, the widespread use of this technology has raised serious concerns regarding the privacy of sensitive user data. In response to this significant challenge, Federated Learning emerges as a novel approach that enables the training of intelligent models without the need to collect or share raw data. In this research, a face verification system based on Federated Learning is designed. This system leverages a Siamese deep network architecture to assess the similarity between two face images and determine if they belong to the same person. During the training process, the data remains completely confidential on the user’s local device, and only the learned model parameters are exchanged with a central server. The results indicate that the proposed method, while guaranteeing privacy, achieves a desirable level of accuracy and efficiency for implementation in security systems and access control applications.

title: A Load Forecasting Method Based on Trend Feedforward Correction and Deep Residual Learning
authors: Zerong Wang, Hao Dong, Hao Huang, Yitao Dong, Huaiting Hua
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing proportion of new energy integration and the diversification of user consumption patterns in power systems, the volatility and uncertainty of electrical loads have increased significantly, affecting the stability of power grid operation. Accurate and reliable load forecasting is the key to addressing this issue. To this end, this paper proposes a load forecasting method based on Trend Feedforward Correction and Deep Residual Learning. The method first uses CEEMDAN to decompose the historical load sequence into multiple scales, extracting long-term trend components and short-term residual components to achieve a hierarchical representation of load data. Subsequently, LSTM and N-BEATS prediction models are constructed for the trend component and the residual component, respectively. The LSTM is used to predict trend information and generate a trend prior value. This prior value acts as a feedforward input to the N-BEATS model, providing constraints and guidance on the trend direction during its training process. Aided by the trend feedforward, the N-BEATS model can more accurately characterize the short-term fluctuation features of the load sequence, thereby obtaining high-precision residual prediction results. Finally, the trend prediction results and residual prediction results are superimposed to obtain the historical load prediction values. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method performs better than other models in load forecasting tasks.

title: A Markerless Hand Movement Assessment Tool for Upper Limb Rehabilitation post-stroke
authors: Reyez-Alvarez Amaury S, García-Villalobos Andrea, Blancas-Silva Sandra E, Vega-Martínez Gabriel, Toledo-Peral Cinthya L, Romano-Riquer Sonia P, Ramírez-Nava Ana G, Quinzaños-Fresnedo Jimena, Mercado-Gutierrez Jorge A
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Recently, markerless motion capture technologies, based on computer vision and artificial intelligence models, have been proposed as alternatives to wearable sensing technologies for human movement assessment, with advantages on non-invasiveness and accessibility. In this work, the development of a marker-less motion capture tool for hand movement assessment is presented, and a preliminary evaluation of its application during upper limb rehabilitation sessions assisted by Functional Electrical Stimulation for a post-stroke patient with left hemiparesis. The tool is based on the open-source library MediaPipe, which allows human body movement assessment with conventional RGB video cameras. Video and finger goniometry extracted from three rehabilitation sessions, corresponding to four different upper limb tasks assisted by Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES), were recorded and analyzed to evaluate the frame processing time (FPT) and the maximum joint angles (finger extension) achieved during different upper limb target movements (passive hand opening and active-passive ball grasp-and-release). FPT values were similar (0.1001 ± 0.0154 s vs 0.1000 ± 0.0119 s) in rehabilitation sessions recorded with two different computers (laptop vs workstation). In passive hand opening movements, angles were increased between sessions for most finger joints (up to 28.82°). In contrast, for passive-active ball grasp-and-release movements, the angle variability (standard deviation) was increased in time in multiple joints (up to ± 12.22°). These results support the feasibility of the proposed app as a robust, non-invasive markerless tool, for movement assessment during FES-assisted upper limb rehabilitation sessions of post-stroke patients, with advantages over systems with similar aims based on sensorized strategies and conventional PT/OT sessions.

title: Comparison of Microbubble Tracking Algorithms for Ultrasound Localization Microscopy
authors: Uriel Ramilo, Gonzalo Garay, Juan Barolin, Victoria Sorriba, Mónica Bube, Juan Pablo Damián, Carlos Negreira, Alejandra Kun, Javier Brum
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Ultrasound Localization Microscopy (ULM) is a super-resolution imaging technique that allows the visualization of microvascular structures by detecting and tracking individual microbubbles. The performance of ULM strongly depends on the localization and tracking methods employed, affecting metrics such as image saturation, track count, and the geometric properties of reconstructed trajectories. In this work, in vivo transcranial ULM experiments were performed on mouse brains to experimentally compare two widely adopted microbubble tracking algorithms: simple-tracker and u-track. The comparison is based on ULM image reconstruction, saturation curves, track count, and trajectory tortuosity. While simple-tracker yields a higher number of reconstructed tracks and slightly higher image saturation, it also produces more tortuous trajectories. In contrast, u-track generates fewer but smoother trajectories, suggesting improved filtering of highly tortuous tracks that may arise from spurious associations. These results highlight a trade-off between the number of reconstructed trajectories and the generation of more tortuous paths, potentially arising from spurious associations in ULM.

title: Advanced Full Spectrum Application for Endpoint Optimization in High Aspect Ratio Plasma Etching
authors: Yu-Cheng Chou, Canta Tsai, Rem Chang, Pascal Wang, Yu-Hsien Chou, Benjamin Huang
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In high-aspect-ratio (HAR) plasma etching, insufficient sensitivity in endpoint detection (EPD) often leads to substantial variation in endpoint timing. Such fluctuations directly influence the critical dimension (CD) across wafers, ultimately compromising device performance and production yield stability. This study presents a machine-learning-assisted methodology that integrates full-spectrum optical emission analysis with Lam Research’s Equipment Intelligence® Data Analyzer (EI-DA). The approach identifies highly responsive spectral features for endpoint evaluation and significantly accelerating analysis efficiency. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed method achieves a 48% reduction in wafer-to-wafer EPD variation, thereby improving both process and yield stability across wafers.

title: Remote Robot Operation Using Visual-Haptic Fusion : Influence of Object Size
authors: Yutaka Ishibashi, Pingguo Huang, Hitoshi Ohnishi, Konstantinos E. Psannis
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Remote robot operation systems with visual-haptic fusion enable cooperative manipulation by integrating visual information with force feedback. This paper investigates the influence of object size on cooperative manipulation using a simulation-based remote robot operation system with real haptic devices. The proposed system consists of two network-connected subsystems, each equipped with a haptic device, allowing cooperative manipulation of a virtual object using two robot hands. In the investigation, a single user operated two haptic devices simultaneously, while the object weight was kept constant and only its size was varied. The results show that object size significantly affects force characteristics and task performance, highlighting the importance of visual factors in visual-haptic remote robot operation.

title: Research on Virtual Power Plant Trading Rules and Regional Practices
authors: Ran Lyu, Jing Yu, Mingxing Guo, Yanyuan Qian, Li Lan, Su Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: As a new operational model for aggregating distributed resources, the market-based trading mechanism of virtual power plants directly determines their capacity for sustainable development.This paper focuses on leading practices in Guangdong and Shandong provinces, deconstructing the core mechanisms through which virtual power plants participate in electricity markets. Employing comparative analysis, it delves into the differentiated regulatory designs adopted by Guangdong and Shandong during their marketisation processes, while examining how resource characteristics and regional development stages influence the suitability of regional mechanism designs. Subsequently, it further examines the underlying causes of regional mechanism differences by analysing three intrinsic characteristics of virtual power plants: the dual nature of aggregated resources spanning both generation and consumption sides, the absence of fixed grid connection points, and the emphasis on regulation capabilities. Finally, it presents prospects for the development of virtual power plants.

title: From Classroom to $1.4M Savings: Project-Based Operations Research Learning through Industry-Academia Collaboration in Corrugated Packaging
authors: Carlos Beltran-Perez, Alan Miralrio, Juan Carlos Cisneros Ortega, Angélica Hernández-Jaramillo, Magloria Alviso-Lara, Miguel Lorenzo, Jocelyn Herrera
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This study presents a successful industry-academia collaboration between Tecnológico de Monterrey and a corrugated packaging manufacturer conducted during Fall 2022. The project addressed inefficiencies in manual paper selection, where historical data revealed production costs exceeded targets by 0.85% and quality compliance reached only 40.1%. A student team applied project-based learning principles with operations research methods, developing a Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model in LINGO that optimized paper combinations considering type, flute profile, grammage, and inventory availability. Real-world validation demonstrated significant improvements: combined monthly savings of MXN <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 6 4 M} \boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{1 6. 3 {\%}}$</tex> reduction) across validated families while improving quality compliance to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>90 {\%}$</tex> within specification. Beyond industrial impact, this project exemplified experiential learning, developing technical competencies (mathematical modeling, optimization software mastery) and professional skills (problem diagnosis, stakeholder communication, evidence-based decision-making). The LINGO license achieved ROI within 1-2 weeks, with annual savings potential exceeding MXN 31.7M. This work validates how industry-academia partnerships in operations research education simultaneously generate practical value and comprehensive competency development.

title: A Neural Network-Based Framework for Identifying Hazardous Road Segments Using Geometrical Road Attributes
authors: Igor Gavrilov, Pavel Hrubes, Emil Pelikan
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Accurate identification of hazardous road segments is essential for improving road safety and prioritising preventive interventions. This study proposes a neural network-based framework for predicting hazardous road segments by leveraging detailed geometrical properties of roads, including lane number, lane width, and curvature. The proposed framework was applied to the D1 highway in the Czech Republic, using geospatial data and reported road accidents from 2024. The road geometry was divided into 10 m sectors, from which sequences of 300 m were generated to form input for a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. The model was trained using a weighted binary cross-entropy loss function to address class imbalance, where hazardous segments represented only 29.2 % of samples. The trained model demonstrated strong predictive performance, achieving a weighted <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> score of 0.895 and a macro F1 score of 0.906, which effectively distinguished between hazardous and non-hazardous highway segments. The results demonstrate that integrating detailed road attributes within an LSTM-based framework can effectively capture spatial dependencies and non-linear relationships associated with accident occurrence. The framework provides a scalable approach for identifying high-risk segments across various road types, offering valuable support for data-driven decision-making in transportation safety management.

title: A Unified Blockchain Framework for Smart City Resource Management with Convertible Tokens
authors: Amirhosein Mansouri, Mohammadhossein Ghorbi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel blockchain-based framework for smart city resource management, integrating energy, water, and gas consumption within a unified, tokenized ecosystem. Building upon previous research that explored blockchain applications for individual resource management, this work presents a holistic solution where resource efficiency is incentivized through a tokenized reward system. Consumers are rewarded with utility-specific tokens for conservation efforts and penalized for exceeding pre-defined consumption thresholds. The key innovation lies in the interoperability of these tokens. The proposed system allows for the dynamic conversion of tokens across different utilities. For instance, users can leverage water conservation tokens to offset penalties incurred from excessive gas consumption. This cross-resource exchange mechanism promotes resource optimization across multiple domains. In this method, the conversion rate of tokens to each other is determined based on the availability of each resource, its value, and environmental issues. This paper details the system's architecture, including smart contract implementation, and the dynamic conversion mechanism. Furthermore, it analyzes the potential impacts on resource sustainability and consumer behavior within the smart city context, ultimately paving the way for more efficient, equitable, and resilient urban environments.

title: New Approaches to Analyzing Data from Smoke Topography Devices: A Pilot Analysis
authors: Vesna Zeljković, Junjia Zhu, Joshua E. Muscat
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: The United States paid more than $600 billion in 2018 because of cigarette smoking according to CDC. It has become increasingly recognized that the harm associated with tobacco products is not just a function of the frequency of use but how they are consumed, which reflect the physical and chemical characteristics of the tobacco product. This can be objectively measured through the use of smoking or vaping topography devices, which mechanically record inhalation information using a pressure transducer. The default topography measure typically used for research is summary measure such as puff volume. However, other individual measurements of smoke or vape flow through the device are potentially valuable for understanding exposure information, such as flow velocity which reflects the depth of inhalation into the lungs. In the current study, we illustrate the multidimensional aspect of these topography flow parameters, using data taken from a population-based study, that aim to better understanding the mechanics of factors that affect the dose of tobacco inhalation. The results show that component measures such as maximum inhalation flow have variability both within and between users. These data could serve as predictors of tobacco harm.

title: Combining HLS and Computation Coding for Balanced Matrix-Vector Engines on FPGA
authors: Sayanti Pal, Alexander Lehnert, Marc Reichenbach
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Limited number of DSP48 blocks versus abundant LUTs constrains the throughput of FPGA-based constant matrix–vector multiplications (MVM). We exploit Linear Computation-Coding (LCC) to build multiplier-less, deeply pipelined adder graphs on LUTs and combine it with a DSP-mapped HLS datapath via a pragmatic column-split partition. This hybrid mapping achieves balanced DSP and LUT utilization without sacrificing throughput, resulting in a scalable, high-performance MVM on FPGAs.

title: A Planning Method for Site Selection and Capacity Determination of HVDC Cascaded Power Transmission from Long-distance High-Proportion Clean Energy Bases
authors: Xueying Guo, Yuwei Zou, Cheng Lei, Zhengchao Liu, Xudong Pan, Qin Zeng
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a planning method for site selection and capacity determination of HVDC cascaded power transmission from long-distance high-proportion clean energy bases. First, alternative cascaded ends are determined based on the provinces along the HVDC corridor. Subsequently, monthly power balance analysis of cascaded ends and receiving ends is conducted to calculate the alternative installed capacity of supporting power sources, which is facilitated by capacity complementarity between the two ends. Capacities of converter stations at both cascaded and receiving ends are specified based on the correlation between the alternative installed capacity and HVDC transmission capacity. Further, carbon emissions and investment costs of each cascaded scheme are evaluated, thereby forming the alternative installed capacity matrix, carbon emission matrix, and investment matrix—all of which undergo normalization processing. Finally, an optimization objective function for cascaded schemes is constructed, and its maximum value is solved to ultimately derive the optimal HVDC cascaded power transmission scheme. This paper conducts a case study, using the ±800kV/8GW HVDC project for verification. The method of the paper can provide a reference for the planning and construction of HVDC cascaded power transmission and the selection of cascaded ends in power grid. It improves the economy of cross-regional HVDC power transmission and promotes the full utilization of cross-regional corridor resources.

title: Research on Dynamic Equivalent Modeling of Wind Power Cluster Based on Transformer-BiGRU Network Data-driven
authors: Cong Peng, Ke Liu, Yi Yuan, Ming Nie, Pengju Sun
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of difficult parameter acquisition and high requirements for model completeness in the planning stage or complex terrain scenarios of traditional wind power cluster modeling methods, a data-driven equivalent modeling method based on Transformer-Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit(BiGRU) model is proposed, and the Grey Wolf Optimization(GWO) algorithm is used to optimize the hyper-parameters of the proposed model. Firstly, taking a wind power cluster as an example, input data (including wind speeds and voltage at the point of common coupling) and output power data of the wind power cluster under the voltage drop disturbance at the grid-connected point are collected to construct a historical dataset. Furthermore, the Transformer-BiGRU model is trained, and the validation set is used to evaluate the model performance and implement early stopping to obtain initial model parameters. Then, the GWO algorithm is used to optimize the hyper-parameters of the model, yielding the optimized model. Finally, the optimized model is used to predict and evaluate the test set, realizing accurate equivalent modeling of the dynamic response of the wind power cluster. This study provides a fast analysis method for power grid planning and operation personnel, achieving a good balance between accuracy and efficiency.

title: Modeling and Transient Synchronization Stability Analysis of Fractional-Order Converters
authors: Jianping Wu, Xuyang Pan, Wei Luo, Shu Zhang, Yanpeng Xiao, Jijun Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the transient synchronization stability issue in fractional-order converters, this paper proposes an equal-area criterion based on fractional calculus. The virtual power angle characteristics of the fractional-order converter are qualitatively analyzed, along with the corresponding acceleration and deceleration areas. Theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that, compared to a traditional converter, the fractional-order converter system exhibits a reduced acceleration area and an increased deceleration area. This verifies that the fractional-order converter possesses an enhanced stability margin, which can improve the system's transient synchronization stability.

title: Trident: Efficient FPGA Acceleration of XMSS Tree in Post-Quantum Signature Scheme SLH-DSA
authors: Tianyou Bao, Joshua Ennis, Kirill Morozov, Jiafeng Xie
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: The emergence of quantum computing poses significant threats to conventional cryptographic systems, necessitating the efficient hardware acceleration of Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC), especially on the Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) platforms. SPHINCS<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup>, recently standardized by NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) as SLH-DSA (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm), represents the only hash-based digital signature scheme. Its practical deployment, however, is restricted by computationally intense operations, particularly in the eXtended Merkle Signature Scheme (XMSS) tree, where WOTS+ (Winternitz One-Time Signature Plus) public key generation consumes the majority of signature generation cycles. With this background, this paper presents Trident, an innovative FPGA-based hardware accelerator that addresses critical performance and resource challenges in XMSS of SLH-DSA. First, we propose a triangle hash unit architecture that enables parallel execution of up to three hash operations simultaneously, directly addressing the computational bottleneck in XMSS tree construction and WOTS+ chain operations. Second, we develop an optimized memory caching scheme that reduces on-chip memory requirements via intermediate value management. Third, we implement the Trident on FPGAs and comprehensively evaluate it across all parameter sets at multiple security levels, i.e., up to 8.6× improvement in signature generation and up to 5.4× speed-up in verification operations. Extended Hypertree evaluation shows a 34.6× area-delay product (ADP) improvement on UltraScale+ FPGA for SLH-DSA-128s. This Trident represents a significant advancement toward practical SLH-DSA deployment in FPGA environments.

title: Exploring Physics Education: Andromeda-Adventure a Serious Immersive Game
authors: Sergio Ruiz-Loza, Linda M. Medina Herrera, Yoel Ledo Mezquita, A. K. Tomatani-Sánchez, Horacio A. Figueredo Rodríguez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This study evaluates the effectiveness of Andromeda, an augmented reality serious game designed to support learning of physics concepts. By integrating AR with game-based learning, Andromeda offers an immersive and interactive environment to explore energy and momentum conservation principles. Using pre- and post-tests with experimental and control groups (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex> = 118), results show that the experimental group achieved learning gains 18 points higher (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-100$</tex> scale) than the control group, a statistically significant difference (t-test, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}=\mathbf{0. 0 4}$</tex>). Student perception surveys also reflect positive attitudes toward the use of mobile AR learning tools. Overall, findings indicate that Andromeda significantly enhances conceptual understanding and problem-solving skills in undergraduate physics courses.

title: A Bayesian Neural Network Approach for Probabilistic Breaking Force Prediction of ACSR Conductors with Uncertainty Decomposition
authors: Xiaoyu Luo, Ganyu Wang, Ming Nie, Yongchun Liang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel method for assessing the tensile strength of ACSR conductors by combining finite element analysis (FEA) with Bayesian neural networks (BNN). To address the limitations of traditional Monte Carlo methods that neglect the helical geometry and mechanical coupling between strands, a three-dimensional finite element model incorporating spiral contact was first established, and parametric analysis was conducted to generate training data. Subsequently, a Bayesian neural network was constructed to achieve high-precision prediction and uncertainty quantification of the breaking force probability distribution. The effectiveness of the proposed method was validated using typical ACSR conductors. Results indicate that compared with traditional methods, the proposed approach improves prediction accuracy by approximately 15% and distinguishes between epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties, providing a scientific basis for reliability design of transmission lines.

title: Wearable Metabolic Sensing for Thermal Comfort Control in Smart and Energy-Efficient Buildings
authors: Pei Zhang, Huihe Qiu
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: At the building and city scale, conventional HVAC systems typically follow fixed temperature setpoints, leading to comfort complaints and energy waste as occupants' thermal states vary over time. Human-centric HVAC control offers a promising solution to improve indoor well-being while reducing building energy use, but it requires accurate, real-time estimation of individuals' metabolic rates for thermal comfort prediction. This study presents a wearable-friendly approach for estimating metabolic rate and, based on this method, develops a wristworn measurement system tailored for next-generation smart buildings. The proposed system replaces bulky calorimetry with a compact, physics-based sensing framework that integrates heart rate, whole-body unit heat loss, skin resistance, and body muscle percentage. Whole-body heat-loss estimation is improved by incorporating a segment-based convective heat loss coefficient, enabling more consistent mapping from wrist measurements across varying environmental conditions. Overall, this work provides scalable, occupant-specific inputs to comfort models and supports the development of closed-loop, human-centric HVAC strategies that simultaneously enhance comfort and reduce energy consumption.

title: Flight Toward Learning: Integrating the DRL Simulator as a Motivational and Educational Strategy in STEM Early Engineering Students
authors: Juan-Gabino Diaz-Martinez, Irandi Gutiérrez-Carmona, Hirohisa Kojima, Sajjad Keshtkar
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper presents an educational innovation implemented at Tecnológico de Monterrey aimed at enhancing STEM motivation, engagement, and foundational engineering skills among high school and early engineering students. The intervention employed a progressive training sequence using the Drone Racing League (DRL) Simulator, transitioning from keyboard input to gamepad controllers and ultimately to professional-grade radio transmitters, culminating in real-flight practice with FPV drones. Two validated motivational instruments—the MUSIC® Model of Motivation Inventory and the MDI-EE (Motivational Diagnosis Instrument for Engineering Education)—were administered before and after the intervention. A pre-experimental one-group pre/post design was used due to institutional constraints that prevented the formation of a control group. Results showed significant increases across all motivational dimensions, including autonomy, perceived usefulness, interest, self-efficacy, and study predisposition. The revised version of this work also incorporates a structured review of related literature, clarification of the research gap, expanded sample and methodological justification, and theoretical grounding based on Self-Determination Theory and Flow Theory, along with explicit study limitations and directions for future research. Overall, the findings suggest that progressive simulator-based drone training is an effective, replicable, and scalable strategy for fostering STEM engagement and strengthening the connection between theoretical concepts and practical engineering experiences.

title: Human-Centric Anomaly Detection in Surveillance Videos Using YOLO-World and Spatio-Temporal Deep Learning
authors: Mohammad Ali Etemadi Naeen, Hoda Mohammadzade, Saeed Bagheri Shouraki
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Detecting anomalous events in surveillance video streams poses substantial challenges, primarily due to the unpredictable nature of abnormal behaviors, severe data imbalance, and interference from complex, scene-dependent backgrounds. In this study, we present a deep learning–based anomaly detection framework that explicitly prioritizes human-related visual information while incorporating sequence-level learning for multi-category event classification. The framework begins by localizing human instances in video frames using YOLO-World, an open-vocabulary detector grounded in vision–language representations, followed by ByteTrack to preserve identity consistency over time. To mitigate the influence of irrelevant environmental context, non-human regions are deliberately de-emphasized via Gaussian blurring, enabling the model to focus on behaviorally salient motion and appearance cues. Spatial features are extracted using an InceptionV3 backbone initialized with ImageNet weights, while long-range temporal dependencies are captured through a bidirectional LSTM architecture for final classification. The proposed approach is assessed on a five-category benchmark derived from the UCF-Crime dataset, including Fighting, Burglary, Explosion, Arson, and Normal events. Experimental results yield a mean classification accuracy of 92.41% obtained from three distinct evaluations, with all categories achieving F1-scores above 0.85. Additional analyses based on ROC characteristics, confusion matrices, and macro- and weighted-level metrics further verify the robustness of the proposed method and its ability to generalize under class-imbalanced conditions. Overall, the findings indicate that emphasizing human-centric foreground information leads to more reliable detection of anomalous activities under complex visual conditions.

title: ESD Breakdown Voltage and Leakage Improvement Through Lithography Process Optimization
authors: Vishal Singh Bartwal, PB Sahoo, Derrick Oon, Akshai Viswanathan, Tan Tze Hian, Karam Veer
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: An increase in leakage related wafer sort failures was observed in Bipolar-CMOS-DMOS (BCD) technology and was found to correlate with reduced breakdown voltage (BV) of a lateral PNP based electrostatic discharge (ESD) structure. When the BV shifts to a lower value, the device turns on earlier and may partially conduct under normal wafer sort bias conditions, resulting in elevated leakage and yield loss. This work investigates the sensitivity of ESD BV to lithography process variation, specifically critical dimension (CD) and overlay of the NWELL layer. Inline analysis revealed tool-to-tool variation in overlay performance, with higher overlay variation showing a correlation to reduced BV and yield loss. A targeted design of experiments (DOE), including focus exposure matrix (FEM) and overlay scaling, was performed to quantify the impact of CD and overlay variation. The results confirmed that increased CD and overlay deviation reduce BV because of the NWELL encroaching toward PWELL across the shallow trench isolation (STI) region and increasing local electric field concentration. To improve process robustness across lithography tools, CD retargeting and tighter overlay control were implemented as preventive action. In addition, retrofit mask modification was introduced to resize NWELL boundary and reduce sensitivity to overlay and CD variation, ensuring stable operation across all tools within normal process variation. These actions improved BV margins by 11% and eliminated leakage-related sort failures across production lots.

title: A Compact and Reliable ACT Gate Driving Scheme Based on Active Clamp Paradigm
authors: Fucheng Liu, Jinpeng Wu, Chunpin Ren, Hui Liu, Han Wang, Lin Dong, Xiangyu Zhao, Qingtao Meng, Jiapeng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The advent of Avalanche Controlled Thyristor (ACT) has addressed the shortage of the turn-off capability of Integrated Gate Commutated Thyristor (IGCT), establishing it as a promising candidate for DC circuit breaker (DCCB). However, its practical application is constrained by the complexity of the gate driver, which conventionally necessitates an additional blocking module for the blocking state. This article proposes a novel ACT gate driving scheme based on active clamp paradigm. By reconfiguring the turn-off module to provide the blocking function, the proposed topology eliminates the need for an additional blocking module. A 4.5 kV ACT prototype was developed to validate the scheme. Experimental results confirm that the proposed driver maintains same functionalities of conventional ACT. Furthermore, the proposed scheme reduces the driver footprint by 18% and static power loss by 15% while offering a more favorable reliability basis than the conventional scheme. These attributes position the proposed scheme as a promising solution for DCCB applications.

title: AI Driven Multimodal Arrival Time Prediction for Urban Autorickshaw and Bus Networks Using GPS Telemetry and Demand–Supply Analytics
authors: Sundarakrishnan Narayanan, Tanmay Umesh, Sohan Varier, Tarun Bhupathi, Mohana
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a unified system for predicting urban travel times across buses and auto-rickshaws in Bengaluru, with an emphasis on real-time accuracy and smooth multimodal travel. The work covers three linked tasks: forecasting when a bus will reach its upcoming stops, estimating auto-rickshaw ride durations, and combining both to compute end-to-end auto–bus–auto journeys. Thisworkwas developed as a part of The Bengaluru Last Mile Challenge 2025 in collaboration with the Indian Institute of Science, BMTC and Namma Yatri.The bus component processes large volumes of GPS telemetry across 7509 BMTC bus routes in the city. A sequence-modeling approach based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with an attention module, is used to learn the temporal movement patterns from historical trajectories, and predicted arrival times are further refined using Kalman Filtering to smooth temporal inconsistencies. Using a fixed sequence length of 60 with a horizon of 6, the model captures short-term dynamics while remaining efficient for large-scale deployment. The system achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 85.84 seconds for bus arrival prediction tackling real world challenges such as Noisy GPS signals and route deviations. The auto-rickshaw module estimates acceptance time, pickup delay, and in-ride travel time using demand–supply patterns, the street network, and time-varying speed data. Via comparison to real life events, the module was able to achieve and error of ~0.54 minutes for acceptance time, ~1.55 minutes for pickup time and ~1.75 minutes for journey time predictions. These two modules are then fused by running one after another, to capture waiting times, transfer reliability, and day-to-day traffic fluctuations. The overall framework is designed to be interpretable, efficient, and resilient to noisy or incomplete data, making it suitable for real-time city-scale deployment. The resulting system is used to predict real-time multimodal travel time estimation for first and last mile integrated journeys, supporting intelligent transport systems, multimodal route planning, and urban mobility analytics.

title: Caf4AVC: LLM-Enhanced Collaborative Framework for Attribute Value Canonicalization in Open KBs
authors: Ying He, Qiang Yang, Yaxin Wang, Dan Gao, Zhouhong Gu, Zhixu Li, Yanghua Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Open Knowledge Bases (Open KBs) are fundamental to knowledge-driven applications, including semantic search, knowledge reasoning, and recommendation systems. However, the presence of redundant and ambiguous expressions within Open KBs significantly hinders their application. This highlights the urgent need for Open KB canonicalization, particularly of attribute values, which comprise nearly 40% of the facts within Open KBs. Unlike entities and predicates, attribute values are inherently sparse and diverse, posing unique challenges for their canonicalization. However, existing studies mainly focus on entities or predicates, leaving attribute value-level noun phrase canonicalization (NPC-AV) underexplored. Large language models (LLMs), with their strengths in common-sense reasoning and fault tolerance, have shown promise in Open KB canonicalization. Yet, current LLM-based approaches often rely heavily on LLM responses, overlooking their high computational cost and potential errors. In this paper, we introduce Caf4AVC, a collaborative framework that integrates clustering-based methods and LLMs for the NPC-AV task. We further propose an innovative two-factor authentication correction mechanism and an adaptive threshold-based selection strategy to address these limitations. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world Open KB datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, achieving a 17.52% reduction in LLM call costs and a 6.3% average performance improvement compared to competitive methods. The code and dataset are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/hedyHe/Caf4AV</uri>.

title: Post-Die-Bond Defect Detection in Semiconductor Packaging AOI Under Extreme Data Scarcity: Copy-Paste Augmentation and Leakage-Aware Evaluation
authors: Der-Fa Chen, Yanyu Lai, Chih-Chung Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this study, we target the early-production bootstrap stage of an automatic optical inspection system, where a new inspection station or production line has started collecting data but true defect cases remain extremely scarce.We evaluated same-station defect localization at an inspection station between die bonding and wire bonding in a semiconductor packaging line. After exact image-level deduplication, the dataset contained 135 images, including 21 defective images and 30 annotated boxes. Because one physical defect could appear in more than one camera view, these annotations corresponded to 19 independent physical defects across 14 die locations. The practical question is whether a first useful defect-localization model can be built before enough defective samples accumulate for conventional supervised training. The experiments used a standard one-stage detector, offline Copy-Paste augmentation, and leakage-aware grouped cross-validation that kept repeated views of the same physical defect in the same fold. Under three random seeds with five folds per seed, the raw baseline reached a mean Average Precision at 0.50 IoU (mAP50) of 0.563, while the CP80× setting reached 0.957 with recall 0.935. As an anomaly-detection reference, PatchCore reached an image-level area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.977 ± 0.020 for separating defect-free and defective images, but it does not produce three-class bounding boxes. These results indicate that fold-local data construction and leakage-aware validation can make same-station early detector bootstrapping practical when defects are rare, while station-specific background regularity and the absence of cross-station validation remain external-validity limitations.

title: SMART: Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking in Complex Motion Scenarios
authors: Zijing Zhao, Xiang Wei, Zhihui Li, Xiaojun Chang, Shunli Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) detects multiple objects in videos and assigns unique identities across consecutive frames, generating complete motion tracks. Existing methods typically associate fixed-size predicted tracks with detections above a threshold, but objects with complex motion patterns, such as sudden changes in speed or direction, often cause motion estimation errors, leading to missed associations and fragmented tracks. To tackle this, we analyze the impact of attributes like speed, size, and detection confidence on tracking performance. Based on this, we propose an adaptive scaling strategy that dynamically adjusts the size of predicted tracks to create adaptively scaled bounding boxes. These bounding boxes enable a Motion-Adaptive IoU (MA-IoU) association strategy for more robust matching between detections and predicted tracks. Additionally, we introduce a self-supervised training method to optimize the adaptive scaling network and MA-IoU across diverse scenarios. Finally, we cascade MA-IoU to develop SMART (Scalable Motion-Adaptive Robust Tracking), a robust and efficient multi-object tracker. SMART achieves state-of-the-art results on BEE24 and DanceTrack. On the BEE24 benchmark, SMART achieves 67.2 MOTA, 63.4 IDF1, and 47.9 HOTA, demonstrating superior performance in handling objects with complex motion and indistinguishable appearances. This comprehensive approach addresses the limitations of prior methods, offering a significant advancement in multi-object tracking.

title: Sensorless Control of Dual Three-Phase PMSM Using a Subspace Decoupling-Based Fuzzy Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Observer
authors: Luhui Xu, Zhiguo Zhao, Peng Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: To address the chattering, parameter sensitivity, and limited operating-condition adaptability of conventional sliding mode observers (SMOs) in sensorless control of dual three-phase permanent magnet synchronous motors (DTP-PMSMs), this paper proposes a subspace-decoupling-based integrated method combining a fuzzy super-twisting sliding mode observer (F-STSMO) and grey wolf optimization (GWO). In the proposed method, fuzzy control is integrated with the super-twisting sliding mode observer (STSMO) to construct a fuzzy time-varying-gain observer. The stability of the observer under time-varying disturbances is demonstrated, which effectively suppresses chattering and improves the observation performance over a wide dynamic range. By exploiting the multiple degrees of freedom of DTP-PMSMs, a GWO-based parameter estimation algorithm is developed. The sensitivity to initial settings and the convergence conditions are discussed, enabling high-accuracy real-time motor parameter estimation and enhancing the robustness of the STSMO against parameter variations. Furthermore, the Lyapunov stability constraints of the integrated F-STSMO and GWO method are analyzed. Experimental results show that the proposed integrated F-STSMO and GWO sensorless control method achieves a resistance estimation error of 0.9% for the DTP-PMSM, reduces the rotor position error caused by parameter variations by 88.6%, and keeps the rotor position error fluctuation within 2.51° under both steady-state and dynamic operating conditions.

title: Short Time-scale Analysis of Dead-time Effect in Dual Active Half-Bridge Converter
authors: Su-Bin Kang, Hyeon-Sik Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In this article, the dead-time effects of a dual active half-bridge (DAHB) converter are analyzed on a short time scale. Dead time distorts the ac-side voltages of both bridges, leading to polarity reversal and phase drift. Consequently, power transfer characteristics depend not only on the phase-shift ratio and voltage ratio but also on the dead-time value. A detailed short time-scale analysis of the operating waveforms is presented over one switching cycle, incorporating various operating states such as buck, match, and boost conditions. From this analysis, precise power flow equations are derived, revealing that the power characteristics become non-monotonic and exhibit power plateau regions depending on the operating modes. Furthermore, a dead-time compensation algorithm is proposed to mitigate these distortions and linearize the output voltage control. Finally, the validity of the derived model is confirmed through simulation and experimental results under extensive power flow conditions.
