title: UC-Secure Multi-Factor Authentication with Dynamic Password Recovery and Fine-Grained Access Control_supp1-3700850.pdf
authors: Ding Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) has been widely applied in various fields, including smart homes, autonomous driving, and mobile communication. Although a number of MFA schemes with different security goals and properties have been proposed, most of them are found to pay little attention to password forgetting and loss issues, which may lead to the permanent loss of the account. Additionally, little effort has been devoted to designing MFA schemes with fine-grained access control to perform authentication flexibly. Therefore, the above issues raise the question of “ how to construct a MFA scheme with dynamic password recovery and fine-grained access control?”. In this paper, we, for the first time, introduce attributes and a dynamic password recovery method to propose a multi-factor authentication scheme with dynamic password recovery and fine-grained control, named MFA-DPRF. In MFA-DPRF, authentication succeeds only when a user provides both a valid password and a set of attributes satisfying the specified access policy, thereby enabling fine-grained access control. Particularly, flexible access control can be achieved by verifying if user’s attributes meet the specific access policy. Furthermore, a dynamic password recovery method based on secret questions and the secret sharing technique has been designed to address password forgetting and loss issues. Users can not only recover the original password, but also update secret values such as security questions to enhance security. The security of MFA-DPRF can be reduced to the computational Diffie-Hellman problem under the Random Oracle Model. In addition, we analyze the security of MFA-DPRF in the universally composable (UC) framework, which ensures desirable properties such as composable security. The informal analysis proves that MFA-DPRF is secure against known attacks. Compared with the state-of-the-art works, performance analysis shows that MFA-DPRF is superior in security and efficiency.

title: Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter for Robust Cellular/MEMS-INS Localization
authors: Xumin Feng, Tian Jin, Honglei Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)-denied urban environments, cellular/microelectromechanical system-inertial navigation system (MEMS-INS) integrated positioning provides a promising solution for continuous navigation. However, in such environments, signal propagation is severely affected by multipath, blockage, and environmental dynamics, leading to heterogeneous measurement uncertainties, including time-varying noise, impulsive outliers, and persistent NLOS biases, which are difficult to handle within conventional estimation frameworks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Variational Bayesian Joint Measurement Anomaly and Noise Inference Filter (VB-JMANIF) for robust positioning. The proposed method explicitly models NLOS-induced biases, impulsive outliers, and time-varying measurement noise, and jointly integrates anomaly classification, bias estimation, noise adaptation, and state update within an error-state cubature Kalman filter (CKF) framework. Simulation results demonstrate that VB-JMANIF consistently outperforms the conventional CKF and several state-of-the-art robust and noise-adaptive methods in terms of positioning accuracy, estimation stability, and robustness. Field experiments using real cellular signals further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, achieving horizontal positioning Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) values of 4.7 m and 6.1 m under moderate and severely degraded urban measurement conditions, respectively, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability in real-world environments.

title: A Methodology for Development and Characterization of PVA-Based Phantoms for Breast Elastography
authors: Alexey García Padilla, Ivonne Bazán Trujillo
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Breast elastography is a group of ultrasound imaging techniques based on tissue stiffness. Validation of these techniques requires constructing phantoms in a controlled manner to obtain reproducible acoustic and mechanical properties and ensure experimental repeatability. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used for phantoms due to their stability, durability, and ease of construction in a wide range of medical applications. This paper presents a methodology for fabricating PVA phantoms for breast elastography with two types of ultrasonic scatterers: graphite and Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>. A methodology was described for characterizing mechanically the phantoms using the Young's modulus by a computerized numerical control (CNC) system. Phantoms were constructed to emulate the Young's modulus of healthy breast tissue and benign tumors. The methodology employed for the construction of healthy tissue phantoms involved the use of a 10% PVA concentration, in combination with a single freeze-thaw cycle (FTC). The mean Young's modulus obtained using graphite was 6.12 kPa, while for Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> was 12.14 kPa for healthy tissue. For benign tissue phantoms, a 15% PVA concentration was utilized, in combination with two FTCs. The mean Young's modulus resulting from the use of graphite was found to be 38.50 kPa, while for Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, it was 39.21 kPa for benign tissue.

title: Analysis of Resistance Performance of High Dose Boron Doping
authors: Chea Hui Lee, Bohesharvind N. Murthi, Hank Chen, Wan Chun Zhang, Leonard M. Rubin
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Thanks to their flexibility, single wafer implanters, such as the Axcelis Purion H200 are capable of delivering device electrical performance comparable to conventional multiple wafer machines, despite different machines having different beamlines and scanning systems. In this work, we study the impact of dose rate of a very high dose p-type implantation, boron 15 keV 8.50×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">15</sup> ions/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> on a polysilicon layer and compare the sheet resistance between a single wafer high current implanter, Axcelis Purion H200, and a 13-wafer batch high current implanter, Axcelis GSD200E<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Different energies, dose, beam current, chiller temperature, implant mode and vertical scan speed are implanted on the Purion H200 on this low energy/high dose polysilicon layer to match the sheet resistance performance of the batch tool. On the Purion H200, the energy trim factor (deceleration) has been selected for a better polysilicon sheet resistance performance matching with minimal recipe modification to reduce logistical efforts.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Ridge-Meta Ensemble Model for Electrical Load Forecasting in Integrated Energy Systems
authors: Ruiting Shen, Changhao Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Reliable electrical load forecasting is the backbone for precise operation and intelligent scheduling of Integrated Energy Systems (IES). This study proposes a ridge-meta ensemble approach using ridge regression to merge three gradient boosting techniques: XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, and predict electrical energy consumption. By fusing the three gradient boosting models with a ridge regression meta-learner, the framework balances high accuracy and computational efficiency. Validation uses 2021-2023 hourly electrical load data from Arizona State University's Tempe Campus, a real-world dataset with complex meteorological and temporal correlations. Test results show the ridge-meta ensemble model achieves an R-squared value of 0.8973, with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1079.31 kilowatts and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 1490.39 kilowatts. Compared to traditional linear regression, this represents a 46.15% reduction in MAE and a 42.93% reduction in RMSE. Through SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis, we found that wet-bulb temperature and seasonal fluctuations are the primary factors influencing load predictions. With low computational overhead, the proposed model offers a feasible solution for real-time IES scheduling and management.

title: Assessment of New Energy Carrying Capacity and Identification of Weak Links in Distribution Networks
authors: Dexiang Li, Huijie Sun, Zhijian Du, Kaihua Sun, Tianlu Luo
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper investigates carrying capacity assessment and weak-link identification for new energy integration in distribution networks under the background of new power systems, with consideration of the complexity introduced by diverse energy injection characteristics. First, an optimal power flow model based on second-order cone programming (SOCP) is established, which incorporates operational constraints to evaluate the effective distribution capacity under different scenarios, thereby overcoming the limitations of traditional static analysis. Then, a weak node identification method based on hosting capacity and a multi-dimensional constraint factor analysis system are proposed. Finally, case verification is conducted using an improved IEEE 13-node distribution system. The results show that this method can accurately evaluate the spatial distribution characteristics of system carrying capacity, effectively identify system weak links and their constraint factors, providing important references for new energy planning decisions and system optimization.

title: Equivalent Modeling Method for Virtual Synchronous Generator Converters Based on Hierarchical Clustering
authors: Kesheng Wang, Dongxing Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy sources, a large number of power electronic devices are integrated into power systems, leading to a significant increase in system complexity. Fast and accurate electromagnetic transient simulation of power systems with inverter-based resources therefore becomes essential. By drawing on the coherency based equivalencing criteria developed for synchronous generators, this paper proposes a coherency identification method suitable for virtual synchronous generator converters. The proposed method adopts the similarity of converter power angle and voltage dynamic responses during fault conditions as the clustering criterion. Hierarchical clustering is applied for coherency identification, and parameters of the equivalent converters are subsequently aggregated. Finally, a multi converter system with virtual synchronous generator control is established in PowerFactory, and the dynamic responses of the equivalent model and the detailed model are compared under typical fault scenarios. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high equivalencing accuracy while significantly improving simulation efficiency.

title: An Adaptive Voltage Control Approach Employing Distributional Soft Actor-Critic for Coordinated PV and BESS Systems
authors: Yue Wang, Zhijia Du, Xiaochun Xu, Dengcai Gong, Xiaowei Miao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The penetration of distributed photovoltaic generation and battery energy storage in distribution networks is increasing rapidly, which intensifies the difficulty of voltage regulation and loss management under stochastic power injections. This paper proposes an adaptive voltage control strategy based on distributional soft actor-critic for coordinated photovoltaic and storage operation. The voltage and var optimization task is formulated as a Markov decision process, where the agent observes key operating variables such as nodal injections, bus voltages, and the storage state of charge, then outputs continuous setpoints for photovoltaic active and reactive power as well as storage charging and discharging power. By learning a distributional value representation, the proposed method mitigates value overestimation and improves training stability under strong renewable fluctuations. Case studies on a modified IEEE 34-bus feeder indicate that the proposed controller suppresses voltage violations while reducing real power losses, and it achieves faster and more stable convergence than deep deterministic policy gradient and soft actor-critic baselines.

title: A Mean-Value Performability Assessment of IoMT Systems Based-on Stochastic Reward Net and Fuzzy Lz-transform Method
authors: Moraru Victor, Guțuleac Emilian, Alexei Anatol, Gribincea Dorin, Cuzmin Simion, Seniușin Anton
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents a predictive approach for uncertainty mean-value performability modeling and assessments framework of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) System (IoMTS) based-on the Stochastic Reward Nets with Fuzzy parameters (FSRNs) and Fuzzy Lz-transform method (FLz-TM). This framework allows the development of compact models and efficient evaluation of the availability and cost security impact in which is used a proactive defense time-based Moving Target Defense technique. The application of FSRN and FLz-TM considerably improve computational efficiency of performability analysis, avoiding the curse of IoMT system states dimensionality. A numerical case study for mean-value performability analysis of a particular IoMT is given to show the effectiveness of proposed approach.

title: How Machines Learn to Decide When to Alarm: A Case Study on Temperature Control
authors: Amir Hossein Baradaran, Mahdi Naghibi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Temperature control is a critical factor in many industries, including the plastics and packaging industries, where precise thermal management is essential for product quality and operational safety. This highlights the importance of temperature control systems and alarm mechanisms to notify operators when conditions deviate from acceptable ranges. The application of intelligent methods in such industries can significantly enhance control accuracy and operational reliability. Despite this, no prior studies have investigated the use of machine learning to learn the behavior of a Kiefel machine’s temperature control system. This article presents an innovative approach in which machine learning algorithms are trained to replicate the on/off behavior of a temperature controller. Various supervised learning models, including Naive Bayes, Support Vector Machines (SVM) with different kernels, Logistic Regression, and k-Nearest Neighbors, were evaluated and compared. Results show that the SVM with a radial basis function (RBF) kernel achieved the highest performance, reaching an accuracy of 99.94%. The model’s performance was further analyzed using additional evaluation metrics, confirming its reliability in accurately learning and reproducing the temperature control behavior. This study highlights the potential of machine learning to model industrial control logic and establishes a foundation for intelligent, data-driven control and alarm systems.

title: Anode Screen Effect on the Electroplated Nickel Layer
authors: Sanjoy Paul, Vilmarie Soler, John Pennacchia, John Garant, Brian O’Hara, Elizabeth Karapetyan, Edward Swinnich
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper presents a development work to reduce foreign material (FM) counts and improve yield. The FM (>50 μm) originated from the nickel (Ni) anodes in the electroplating process. To resolve this FM issue, a Ni anode screen was introduced in the Ni electroplating bath in front of the anodes. To compare the Ni plating process, we processed wafers with and without the Ni anode screen for stress, grain structure, and plated thickness analysis. A significant FM reduction was observed with similar stress level. However, we observed a 4x plated height increase with the Ni anode screen when compared to the process without the Ni anode screen. A possible reason could be the change in current density due to the screen incorporation and Ni grain structure.

title: Low-Cost Concrete Manipulatives for Multivariable Calculus: A Classroom Pre-Post Study on Selfefficacy and 3D Reasoning
authors: Juan Felipe Carmona, Yiby Morales, Jose M. Nieto-Jalil
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This classroom-embedded study investigates whether low-cost, paper/cardboard manipulatives can strengthen students' perceived competence on core three-dimensional topics in Multivariable Calculus. We implemented a foldable, L-shaped modeling base with tangible surface pieces to support hands-on exploration of surfaces, accumulation for multiple integrals, and coordinate transformations, coupled with a standardized lesson sequence (conceptual activation, guided construction and exploration, representation bridge, exit ticket, and reuse). Four course sections (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{1 0 0}$</tex> students) used the materials; inferential analyses focus on one representative section with anonymous pre (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n}=29$</tex>) and post (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n}=26$</tex>) surveys. Two self-efficacy items targeted translations of planar curves and the representation of 2 D equations as 3 D surfaces. The share selecting the top two confidence categories increased from 0.707 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.923(\Delta=0.216$</tex>; Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$h=0.58$</tex>; two-sided <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p=0.0040$</tex>; 95 % CI <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$[0.078,0.354]$</tex>; normalized gain <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{g}=0.74$</tex>). Item-level contrasts were 69.0 % to 88.5 % for translations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(z=1.75,\ \mathrm{p}=0.0805$</tex>) and 72.4 % to 96.2 % for 2D to 3D representation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{z}=2.38, \mathrm{p}=0.0175)$</tex>. Results suggest that haptic-visual coupling, when explicitly bridged to sketches and symbolic expressions, is a scalable way to enhance students' readiness to engage surfaces, solids, and coordinate changes. Limitations include self-report outcomes, unpaired samples, and a single-site context.

title: Exploratory SVM Modeling of Facial Angular Asymmetry Using the Triglyceride-Glucose Index
authors: Jesús Fausto Córdova-Manzo, Gabriela Flores-Mondragón, Ángel Eduardo Llanas-Soto, Jana Liseth Tovilla-Trejo, Jairo Rahé López-Sánchez, Ángel Adrián Pascacio-Glaizar, Arturo Vera-Hernández, Josefina Gutiérrez-Martínez
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Peripheral facial palsy (PFP) exhibits heterogeneous motor impairment that may be influenced by systemic metabolic status. This study evaluates the joint contribution of total facial angular asymmetry (AsAnT) and the triglyceride–glucose (TyG) index to distinguish PFP patients from controls. A dataset of 120 subjects was analyzed using statistical testing, decision trees, and Support Vector Machines (SVM). PFP patients showed significantly higher AsAnT and TyG values (p < 0.001), with a weak but significant association between both variables. SVM classifiers achieved stable cross-validated accuracy (~72%) across feature representations. These findings indicate a weak but statistically significant association between facial angular asymmetry and metabolic status, supporting the feasibility of exploratory, hypothesis-generating modeling rather than causal inference.

title: Quantum-Resistant Cryptography for Secure IoT Communication Systems
authors: Aakash S, Kalish S, Nivetha S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems has increased the need for communication mechanisms that can remain secure against emerging computational threats. Conventional asymmetric cryptographic schemes, including RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), are vulnerable to advances in quantum computing due to the ability of Shor's algorithm to undermine their underlying mathematical assumptions. To address these challenges, this paper presents PQ-IoT SecureCom, a quantum-resistant communication framework built using NIST-standardized post-quantum cryptographic primitives. The proposed framework employs ML-KEM (derived from Kyber) for secure key encapsulation and ML-DSA (derived from Dilithium) for digital authentication, combined with AESGCM to ensure data confidentiality and integrity. Additional security mechanisms, including PQC-based device certification, periodic session key updates, and nonce-based timestamp validation, are incorporated to mitigate replay and impersonation attacks. The framework also supports a hybrid operational mode that enables performance comparison between classical and postquantum cryptographic schemes. System behavior is monitored using a real-time dashboard that records cryptographic overhead, communication metrics, and detected security events. Experimental evaluation shows that ML-KEM and ML-DSA significantly enhance security while introducing computational overhead that remains acceptable for contemporary IoT devices. Through automated attack simulations and detailed event logging, PQ-IoT SecureCom demonstrates the practical feasibility of deploying scalable post-quantum security in IoT communication systems.

title: Exploring Mentor Motivation in Participating in an International Congress on Engineering Education
authors: Alejandro Adorjan, Maite Korona, Cecilia Castro, Mariana Porta
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Engineering mentoring programs promote student participation throughout their academic years, fostering the development and reinforcement of soft skills essential to their personal and professional growth. In this study, we posed the following research question (RQ): What are the primary motivations that lead engineering students, who serve as mentors, to voluntarily participate in the organisation of an International Conference on Education? The methodological approach was mixed, combining quantitative and qualitative methods. Regarding the findings, although the motivational aspects are diverse and complex, a primary initial insight concerns the role of certification and a sense of institutional belonging. Certification was one of the main factors that mentors cited as key to their participation. For future research, we plan to continue exploring extracurricular activities, further develop the survey, and identify mentoring program activities that strengthen the soft and interpersonal skills essential for future engineering professionals.

title: BI-RADS Classification of Breast Tumors in Ultrasound Using Convolutional Autoencoder-Based Feature Extractor
authors: W. Gómez-Flores, W. C. A. Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: A convolutional autoencoder (CAE)-based feature extractor is presented for the classification of breast tumors in ultrasound (BUS) images into BI-RADS categories. The model uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) for BI-RADS classification, taking encoder feature maps at multiple abstraction levels as input. Both CAE and CNN parameters are learned simultaneously via a loss function that combines mean squared error (focused on minimizing image reconstruction error) and categorical cross-entropy (focused on minimizing BI-RADS classification error). The experiments use a dataset consisting of 1875 BUS images, with tumors annotated according to BI-RADS categories 2-5. The input BUS images are masked to enhance the shape of the tumoral region. The results indicate that this approach outperforms four pre-trained CNNs and two CAE-based methods in BI-RADS classification, achieving a global accuracy of 0.68. When BI-RADS predictions are dichotomized into benign (categories 2 and 3) and malignant (categories 4 and 5) classes, the approach reached a sensitivity of 0.90 and an AUC of 0.92. The improvement in classification performance is attributed to the model’s ability to incorporate features of varying complexity, from general features in the initial layers to more subtle features in the latent space. This characteristic differentiates this method from traditional CAE-based classification techniques, which typically rely solely on features from the latent space.

title: Day-ahead Robust Pricing Strategy for Load Aggregators Considering User Response Uncertainty
authors: Jialu Li, Yixue Fan, Yanzhe Ren, Rui An, Yuxiong Huang, Zhaohong Bie
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: As load-side resources are increasingly recognized as vital components for enhancing power system flexibility, their integration into market-clearing and settlement frameworks has become a critical priority. A Robust Differentiated Pricing strategy is proposed in this paper to address the stochastic perturbations of end-users and the truncation-based assessment risks encountered during the execution phase of electricity markets. Although Load Aggregators (LAs) are established as essential intermediaries for managing fragmented demand-side resources, they are subjected to significant profit risks arising from unpredictable user behavior and stringent penalties for response deviations. To mitigate these risks, the power deviation characteristics of heterogeneous resources—specifically Interruptible Loads (IL), Directly-Controlled Loads (DLC), and Transferred Loads (TL)—are extracted through Monte Carlo sampling. A stochastic-informed robust pricing framework is subsequently developed to maximize the comprehensive revenue of the LA by optimizing differentiated internal incentive prices. It is demonstrated through simulation results that response redundancy is effectively induced by the proposed R-DP scheme by shifting the response center toward a secure median range within the assessment interval. Consequently, settlement security and profit stability are significantly enhanced, and the uncertainties of user responses in complex market environments are successfully hedged.

title: Experimental Evaluation of Planar Inductors for Application in Buck-Type DC-DC Converters
authors: Olimpian T. Paulet, Dorin O. Neacsu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of using planar inductors within the functionality of a Buck converter, utilizing the EVAL_TDA38826_1VOUT evaluation platform. Two construction topologies for planar inductors are analyzed, the circular geometry topology and the rectangular geometry topology, both in forced continuous conduction mode at various switching frequencies. The experimental section examines the waveform shapes for the converter's output voltage and the switching node specific to the switching transistor. The study discusses the results obtained from testing the planar inductors under identical operating conditions and, finally, provides conclusions based on the results obtained.

title: Effect of Semiconductor Surface Characteristics & Cleaning Chemicals on Contact Angle Measurement
authors: Tatt Wai Wan, Pei Li Ooi, Yong Sheng Chong, Masrina Mohd Nadzir
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Contact angle (CA) measurement is a powerful technique for characterizing material surface properties. However, the typical application of Goniometer in semiconductor manufacturing is on blank wafer surface wetting analysis. In this study, a customized CA measurement setup is developed to measure CA on structured wafers with different chips’ density. With the aid of ImageJ software, the CA of the droplet images taken from the setup is analysed. The setup demonstrated an increase in CA on structured wafers from low, mid to high chips’ density due to the higher density of scrap lines. Lower CA is also observed on implanted silicon (Si) surface. After the cleaning with 10% H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>, all these wafers show further reduction of CA due to the removal of contaminants. The setup also demonstrated that the lowest CA of the structured wafer can be achieved when the cleaning chemicals combinations include diluted hydrofluoric acid (DHF). Lower CA is preferred during the wafer cleaning process not only due to the higher hydrophilic surface that favoured the wetting of chemicals but also the reduction in the affinity of bubbles on the wafers. Higher or longer affinity of bubbles on the wafer surface will result in wafer floating or ineffective cleaning during the wet process, which should be avoided in any other times.

title: Emerging Trends of Gender Bias Within Students' Evaluation of Teaching (SET) in Higher Education: a Mini-Review and Bibliometric Analysis
authors: Efraín Abdiel López Velázquez, Asad Abbas, Bahareh Berenjforoush Azar, Soumaya Abdellatif
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Reflecting the global commitment to equitable education articulated in the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals, this study investigates students' evaluation of teaching (SET) instruments widely used in higher education, which often contain many biased elements. This mini-review and bibliometric analysis aimed to identify major thematic breakthroughs in SET research, particularly regarding scientific production and collaboration, and to reveal trends in gender bias and its association with SET. To achieve these objectives, the mini-review and bibliometric analysis performed used the Scopus database, focusing on publications from 2020 to 2024. Data from seven papers were analyzed using PRISMA guidelines and Biblioshiny data visualization tools to synthesize the scholarly work. Key findings revealed a declining trend in recent scholarly publications about SET. United States of America notably leads the world in global scientific contributions in this area. Significantly, the analysis consistently highlights pervasive gender and intersectional biases that influence SET outcomes. These biases often disadvantage specific instructor demographics and tend to favor same-gender affinity among evaluators. This study provides a synthesized understanding of SET gender bias through its robust methodological framework. It offers valuable insights for promoting more equitable evaluation practices and fostering inclusive academic environments.

title: A Robust LinUCB for Optimal User Selection in Fatigue-Aware Demand Response
authors: Hongrui Chen, Yuanshi Zhang, Xiaojing Qi, Yan Li, Rushuai Han, Weiqi Pan, Manshang Wang, Xiankai Sun
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Optimal user selection in demand response (DR) programs is challenging due to the non-stationary compliance behaviors of residential users. Existing learning-based dispatch algorithms primarily focus on expected response rates, leaving them vulnerable to extreme tail-risk defaults induced by cumulative user fatigue. This paper formulates the DR target tracking problem as a partially observable Markov decision process and proposes the Robust LinUCB algorithm. To overcome the structural model misspecification of linear estimation, an empirical Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) metric is introduced to precisely quantify historical prediction residuals. Furthermore, a dynamic penalty mechanism is developed by embedding real-time fatigue as an exponential multiplier into the CVaR evaluation. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly minimizes tracking regret and successfully prevents systemic fatigue breakdowns under various operational pressures.

title: Enhancing Cybersecurity Education with Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Virtual Teaching Assistant Approach
authors: Gaurab Baral, Sushant Shrestha, Mithlesh Sah, Ankur Chattopadhyay
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper presents a novel research study based upon the development and usage of a unique virtual teaching assistant (vTA) powered by a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) model using OpenAI's conversational agent technology. vTA was built as an AI (Artificial Intelligence) powered chatbot and was used in two graduate-level online cybersecurity courses: Risk Management and Data Privacy. It answers questions on course materials and helps support the student users with the online teaching & learning process, including online correspondence related to the class topics, materials and assignments. We describe the vTA system architecture, as well as the student usage patterns, and gather feedback plus learner experience data from 96 graduate students across the 2 cybersecurity classes through an online survey questionnaire. Our collected survey data indicates that participating students found vTA to be helpful as a supporting medium. Additionally, we analyze the student interactions with vTA using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to uncover key themes and to assess how well student questions aligned with the vTA system's responses. Based on the promising results obtained through this preliminary pilot study, we determine the efficacy and impact of vTA integrated online instruction. To our knowledge, this is one of the first applications of a RAG-powered virtual teaching assistant in cybersecurity education, and it is also a first-of-its-kind study on the utilization of an AI chatbot in cybersecurity teaching as a supplementary aid in distance learning mode. Our overall findings suggest that AI chatbots, like vTA, can meaningfully enhance learning in online cybersecurity education.

title: A Framework for Critical AI Literacy in Engineering Education: Preparing Students for an Algorithmic Future
authors: Lydia Velázquez-García, Antonio Cedillo-Hernández, Roberto Martínez-Román
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The accelerating addition of artificial intelligence (AI) into engineering practice and education demands more than technical proficiency—it requires a literate, critical mindset capable of navigating algorithmic systems, ethical dilemmas, and socio-technical transformations. This paper presents a conceptual framework for Critical AI Literacy (CAL) tailored to engineering education. Based on contemporary research in AI literacy, data ethics, and engineering education, the framework delineates four interrelated dimensions: Awareness, Technical Fluency, Ethical Agency, and Critical Reflection, and includes them in educational pathways for addition into undergraduate engineering programs. By shifting from an instrumental view of AI competence (i.e., “how to use” AI) to a reflective, agency-centered orientation (i.e., “how to understand and question” AI), the proposed CAL framework offers educators and program designers a structured lens for developing engineers who are not only technically capable but also ethically and critically aware. Implications for curriculum design, faculty development, and future research are discussed. As a conceptual contribution, this paper is intended as a foundational proposition for future empirical validation and development.

title: Study on Broadband Power Fluctuations and Losses of DC-Link Capacitors in T-Type Three-Level Flexible Interconnection Equipment
authors: Lei Luo, Xueying Zhang, Huazhen Cao, Ran Cheng, Yao Duan, Minhua Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Flexible interconnection equipment facilitates fault transfer and power smoothing while enhancing the integration of stochastic sources and loads. However, insufficient reliability, specifically DC link capacitor failure, restricts large scale application. This paper investigates T-type three-level flexible interconnection equipment, focusing on the degradation mechanism of DC-link capacitor reliability induced by harmonic currents and thermal stress. Under sine-wave pulse width modulation (SPWM), a double Fourier series expansion method is employed to develop frequency-domain models for switching functions and capacitor currents. The harmonic distribution characteristics of the current and their variation with the carrier phase time delay τ are revealed. Subsequently, a frequency-domain calculation method for capacitor losses based on an energy coefficient is proposed. Through linearization, the model is further simplified into an analytical expression directly correlated with system power. Compared to traditional methods, the proposed approach accurately quantifies the regulation effect of the carrier time delay τ on capacitor harmonic stress and establishes a simplified model linking capacitor losses to system power (P, Q). This addresses the issues of high computational burden and low accuracy inherent in conventional models, providing theoretical support for the reliability design and online operational optimization of capacitors in flexible interconnection equipment.

title: Sensitivity Enhancement of Quantum Radar Using Entangled Single Photon Source for Military Defense Security
authors: Simanchalo Panigrahi, Tejaswini Patro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Quantum radar represents a promising advancement over classical radar systems by exploiting quantum mechanical phenomena such as entanglement and quantum measurement to overcome fundamental detection limitations. This paper reviews the evolution of radar technology from conventional radar frequency systems to quantum radar architectures, with emphasis on sensitivity enhancement and detection of RF-stealth targets for military defense applications. Classical radar parameters such as radar range, transmitted power and cross section are revisited and extended to their quantum analogues. The role of entangled single photon sources in improving detection performance under high noise and jamming environments is discussed. Context of microwave quantum radar is reviewed along with photonic implementations. This paper further highlights the importance of quantum measurement theory, including system-apparatus entanglement, density matrix formalism and wave function collapse in extracting information from quantum radar systems. Although significant experimental challenges remain, recent advances indicate strong potential for future development of quantum radar in next generation defense and surveillance systems.

title: An Explainable MFCC-Based Deep Learning Framework for Song Emotion Recognition
authors: Hesamoddin Pourrostami, Mousa Nazari, Mohammad M. AlyanNezhadi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Automatically identifying the emotional elements within a piece of music and understanding music sentiment through audio captures an essential step within the realm of affective computing and music intelligence. This paper introduces a hybrid architecture that combines CNN, BiLSTM, and Attention, utilising CNN to analyse MFCC features extracted from audio signals, BiLSTM to model the audio sequence, and Attention to pinpoint emotionally salient regions. In addition to the mentioned features, the proposed architecture incorporates cost-sensitive learning to enhance performance and mitigate data imbalance. The MFCC-based deep learning framework is applied to song music in the RAVDESS dataset. The proposed method achieved an overall accuracy of 91.6% and a macro F1-score of 92.3%. Explainable AI, utilising Gradient-Based Feature Importance, focuses on the low-spectral to mid-spectral regions where the audio is emotionally and temporally impressive. These confirm that the proposed architecture is streamlined to label singing through music analysis emotionally. This can be extended to various applications, such as affective music in music therapy and Human-computer interaction.

title: Implementation and Testing of B-Dot Algorithm on CubeSat 2U Nanosatellite Prototype
authors: Andrei Spatari, Vladimir Melnic, Maxim Kapusteanski, Alexei Martîniuc
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents the design and testing of an attitude determination and control system (ADCS) for a 2U CubeSat prototype, using magnetorquers and the B-dot algorithm. The choice of this solution is motivated by the low power consumption and low mass, compared to reaction wheels. The proposed system integrates hardware components (STM32 microcontroller, MPU9250 sensor, communication and data storage modules) and a software architecture capable of implementing the B-dot algorithm for angular velocity reduction and satellite orientation. Experimental results show that the algorithm enables the stabilization and reorientation of the prototype, but with a relatively long response time, caused by the low magnetic moment of the coils. The study highlights the feasibility of the solution, but also the need to optimize the magnetorquers design to improve performance.

title: Faster Estimation of Hilbert Transform for Photoacoustic and Ultrasound Biomicroscopy Applications
authors: Raymundo Cordero, João Carlos Machado, Krishynan Shanty Fernandes, Isabel Nunes Ramalho Da Rocha, Juliana Oliveira Da Silva Fernandes
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Nowadays, Ultrasound Biomicroscopy (UBM) and Photoacoustic (PA) are powerful approaches for the development of high-resolution, non-invasive biomedical imaging systems. Both UBM and PA produce radiofrequency (RF) signals whose envelopes are used to reconstruct the tissue images. These envelopes can be obtained through Hilbert transform. Usually, the imaginary part of the analytic signal related to the RF signal is used to efficiently get the Hilbert transform. However, as many tissue samples are analyzed in UBM and PA, faster digital processing methods, including a faster envelope detection method, are required to implement imaging systems. This paper proposes a simplified method to calculate the Hilbert transform based on data splitting and the properties of Fast Fourier transform (FFT). The splitting data strategy used in FFT is applied to reduce the number of arithmetic operations used to get the imaginary part of the analytic signal. Thus, different from other methods such as filtering or convolution, the proposed approach is mathematically equivalent to the method to get the Hilbert transform through the analytic signal. Tests prove that the proposed approach allows a faster estimation of the Hilbert transform for PA and UBM applications.

title: A Comparative Study on Data Structures for Power Electronical Circuit Topology Derivation
authors: Botao Cao, Ke Jin, Xujun Jiang, Xingshuo Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The conventional topology derivation methods suffer from low efficiency and difficulties in exhaustively enumerating all possible topological structures. New approaches utilizing different graph-based storage structures still lack a comparative study on the applicability of various storage formats under different circuit conditions. Using the Buck converter as an example, this paper conducts a systematic comparative study of three graph-based storage structures, which include the adjacency matrix, adjacency list, and orthogonal list. Their performance is evaluated across three key dimensions: memory usage, runtime efficiency under different circuit scales, and capability to handle bidirectional components. It is indicated that each structure exhibits distinct advantages: the adjacency matrix is suitable for small-scale topology verification; the adjacency list offers balanced performance in medium-scale circuits; while the orthogonal list achieves optimal efficiency in large-scale circuits with multiple bidirectional components. This study provides a quantitative basis for selecting data structures in automated topology derivation. All findings are thoroughly validated through simulations.

title: Power-Loop-Embedded Capacitor-Current Active Damping for Low-THD Islanded VSG–LCL Inverters
authors: Weide Guan, Peiling Jiang, Yang Zhou, Su Liu, Yingxi Zhu, Zhenbang Wang, Yang Chen, Zihao Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Islanded microgrids employing virtual synchronous generator (VSG) controlled inverters with LCL filters are prone to resonance-induced harmonic amplification due to the absence of grid stiffness and damping. This paper proposes a power-loop-embedded active damping strategy, where the filter capacitor current is fed back and injected at the interface between the VSG power-loop output and the voltage-control reference. The proposed injection emulates an equivalent virtual resistor in parallel with the LCL capacitor, thereby increasing the effective damping ratio and suppressing resonance peaks without sacrificing the desired VSG dynamics. A voltage–current dual-loop regulator is combined with the damping path to ensure stable voltage/frequency regulation and improved power quality in islanded operation. MATLAB/Simulink results on a 20-kW, 380-V prototype inverter with an LCL filter (L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf>=200 μH, L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>=40 μH, C=4.4 μF) verify that the proposed method reduces the output-current THD from 44.14% to 11.18% under rated resistive loading and maintains robust transient behavior under 0-20 kW load steps in islanded mode.

title: Study of Propellants for Muzzle Velocity Enhancement in Medium Caliber Gun Ammunition
authors: Durlav Swain, Deepak Govindraj, Nagendra Kumar, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The paper is focused on the enhancement of muzzle velocity for small and medium caliber gun ammunitions, typically those used by Army Air Defence, with an aim to cater for present day highly agile and small air targets such as Drones and Unmanned Aerial Systems. The paper covers the design aspects of lab feasible and institutionally permissible, gun barrel along with the requisite sub parts and accessories which could facilitate testing of gun propellant performance, in terms of chamber pressure generation and muzzle velocity achieved. The paper includes experimental test results on five types gun propellants with different composition primarily Nitrogen rich materials, which channelised the way forward for further research work.

title: Calculation Method for Induced Voltage in Non-Fully Parallel Lines
authors: Chenglin Liu, Caibo Liao, Xianfa Tian, Wanqing Wang, Jinbo Li, Hongliang Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate assessment of induced voltage in non-fully parallel transmission lines is crucial for operational safety. Traditional methods are challenged by non-uniform electromagnetic coupling in such lines due to their complex spatial configurations. A calculation method based on an improved two-dimensional equivalent model is therefore proposed. Conventional approaches are constrained by either complex modeling requirements or limited applicability to fully parallel lines. Through finite element simulations, the influence of spatial geometric parameters on electromagnetic coupling was investigated. Subsequently, an improved two-dimensional equivalent model incorporating correction factors was developed. The proposed method requires no complex modeling procedures. It can be implemented by modifying capacitance and inductance matrices based on traditional calculations for fully parallel lines. Applications include non-parallel configurations, cross-spanning conditions, and various grounding scenarios. In case validation, calculation errors were determined as 2.4% for electrostatic induced voltage and 5% for electromagnetic induced voltage. The accuracy and practical applicability of the method were confirmed. This provides reliable technical references for safety evaluations during de-energized maintenance operations.

title: Research on EMT Simulation of Power Systems Based on Coordination of ADPSS and MATLAB/Simulink
authors: Minli Gao, Haiyan Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the in-depth advancement of new-type power system construction, power systems are exhibiting the "dual-high" characteristics of high proportion of renewable energy and high proportion of power electronic equipment integration. This places higher demands on the accuracy, flexibility, and modeling efficiency of electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations. A key limitation of traditional EMT simulation tools is the inherent difficulty in developing custom component models, a process characterized by significant complexity and time consumption. Consequently, these platforms create a bottleneck for the timely evaluation and validation of control algorithms for next-generation power electronic systems. To address this issue, this paper proposes an ADPSS-MATLAB/Simulink co-simulation method for power system EMT analysis, integrating the high-precision EMT simulation capability of ADPSS with the powerful numerical computation and modeling advantages of MATLAB/Simulink. Firstly, the core characteristics of ADPSS and MATLAB/Simulink and their interface interconnection principle are elaborated. Based on the ADPSS User-Defined Model (UDM) interface specification and combined with the MATLAB Embedded Coder code generation functionality, a complete technical workflow is established: "MATLAB/Simulink modeling - automatic code generation - ADPSS interface encapsulation - co-simulation validation". Key steps including model conversion, interface function customization, dynamic link library generation, and configuration file writing are designed in detail. Taking a Dual Active Bridge (DAB) DC-DC converter as the simulation object, employing a single phase-shift control strategy for validation, the control model is built in Simulink while the main circuit is constructed in ADPSS. EMT simulations are carried out in both a pure ADPSS environment and the ADPSS-MATLAB co-simulation environment. By comparing key simulation results, such as the transformer primary and secondary voltages, the engineering feasibility of the proposed ADPSS calling MATLAB co-simulation method is verified, providing a highly practical technical solution for the development and application of custom models in power system EMT simulations.

title: Innovative Generative AI-Resistant Assessment Methods for Engineering Education in the AI Era
authors: Khaled Shaaban
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The use of advanced generative AI presents a significant challenge to the integrity and effectiveness of traditional engineering assessments. This work addresses this challenge by proposing various assessment methods designed to be resistant to AI use. These methods change the focus from productbased and text-heavy outputs to process-oriented and competencybased evaluations. These methods are built on concepts emphasizing hands-on creation, real-time performance, reflection, and complex interpersonal skills. The strategies range from inclass practical exams and project-based learning to oral examinations, peer reviews, and design reviews. The different methods outlined in this work provide a clear pathway forward, demonstrating that the most effective defense against generative AI is not more sophisticated detection, but more human-centered pedagogy. By focusing assessments on uniquely human skills such as hands-on creation, real-time adaptation, and ethical reasoning, engineering instructors can not only maintain academic integrity but also more effectively foster the professional competencies required of future engineers.

title: Beyond Geography: A Multidimensional Approach for New Geography Economic and Practical Application in Engineering Education
authors: Yuxuan Xiong
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper builds on Krugman's New Economic Geography (NEG) by introducing a framework that extends traditional geography-only cluster analysis to include individual-level characteristics using clustering algorithms and Kernel Density Estimation (KDE). Rather than depending exclusively on geographic proximity, this multidimensional approach conceptually integrates diverse individual-level attributes to enable a richer and more detailed identification of agglomeration patterns. This paper offers guidance and a conceptual foundation for future empirical research aiming to capture the complex interaction between geographic and non-geographic factors that influence economic clusters. To better illustrate how the method generalizes to social sectors, we apply it conceptually to engineering education setting: institutions are embedded in a joint space of latitude-longitude and characteristics such as research intensity, faculty size, endowment, etc. We then relate multidimensional density to outcomes including graduate placement rates, research output (e.g., publications and citations), and external funding. Furthermore, to bridge macro-level patterns with micro-level behaviors, this paper proposes a visionary LLM-driven simulation, utilizing the latest agent-toagent (A2A) technology. In this framework, each individual in a cluster is represented by an LLM-based “agent” that is aware of its own profile and characteristics. These agents make decisions based on non-linear behaviors, leveraging the current state-of-the-art agent-based modeling (ABM) techniques. Although the agent-to-agent simulation is conceptual at this stage, it sets the foundation for future work that combines spatial econometric models with agent-based methods to gain a deeper understanding of how clusters form and evolve.

title: Impact of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization on Banknote Recognition
authors: Nima Sharifinia, Mohammad Javad Parseh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The detection of old and worn banknotes is crucial for banking automation and counterfeit prevention. Traditional methods rely on machine learning models, which require large datasets and high computational resources. This study proposes an efficient image-processing-based approach for detecting worn banknotes without machine learning. The method employs grayscale histograms, HSV histograms, and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to enhance contrast and extract wear-related features such as discoloration, faded ink, and creases. We further introduce a novel grid-based evaluation mechanism with Best-Match Voting and Thresholding to localize key regions and reduce misclassification. Experimental results show that combining CLAHE with the grid mechanism ensures reliable detection while maintaining computational efficiency, making the approach suitable for real-time automated banking systems.

title: Challenges in Generalizing Automated Breast Density Classification Across Mammography Datasets
authors: Rodrigo Leite Prates, Wilfrido Gómez-Flores, Wagner C. A. Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Breast tissue density is associated with increased breast cancer risk and reduced lesion visibility in mammography, motivating the development of automated density classification methods. However, generalization across heterogeneous datasets remains a major challenge. This study investigates factors that limit the transferability of automated breast density classification models across public digitized mammography datasets. We systematically evaluate preprocessing strategies and four convolutional neural network architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and EfficientNetV2-L) under both within-dataset and cross-dataset settings, including contrast normalization, artifact handling, pectoral muscle removal, and view-specific training strategies. In particular, we analyze single-view training, in which models are trained on a single mammographic view and tested across all available views to assess cross-view generalization. Experiments conducted on two public digitized mammography datasets show that VGG16 and ResNet50 consistently achieve the most stable and competitive performance across datasets. In within-dataset evaluations, the best models achieve per-class F1-scores above 0.80, while cross-dataset performance degrades substantially, with F1-scores typically ranging from 0.50 to 0.70. Overall, the results highlight the impact of dataset shift on breast density classification and provide practical recommendations for building robust, reproducible systems suitable for deployment across heterogeneous clinical environments.

title: A System for Evaluating Compliance with Instructional Design Standards: A Formal Specification Approach Using Semantically-Rich Models
authors: Martin Gil-Oyarzun, Juan Felipe Calderon, Maria Elena Truyol, Monica Quezada-Espinoza
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The growing demand for quality assurance in digital learning highlights limitations in traditional instructional design, especially in evaluation and adaptability. This paper presents a formal system that uses semantically-enriched JSON-LD representations to encode pedagogical elements (learning outcomes, assessments, and activities) and their alignment. The system enables automated validation against standards like Quality Matters, fostering consistency and transparency. A case study demonstrates its practical use in curriculum refinement. Key features include extensibility for complex models, integration with virtual learning environments, and compatibility with AI agents. This approach supports scalable, interoperable, and qualityassured digital ecosystems, aligned with educational innovation goals in Latin America.

title: Robot Teaching Assistant for Answering Student Questions in Higher Education: A Pilot Study
authors: Fuad Budagov, Mohammad Tariq Meeran, Tarmo Robal, Ali Atakan Basaran
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: With the increasing scarcity of teaching staff and their unavailability to provide personalized approaches, eyes are on technology. Could semi-autonomous robots, as teaching assistants, take over some of the burden from the shoulders of teachers? In this paper, we present a pilot study on the use of a robot as a teaching assistant in a higher education laboratory setting to support students' learning path by allowing them to accumulate knowledge through question-answering interaction with a robot teaching assistant. This robot teaching assistant is a companion of a teacher to provide support in teaching large groups of students, where a single lecturer may struggle to respond to all student inquiries within the allotted time. The robot teaching assistant was pre-trained with course-specific material, enabling it to answer topic-related questions directly. The findings from the pilot study highlight the potential of robot assistants to provide accessible, on-demand academic support and to complement teaching in practical learning environments. This study contributes to the growing body of research on robotassisted education and demonstrates the applicability of robots as teaching assistants in higher education.

title: Research of PCB Rogowski Coils for On-line Monitoring Partial Discharge
authors: Chunming Wei, Bo Zhu, Chao Xu, Long Tan, Zhongzhi Qu, Jizheng Yang, Siming Gao, Ruihui Han
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Power cables are widely used in transmission and distribution networks, and their insulation condition is related to the safe operation of the entire power grid. In this paper, a new type of current sensor with a PCB-shaped Rogowski coil structure is utilized to detect the partial discharge signals inside the cable. The main components of the coil were elaborated in detail. By using MATLAB software, the influences of parameters such as the length of the unit board, cross-sectional diameter, number of coil turns, stray capacitance, and load resistance on the frequency characteristics and sensitivity of the coil were analyzed. The design principles of the coil were proposed, providing a theoretical basis for the design and manufacture of this sensor. The frequency characteristics of the coil were tested, and the results showed that the coil could truly reflect the waveform of the measured current without significant attenuation. Partial discharge tests were conducted on the cable, and the results indicated that the coil had a relatively high sensitivity. Moreover, the most prominent feature of this coil current sensor is that it can be installed on-site without power interruption and will not affect the original operating state of the equipment under test, laying a technical foundation for the promotion and application of PCB-type Rogowski coils in power systems.

title: Sustainable Lean-Based Integrated Optimization for Urban Waste Sweeping with Non-Motorized and Motorized Fleets and Operational Constraints
authors: Milad Rahimi, Javad Rezaeian, Iraj Mahdavi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Municipal solid waste collection and management constitute one of the fundamental challenges facing contemporary urban environments, which has gained increasing complexity due to population growth and escalating waste generation rates. This research presents an integrated mathematical model for optimizing municipal solid waste collection processes utilizing heterogeneous motorized and non-motorized fleet configurations while incorporating real-world operational constraints. The proposed model simultaneously addresses location allocation, routing, scheduling, and resource allocation problems. The primary innovation of this study lies in the consideration of perishable wet waste degradation, lean concepts, restricted time windows, priority-based street cleaning operations, and concurrent utilization of motorized and non-motorized fleet systems. The model encompasses six objective functions: minimization of total costs, maximization of collection efficiency, maximization of accumulation facility quality, maximization of collection priorities, minimization of waste spoilage, and minimization of idle capacity and delays. The NSGA-III metaheuristic algorithm was employed for problem resolution. Results from five numerical examples with varying difficulty levels demonstrate that the proposed model achieves up to 18% cost reduction, 23% efficiency improvement, and 15% waste spoilage reduction compared to conventional approaches. Sensitivity analysis reveals that waste spoilage rate exerts the most significant impact on system performance.

title: A Statistical Method for Cable Main Tray Materials Based on PDMS Database Research and Development
authors: Yulei Liu, Xuecui Ma, Weiming Zhang, Min Gao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The statistical analysis of main tray materials is a critical task in the design of main tray layouts. This paper, based on in-depth research into the PDMS 3D main tray component library and the statistical depth of the main tray material list, proposes a statistical method for materials based on the PDMS 3D model. It also uses PML language to write a statistical program. Finally, this method was experimentally verified in the design of main tray layouts for a certain nuclear power plant, with the experimental results showing that this program can effectively improve the efficiency and accuracy of material statistics for cable main trays.

title: Live Learning Methodology: Its Impact on Satisfaction, Perceived Usefulness, and Competency Development in Engineering
authors: Rosalino Rodríguez-Calderón, Alejandro Martínez-Borquez, Roberto Rosas-Rangel
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: In this paper, the implementation of a living learning methodology in the course “Engineering and Science Modeling” at Tecnológico de Monterrey is presented. This course is distinguished by integrating academic and extracurricular elements, providing flexible, collaborative, and open experiences. As a case study, two activities were carried out according to the methodology, one in a public recreational area and the other in a science fair format. The results of the qualitative/quantitative survey (Likert scale of 1 to 10) demonstrated outstanding success of these activities, with average scores above <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.5 / 10$</tex> and 90 % positive comments in the sentiment analysis. Additionally, the correlation analysis validated the central premise of the study by finding a positive and robust association between student satisfaction and their perception of usefulness and the development of professional competencies. In conclusion, this dynamic methodology generated a highly satisfactory learning experience, which was directly correlated with the perceived achievement of academic objectives, confirming its effectiveness in linking students' emotional engagement with learning outcomes.

title: A Static Voltage Stability Index Evaluation Method Considering Distribution Network Resilience
authors: Yun Zhang, Zhen Zheng, Jingde Chen, Yaqing Li, Huamin Yan, Yilin Zhu, Feihang Lv, Haijun Xing
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the high penetration of renewable energy, distribution networks are evolving into multi-source interconnected networks, posing significant challenges to static voltage stability and resilience. To mitigate instability risks from inappropriate distributed generation (DG) placement and sizing, this paper presents a resilience-aware static voltage stability evaluation method using the Lu-index. Selected for its excellent linearity and real-time performance, this index overcomes the limitations of traditional indices in dynamic scenarios and high R/X ratio networks. Numerical simulations on the IEEE 33-node system quantify the impacts of DG placement, capacity, power factor, and load growth. Results reveal a non-monotonic "U-shaped" voltage stability trend with increasing DG capacity and demonstrate superior stability for mid-feeder DG integration compared to feeder head or feeder end placement. Reverse power flow under light-load conditions is identified as a key terminal integration instability risk. These findings provide theoretical and quantitative support for the scientific planning of DGs in modern distribution networks.

title: LuzIA: Transforming University Self-Study with Artificial Intelligence
authors: Delia Aurora Galván Sánchez, Maritza Peña-Becerril, Claudia Camacho-Zuñiga
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Amid the fast dissemination of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into educational institutions, instead of a polarized viewpoint, either hype-AI or hypo-AI, this innovative work proposes an ethically balanced human-AI pedagogical model. Our research demonstrates how a pedagogically guided generative AI tutor, such as LuzIA, can support self-study processes in higher education while preserving human instructional authority. The study followed a quasi-experimental design involving two groups-experimental <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=30)$</tex> and control <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=29)$</tex>-of undergraduate business students. The intervention was implemented in a Mathematics Reasoning course in August-December 2024. Through a mixed-methods approach, we analyzed the differences in average final-test scores as well as students' responses to a Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) questionnaire. The findings suggest design requirements for these tools and highlight their potential to provide flexible and personalized support without overburdening the instructor. Furthermore, students' responses reframe technology acceptance as a function of learning efficacy rather than usability alone.

title: Secure Consortium-Based Blockchain-IPFS Framework for Scalable Electronic Health Record Management
authors: Kusum Lata, Khyati Grover
publish: Proceedings of the 8th ACM International Symposium on Blockchain and Secure Critical Infrastructure
abstract: None

title: Machine Unlearning in Smart Grids: A Cautionary Tale of Infrastructure Vulnerability_supp1-3701875.pdf
authors: George Paphitis
