title: Mechanistic Modeling of Stress Evolution in Electrodeposited Copper Films At Room Temperature After Deposition
authors: S. Urich, M. Petersmann, E. Gorgasser, J. Koolen, W. Robl
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: The evolution of microstructure and stress in electroplated metal films is a complex interplay of microstructural mechanisms, commonly denoted as self-annealing. The process even takes place at room temperature, where it can span over large timescales due to the low thermal activation energy provided. The effect is highly relevant in the semiconductor industry, because it strongly influences the crystallinity of the Cu films and the bow evolution of wafers, hence an understanding of the active mechanisms and their mutual interplay is crucial. Starting from a well-established model for self-annealing including the effects of: 1) curvature driven pressure, 2) elastic corrections due to substrate constraints, and 3) plastic yielding, this work extends this view by adding the effects of 4) solute drag of impurities at moving grain boundaries (GBs), 5) vacancy diffusion and annihilation at GBs, and 6) the enhanced diffusivity due to high dislocation densities. Slower grain growth due to a typical level of impurities over time is demonstrated. Also a special case of a columnar microstructure obtained under certain conditions is treated. It is demonstrated that the current model is able to depict the highly different temporal stress evolutions (different times needed to reach a different stress plateau) of the random and columnar microstructure including a physically sound interpretation of fitting parameters.

title: Causal Decomposition of PPG Signals for Cuffless Blood Pressure Estimation
authors: Xinyue Song, Deqi Chen, Andrew Lowe, Zhizhong Fu, Xiaoping Chen, Xiaorong Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: While photoplethysmogram (PPG) signals are physiologically linked to cardiac activity and widely used for cuffless blood pressure (BP) estimation, their correlation with BP remains limited in reliability due to inter-individual heterogeneity. This study aims to isolate the PPG components that have a strong causal relationship with BP, and leverage them to construct reliable cuffless BP estimation models. Methods: We propose a causal decomposition framework combining ensemble empirical mode decomposition (EEMD) with counterfactual inference to isolate physiologically causal components in PPG signals. First, PPG signals are adaptively decomposed into multi-scale intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) via EEMD. Then counterfactual PPG sequences are generated through sequentially excluding each IMF, and their causal relationships with BP are quantified via structure causal modeling to identify hemodynamically significant components. Subsequently, robust cuffless BP estimation models are constructed by selectively incorporating components demonstrating strong causal effects. To validate the framework, we benchmark our causality-based models against conventional approaches: pulse arrival time (PAT)-based physiological model, gradient-boosted regression tree (GBRT)-based feature model, and convolutional neural network (CNN)-based time-series model. Result: mid-frequency PPG components (IMF4-6) showed strongest BP causality, with CNN model achieving superior estimation performance over PAT and GBRT when utilizing these components. Using IMF45 components, the CNN model achieved BP estimation mean absolute errors of 5.55/3.45 mmHg (systolic/diastolic BP), improving accuracy by 26.39%/17.86% over original PPG. Conclusion: Specific PPG frequency bands exhibit physiologically meaningful causal BP relationships. Our causality-driven approach enhances both accuracy and interpretability in cuffless BP estimation. Significance:This work establishes a theoretical framework for causal feature selection in BP estimation, and a novel paradigm for physiological signal analysis through causal decomposition.

title: U-Shaped Multiscale Difference Convolution Network With Statistical Channel Attention for Lithium-Ion Battery SOH Estimation
authors: Chuang Chen, Lei Chen, Jiantao Shi, Dongdong Yue, Ge Shi, Dan Bao, Dongzhen Lyu, Hongtian Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Accurate state of health (SOH) estimation for lithium-ion batteries remains challenging due to the nonlinear and nonstationary nature of degradation processes, which often involve local fluctuations, long-term aging evolution, and occasional regeneration behavior. Existing methods are still insufficient in jointly modeling fine-grained local variations, global degradation trends, and stage-dependent feature differences under complex operating conditions. To overcome these challenges, this study proposes a U-shaped multiscale difference convolution network (U-MSDCNet) for SOH estimation. The proposed method improves local degradation sensitivity, enhances multiscale temporal feature learning, and promotes adaptive cross-level feature integration within a unified framework. Experiments on the public NASA dataset and the laboratory-collected WRBD dataset demonstrate that U-MSDCNet achieves superior performance over representative baseline methods in estimation accuracy, degradation trajectory tracking, and predictive stability. These results confirm the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed framework for battery SOH estimation.

title: Automated Wafer Dispatching through Greedy Multi-Heuristic Scheduling in High-Mix Low-Volume Fabs
authors: Sean Mondesire, Tori Wright, Hasan Bazzi, Bulent Soykan
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Wafer route dispatching in semiconductor high-mix low-volume (HMLV) fabs is a dynamic job shop scheduling problem characterized by re-entrant flows, stochastic processing, and tool failures. In practice, manufacturing execution systems (MES) require dispatch decisions that are both fast and auditable, creating a gap between simple priority dispatching rules (PDRs) and computationally expensive optimization or learning-based policies. This paper proposes a Greedy Multi-Heuristic (GMH) dispatcher that scores each feasible lot–tool pair using a weighted composite objective whose component weights can be optimized with machine learning to adapt the relative importance of (i) processing time, (ii) due-date urgency with an overdue penalty, and (iii) remaining operations. GMH is evaluated in a decoupled micro-discrete event simulation (mDES) architecture that mirrors an MES decision-service interface. In this study, GMH consistently achieves the lowest average makespan and is the most frequent best performer (18/30 trials), while reducing exposure to worst-case outcomes relative to single-rule dispatchers. These results position GMH as a practical multi-objective, explainable dispatcher for advisory control and scenario-based optimization in semiconductor manufacturing, with an adaptable weight structure that can learn fab-specific priorities from simulation or production data.

title: Transformer Fault Diagnosis and Identification Method based on Machine Learning and Association Rules
authors: Jinbo Li, Caibo Liao, Wenhao Gong, Zhaoguo Li, Xianfa Tian, Chenglin Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To overcome the limitations of traditional transformer fault diagnosis methods, such as inconsistent diagnostic outcomes and insufficient accuracy, this paper introduces an integrated fault diagnosis approach. The proposed method is based on multi-method fusion and employs a light gradient boosting machine for improved performance. Firstly, diagnostic results from five conventional transformer fault diagnosis methods are combined. These methods include the IEC ratio method, the Duval triangle method, the Duval pentagon method, the Key gas method, and the Rogers ratio method. The results are then integrated with data on six types of dissolved gases. An 11-dimensional fusion feature set is subsequently constructed from this integration. Then, a tree structure probability density estimation algorithm is used to optimize the light gradient boosting machine model to achieve accurate identification of the nature of transformer faults. Secondly, association rule analysis is introduced to explore the inherent relationship between fault features and specific fault types, so as to realize the diagnosis from the nature of the fault to the specific fault type. The results show that the optimized model achieves an accuracy of 91.73% on the test set. This performance is superior to that of traditional machine learning algorithms, including K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forests, and Gradient Boosting Decision Trees. Finally, the effectiveness of the fault diagnosis algorithm was verified through a case study of a 500 kV transformer, demonstrating the effectiveness and application value of the method in engineering practice.

title: From Metrics to Decisions: Interpreting Route Deviation and Service Quality Indices in Public Transport Planning
authors: Marketa Jirmanova, Patrik Horazdovsky, Ondrej Pribyl
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Designing public transport services in suburban and peripheral areas requires balancing route efficiency, service quality, and social accessibility. While several indices exist to evaluate route directness and level of service, their interpretation under low and heterogeneous demand conditions often leads to ambiguous planning conclusions. This paper examines how operational performance indicators and route-quality measures behave under varying demand assumptions and how they can be translated into decision-relevant insights. Using a case study of selected radial bus routes, real-world operational data are combined with simulated demand scenarios to analyze the sensitivity of passenger-weighted route deviation measures and travel-time-based service indicators. The results show that identical route configurations may yield conflicting quality assessments depending on passenger exposure, highlighting a structural efficiency-equity dilemma. To address this issue, a refined passenger-weighted route deviation indicator is introduced as a penalty-oriented, policy-aware diagnostic tool. Combined with operational reliability measures, the proposed approach supports transparent decisions on route restructuring, infrastructure investment, and the use of demand-responsive services.

title: Research on Multi-Time-Scale Adaptability Evaluation and Active Power Balance Optimization Method for Demand-Side Aggregators Considering PV Output
authors: Linyuan Wang, Qian Wu, Yang Wang, Shan Cai, Yingjun Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) power generation in new power systems, the uncertainty and volatility of PV output pose severe challenges to the active power balance service of distribution networks. Meanwhile, the randomness of load demand further complicates the adaptability analysis of demand-side aggregators. Firstly, a PV output interval model is established based on the Beta distribution of light intensity to characterize its uncertainty. Secondly, a load demand uncertainty interval model is constructed by combining empirical mode decomposition and sparse Bayesian learning (SBL). On this basis, a reliability-economic oriented multi-time scale adaptability evaluation model for demand-side aggregators is proposed, which integrates adjustable capacity range, response delay, operation cost and uncertainty deviation indicators. Then, a solution method based on fuzzy sampling and multi-objective particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm is presented to solve the proposed evaluation model. Finally, simulation results verify the effectiveness of the adaptability evaluation method for active power balance service.

title: Enhancing Predictive Maintenance Capability and Diagnostics of Wafer Handling Within CMP Tools
authors: Rohit Ganesh
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This work documents the systematic deployment of a wafer handling diagnostic tool across multiple CMP platforms; focusing on the methods and engineering practices required to integrate an instrumented wafer into routine factory operations. We outline the structured approach used to establish fleetwide baselines, verify dimensional clearances, develop vibration-integral control parameters, and phase its introduction into factory interface, cleaner, polisher, and high-impact subsystems. By emphasizing implementation methodology as well as outcomes, this paper provides a replicable roadmap for fabs seeking to adopt instrumented-wafer diagnostics for CMP handling characterization and predictive-maintenance enablement.

title: Analysis of Lightning Impulse Current Distribution in MMC Valve Towers
authors: Yifan Wang, Mingquan Zeng, Shengqiang Guan, Dun Qian, Ting Xu, Zhigang Liu, Xingwen Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the widespread application of High-Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) technology, the Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC) has emerged as the core topology. Ensuring the stable operation of numerous power submodules (SMs) under external electromagnetic interference is critical. To investigate the distribution characteristics and propagation paths of impulse currents within the MMC valve tower under the condition of arrester failure, this study establishes a point-to-point MMC-HVDC transmission model. This paper analyzes the variations in arm current under lightning impulse currents and statistically examines the specific flow paths within the SMs based on control signals and current direction. Simulation results indicate that due to the phase difference in ground potential at the three-phase arm reactors, the arrester at the highest potential acts as the primary discharge channel. Consequently, the intrusion levels of the lightning impulse current across the three-phase arms are uneven and depend on the instant of the lightning strike. The set of possible flow paths within the arm power SMs is determined by the current polarity, while the control algorithm dictates the unique specific path taken by the SM within that set. Furthermore, the superposition of the impulse current and the operating current forces the arm current to cross zero, resulting in a change in the capacitor ’ s charging/discharging state and transiently accelerating or decelerating this process.

title: Multi-Objective Active Damping Optimization for Grid-Forming Converters in Highly Resistive Grids
authors: Yaci Yu, Yuzhe Wang, Ye Tao, Yanyu Ma, Tianlong Liu, Yuxiang Kuang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The stability of grid-forming converters is severely compromised under highly resistive weak grid conditions, where a low grid impedance angle induces strong cross-coupling between active and reactive power loops. Although increasing the active damping coefficient effectively widens the stable impedance angle range, it inevitably provokes a sluggish transient response and deep over-damping. To break through this performance bottleneck, this paper proposes a multi-objective optimization design paradigm for the active damping parameter of virtual synchronous generators. First, a small-signal model is established to reveal the underlying instability mechanism, and a two-dimensional stability boundary map is constructed to quantify the steady-state adaptability enhanced by active damping. Subsequently, a comprehensive cost function is formulated to synergistically penalize both steady-state instability risks and transient tracking sluggishness. By identifying the Pareto-optimal damping configuration, the proposed method strikes an optimal balance between robust operation in extreme resistive grids and rapid, low-overshoot tracking during transient power steps. Comprehensive time-domain electromagnetic simulations validate the theoretical stability boundaries and demonstrate the superiority of the optimized parameter design across diverse grid scenarios.

title: Accelerating Smart City Resiliency: AI-Agent Approach to Isotope Detection Development
authors: J. Zbirovsky, K. Stanek, M. Jerabek, L. Kuklisova-Pavelkova
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Urban resilience requires rapid deployment of monitoring systems capable of detecting radiological threats in transport networks without disrupting normal operations. This paper presents a multi-agent AI framework designed to accelerate the development and integration of isotope detection systems within smart city ecosystems. The proposed framework employs a hierarchical orchestrator-executor architecture in which specialized AI agents collaborate to generate synthetic training data via GEANT4 Monte Carlo simulations of NaI(TI) scintillation spectra, automate the development workflow including script generation, test-driven development, and result aggregation, and prepare a data foundation for deploying classification models at urban transport checkpoints. The modular architecture with a TCP API interface enables seamless integration with existing smart city platforms and scales for metropolitan deployment. This work demonstrates that heterogeneous LLM-based multi-agent orchestration substantially accelerates radiological simulation pipelines and establishes the direction for autonomous radiological monitoring at smart city transport nodes.

title: Transient Characteristics of Large-Scale Renewable Energy through Hybrid DC Transmission System under AC Faults
authors: Jiaze Lv, Kehao Li, Xinshou Tian
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Under the "dual carbon" goal, renewable energy bases located in desert, gobi and wasteland regions of western China need long-distance power transmission. However, weak grid characteristics tend to cause voltage instability. Traditional Line-Commutated Converter based High-Voltage Direct Current (LCC-HVDC) is prone to commutation failure, while pure Modular Multilevel Converter based High-Voltage Direct Current (MMC-HVDC) is costly. Hybrid High-Voltage Direct Current (Hybrid DC) transmission provides a good balance between economy and flexibility. Most existing studies focus on hybrid topologies at the receiving end, ignoring the interaction between renewable energy and hybrid DC systems under weak grid conditions at the sending end. This paper proposes a symmetric hybrid cascade topology for both sending and receiving ends, reveals the transient interaction mechanism among wind turbines, Line-Commutated Converter (LCC) and Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC) under Alternating Current (AC) faults at the sending end, quantifies the overvoltage risk caused by reactive power surplus, and proposes a hierarchical coordinated control strategy to achieve voltage support and power balance during faults.

title: Influence of the parameters of ultrasound transducer in the heating induced in biological tissues
authors: Victoria Hernández Mederos, Jorge C. Estrada Sarlabous, Eduardo Moreno Hernández, Loidel Barrera Rodríguez, Manuel Cruz Rodriguez, Wagner Coelho Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: In this paper we study the heating of a three layer biological tissue induced by the ultrasonic pulse emitted by a curved transducer. The thermal diffusion effect of the ultrasound is computed solving a model defined in terms of Helmholtz and Pennes partial differential equations. Isogeometric analysis (IgA) is proposed as an effective tool to compute the acoustic pressure field solution of the Helmholtz equation. In combination with the method of lines, IgA is also used to compute the temperature field solution of the Pennes equation. Numerical experiments show that the heating pattern and the maximum temperature depend strongly on the values of the parameters under study. In particular, very high temperatures are attained at the acoustic focus for lens with large aperture or high curvature. Moreover, increasing the amplitude of the pulse, the temperature field pattern is preserved, but the temperature values increase substantially. The conclusion is that parameters must be carefully chosen to ensure the success of any treatment based on raising the temperature to a certain range in a target region.

title: Tracing Method for Limiting Factors of Photovoltaic Hosting Capacity in Rural Distribution Networks
authors: Fengzhang Luo, Ranfeng Mu, Shengyuan Wang, Kuangyu Kong, Shiqian Ma, Jing Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the continuous growth of distributed photovoltaic (PV) installations in rural areas, distribution networks are facing increasing operational constraints in terms of voltage control, reverse power flow, line thermal stability, and flexible regulation capability. To analyze the key factors affecting the PV hosting capacity of rural distribution networks, a distributed PV hosting capacity calculation model is first established based on the reverse loading constraints of distribution transformers and medium-voltage feeders. On this basis, the influence mechanisms of energy storage devices, flexible controllable loads, and line loading balance measures on PV hosting capacity are analyzed from two perspectives: system regulation capability and network operating conditions, and corresponding mathematical models are constructed. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method are verified using the IEEE 33-bus distribution system as a case study.

title: Artificial Intelligence Assistance Versus Collaborative Teamwork in Engineering Mathematics Assessments
authors: Jaime Castro, Ivan Ongay-Valverde
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The objective of this study was to compare the impact of using AI tools versus traditional teamwork evaluating both performance measured in grades and students' perception of utility and learning experience. A quasi-experimental design was implemented with two groups of first-year engineering students: one group completed an assessment individually with access to AI tools, while the other worked collaboratively in teams without AI support. Quantitative data were collected from exam scores and complemented by perception surveys. We observed that AI did not guarantee correct answers nor deep learning. Our results align with the recommendation of a hybrid and critical use of AI in higher education as well as it reiterates the danger of overreliance on AI tools.

title: Unsupervised Pattern Classification for Dual-Layer EPE Characterization in Advanced Logic Devices
authors: Roy Anunciado, Konstantin Nechaev, Willem van Mierlo, Thiago Guzella, Jeff Hsia, Stijn Schoofs, Guillaume Schelcher, Stefan Van Der Sanden
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This work presents a simulation-driven pattern classification methodology tailored for multi-layer logic device applications. Leveraging aerial image data of both line-space and contact hole patterns, we demonstrate the approach on a two-layer stack. The methodology integrates data science techniques to extract and cluster similar patterns, enabling the identification of EPE-critical pattern groups. These critical pattern groups provide valuable insights for lithography process setup, inline monitoring, and advanced process control strategies.

title: LiveGraph: High-Performance On-FPGA Dynamic Graph Updating Framework
authors: Yufeng Luo, Peikun Hong, Jing Wang, Feiyang Wu, Chao Li, Minyi Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Dynamic graphs are ubiquitous in real-world scenarios, demanding both timely updates and low-latency responses. However, existing FPGA-based solutions still face three major limitations in supporting such workloads: • Heavy CPU dependence [3] • Inadequate support for dynamic graphs [1] , [2] • Poor support for irregular updates [4]

title: Prototyping Sustainability: A Project-Based Learning Approach to Ethics and Social Responsibility in Engineering
authors: Camilo Lozoya, Cecilia Gonzalez-Espinoza, Octavio Lasso, Luis Orona
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Project-Based Learning (PBL) oriented toward sustainability, particularly in electromobility and energy efficiency, provides undergraduate engineering students with a hands-on framework to strengthen critical thinking, problemsolving, and collaborative skills. As part of the learning experience, students work in teams to investigate how the technologies explored in these disciplinary areas can positively impact the environment and the management of natural resources. The development of functional prototypes, including electric vehicles, energy-saving devices, and photovoltaic applications, serves as an effective strategy to connect theoretical knowledge with real-world challenges. This approach not only reinforces technical competence but also cultivates social responsibility, ethical awareness, and a sustainability-oriented mindset. Using the Vallaeys Social Responsibility Management Model and a pre-test/post-test statistical analysis, we evaluated the impact of this PBL intervention on a group of Mechatronics Engineering undergraduates. Results show significant improvements across the three assessed dimensions: sustainability knowledge management, ethics management, and community social responsibility; indicating a positive shift in students' perceptions following the project experience.

title: Dynamic Distributionally Robust Optimization for Electric Vehicle Aggregators with User Default Risk under Direct Green Power Supply
authors: Zhongzhong Chen, Xiaofei Geng, Xiangming Dong, Lianghao Li, Jiawei Hu, Qiufan Yang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the user default risks (such as early departure and changes in expected electricity demand) faced by electric vehicle (EV) aggregators in their operations, a dynamic distributionally robust optimization model is proposed. The model aims to maximize the aggregator's operational profit, comprehensively considering the costs of direct green power supply, power purchase/sale from the grid, energy storage maintenance, and EV charging/discharging scheduling, while incorporating constraints such as power balance, charging/discharging power, and electricity capacity. To characterize user default risks, typical default scenarios and their probabilities are derived by analyzing historical data including user arrival time, the time difference between planned and actual departure, and the difference between expected and actual electricity demand. A fuzzy scenario set is constructed using the 1-norm and ∞-norm to describe uncertainties, forming a max-min-max three-layer optimization model, which is solved by the column-and-constraint generation (C&CG) method. Case simulations show that the proposed model can implement a "charging at low prices and discharging at high prices" strategy under the worst-case scenario, prioritizing green power consumption. Compared with the unoptimized scenario, the average profit in typical scenarios increases by 11.9% and the green power absorption rate rises by 83%. Dynamic distributionally robust optimization outperforms stochastic optimization and robust optimization in terms of profit; rolling optimization can narrow the uncertainty set and enhance adaptability to direct green power supply scenarios. This study provides an effective solution for aggregators to mitigate default risks, improve operational efficiency, and boost green power absorption.

title: MUSIC to Reduce Defects and Improve Throughput on Implants for SiC MOSFETs
authors: Bala Subbu, Leonard M. Rubin, HC Jayashankar, Jaganathan Sampathkumar, Wanchun Zhang, James Thomas, JunSheng Chan, Dwight Roh, Fulvio Mazzamuto
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Multi-Step Implant Chain (MUSIC) is a technique which leverages the architecture of the Axcelis Purion beamline to implant a sequence of recipes as a per-wafer chain rather than the typical per-FOUP chain. This can result in significant throughput advantages over per-FOUP chaining or individual implants. We improved the throughput of a series of 2 implants by 60-70% over running them individually and observed no difference in the device electrical parameters.

title: EFT-EEC: Achieving Elastic Energy Saving of TCAM Flow Tables in SDN Data Plane under Network Traffic Jitters_supp1-3701623.pdf
authors: Jinyuan Zhao
publish: N/A
abstract: SDN data plane generally utilizes TCAM to accommodate flow tables for fast packet classification, which also leads to serious problem of high energy consumption. Existing techniques are difficult to stably achieve satisfactory energy-saving effect especially under network traffic jitters. To address this problem, this paper first designs an elastic energy-saving cache to always keep sufficient number of active exact flows for stably high cache hit rates even under network traffic jitters. Particularly, we adaptively adjust the number of cache segments, in terms of the relationship between current cache hit rate and its preset expected range, to maintain high cache hit rate. Meanwhile, we regulate the threshold of packet inter-arrival time for identifying active exact flows, in accordance with current cache occupancy rate, to match the number of active exact flows with cache capacity. Furthermore, we theoretically derive cache occupancy rate based on randomly mapping assumption of each active exact flow and cache hit rate based on the assumption of flow activity degree model. Subsequently, we build an elastically energy-saving flow table storage architecture, by applying the elastic energy-saving cache to always enable a majority of incoming packets to bypass energy-hungry TCAM flow table lookups. Finally, we set up an experimental SDN platform to evaluate its performance on real network traffic traces. Experimental results indicate that our built flow table storage architecture steadily achieves high energy saving rates around 82.43% even under network traffic jitters, with the increase of 6.32˜7.55% compared to the state-of-the-art one.

title: Robust Random Graph Matching in Dense Graphs via an Approximate Message Passing Type Algorithm
authors: Zhangsong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: In this paper, we focus on the matching recovery problem between a pair of correlated Gaussian Wigner matrices with a latent vertex correspondence. We are particularly interested in a robust version of this problem such that our observation is a perturbed input (A + E,B + F) where (A,B) is a pair of correlated Gaussian Wigner matrices and E, F are adversarially chosen matrices supported on an unknown ϵn ∗ ϵn principal minor of A,B, respectively. We propose an approximate message passing (AMP) type iterative algorithm that succeeds in polynomial time as long as the correlation ρ between (A,B) is a non-vanishing constant and ϵ = o( 1/(log n)20 ). A key distinction from standard AMP is the introduction of a time-dependent matrix multiplication step within the iteration, which simultaneously enlarges the feature dimension and cancels the correlation during the iteration. The main methodological inputs for our result are the iterative random graph matching algorithm proposed in [25], [26] and the spectral preprocessing procedure proposed in [48]. To the best of our knowledge, our algorithm is the first efficient random graph matching type algorithm that is robust under any adversarial perturbations of n1−o(1) size.

title: MoDEpression: Exploring Individual Differences in personalized Depression Diagnosis using Mixture-of-Expert
authors: Junjie Liang, Peng Cao, Kun Hu, Jinzhu Yang, Fei Wang, Osmar R. Zaiane
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Depression is a widespread mental health disorder affecting individuals in diverse ways. Early detection of depression is crucial for treatment. Previous depression detection methods failed to consider the complexity and variability of capturing depression symptoms, particularly across different individuals and modalities, inevitably leading to poor performance. To address these challenges, we present MoDEpression, a novel multimodal framework that leverages Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to model individual differences in depression diagnosis. MoDEpression utilizes a dynamic routing mechanism, which adapts expert selection based on the unique characteristics of each individual, improving diagnosis accuracy across diverse patient profiles. Specifically, we propose a Hierarchical Difference-Aware Mixture-of-Multimodal-Experts (MoME), which integrates two components: the Global Difference-aware MoME to capture individual-level variability and the Local Difference-aware MoME to model fine-gained patch-level differences within each instance. This approach enables the model to handle complex, multimodal data, including video and audio, effectively addressing individual differences in depression expressions. Experimental evaluations on multiple publicly available datasets demonstrate the superiority of MoDEpression over existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both regression and classification tasks. Particularly, compared with the state-of-the-art, MoDEpression achieves relative improvements of 5.04% in MAE and 1.26% in RMSE on the AVEC2014 dataset. Furthermore, this study conducts comprehensive in-depth dissections of MoDEpression to explore the differences modeling capability and provides valuable insights for further personalized depression detection research.

title: SepFPL: Structural Entropy Enhanced Privacy Preserving Personalized Federated Prompt Learning
authors: Xin Liu, Yiming Wang, Jie Chen, Chunyang Liu, Shuhai Wang, Yulong Fang, Zhifeng Hao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: The deployment of Multimodal Large Language Models in Federated Learning has accelerated Federated Prompt Learning (FPL) as a parameter-efficient tuning paradigm. However, heterogeneous client data make it difficult to simultaneously achieve personalization, cross-client generalization, and privacy protection under strict privacy and communication constraints. To address this trilemma, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SepFPL</small>, a structural-entropy-enhanced privacy-preserving personalized FPL framework. <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SepFPL</small> decomposes prompts into global, cluster-level, and local components to separate common, community-specific, and client-specific knowledge. For local prompts, it adopts a hybrid low-rank-plus-residual parameterization that preserves dominant task semantics while retaining long-tail personalization under privacy noise. At the aggregation level, <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SepFPL</small> constructs a dynamic collaboration graph from cluster-level gradient trajectories and applies two-dimensional structural entropy minimization to discover hierarchical communities, enabling structure-aware knowledge routing and bi-level aggregation. For privacy, <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SepFPL</small> uses on-device Gaussian randomization for client-resident local updates, which are not externally released, and applies centralized differential privacy to shared prompts through client-side clipping and server-side noise injection. A time-adaptive budget scheduler further anneals noise intensity to improve convergence. Theoretical analysis bounds cumulative privacy loss via Rényi Differential Privacy. Extensive experiments on multiple visual benchmarks show that <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SepFPL</small> consistently outperforms representative baselines in personalization accuracy, generalization, and privacy robustness under identical or stricter privacy budgets, thereby providing a unified solution to the personalization-generalization-privacy trade-off in federated prompt learning.

title: UniMM: A Unified Mixture Model Framework for Multi-Agent Simulation_supp1-3700402.pdf
authors: Yue Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Simulation plays a crucial role in assessing autonomous driving systems, where the generation of realistic multi-agent behaviors is a key aspect. In multi-agent simulation, the primary challenges include behavioral multimodality and closed-loop distributional shifts. In this study, we formulate a unified mixture model (UniMM) framework revisit mixture models for generating multimodal agent behaviors, which can cover the mainstream methods including regression-based continuous mixture models and discrete NTP GPT-like discrete models. Furthermore, we introduce a closed-loop sample generation approach tailored for mixture models to mitigate distributional shifts. Within the UniMM framework, we recognize critical configurations from both the model and data perspectives. We conduct a systematic examination of various model configurations, and comprehensively characterize their effects including positive component matching, continuous regression, prediction horizon, and the number of components. Moreover, our investigation into the data configuration highlights the pivotal role of closed-loop samples in achieving realistic simulations. To extend the benefits of closed-loop samples across a broader range of mixture models, we further introduce a temporal disentanglement-and-alignment mechanism to address the shortcut learning and off-policy learning issues. Leveraging insights from our exploration, the distinct variants proposed within the UniMM framework, including discrete, anchor-free, and anchor-based models, all achieve state-of-the-art performance on the WOSAC benchmark.

title: Unsupervised Contrastive Transformer Hashing for Cross-Modal Remote Sensing Retrieval
authors: Zhan Yang, Weilu Long, Binghong Chen, Longzhi Sun, Yinan Li, Xiaoyao Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep-time Digital Earth (DDE) intends to harmonize the deep-time earth data and open geoscience knowledge. To assist the objective, efficient cross-modal retrieval methodologies are essential to process heterogeneous earth observation data. Hash learning maps diverse modalities data into binary codes, providing a viable solution for large-scale remote sensing data retrieval, while the Transformer architecture enhances the accuracy and relevance of generated models by dynamically integrating knowledge in an unsupervised paradigm. In consequence, the combination of Transformer and hash learning replaces the dense vector search with Hamming distance, significantly reducing storage requirements and increasing retrieval efficiency via compact binary codes. In this paper, we propose a novel unsupervised Contrastive Transformer Hashing approach for cross-modal Remote Sensing data retrieval, denoted as CTHRS. Initially, a Transformer-based multi-modal fusion network is developed, which bridges the semantic gap between remote sensing images and texts via self-attention mechanism and KL divergence alignment. Subsequently, a neighborhood structure preserving mechanism is presented, which maintains the intra-modal semantic coherence through dual similarity matrices derived from both original and hashing representations. Furthermore, a contrastive hash learning objective is designed, which combines intra-modal decomposition and cross-modal alignment losses. Comprehensive experiments on RSICD, RSITMD, and UCM datasets demonstrate the superior performance of CTHRS to state-of-the-art hashing approaches. The codes and datasets are available at: https://github.com/holdibson/CTHRS.

title: Task and Motion Planning for Mobile Tracking Robot in Automatic Inspection of Large-Scale Freeform Surface Parts
authors: Yongpeng Tang, Yaonan Wang, Xieyi Gao, He Xie, Haoran Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article introduced a dual-robot measurement system with a mobile scanning robot (MSR) and a mobile tracking robot (MTR), and proposed a novel task and motion planning method for the MTR. This work aims to deal with two subproblems in the automatic inspection task for the MTR, i.e., the tracking station placement problem (TSPP) and the safe motion trajectory planning problem (SMTPP). First, we analyzed multiple task and motion constraints, including station transition constraints (STCs), tracking volume constraints (TVCs), safe region constraints (SRCs), and dynamical feasible constraints (DFCs). Second, STCs and TVCs are introduced to the TSPP, which is modeled as a set covering problem further. An effective partial-state sampling strategy is proposed to generate candidate stations toward the target object. Then, a novel image-based approximate convex decomposition algorithm (IACD) was presented to reconstruct a 2-D complex environment as a group of convex safe sub-regions. These sub-regions and boundary information make up the graph of convex set (GCS), which provides a powerful approach to guide the movement of the MTR. Finally, we formulated the SMTPP as an optimal boundary value problem (OBVP) with the SRCs and DFCs and proposed a GCS-based method named GCS-OBVP to generate a safe motion trajectory of the MTR. Extensive experiments are designed to demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed methods.

title: SSDP-Net: Spatial-Spectral Difference Prior-guided Pansharpening Network
authors: Shuying Huang, Jinyu Hu, Yong Yang, Xiaozheng Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Pansharpening aims to fuse panchromatic (PAN) images and low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) images to generate high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images. Existing deep learning-based methods often overlook the pixel-level differences between these images, leading to inaccurate feature representation and limited fusion performance. To address this issue, this paper proposes a spatial-spectral difference prior guided network (SSDP-Net) for pansharpening. Its core idea is to explicitly model and utilize the spatial and spectral differences between PAN and LRMS images, and treat these differences as prior information to guide accurate feature learning. The network adopts a multi-branch architecture to separately model spatial and spectral features, and realizes hierarchical fusion of complementary information across multiple scales. This design not only ensures the effective preservation of spatial details but also maintains the spectral consistency of the fusion results. Extensive experimental results on multiple datasets show that the proposed SSDP-Net achieves excellent fusion performance with high spatial and spectral fidelity, outperforming various state-of the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. The source code of this work will be released on GitHub.

title: An Evaluation of Role-Based Multi-Agent Code Generation on Repository-Scale Problems
authors: Benedetta Donato, Noah Hagar-Dent, Aaron Worsnop, Leonardo Mariani, Valerio Terragni
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate high-quality code for narrow tasks, but reliably producing coherent, complex systems remains challenging due to, for instance, limited project-level context and hallucinations. This paper investigates the extent to which a multi-agent system for code generation, driven by a structured Software Requirements Specification (SRS), can overcome these limitations, paving the way for large-scale code generation. We evaluated this system on twelve Java repositories of different complexity, discovering that multi-agent systems can generate code that is more similar to the developers’ code compared to the code produced by individual LLMs, while also revealing a substantial gap between the code produced by agents and the code produced by developers.

title: Trajectory-based actuator identification via differentiable simulation
authors: Vyacheslav Kovalev, Ekaterina Chaikovskaia, Egor Davydenko, Roman Gorbachev
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Accurate actuation models are critical for bridging the gap between simulation and real robot behavior, yet obtaining high-fidelity actuator dynamics typically requires dedicated test stands and torque sensing. We present a trajectory-based actuator identification method that uses differentiable simulation to fit system-level actuator models from encoder motion alone. Identification is posed as a trajectory-matching problem: given commanded joint positions and measured joint angles and velocities, we optimize actuator and simulator parameters by backpropagating through the simulator, without torque sensors, current/voltage measurements, or access to embedded motor-control internals. The framework supports multiple model classes, ranging from compact structured parameterizations to neural actuator mappings, within a unified optimization pipeline. On held-out real-robot trajectories for a high-gear-ratio actuator with an embedded PD controller, the proposed torque-sensor-free identification achieves much tighter trajectory alignment than a supervised stand-trained baseline dominated by steady-state data, reducing mean absolute position error from 14.20 mrad to as low as 7.54 mrad (1.88×). Finally, we demonstrate downstream impact for the same actuator class in a real-robot locomotion study: training policies with the refined actuator model increases travel distance by 46% and reduces rotational deviation by 75% relative to the baseline.

title: Quantum-Secure Timestamp for the Integrity of Classical Signatures in Quantum Transition
authors: Kyu-Hyun Kang, Nam-Su Jho, Taek-Young Youn
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: As the development of quantum computers becomes a reality, concerns about the security of classical cryptography are growing. In the case of signature schemes, integrity must be guaranteed for a sufficient period of time after their generation. However, during the transition to the post-quantum era, it is difficult to guarantee the integrity of signatures previously generated using classical cryptography. In this paper, we design a quantum-secure timestamp that is secure even in the quantum era, utilizing a Time Stamping Authority, even for general users who cannot use post-quantum signatures, and we propose a transformation technique to enhance the security of classical signatures. Also, we focus on the security of classical signatures and the generation of hybrid signatures in the specific context of the transition between classical and post-quantum environments by defining the transition point based on quantum resistance. Finally, we analyze the security and performance of our timestamp scheme and demonstrate that it can guarantee the integrity of signed messages both now and in the post-quantum era.

title: EDepth: fficient Self-supervised surround-view Depth Estimation with xplicit Geometric Enhancement_supp1-3701560.mp4
authors: Juan Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Surround-view depth estimation is critical for robotic perception and autonomous driving. Existing methods rely on implicit cross-view feature learning, which incurs a high computational cost. In this paper, we propose E<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>Depth, an efficient self-supervised surround-view depth estimation framework that explicitly leverages geometric and signal priors at each pipeline stage. First, hierarchical volumetric fusion is performed to align multi-level features across cameras with controlled memory consumption. Second, wavelet-domain edge enhancement is introduced to recover sharper depth boundaries without external supervision. Finally, an explicit pose estimation network guided by noisy motion priors is designed to stabilize training and improve depth scale reliability. Extensive experiments on the DDAD and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate that E<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>Depth achieves a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, supporting practical deployment on real-world platforms.

title: Balanced Predictive Attractor Networks: A Compact Excitatory–Inhibitory Testbed for Anytime and Sequential Inference
authors: Jeet Bandhu Lahiri, Siddharth Panwar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Cortical circuits operate with tightly balanced excitatory and inhibitory interactions and attractor-like dynamics, two organising principles that are largely absent from modern deep networks. We introduce Balanced Predictive Attractor Networks (BPANs), lightweight recurrent classifier heads that attach to conventional feedforward backbones. A BPAN head is a discretetime dynamical system with separate excitatory and inhibitory populations, Dale-like sign constraints, and an explicit E/I balance regulariser. It is unrolled for a small number of steps, trained end to end with cross-entropy, and at inference supports anytime prediction by halting once the softmax confidence exceeds a threshold. We present BPAN not as a state-of-the-art architecture but as a compact, interpretable, and biologically inspired testbed in which E/I balance, attractor-like dynamics, and anytime inference can be studied jointly. Evaluations cover four image datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN) and three sequential evidence-integration tasks (Cluttered Translated MNIST, Streaming classification, and Sequential CIFAR patches), with MLP, ACT, PonderNet, Multi-Exit, GRU, LSTM, and Transformer baselines. On MNIST, BPAN reaches 97.9% at hidden size H=256 with 152,650 parameters, while ACT, PonderNet, and Multi-Exit require four to five times as many. With a confidence threshold of 0.9, BPAN halts in about 1.4 of T=6 recurrent steps, giving a 4.2× compute reduction with no measurable accuracy loss; similar behaviour is observed on Fashion-MNIST and on SVHN with a shared convolutional backbone. A sensitivity analysis over recurrent depth T, the balance weight λbal, and the E/I ratio shows that all reported behaviours are stable under reasonable hyperparameter variation. The paper also reports honest negative results on sequential tasks, where BPAN’s capacitylimited core trails GRU, LSTM, and Transformer baselines by 12 to 22 percentage points in exchange for a six- to twenty-fold parameter reduction. We also include a theoretical analysis that characterises contractivity, convergence rate, and the dynamical role of Lbal, and we discuss when BPAN’s particular trade-offs are worthwhile.
