title: EMACA: Efficient and Mobility-Aware V2E Authentication with Collaborative Agents in VANETs
authors: Yun Wang, Yingjie Xia, Xuejiao Liu, Jiachi Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: As a representative of high-mobility Internet of Things (IoT) systems, Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs) require time-critical vehicle-to-edge (V2E) interactions for real-time data exchange. Consequently, ensuring the reliability and responsiveness of V2E authentication is crucial for maintaining seamless network services. However, the highly dynamic spatio-temporal distribution of vehicles and the domain-bound nature of authentication tasks often lead to service bottlenecks and load imbalances across edge domains, thereby increasing end-to-end authentication latency. To address this issue, we propose EMACA, an efficient and mobility-aware collaborative orchestration framework for V2E authentication, which autonomously optimizes the association between Roadside Units (RSUs) and Mobile Edge Computing (MEC). In this framework, each MEC hosts a collaborative AI agent, and a Multi-Agent Deep Q-Network (MADQN)-based decision engine coordinates dynamic RSU-MEC association adjustments to reduce average authentication latency by balancing V2E authentication loads. Furthermore, we integrate a mobility-aware prediction model leveraging attention mechanisms and graph convolutions to forecast future authentication demand and support proactive association decisions. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that EMACA significantly reduces average authentication latency by 9.19%–31.41% and decreases load standard deviation among MECs by 22.25%–65.60%.

title: Towards Mitigating Unwanted Recommendations with Risk-Controlling Recommender Systems
authors: Giovanni De Toni, Erasmo Purificato, Emilia Gómez, Andrea Passerini, Bruno Lepri, Cristiann Consonni
publish: ACM Transactions on Recommender Systems
abstract: None

title: Evolving Air Threat: Impact of Technological Upgrades in Field of Avionics, Sensor to Shooter Linkages, BVR & Stand off Capabilities and Extensive Proliferation of UAS
authors: Sachidanand Dwived
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The contemporary air threat environment is undergoing fundamental transformation driven by advances in avionics, proliferation of precision stand-off weapons systems, and the rapid maturation of unmanned aircraft / systems across the conflict spectrum clubbed with modern sensor fusion, network-centric architectures. The traditional air defence concepts and legacy air defence systems face critical obsolescence if modernization efforts do not match the pace of threat evolution. Strategic implications of the evolving hybrid aerial threat which encompasses variety of vectors ranging from fifth generation aircrafts to small sized Unmanned Aerial Systems(UAS) are extensive, including compression of decision timelines at all levels, profound deterrence challenges, economic vulnerability to cost-imposition strategies, and particular relevance to regional security dynamics. The paper aims to bring out aspects of evolved air threat in view of technological upgrades and assess its manifestation in the recent US operation in Venezuela.

title: Battlefield 2035: Emerging Contours, Adversarial Postures and Implications for India's Capability Development
authors: Sachin Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The character of warfare in 2035 will confront India with a complex, multidimensional threat environment shaped primarily by Sino–Pak collusivity and the deliberate exploitation of disruptive technologies to achieve force and technology asymmetry. This paper argues that India's preparedness for this battlespace must be rooted not in theoretical constructs, but in prevailing and observable operational realities. Recent manifestations of Grey Zone aggression—such as GPS spoofing, terror-linked low-signature attacks, and chemical agent recoveries—underscore a continuum of conflict that already blurs the boundaries between peace and war. These indicators, when viewed alongside rapid advances in artificial intelligence, cyber capabilities, space-based systems, and autonomous platforms, demand a comprehensive reassessment of how future wars will be initiated, fought, and won. The study posits that wars of 2035 will be inherently multi-domain, with decisive actions unfolding simultaneously across land, air, maritime, cyber, and space domains, often commencing well before physical contact. China's pursuit of algorithmic dominance and information superiority, combined with Pakistan's emphasis on hybrid and Grey Zone disruptions, presents India with a dual challenge that cannot be addressed through incremental modernisation. India's survivability and success will hinge on the speed of technological transformation, the institutional adoption of Multi-Domain Operations frameworks, and the ability to operationalise disruptive technologies faster than adversaries. The paper concludes that failure to modernise is not an option; the coming decade is critical for building capabilities that ensure deterrence, resilience, and victory, particularly by securing advantage in the opening moments of future conflict.

title: An Improved LSTM Photovoltaic Prediction Method Considering Similar Day Selection
authors: Siyuan Cao, Zhuangzhuang Yu, Zijian Wang, Linjun Shi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Photovoltaic power generation exhibits high volatility and intermittency. To improve the accuracy of photovoltaic power prediction, this paper proposes a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network prediction model combined with Similar Day Selection (SDS). We analyze and compare three different selection indicators: Weighted Euclidean Distance, Gray Relational Analysis (GRA), and Cosine Similarity. Historical days with the most similar meteorological conditions to the prediction day are selected as the training set and input into the improved LSTM network. Experimental results show that the model based on Weighted Euclidean Distance achieves the lowest Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and outperforms other traditional methods.

title: GraphLeap: Decoupling Graph Construction and Convolution for Vision GNN Acceleration on FPGA
authors: Anvitha Ramachandran, Dhruv Parikh, Viktor Prasanna
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Vision Graph Neural Networks (ViGs) model an image as a graph of patch tokens, enabling adaptive, feature-driven neighborhoods. Unlike CNNs with fixed grid biases or Vision Transformers with global token interactions, ViGs rely on dynamic graph convolution: at each layer, a feature-dependent graph is built via k-nearest neighbor (kNN) search on current patch features, followed by message passing. This per-layer graph construction is the main bottleneck—consuming 50–95% of graph convolution time on CPUs and GPUs—and scales by O(N<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) where N is the number of patches, creating a sequential dependency between graph construction and feature updates.In this paper, we introduce GraphLeap, a simple yet novel reformulation that removes this dependency by decoupling graph construction from feature update across layers. GraphLeap performs the feature update at layer ℓ using a graph constructed from the previous layer’s features, while simultaneously using the current layer’s features to construct the graph for layer ℓ+1. This one-layer lookahead dynamic graph construction enables concurrent graph construction and GNN message passing, yielding a new class of ViGs, which we call GraphLeap. While using prior-layer features for graph construction can introduce minor accuracy degradation, we show that lightweight fine-tuning for a few epochs is sufficient to recover original accuracy. Building on GraphLeap, we present the first end-to-end FPGA accelerator for Vision GNNs. Our design is a streaming, layer-pipelined architecture that overlaps a kNN graph construction engine with a feature update engine, exploits node and channel-level parallelism, and enables efficient on-chip dataflow without explicit materialization of edge features. Evaluated on isotropic and pyramidal ViG models deployed on an Alveo U280 FPGA, our approach achieves up to 95.7× speedup over CPU and 8.5× speedup over GPU baselines, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time Vision GNN inference and motivating future hardware– algorithm co-design for graph-based vision models. Code is available at https://github.com/anvitha305/GraphLeap.

title: Microclasses Gamification Test for Students Using an Android Robot as an Assistant
authors: Jose Luis Ordoñez-Avila, Isabel Mendez, Maria Elena Perdomo, José Fernando Ibarra Dubón, Karben Selim Mejía López, Jesús Humberto Rosales Agüero
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper presents an experimental study on the effectiveness of android-assisted microclasses combined with gamification and personality-aware learning strategies in engineering education. The instructional approach employs ADAI, an autonomous android robot that delivers lectures, manages classroom interaction, and administers diagnostic and post-instruction assessments using computer vision, natural language processing, and speech synthesis. Microclasses were conducted on Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), medical robotics, and exoskeleton technologies. Learning outcomes were evaluated using a pre- and post-class assessment design, while learner behavior and engagement were analyzed through validated personality and playertype models, including the Big Five (OCEAN), gamified player types, and Bartle's taxonomy. Results show a clear improvement in learning performance, with diagnostic accuracies between 51% and 62% and post-instruction results reaching 65% to 87%. Additionally, students with higher Conscientiousness levels and Socializer profiles preferred structured and collaborative activities, whereas reward and interface elements showed no significant influence. These findings support the potential of personality-aware, activity-centered android-assisted instruction in engineering education.

title: Energy-Efficient and Resilient (DCR) PIC-like Receiver for Joint MAI/Crosstalk Mitigation in 2D-OCDMA/DWDM-PON Systems
authors: Octave Ringar, Djedouboum A. Christian, Mbainaibeye Jerôme, Ahmed D. Kora, C. Aupetit-Berthelemo
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: This paper proposes an energy-efficient Parallel Interference Cancellation (PIC-like) receiver for DWDM-PON employing 2D-OCDMA with Multi-Pulse Position Repeater (MPPR) codes. The architecture addresses two critical factors: crosstalk interference from narrow channel spacing (Δλ = 0.8–3.2 nm at 25 GHz) and Multiple Access Interference under high user loads. Unlike Sequential Interference Cancellation (SIC) with quadratic complexity O(N<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), our receiver achieves linear complexity O(I·N·L) through parallel interference estimation. The integrated Dynamic Channel Reconfiguration (DCR) mechanism automatically detects degraded channels when SIR<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> < τ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> and reallocates spectral pulses. Using MPPR-8×128 codes with N=64 users, results demonstrate: (1) BER of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−9</sup> to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−11</sup> versus 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>-10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup> for SIC, (2) power consumption below 100 mW versus 140–170 mW for SIC, (3) 70% downtime reduction under 20% channel failures, and (4) 25% spectral overlap tolerance. DCR incurs minimal overhead (~12 kbps, <0.1% ONU capacity) with 2 μs reconfiguration latency. The architecture provides a scalable, fault-resilient solution meeting NG-PON requirements.

title: Design Space Exploration for Layer Pipelined DNN Accelerators Based on FPGAs
authors: Yiwei Wang, Chang Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Design space exploration (DSE) aims to search for the best design architecture for a given neural network on a target device. In this paper, we propose a DSE engine for FPGAs, considering multi-core layer-pipelined designs. Our main contributions include (1) an accurate model of resource usage and computation throughput for pipeline designs, (2) a DSE solver with consideration of both on-chip resource and off-chip memory bandwidth limitations for given FPGA boards and (3) a configurable computing core. The balance between computation throughput and data transmission for pipeline designs is considered in our DSE engine to achieve the best performance. Experimental results on ResNet-18 and VGG-16 demonstrate that our pipeline designs achieve 1.17×–4.41× performance improvements compared with state-of-the-art baselines. The results also show that group-based pipeline designs may outperform full pipeline designs due to limited off-chip memory bandwidth and on-chip memory resource.

title: Simultaneous Dual-Robot Control for Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation on the InVesalius Platform
authors: Marcio Campos, Victor Hugo Malheiro, Thais Marchetti, Lucas Bettioli, Victor Hugo Moraes, Carlo Rondinoni, Renan Matsuda, Oswaldo Baffa
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) is a non-invasive technique used to modulate cortical excitability, requiring millimeter-level precision for effective application. This study presents the integration of a simultaneous dual-robot control system into the open-source InVesalius neuronavigation platform to facilitate dual-site TMS research. The methodology involved developing a PUB/SUB communication architecture and a dedicated anti-collision algorithm to synchronize two robotic arms. Results demonstrate a successful dual-navigation interface with independent visual feedback and synchronized target tracking. This framework lowers technical barriers for interhemispheric connectivity research by providing a powerful open-source tool for the scientific community.

title: Parking in Cities: Analysis of Data from Paid Parking Zones
authors: K. Navratilova, T. Tichy, J. Broz, J. Ruzicka, E. Hajciarova
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Most cities today use paid parking zones as the main tool for optimizing the use of parking spaces and public space. This paper describes the main research activities in the field of parking, including aspects where parking regulation has a major impact, and, above all, presents the outputs of the data analysis, which focused on the capacity of parking spaces, pricing, use, and compliance with defined rules. The analysis shows that price can be effectively used as a regulatory tool to reduce parking duration and increase the turnover of parked vehicles. In almost 30 % of cases where parking time was monitored, a strong link to the price of a parking space was found. At the same time, the analysis shows that price increases do not have a major impact on compliance with parking rules. The application of appropriately set regulation proves that enduser behaviour can be influenced, including the optimal use of public space, in order to create cities that are accessible and habitable for different types of users.

title: Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Resilience Assessment of Pelagic Island Microgrids
authors: Mingxiao Zhang, Difei Tang, Ziqi Zhang, Yuanyi Chen, Zhenya Ji, Peng Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Pelagic island microgrids integrating Power-Water-Hydrogen (P-W-H) systems face significant resilience challenges under extreme weather. Conventional physics-based simulations for resilience assessment are often computationally intensive, hindering rapid decision-making. This paper explores the feasibility of applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to address this challenge. We first generate a dataset using a multi-layer network simulation that captures cascading failure dynamics. Subsequently, we employ a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) strategy to fine-tune a foundation model, enabling it to learn domain-specific patterns from limited data. Case studies demonstrate that the fine-tuned model achieves over 75% prediction accuracy using only 50 training samples, significantly outperforming generic models. Furthermore, the inference speed is accelerated by orders of magnitude compared to numerical simulations. These results validate the potential of LLMs as efficient, data-driven tools for rapid resilience assessment in island energy systems.

title: From Crowds to Gridlock: A Joint Early-Warning Model Linking Mall Crowding to Corridor Congestion in Metro Manila
authors: Herminiño Lagunzad, Warren Panizales, Mikee Gonzaga, Romalyn Purificacion, Maribel Ruiz, Kris Garcia
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Urban congestion in Metro Manila is not driven by commuter peaks alone; short-horizon “activity pulses” around malls and venues, modulated by weather and calendar effects, also trigger downstream slowdowns. The researcher presents the Joint Early-Warning Algorithm (JEWA), a production-oriented system that fuses mall activity (entries/exits, crowding, dwell, parking occupancy, promo/incident flags), traffic state (segment speeds and neighbors), and exogenous context (rain, temperature, holidays/paydays) to forecast corridor conditions 30–60 minutes ahead. A feature store computes windowed statistics, lags, and burst indicators and aggregates mall signals to road segments via a learned mall→corridor influence graph. JEWA serves a two-head gradient-boosting model, a calibrated classifier for the probability of High congestion, and a regressor for average speedwith corridor-specific thresholds and hysteresis to convert scores into stable, actionable alerts. In a pilot across four corridors (68 segments; six weeks of 5-min data), JEWA outperforms traffic-only baselines, achieving PR-AUC up to 0.62 (30 min) and 0.54 (60 min), Recall@ τ ≥ 0.85/0.80 with acceptable precision, and reduced speed-forecast error (MAE 3.2 – 3.9 kph). Monitoring and MLOps (freshness SLAs, drift checks, calibration, canary releases) sustain reliability and low serving latency. Results indicate that place-based signals combined with weather and traffic enable few, early, and trustworthy alerts that can support operational decisions on key Metro Manila corridors.

title: Multi-Objective Expansion Planning of Integrated Energy Systems Considering Economy, Low-Carbon and Reliability
authors: Hui Wang, Yuanbing Xiao, Huamin Yan, Zhen Zheng, Mingzhu Ren, Jingjing Lu, Lu Sun, Linwei He, Ye Tang, Haijun Xing
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Under the "dual carbon" goals, to improve the reliability of integrated energy systems and address the challenges of low-carbon transformation, this paper proposes a multi-objective expansion planning method. This method aims to solve the problems of source-load forecast error uncertainty and carbon trading price volatility that are often overlooked in existing planning studies. First, Latin hypercube sampling is used to generate a set of uncertainty scenarios for source-load forecast errors, which are then reduced through clustering. On this basis, a bi-level optimization model is constructed: the upper model optimizes equipment capacity allocation and the grid/gas network structure in coordination to minimize annual total cost, carbon dioxide emissions, and the proportion of purchased energy; the lower model optimizes equipment output to minimize the operating cost of a typical day. Finally, case simulations verify the effectiveness of the proposed method, which significantly reduces carbon emissions, improves system energy supply reliability, and effectively coordinates the trade-off between economic, low-carbon, and reliability objectives.

title: Condyle–Zygomatic Distance as a Functional Ultrasonographic Parameter for Temporomandibular Joint Mobility
authors: Mariana Silva Campos, Carla Duque Balaguer, Thayanne Brasil Barbosa Calcia, Ricardo Souza Tesch, Wagner Coelho de Albuquerque Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Ultrasonography has emerged as a valuable imaging modality for temporomandibular joint (TMJ) assessment due to its non-invasive nature, absence of ionizing radiation, and ability to provide dynamic evaluation. Among the proposed ultrasonographic parameters, the condyle-zygomatic distance (CZD) has been suggested as a measure of condylar translation during mandibular movements. This study aimed to explore the methodological feasibility and functional behavior of CZD measured by B-mode ultrasonography in healthy individuals as a preliminary phase of an ongoing investigation. Ten asymptomatic adult volunteers underwent bilateral TMJ ultrasonographic examination during closed-mouth and maximum unassisted mouth-opening positions. CZD measurements were obtained bilaterally and compared with maximum interincisal distance values to explore the relationship between ultrasonographically assessed condylar translation and global mandibular opening. CZD values increased consistently from the closed-mouth to the open-mouth position in all joints, with a mean proportional increase of approximately 47-48%. No statistically significant differences were observed between right and left joints, indicating bilateral symmetry. Correlation analysis did not demonstrate a significant association between CZD excursion and interincisal distance. These findings provide an initial characterization of CZD behavior under a standardized acquisition protocol. Future analyses in a larger sample will enable formal evaluation of measurement reliability and broader clinical applicability.

title: Cybersecurity Risk Analysis in Critical Transport Infrastructure: Potential of Probabilistic Models
authors: Martin Zajíček, Miroslav Vaniš, Zdeněk Lokaj, Martin Šrotýř
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: This paper reviews current approaches to cybersecurity risk analysis in critical transport infrastructure, with particular focus on their applicability to complex cyber-physical environments such as tunnel control systems. Modern transport infrastructures are characterised by strong interdependencies between digital control technologies and physical operational processes, which increase cybersecurity risks and the potential for cascading failures. The review shows that existing methodologies are based on standards-driven and governance-oriented frameworks. While these approaches provide structured procedures for risk identification and regulatory compliance, they remain largely qualitative, static, and limited in their ability to represent uncertainty, system interdependencies, and dynamic cyber-physical interactions. To address these limitations, the paper discusses the potential of probabilistic modelling approaches capable of supporting decision-making under uncertainty. Probabilistic graphical models offer mechanisms for modelling complex dependencies, integrating expert knowledge with available data, and improving analytical support for cybersecurity risk management. The findings highlight the need to complement compliance-oriented methodologies with probabilistic and decision-oriented approaches to enhance cybersecurity resilience in critical transport infrastructure.

title: EFD-SE-DPCNN: An EEG-Based Deep Learning Framework for Early Detection of Alzheimer's Disease
authors: Saba Haji Molla Rabie, Sedigheh Ghofrani, Hamideh Barghamadi, Mahdi Eslami
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper presents an effective deep learning (DL) framework for nonstationary EEG signal processing. We propose a novel approach that combines Empirical Fourier Decomposition (EFD) for robust feature extraction, a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) attention mechanism for channel-wise feature enhancement, and a one-dimensional Deep Pyramid Convolutional Neural Network (DPCNN) for classification. The proposed approach, EFD-SE-DPCNN, emphasizes the frequency complexity of EEG data and improves diagnostic performance by selectively enhancing meaningful patterns. The achieved results for early detection of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) are 97.76% accuracy, 97.80% sensitivity, and 98.90% specificity. While our method focuses on early AD detection, it can be generalized to other brain disorders, such as Bipolar disorder and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), offering a versatile framework for EEG-based diagnosis.

title: Ghost pattern transfer defects in SPM litho rework
authors: Alma Vela Ramirez, Robin Schulz, M. Sankarapandian, Alexander Hamer, Christopher Carr, Dmitri Kabakov, Taiki Hinode, Glenn Marshall
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Sulfuric peroxide mixtures (SPM) are utilized for litho trilayer materials rework using commercial single and batch tools. Ghost pattern defects encountered in the batch rework process are studied and the process optimized for full removal of the trilayer material without ghost pattern defects.

title: Bridging the Industry-Academia Gap: A Faculty Externship Model for Engineering Education
authors: Khaled Shaaban
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: A persistent challenge in engineering education is the widening gap between theoretical academic instruction and the rapidly evolving demands of industry practice. When faculty expertise is not complemented by current, hands-on professional experience, graduates often lack the applied skills and professional fluency required by employers. This work details the successful structure and impact of a faculty externship program designed to systematically bridge this gap. Utah Valley University's program is presented as a replicable model that places engineering faculty directly into industry settings. To promote robust participation, a key feature of the program is its provision of paid externships for faculty for short-term placements of up to two weeks in duration, with all benefits maintained and no financial or administrative burden placed on host companies. The program is strategically structured around partnerships with local industry and scheduled during the summer term to maximize accessibility and alignment with the academic calendar. This incentive structure actively encourages both faculty engagement and industry partnership. The externship provides critical, firsthand insight into essential industry competencies, including the prioritization of soft skills such as project management and client communication; the use of modern collaborative and data visualization tools; and the problem-solving mindset required for open-ended, real-world challenges. The program establishes a direct pathway from industry experience to pedagogical impact, informing immediate curriculum updates, the integration of current standards and software, and the enhancement of course relevance. This work argues that such structured, incentivized externship programs are not merely a supplementary benefit but a core component of effective faculty development.

title: Research on Low-carbon Economic Dispatch of IES Considering Cooperative Operation of Data Center and Shared Energy Storage with CSP-ORC
authors: Junyi Li, Yan Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the "electricity-determined-by-heat" rigidity and high carbon emissions in data center (DC) integrated energy systems(IES), this paper proposes a mixed-integer linear programming model for low-carbon optimal dispatch based on multi-dimensional flexible response and shared energy storage (SES). At the source side, a concentrated solar power and organic Rankine cycle(CSP-ORC) cascade utilization is introduced to break traditional heat-power coupling barriers. At the load side, DC spatiotemporal shiftable computing power and waste heat recovery potential are deeply excavated. Simultaneously, traditional self-built storage is reconstructed into an adaptive SES leasing mode to resolve heavy asset investment barriers. Furthermore, a ladder-type carbon trading mechanism is incorporated to strengthen the economic orientation of emission reduction. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a global optimum of low-carbon economy, reducing average daily system operation costs by 52.7% and actual carbon emissions by 15.7% compared with traditional rigid dispatch models. This research verifies that combining SES mechanisms with hydrogen micro-circulation and multidimensional flexible response provides practical theoretical support for zero-carbon data center microgrids.

title: In-Place Sorting Algorithms: A Comparative Analysis
authors: Mohammed Alaa Ala’anzy, Amani Ibraheem, Khaled Mohamad
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: In-place sorting algorithms are essential for optimizing performance in environments with limited memory resources. This study presents a detailed comparative analysis of thirteen in-place sorting algorithms, including widely used techniques such as quick sort and heap sort, as well as less conventional methods like pancake sort, Gnome sort, and Smooth sort. A dual-method approach is employed: theoretical evaluation assesses time and space complexity, stability, and algorithmic adaptability, while empirical experiments benchmark performance across varying input sizes, ranging from 10 to 100,000 elements, using uniformly distributed data. All algorithms were implemented in Java and tested under consistent conditions, with execution time measured using nanosecond precision. Results reveal that no single algorithm performs best across all scenarios. For example, quick sort and heap sort excel in large datasets, while simpler algorithms like bubble sort and insertion sort are more efficient for small or nearly sorted inputs. This research offers practical insights into the strengths and trade-offs of each algorithm, guiding developers and researchers in choosing the most suitable in-place sorting strategy based on data characteristics and resource constraints.

title: Real-Time Speech Emotion Recognition Using Acoustic Features and a Probabilistic Heuristic Classifier
authors: Olesea Borozan, Silvia Munteanu, Nicu Drumea, Andrei Clima
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents a real-time speech emotion recognition system based on acoustic feature extraction and a probabilistic-heuristic classifier, intended for intelligent monitoring and the management of exceptional situations. The proposed model formalizes the process of voice signal analysis by segmenting the signal into short frames, extracting a vector of relevant acoustic features, and combining probabilistic inference with heuristic rules to determine the emotional class. The system was implemented on a resource-constrained embedded platform using the ESP32 microcontroller and the INMP441 digital microphone, enabling near-real-time voice signal acquisition and processing, as well as communication via Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. Experimental validation included 50 tests based on short utterances spoken under different emotional states. The system achieved recognition rates of 87% for anger, 92% for fear, 48% for sadness, 94% for the neutral emotional state, and 75% for panic. These results confirm the functionality and feasibility of the proposed solution, as well as its potential for integration into intelligent systems for the early detection of relevant affective states in critical contexts.

title: A Novel Hybrid Approach for Backlit Image contrast Enhancement
authors: Sajjad Ghadi, Mohammad Reza Hassanzadeh, Ali Kermani
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Backlit images often suffer from poorly visible foreground regions due to excessive background illumination, reducing both perceptual quality and usability in automated analysis. This paper presents an adaptive deep learning framework that enhances contrast in underexposed areas while preserving texture and natural appearance. The method detects low-illumination regions, generates multiple enhanced variants, and refines them through a convolutional neural network. This work is the first to integrate a network, specifically trained on backlit images using the Backlit Image Dataset (BAID) dataset, into a fusion-based enhancement method, optimizing it for brightness and contrast within this architecture. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods, delivering visually consistent, high-quality images without introducing artifacts.

title: Unified Discrete-Time Modeling and CCS-MPC for Bidirectional Four-Switch Buck–Boost Converters With Seamless Buck/Boost Transition
authors: Weide Guan, Su Liu, Yang Zhou, Peiling Jiang, Yingxi Zhu, Zhenbang Wang, Zihao Liu, Yang Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Bidirectional four-switch buck–boost (FSBB) converters are widely adopted in energy storage and EV-related DC interfaces where wide operating ranges and frequent buck/boost transitions are unavoidable. However, conventional cascaded PI control is highly sensitive to operating-point variations; abrupt mode transitions driven by input-voltage fluctuations can therefore induce transient excursions or even sustained oscillations, degrading dynamic performance and robustness. To address these issues, this paper proposes a unified discrete-time modeling framework and a continuous control set model predictive control (CCS-MPC) strategy for FSBB converters. Based on a unified discrete-time state-space model that captures both buck and boost dynamics, the controller minimizes a multi-objective cost function using output-voltage error and inductor-current error and determines the duty cycle in a single predictive optimization loop, enabling seamless transitions without additional mode-dependent heuristic logic. Meanwhile, duty-cycle saturation and proactive current limiting are incorporated to constrain the predicted inductor current within predefined safety bounds during large-signal disturbances. Simulation results verify that CCS-MPC significantly improves dynamic performance and robustness over PI control during input-voltage variations and abrupt load changes. The controller realizes seamless mode transitions with millisecond-scale settling, reduced voltage excursions, and enforced current-limiting protection under large-signal disturbances.

title: DRL-Based Dynamic Pricing Strategy for Electric Vehicle Charging Stations on the V2Sim Simulation Platform
authors: Xinyi Zeng, Yixin Tang, Qiyu Wang, Tao Qian
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a dynamic pricing strategy for electric vehicle charging stations based on PPO-LSTM, validated on the V2Sim high-fidelity simulation platform. The strategy combines the temporal modeling capability of LSTM with the continuous control of PPO, using real-time electricity prices as continuous action outputs to achieve refined regulation of charging pricing. Experimental results show that PPO-LSTM significantly outperforms fixed pricing, time-of-use pricing, and PPO-MLP in terms of convergence speed, training stability, and load balancing, achieving nearly double the improvement compared to fixed pricing while substantially reducing the number of voltage violations. Further validation on an asymmetric road network demonstrates the strategy's strong generalization ability.

title: Intelligent Control System of Simultaneous Cooling and Heating in Carbon Dioxide District Energy Systems
authors: Meisam Sadi, Ahamad Arabkoohsar
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The move towards decarbonization of residential and industrial heating and cooling is inevitable. District energy systems are promising systems for this purpose. In this paper, an intelligent control system for CO2-based district energy systems is proposed when dealing with simultaneous heating and cooling loads, for a case where one load is dominant and one is non-dominant. For such systems, especially in residential cases, it is common to have dominant and non-dominant demand. Simultaneous management of thermal responses for this case can increase system cost. In this paper, the integrated intelligent control system with a combination of customer heat pumps, cold and hot storage, and a carbon dioxide district energy system can not only reduce the initial investment cost, but also meet the cooling and heating demand without any problems. The suggested system improves the capacity utilization factor by 2% and has the capability to increase it further.

title: Research on the Analysis Method of the Disaster - Causing Mechanism of Extreme Meteorological Events to Electric Power Systems Based on SHAP - driven Explainable Artificial Intelligence
authors: Shi Cong Sun, Xu Chen Lu, Guan Hua Li, Quan Ying Liu, Jing Wei Han, Zhi Chao Ren
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With worsening global climate change, extreme meteorological events are increasingly frequent, threatening the safe and stable operation of power systems. This study explores the disaster-causing mechanism of these events on power systems to clarify the complex interactions between extreme meteorological factors and power system operation parameters, providing a scientific basis for the power sector to establish risk early-warning systems and coping strategies. Using the SHAP-enabled Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) approach, we quantify each factor’s influence on power-system disasters by calculating the marginal contribution of input features to model output. Empirical results show that extreme meteorological factors (e.g., strong wind, heavy rainfall) are strongly correlated with abnormal fluctuations in power system operation parameters (e.g., line current, voltage), and the nonlinear interaction between these factors is a key driver of such disasters. The findings provide theoretical and practical support for the power sector to enhance response to extreme meteorological events and maintain power system operational resilience.

title: Reduction of Plasma Etch Induced Defects Using Variable Delay Time Buffer Station
authors: Jeff J. Ye, Raviteja Pagadala, Allan Fang, William Simpson
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In a DRAM contact polysilicon (poly-Si) etch process, an excursion occurred after converting to a new process flow, in which poly-Si void defects formed on wafers as a function of queue time (Q-time). Based on data analysis and failure analysis, we identified the root cause and developed a new dry etch process with a variable-delay buffer station to substantially reduce outgassing of etch by-products, lower contamination concentration and improve yield.

title: Multi-Agent Large Language Model Framework for Power Grid Emergency Material Support: Construction and Application
authors: Zhenqi Yang, Lu Cui, Ling Wang, Xiaoying Zhu, Xiaohan Qi, Liu Yang, Zhipu Li, Heng Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Aiming at the critical challenges in power grid emergency material support, such as the difficulty of multi-source heterogeneous data fusion and the " LLM hallucinations " of generalized Large Language Models (LLMs), this paper proposes a multi-agent framework (MAS-KG) tailored for the complex environment of the Jibei Power Grid. The framework innovatively integrates an Emergency Material Security Knowledge Graph (EM-KG) with three functionally decoupled agents. Experimental results within the Jibei region (characterized by high-altitude terrain and extreme ice/wind hazards) demonstrate that the framework suppresses the hallucination rate in professional tasks from 38.5% to below 2.4%. The logic compliance rate of generated plans reached 97.5%, providing a highly reliable and professional decision-making paradigm for regional power grid emergency management.

title: Instrumentation and Control Design and Optimization for the Feedwater System of the Energy Station
authors: Yi Zhang, Hao Fu, Qingfu Hao, Feifei Liang, Bei Wang, Zhongrui Lv
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In response to China's green and low-carbon strategy and to promote comprehensive nuclear energy utilization, a nuclear power plant has pioneered the construction of an industrial energy station. This facility uses nuclear-generated steam to meet local petrochemical industry demands, reducing energy consumption and environmental pollution. As the world's first industrial nuclear steam supply project, it employs steam conversion technology, utilizing secondary circuit main steam as the heat source to heat tertiary circuit industrial feedwater. The feedwater system, a critical component of the tertiary circuit, pressurizes, preheats, and deoxygenates the water before supplying it to the evaporator. This paper presents a case study of this nuclear steam supply project, providing an overview of the energy station's process systems with focus on feedwater system functions and configuration. The main control schemes are analyzed and designed, with innovative logic optimizations including one-click pump switching and feedwater pump current-limiting protection proposed. These enhancements improve system stability, safety, and economic efficiency, offering valuable reference for instrumentation and control design in future nuclear steam supply projects.

title: A Comparative Study on the Performance of Supraharmonic Detection Methods
authors: Tongxun Wang, Yijie Chen, Yaqiong Li, Lei Zhao, Xiaobin Mu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The increasing integration of new energy sources and power electronic devices has made supraharmonic (2–150 kHz) detection a critical concern. While existing methods for supraharmonic detection have unique characteristics, they often present limitations. This paper analyzes seven mainstream detection methods, including full sampling, equidistant sampling, three-cycle sampling, and compressed sensing methods, etc. The performance of these methods is systematically evaluated in terms of sampling accuracy, data processing volume, and noise immunity. The advantages, disadvantages, and applicable scenarios of each method are analyzed, and finally the direction for future research on supraharmonic detection is pointed out.

title: A Multi-Site Joint Forecasting Method for Wind Power Based on Dynamic Graph Convolution and Attention Mechanism
authors: Jingyi Chen, Song He, Yuhan Qin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Within the context of high-penetration renewable energy grid integration, beyond achieving high single-site prediction accuracy, modern grid dispatch requires wind power forecasting frameworks that explicitly capture inter-site correlations while maintaining interpretability and operational applicability. To address the challenge that correlations among multiple wind farms dynamically vary under changing meteorological conditions, which cannot be effectively captured by traditional fixed adjacency matrices, this paper proposes a dynamic graph convolution with attention (DGCA) framework for engineering applications. We construct a dynamic adjacency matrix that incorporates both geographical priors and data-driven correlations, using multi-site historical power data and multi-dimensional meteorological features as inputs. By introducing graph attention to adaptively allocate neighbor contributions and temporal self-attention to strengthen cross-time dependencies, we ultimately generate power forecasts through a lightweight time-series module. An engineering deployment process and ablation experiments are also presented in this paper, demonstrating that dynamic correlation graphs and attention mechanisms play a crucial role in enhancing generalization capabilities and enabling cross-site collaborative prediction.

title: Mitigation of Particle-Induced Defects in HDP-FSG Deposition
authors: Ting Hui Ying, Lau Ker Peng, Ang Choon Wooi, Vicky Kong Wei Hau
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Fluorosilicate glass (FSG) is widely used for Inter-Metal Dielectric (IMD) layer due to existence of Fluorine-doped, allowing for a low k-value film. However, this also acts as a weakness allowing it to be more prone to formation of backside particles during High Density Plasma (HDP) chamber deposition. Though these backside particles should be removed after clean or planarization step (CMP) depending on the integration flow, Tungsten puddle (W-Puddle) defects are still being observed, and yield is being affected. In this report, we will be analyzing the cause of this discrepancy and through optimization, we achieved a 55% improvement in mean and 47% improvement in spike for W-Puddle defects count.

title: Applying Flipped Classroom with a Remote Laboratory for Photovoltaic Energy Efficiency Experimentation
authors: Alex Villazón, Andrés Gamboa, Omar Ormachea, Renán Orellana
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The flipped classroom methodology combines theoretical and practical learning by encouraging students to work with theoretical content at home and practical in-class activities. The purpose of this study is to provide an active learning approach combining flipped classroom and the incorporation of hands-on experimentation with remote laboratories to improve student engagement and performance, especially in complex subjects or engineering areas, where traditional lecture-based methods are not sufficient. To evaluate the effectiveness of this active learning approach, we conducted a pilot study with a selected group of students, using a combination of predictive and post-experimentation assessments and feedback mechanisms. In the pilot, hands-on experimentation was carried out using our newly developed photovoltaic (PV) solar remote laboratory, to improve the understanding of the influence of altitude on PV energy efficiency, which cannot be achieved with simulations or with a real laboratory deployed in a single location. The results showed a significant improvement in the post-experimentation scores from 50% average to 79% and high student satisfaction with the learning experience. The findings of this pilot study provide valuable insights for the future adoption of similar methodologies and the implementation of the proposed approach.

title: Edge-Induced Via Resistance Failures in BEOL RFCMOS Process
authors: Aleena Umar, Leong Voon Kiong, Tubati Sricharan, Peck Yan Zheng
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In advanced semiconductor manufacturing, electrical failures localized to the wafer's extreme edge present a persistent challenge, often eluding conventional detection due to their subtle characteristics and peripheral location. This study investigates a recurring wafer edge Via Resistance (Rc) failure in a device, observed at wafer edge during end-of-line electrical testing. It was traced to a specific Chemical Mechanical Planarization (CMP) tool (CMP-A) using Polish Pad Type A, which exhibited a distinct edge removal profile due to its CMP recipe. Comparative analysis showed CMP-A result in ~500Å thinner oxide at the wafer edge, directly correlating with reduced Via Rc. Further investigation established a strong link between extended polish time and aggressive edge thinning, particularly in intermetal dielectric processes with longer polish durations. This paper presents a comprehensive yield engineering approach to uncover the root cause, supported by tool trend data, electrical correlation, oxide thickness, and removal rate profiling. The findings underscore the importance of edge-specific process control and polish time optimization in mitigating marginality-driven failures that manifest at the wafer's extreme periphery.

title: State Space Models over Directed Graphs
authors: Junzhi She, Xunkai Li, Rong-Hua Li, Guoren Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Directed graphs are ubiquitous across numerous domains, where the directionality of edges encodes critical causal dependencies. However, existing GNNs and graph Transformers tailored for directed graphs face two major challenges: (1) effectively capturing long-range causal dependencies derived from directed edges; (2) balancing accuracy and training efficiency when processing large-scale graph datasets. In recent years, state space models (SSMs) have achieved substantial progress in causal sequence tasks, and their variants designed for graphs have demonstrated state-of-the-art accuracy while maintaining high efficiency across various graph learning benchmarks. However, existing graph state space models are exclusively designed for undirected graphs, which limits their performance in directed graph learning. To this end, we propose an innovative approach DirEgo2Token which sequentializes directed graphs via k-hop ego graphs. This marks the first systematic extension of state space models to the field of directed graph learning. Building upon this, we develop DirGraphSSM, a novel directed graph neural network architecture that implements state space models on directed graphs via the message-passing mechanism. Experimental results demonstrate that DirGraphSSM achieves state-of-the-art performance on three representative directed graph learning tasks while attaining competitive performance on two additional tasks with 1.5× to 2× training speed improvements compared to existing state-of-the-art models.

title: Adaptive fuzzy double c-means clustering via knowledge transferring latent factor analysis
authors: Yan Song, Dan Zhao, Weidong Zhang, Hui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Fuzzy c-means clustering for incomplete data has gained special concern, but many results cannot make a delicate imputation and perfect clustering. To this aim, a Nonlinear Representative Non-negative Latent Factor with Knowledge transfer (NRNLF_KT) and Adaptive Fuzzy Double c-means with Sparse Self-representation (AFD_SS) algorithm, namely NA clustering, is developed. The proposed NA algorithm has three remarkable merits: 1) the NRNLF_KT model employs a nonlinear mapping to intentionally relax the non-negativity constraint and constitutes an unconstrained optimization problem; 2) the NRNLF_KT model establishes an auxiliary matrix related to the target matrix but in a different pattern, which contributes greatly to extracting useful knowledge; and 3) the SS technique is introduced to facilitate the simultaneous consideration of global and local information, meanwhile, the particle swarm optimization algorithm is applied to find an appropriate parameter concerning the specific dataset. Finally, experiment results on real-world datasets verify the superiority of the proposed method.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Parkinson’s Disease Detection from Speech Signals Using a CNN–LSTM Architecture
authors: Abdullah Bin Sawad, Muhammad Binsawad
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Voice-based digital biomarkers offer a promising non-invasive and economical method for early diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease (PD); however, developing a clinically interpretable voice-based model with high predictive accuracy remains difficult. In this paper, we introduce an explainable hybrid CNN–LSTM architecture utilizing feature-sequence representation in which manually engineered acoustic biomarkers are transformed to pseudo-temporal sequence data to learn inter-feature relationships and phonation dynamics. Specifically, the CNN layer learns locally correlated features based on the redundant information in the speech signal whereas the LSTM captures more complex sequential relationships related to speech impairments associated with PD. For ensuring clinical interpretability, we use a multi-tiered SHAP-LIME-based explainability approach for validation of selected features and their directionality. Experiments on our newly generated PD-speech dataset show that the proposed model yields 87% accuracy, 0.835 F1-score, and 0.92 ROC-AUC score, outperforming traditional machine learning algorithms as well as single deep learning models. Interpretability evaluation reveals that jitter and shimmer biomarkers are important for disease prediction, corroborating previous studies on PD and speech disorders.

title: A Gated Attention-Based Semi-Supervised Learning Framework for Label-Scarce Tabular Data
authors: Yongeun Jang, Yonggon Jung, Ghil-Guen Oh, Jun-Geol Baek
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Tabular data is a data format commonly used in various industries, including finance, medical services, and manufacturing. However, deep learning research for this data type has lagged behind the progress seen in computer vision and natural language processing. Real-world applications often face severe label scarcity due to high labeling costs. Traditional Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDTs) and existing deep learning architectures struggle to achieve high predictive performance under these constraints. This study proposes the Gated Attention Tabular Encoder (GATE) and a two-stage semi-supervised learning pipeline. GATE effectively captures complex feature interactions by using global context from self-attention to dynamically regulate the flow of feature information. The proposed pipeline uses a teacher-student self-distillation framework to extract robust representations from unlabeled data. Then, it employs SoftNCA-based metric learning alongside high-confidence pseudo-labeling to optimize the embedding space with minimal labeled instances. Experimental results on public benchmark data showed that the proposed framework consistently achieved superior average performance across all label-ratio scenarios. The framework achieved an average accuracy of 62.23% with only 1% of the labeled data. It also achieved the highest average accuracy of 89.26% in a fully supervised setting, confirming its universal competitiveness. Through additional experiments, we confirmed that the proposed pipeline constructs a structurally superior representation space and achieves greater class separation compared to supervised learning models. In addition, in terms of computational efficiency, the proposed pipeline achieved this performance by using only 50% of the training parameters and 33% of the FLOPs compared to the comparative transformer-based model, FT-Transformer, and using only one-hundredth of the number of parameters compared to complex SSL models such as SAINT. This study presents a highly effective and computationally efficient paradigm for learning tabular representations in label-deficient industrial environments. The code is available at https://github.com/yongeun-jang/GATE.

title: Air-Ground Cooperative Sensing and Computing in UAV-Assisted VEC Networks
authors: Zhengqing Sun, Xuhan Chen, Weifeng Zhong, Jiawen Kang, Xumin Huang, Dong In Kim, Shengli Xie
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The rapid development of autonomous driving technologies and the expansion of the Internet of Things (IoT) have intensified the demand for timely and accurate vehicular perception, highlighting the potential of leveraging vehicular edge computing (VEC) systems to support perception tasks. Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), owing to their flexible mobility and line-of-sight advantages, have emerged as promising IoT-enabling aerial platforms to enhance both vehicular perception and computation capabilities in VEC environments. In this paper, we propose an accuracy-oriented and computation-efficient framework for air-ground cooperative sensing and computing, wherein a UAV cooperates with a group of connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) to collect sensing data of the objects around them, followed by data fusion and computation for object classification. We formulate a joint optimization problem involving UAV trajectory planning and sensing task placement, aiming to minimize the sensing accuracy error and task processing delay. The joint optimization problem is reformulated as a Markov decision process (MDP), where a penalty term for constraint violations is incorporated into the reward function to ensure feasibility. Furthermore, we develop an improved twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (TD3)-based algorithm for UAV-assisted cooperative sensing and computing to derive an efficient UAV trajectory control and subtask placement strategy. Results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves superior performance compared to baselines in terms of convergence speed, training stability, and cost-saving, validating its applicability in dynamic UAV-assisted VEC environments.

title: Recent Advances and New Directions in Superconductor Electronic Design Automation Tools
authors: Coenrad J. Fourie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: The successful completion of the IARPA SuperTools project, which significantly advanced the state-of-the-art in superconductor electronic design automation (EDA) tools, enabled superconductor electronics (SCE) designers to synthesize and verify complex circuits such as 64-bit RISC processors. Advances under SuperTools included technology computer-aided design (TCAD), logic and clock synthesis, place-and-route and compact model extraction directly from layouts. A recent DARPA project on homomorphic encryption with SCE circuits demanded improvements in high-level synthesis, place-and-route and timing extraction tools. New lumped-element transmission line models were introduced to simulation tools to handle not just reflections, but also cross-coupling, dispersion and loss on inter-gate transmission lines in very large digital systems. Despite the maturity of large-scale digital circuit design and analysis tools, recent developments in the field have exposed a need to add EDA capabilities for superconductor quantum computing (SQC) design, analysis and verification. SQC end-user demand for tools that model and process flux trapping, vortex displacement, noise, high-precision extraction of weak magnetic coupling, and microwave frequency analysis for resonance and impedance has driven new directions in tool development. In this presentation, we discuss EDA advances post-SuperTools, including the new research and development efforts demanded by SQC applications

title: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Sliding Mode Control for Nonlinear Bidirectional Energy Exchange in Vehicle-to-Grid Systems
authors: Rimsha Ghias, Syed Hassan Ahmed, Atif Rehman, Iftikhar Ahmad, Hammad Iqbal Sherazi, Sayed Osman Madbouly
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning–enhanced sliding mode control (RLSMC) framework for efficient and robust bidirectional energy exchange in vehicle-to-grid (V2G) and grid-to-vehicle (G2V) systems. The proposed approach integrates a Deep Q-Network (DQN) with classical sliding mode control to enable adaptive gain tuning, thereby improving robustness against nonlinearities, parameter variations, and external disturbances. A comprehensive nonlinear model of a hybrid energy storage system, comprising a lithium-ion battery, an ultracapacitor, quadratic buck–boost converters, and a three-phase grid-connected inverter, is developed to evaluate the performance of the proposed controller under both charging and discharging modes. The RLSMC achieves improved dynamic tracking, reduced chattering, and enhanced stability compared to conventional SMC, particularly under sudden load variations and grid disturbances. The controller ensures smoother current transitions, reduced steady-state error, and improved regulation under nonlinear converter dynamics. Extensive simulation results demonstrate the controller’s ability to maintain stable bidirectional power flow and accurate current regulation across a wide range of operating conditions. To validate real-time feasibility, controller-in-the-loop (CIL) experiments are conducted using the Delfino F28369D microcontroller. The experimental results closely align with simulation outcomes, confirming the practical applicability and reliability of the proposed control strategy.

title: Diff-MedSeg: Diffusion Model based Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-Channel Attention
authors: Dengyi Liu, Ming Ma, Hua Fang, Honggang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Medical image segmentation is fundamental to early cancer detection and treatment planning, yet current approaches face persistent challenges in accuracy and generalizability. We propose Diff-MedSeg, a novel diffusion-based framework that leverages a Multi-Channel Attention module to address these limitations. Our key innovation lies in the integration of channel-wise attention mechanisms within the diffusion process, enabling adaptive feature recalibration throughout the iterative denoising procedure. This design allows the model to selectively emphasize diagnostically relevant features while suppressing noise and artifacts. We evaluate Diff-MedSeg on six benchmark datasets comprising three polyp segmentation tasks and three prostate segmentation datasets, demonstrating consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods including U-Net variants, transformer-based architectures, and recent diffusion models. Comprehensive experiments show that our approach achieves superior performance across most evaluation metrics compared to existing methods. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of our multi-channel attention module, demonstrating its crucial role in enhancing segmentation quality, particularly for challenging cases with ambiguous boundaries. These findings establish diffusion-based architectures with attention mechanisms as a promising paradigm for medical image segmentation, with broad applicability to diverse clinical imaging tasks.

title: A DRL-Based Collision Avoidance Method for USVs With Distributed Priority and Curriculum Learning
authors: Zhe Sun, Yunsheng Fan, Guofeng Wang, Xiaojie Sun
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: Collision avoidance safety is a critical concern for unmanned surface vehicles in maritime operations, ensuring success, minimizing casualties, and reducing economic losses. In this study, a deep reinforcement learning based collision avoidance algorithm is proposed to enable safe navigation in locally complex environments. An asynchronous parallel training framework based on distributed priority replay is introduced to optimize the sampling priority mechanism and accelerate training. To enhance training efficiency, a method integrating curriculum learning and a distributed priority-based interactive sample buffer is designed. The n-step bootstrapping method is adopted to refine value estimation. In addition, double learning and dueling network architecture are employed to mitigate overestimation and improve sample efficiency, while a state clipping method is implemented to further optimize training performance. The effectiveness of the proposed algorithm is validated through testing and comparative simulations in the Unity3D environment.

title: YT-SWIN: Context-Adaptive Spectral Fusion for Cross-Modal UAV Remote Sensing
authors: Qurat Ul Ain, Kewen Xia, Ali Zaib Khan
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Accurate object detection in multimodal remote sensing images remains a significant challenge for autonomous all-weather systems, necessitating adaptive integration of complementary RGB and infrared (IR) data under diverse environmental conditions. Existing fusion approaches often rely on fixed, manually designed strategies or complex multi-expert frameworks, which lack the flexibility to dynamically achieve fusion policies in response to real-time sensor degradation. To address these limitations, we propose YT-SWIN, a novel adaptive fusion framework that synergistically combines the YOLOv10n backbone with a Swin Transformer-based attention mechanism to achieve environment-aware multimodal perception. Our work introduces three core modules: first, Self-Supervised Environment Inference Module that learns to characterize weather and illumination conditions directly from input data, eliminating the reliance on labeled environmental metadata. Second, a Learnable Frequency Decomposition Block that constructs optimized spectral representations through data-adaptive filtering, enhancing multimodal feature representation. Third, a Hierarchical Cross-Modal Attention Network (HCAN) built upon lightweight Swin Transformer blocks dynamically gates and fuses RGB and IR features across multiple spatial and frequency scales, conditioned on the inferred environmental context. Additionally, an adaptive Channel Pruning Strategy integrated into the multi-scale feature pyramid maintains real-time inference speeds without compromising detection accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that YT-SWIN achieves state-of-the-art performance, attaining mAP scores of 83.08% on the DroneVehicle dataset and 77.4% on the VEDAI dataset while operating in real time. The framework demonstrates strong generalization capability across environments, establishing a new paradigm for efficient, adaptive, and robust multimodal perception in unstructured outdoor scenarios.

title: Genetic Algorithm-Optimized Neural Network for Maximum Efficiency Tracking of Electrolytic-Capacitor-Less WPT System
authors: Yuchen Wei, Fengjiang Wu, Siwei Wu, Hongchen Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: The use of a matrix converter (MC) and a semi active rectifier in the wireless power transfer (WPT) system enables wide-range buck–boost operation and power regulation while completely eliminating electrolytic capacitors. In this paper, a dual-side closed loop control strategy is proposed to achieve high-quality grid current and constant-current/constant-voltage charging. The system is under-constrained, with the primary MC current serving as a free variable. An efficiency model is established to identify the optimal current that maximizes system efficiency under varying grid voltage, battery voltage, charging current, and mutual inductance. To adaptively track this optimal point, a genetic algorithm-optimized neural network (GANN) based maximum efficiency tracking (MET) method is developed. The genetic algorithm minimizes redundant neurons, achieving higher prediction accuracy and lower computational complexity, thereby facilitating real-time DSP implementation. Experimental results confirm that the proposed GANN-based MET method achieves high efficiency over a wide operating range.
