title: Executing an Industry Plan for Cybersecurity
authors: Doug Suerich, Mayura Padmanabhan, Sri Harish Peddi
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Semiconductor factories are high value targets for cyberattacks. Protecting factories is challenging: the complex chip manufacturing process requires a web of third-party equipment, computer devices, and software — any one of which could expose a vulnerability that hackers can exploit. This paper explores the collaborative efforts of industry players to develop greater security through cooperation.

title: Design of a Secondary Power Supply for Aerospace Solid-State Power Amplifiers
authors: Kun Zhang, Fei Ren, Dongping Yang, Xing Liu, Zhongyang Hu, Weiguang Wang, Zhenlu Zhang, Hongsheng He
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To meet the requirements of low repetition frequency and large pulse width of the power supply for aerospace solid-state power amplifiers, based on the analysis of the circuit working modes and circuit simulation, this paper adopts a push-pull circuit to develop a 32V/10A/320W power supply prototype. Through the input ripple suppression control measures, this secondary power supply enables the normal operation of the product under the conditions of a repetition frequency of 147Hz and a pulse width of 1.6ms, and suppresses the input current ripple within 1.6A, meeting the usage requirements and reliability requirements of the solid-state power amplifier.

title: A Two-Phase LLM-Based Procedure for Processing Spanish-Language Qualitative Teaching Feedback in Higher Education
authors: Edgardo R. Bravo
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Purpose: This study addresses the challenge of efficiently processing Spanish-language qualitative teaching feedback in higher education by developing a systematic approach combining Large Language Models (LLMs) with human oversight. Method: A two-phase procedure was implemented: Phase 1 utilized ChatGPT-4 for generating feedback categories from student comments, followed by expert validation. Phase 2 employed the validated framework to classify individual comments through LLM processing and human refinement. Findings: The procedure yielded eighteen distinct categories encompassing pedagogical and interpersonal dimensions of teaching. The implementation effectively categorizes diverse student feedback while maintaining analytical rigor through human oversight. Originality/Value: This study presents an innovative, resource-efficient approach for Spanish-language institutions to process qualitative feedback, addressing limitations in current NLP solutions while ensuring pedagogical relevance. Research limitations: Current limitations of the procedure include prompt dependency, execution variability, context window restrictions, and the black box nature of LLMs, which impede complete understanding and auditability of the analytical process. Practical implications: The framework enables systematic analysis of qualitative feedback for evidence-based decision-making in faculty development and course improvement initiatives.

title: Scenario-Based Forecasting of Epidemics Using Generative AI and Traditional Methods: An Empirical Evaluation on Epidemiological Data
authors: Yurii Oliinyk, Stefan-Gheorghe Pentiuc
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: The COVID-19 time series are characterized by non-stationary dynamics and volatile spikes, making traditional deterministic models fragile when data distributions shift. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of generative artificial intelligence architectures, including Convolutional Transformers, Variational Autoencoders (VAE), and Time-series Generative Adversarial Networks (TimeGAN) against traditional machine learning regressors for epidemic forecasting. The study analyses point predictions as well as the generation of probabilistic scenarios: optimistic, probable, and pessimistic. To address model heterogeneity, a Robust Median Ensemble is introduced to construct a consensus meta-scenario, providing stability and outlier immunity against individual model failures. Experimental results using COVID-19 data from Romania demonstrate that while traditional methods remain computationally competitive, generative models, particularly when enhanced by TimeGAN-driven synthetic data augmentation, offer significant advantages in quantifying uncertainty. The proposed framework establishes a stable Risk Zone essential for strategic crisis management and short- to medium-term resource planning.

title: AI-Driven Cyber Defense: Advanced Multimodal Learning for Evolving Malware Threats
authors: Ahmed Sarwar Mohammed
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The rapid evolution of sophisticated malware necessitates advanced detection mechanisms beyond traditional signature-based methods. This paper introduces a multimodal deep learning framework for malware classification that integrates static and dynamic analysis. By combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for bytecode image analysis and Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units (BiGRUs) for API call sequence modeling, our approach captures both structural and behavioral malware traits. Experiments conducted on a diverse dataset demonstrate robust classification performance with 84% accuracy, outperforming single-modality baselines. The work highlights the role of AI-driven cybersecurity solutions in engineering education, emphasizing the need for adaptive, intelligent systems in professional training as artificial intelligence becomes integral to real-world defense systems.

title: A Novel Integrated Three-Phase Core and Winding Structure for Hybrid Distribution Transformers
authors: Qingbin Wang, Xin Zhang, Jiawen Huang, Jiaming Liang, Boming Deng, Yufei Hua
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: For low system strength zones, hybrid distribution transformers are commonly employed to provide robust voltage support and effectively manage power quality. Integrated hybrid transformers achieve further size reduction by optimizing core and winding structures, however, existing research struggles to resolve the issue of magnetic saturation in the central yoke. To address this, a three-phase enhanced integrated hybrid transformer is proposed. By optimizing the sequence of the three-phase auxiliary windings, the maximum flux through the central yoke is further reduced, enabling additional reductions in both the volume and cost of the hybrid transformer. Case study validates the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach.

title: KitMec: A Design-Based Proposal of a Modular Educational Kit for Teaching Simple Machines in STEAM Education
authors: Fernando Perez, Juan Del Aguila, Consuelo Cano, Eduardo Ajito, Enrique Carrillo
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This research presents the design, implementation, and validation of KitMec, a modular educational tool developed to support the teaching and learning of simple machines in engineering and design education. The study examines persistent difficulties students face in understanding and applying mechanical principles, particularly in Latin American contexts where access to didactic materials is limited or dependent on costly imported kits. A user-centered, design-based methodology was applied, structured in three stages: exploration, conceptualization, and validation. Six university instructors from industrial design, mechanical engineering, and mechatronics, along with ten design students, participated in semi-structured interviews to identify pedagogical needs. Subsequently, a digital validation involving 29 participants achieved a 72 % acceptance rate. Results indicate that KitMec promotes active learning, improves conceptual understanding, and increases motivation through hands-on experimentation and interdisciplinary collaboration. Its modular, low-cost design enables adaptability across disciplines and educational levels, contributing to inclusive and sustainable STEM/STEAM education in Latin America.

title: DRAMS: Threat and Role of Ground Based Air Defence Weapon Systems
authors: Siddharth Baloni, Anmoldeep Bhullar, Vipin Kumar Singh, Shubham Pandey, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Drones, Rockets, Artillery, and Munition Systems (DRAMS) have fundamentally transformed modern battlefields through coordinated saturation attacks that overwhelm traditional air defense systems. Recent conflicts including the Armenia-Azerbaijan conflict (2020), Russo-Ukraine operations (2022-2025), Israel-Iran confrontation (June 2025) and Operation SINDOOR (May 2025) demonstrate DRAMS' decisive operational impact. Critical challenges in detection, tracking and interception of low-RCS platforms are identified across multiple threat brackets-low altitude (0-10,000 feet), medium altitude (10,000-30,000 feet) and deep envelope (above 30,000 feet). Ground Based Air Defence Weapon Systems (GBADWS) have transitioned from protective shields to critical enablers of combat power, demanding evolution into integrated, AI-enabled multi-layered architectures combining kinetic and non-kinetic effectors with resilient command and control networks. The paper analyzes DRAMS threat evolution, manifestations in recent conflicts and environment scan of Counter-DRAMS capabilities. Recommendations emphasize multi-sensor fusion, indigenous Directed Energy Weapons (DEW) development, quantum-secure C2 architecture and organic Counter-UAS capabilities. Establishment of multi-layered AD architecture, AI-enabled sensor fusion and integration across services as a central pillar are identified as essential for future operational dominance in contested airspace.

title: Agentic Application in Power Grid Static Analysis: Automatic Code Generation and Error Correction
authors: Qinjuan Wang, Shan Yang, Yongli Zhu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper introduces an LLM agent that automates power grid static analysis by converting natural language into MATPOWER scripts. The framework utilizes DeepSeek-OCR to build an enhanced vector database from MATPOWER manuals. To ensure reliability, it devises a three-tier error-correction system: a static pre-check, a dynamic feedback loop, and a semantic validator. Operating via the Model Context Protocol, the tool enables asynchronous execution and automatically debugging in MATLAB. Experimental results demonstrate that the system achieves a 82.38% accuracy regarding the code fidelity, effectively eliminating hallucinations even in complex analysis tasks.

title: Transient Oscillation Suppression Strategy for Grid-Forming Converters Transitioning from Islanded to Grid-Connected Mode
authors: Hao Zhang, Dingming Meng, Liangli Xiong, Chen Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Transient stability issues of grid-forming (GFM) converters during the transition from islanded (IS) mode to grid-connected (GC) mode have attracted increasing attention. Existing studies mainly focus on the analysis of nonlinear transient dynamics under different system parameters, while effective suppression strategies for loss of synchronism during the mode transition remain insufficiently investigated. To this end, this paper first analyzes the loss of synchronism mechanism of GFM converters under virtual synchronous generator (VSG) control during the grid connection process. On this basis, a stability enhancement strategy for the pre-synchronization grid connection of GFM converters is proposed to prevent loss of synchronism at the grid connection instant. By injecting frequency compensation into the active power control loop at the grid connection instant, the proposed strategy enhances transient stability during the IS to GC mode transition. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Flexible Adaptive Virtual Impedance Control Strategy for Grid-Forming Converters to Suppress Fault Current in Multiple Application Scenarios
authors: Xiangchu Xu, Hui Liu, Yangfan Zhang, Jingbo Liu, Yaohan Wang, Yan Li, Chao Gong, Zhenglin Huan
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the problems of poor multi-scenario adaptability and slow transient overcurrent suppression response of traditional virtual resistance, this paper proposes a grid-forming fault overcurrent suppression strategy based on multi-scenario application and flexible virtual resistance regulation. First, a simulation model of the grid-forming converter system is established. The strategy achieves accurate multi-scenario adaptation by combining the short-circuit ratio, power output quality, and system damping ratio requirements of the application scenarios. Second, it deduces the virtual resistance value range based on fault ride-through standards and overcurrent theory to ensure parameter rationality. When a fault occurs, the adaptive regulation strategy based on the instantaneous current magnitude and its rate of change is effectively implemented to improve the dynamic adaptation capability of the virtual resistance to transient overcurrent and dynamically correct the virtual resistance value. Finally, a controller-level hardware-in-the-loop test platform based on a real-time simulator and an actual controller is built, and the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed control strategy are tested and verified through typical operating scenarios. This strategy improves scenario adaptability and dynamic regulation performance of virtual resistance, can quickly respond to transient overcurrent, effectively suppress fault overcurrent, and enhance the system's fault ride-through capability and operational stability.

title: Smart Vision for Clean Cities: Deep Learning-Based Graffiti Detection in Prague
authors: Luuk Milo van Breugel, Segar Met, Vishay Ramcharan, Tomas Horak, Miroslav Svitek
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: The city of Prague, Czech Republic, has one of the most beautiful historic centers in the world. However, it is not immune to vandalism such as graffiti, which affects cultural heritage and contributes to a negative public perception of cleanliness and safety. Traditional methods of graffiti prevention remain labor-intensive and inefficient. This paper presents a smart vision approach for automated graffiti detection in Prague by the use of a Faster R-CNN model with a ResNet-50 backbone and data augmentation. The model is trained in the STORM dataset and achieves a precision of 79%, recall of 70%, and mIoU of 70% at an optimized threshold. Approximately 400 images where collected in a 500 × 500 meter area in the Old Town of Prague, including historical religious buildings and a dense network of narrow medieval streets. These images, analyzed by the model, were able to construct a spatial graffiti-intensity heatmap with hotspots concentrated in narrow streets with limited surveillance, while public squares and big streets showed fewer detections. Despite room for improvement in the model, the generated heatmap can already support more efficient graffiti prevention strategies, including optimized police patrol routes and targeted cleaning programs.

title: Real-Time Infrared Small Moving Object Detection Based on OS-CFAR
authors: Mohammad Rezaee, Yaser Norouzi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper presents a novel algorithm for detecting small moving objects in infrared imagery. The method employs a OS-CFAR detector as the initial stage and integrates temporal information to suppress false alarms, addressing challenges such as complex backgrounds, illumination changes, and camera motion. By combining spatial feature analysis with temporal cues, detection accuracy and stability are improved. A motion comparison step further eliminates objects lacking independent movement, which is critical in environments affected by vibrations or platform motion. Experiments on the ISD dataset show that the proposed approach achieves a strong balance between detection probability, false alarm rate, and processing speed, making it suitable for real-time applications such as intelligent surveillance and small object detection under real-world conditions.

title: Quantifying the Thermal States of Underground Cables Impacted by Renewable Resources Variability in Power Distribution Systems
authors: Yanxi Lyu, Lurui Fang, Xinxin Zhang, Artem Vanin, Xinsong Zhang, Lin Jiang, Eng Gee Lim
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Understanding the thermal state of underground cables plays a critical role for distribution system operators (DSO) for effective asset management. With the increasing available renewable energy sources, the principal determination of cable thermal state, i.e. network load profiles, is altering with great volatility. A new problem, therefore, arises. Under different renewable resources availability, the thermal states of underground cables are unknown. This paper reveals the thermal state of underground cables by two means: 1) daily typical temperature profiles; and 2) probability distribution of daily equivalent aging temperature. First, this paper clusters year-round meteorological data to identify representative operation scenarios, including solar-dominated, wind-dominated, and load-dominated conditions. Within each scenario, given the corresponding load profiles, a bundle of transient temperature profiles for cables are identified. The median thermal profile is extracted as the typical transient temperature profile for this scenario. Second, this paper developed a new equivalent aging temperature (EQT) method to convert each transient temperature profile into one value. The distribution of EQT reflects the aging states of cables of each scenario. The results show that under solar-dominated scenarios, sunset load peak and high environment temperature result in the accelerated thermal aging of underground cables. Comparatively, under wind-dominated scenario, the thermal aging speed is significantly reduced considering the flattened and reduced load profiles.

title: Study on Intra-day Optimal Dispatching and Collaborative Allocation of Energy Storage Capacity of Microgrid Considering Market Mechanism
authors: Siqi Yu, Yeteng Wang, Yan Zhao, Baihui Yuan, Wei Xu, Yuhan Yin, Nana Li, Cheng Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing proportion of new energy installed capacity, its volatility and intermittence bring severe challenges to the safe and stable operation of traditional power systems. As a significant carrier of high proportions of renewable energy access, microgrids can effectively achieve system stability, a reliable power supply, and efficient local consumption of new energy through the scientific allocation of energy storage. This paper focuses on the problem of intra-day optimal scheduling and cooperative allocation of energy storage capacity of microgrid, considering the power market mechanism, and puts forward a model of intra-day optimal scheduling and cooperative allocation of energy storage capacity of microgrid with careful consideration of new energy entering the market, time-of-use electricity price, power generation cost, and other factors. The model aims at minimizing the full-cycle investment and operation cost, and considers key conditions such as power balance and gas unit output. Multi-scenario simulation results indicate that the cooperative optimization of energy storage capacity and charging and discharging power significantly enhances the economic and flexible operation of microgrid regulation. The research proves that the mode of "grid connection + energy storage optimization" gives consideration to economy, cleanliness, and reliability under the current market and electricity price mechanism, and is the optimal scheme for the current microgrid construction.

title: Investigation of a Multi-Stage Progressive Voltage Optimization Strategy for Distribution Networks Utilizing Cluster Partitioning
authors: Xiao Yu Wei, Xiu Fang Gu, Jia Qian Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The rising integration of distributed solar systems into distribution grids has led to more pronounced voltage deviation difficulties due to their variable output. A multi-stage progressive voltage optimization technique utilizing cluster partitioning is offered to address this issue. A cluster partitioning indication system is initially built to split the entire grid into multiple independent clusters. A control demand evaluation model is subsequently developed based on voltage deviation levels to prioritize clusters by severity. A multi-stage progressive optimization model is then developed to successively optimize clusters from most to least crucial, utilizing an advanced particle swarm optimization method for the solution. Ultimately, simulation validation is performed on the IEEE 33-node system. The findings indicate that the proposed technique markedly enhances voltage distribution, efficiently mitigates voltage over-limiting, and diminishes system voltage fluctuations.

title: Advancements in High-Aspect Ratio Container Etch Systematic Edge Yield in 1β DRAM Devices
authors: Brent Brady, Keigo Nakamura, Hironobu Misawa, Alan Chin, Lionking Fu, Cop Wang
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: The following paper discusses novel solutions to conventional 1β dynamic random access memory (DRAM) high aspect ratio container (HARC) etchers with limited edge depth & tilt tuning capability, where container shutdown/tilting are the forefront of systematic yield loss. Design for temperature of the etcher’s edge/focus ring, coupled with active management of auxiliary radio frequency (RF) sheath and reflected sheath power have both shown capability to enhance container etch depth uniformity and tilting profile up to the extreme edge of the wafer. These advancements have reduced edge functional yield loss due to container shutdown by 41%, reduced edge tilt variation by 39%, improved tilt dieloss by 7%, and enabled 10% extension in mean time between cleans (MTBC).

title: Efficient Edge-Based Facial Emotion Recognition using a Compact Vision Transformer
authors: Maryam Nezhad-Moghaddam, Nima Esmi, Fatemeh Borhani, Asadollah Shahbahrami, Georgi Gaydadjiev, Peter de Jonge
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper presents a low-latency and energy-efficient facial emotion recognition (FER) system designed for real-time execution on edge hardware. A lightweight Vision Transformer (ViT-Tiny, patch16-224) is fine-tuned on the FER-2013 and FERD datasets, achieving an accuracy of around 78% on the three-class setting (happy, neutral, sad). To meet the constraints of embedded deployment, the model is exported to ONNX, quantized to INT8, and compiled using the Hailo Dataflow Compiler. The resulting system runs on a Raspberry Pi 5 equipped with a Hailo-8L accelerator, delivering over 85 FPS, and ≈4.8W power consumption, representing nearly a 9× improvement over CPU-only inference. Results demonstrate that transformer-based approaches, when properly optimized for hardware, can support real-time FER on resource-constrained devices while keeping all data processing on-device, providing an inherent privacy benefit.

title: EchoFusion: Multi-View Transformer Architecture with Anatomical Constraints for Comprehensive Echocardiographic Assessment
authors: Mohsen Avesta, Maedeh Avesta, Asma Yousefian Baboukani, Bashir Najafabadian, Mohaddeseh Behjati
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper presents a rigorous theoretical and practical analysis of multi-view transformer architectures for echocardiographic video interpretation. We develop a complete mathematical framework describing the information flow from raw pixel inputs to clinical predictions, establishing formal connections between convolutional feature extraction, temporal modeling, and multi-task learning. Our contribution includes: (1) a comprehensive mathematical formalization of the architecture with complete derivation of forward and backward propagation; (2) theoretical analysis of representational capacity, optimization landscape, and generalization bounds; (3) novel multi-view cross-attention mechanism with formal guarantees on information integration efficiency; (4) auxiliary anatomical segmentation tasks with joint optimization framework; (5) detailed experimental validation protocol with statistical significance testing across 15 clinical metrics; (6) comprehensive ablation studies isolating architectural components; and (7) clinical impact analysis with cardiologist validation. The mathematical framework establishes precise relationships between architectural hyperparameters and clinical performance metrics, providing design principles for future work. Our proposed extensions demonstrate statistically significant improvements a cross 12 of 15 clinical tasks (p< 0.01), with largest gains in measurements requiring spatial-temporal integration (EF MAE reduced from 4.2% to 3.1%). This work bridges theoretical deep learning with clinical echocardiography interpretation, establishing rigorous mathematical foundations for video-based cardiac assessment.

title: A Novel Bi-LSTM for Predicting Vehicle Fuel Consumption Using Limited Vehicle-Level Features
authors: Mohammad Barbod Amirmazlaghani, Mahdi Karami, Nima Rastegar, Siamak Alizadehnia, Seyed Mohammad Mahdi Pazhouh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Vehicle fuel consumption prediction plays a critical role in optimizing driving behavior, enhancing vehicle design, and improving operational efficiency. This study introduces a Bi-Directional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) model to predict instantaneous fuel consumption using only limited vehicle-level features such as vehicle speed, gear shift position, road slope, and acceleration. Unlike traditional models that require extensive engine-level data, the proposed Bi-LSTM model leverages commonly available vehicle-level features, often found in standard vehicle evaluation test cycles, making it a practical tool for simulating vehicle fuel consumption during testing. The Bi-LSTM model is trained and evaluated on real-world driving data collected from a vehicle’s On-Board Diagnostic (OBD) system, and the dataset is augmented using a novel trip-based data augmentation method introduced in this study. The Bi-LSTM model is evaluated across various test trips, demonstrating significant improvements in prediction accuracy over conventional regression models and unidirectional LSTM networks. Experimental results highlight the model's robustness and generalizability, showcasing its potential as a valuable tool for vehicle fuel consumption prediction, even in scenarios with limited data.

title: A Distributionally Robust CVaR Model for Two-Stage Coordinated Microgrid Planning and Benders Decomposition Solution
authors: Shuzheng Wang, Shengyuan Wang, Zhi Wu, Shouhao Wu, Haode Wu, Guyue Zhu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a two-stage coordinated planning approach for an interconnected distribution system with multiple microgrid operators (MGOs) and a distribution system operator (DSO). The model jointly optimizes distributed generation and energy storage allocation under renewable and load uncertainties, considering network constraints and multi-agent energy interactions. Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) is incorporated to capture tail-end risks, while a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) ambiguity set enhances robustness against scenario probability deviations. A Benders/L-shaped decomposition is employed to decouple capacity planning from scenario-based operational subproblems, enabling efficient solution via iterative cut generation. Case studies show that the proposed method reduces exposure to extreme-scenario costs and improves planning stability compared to deterministic and expectation-based stochastic approaches.

title: Application of Artificial Intelligence in Smart Grids: Methods and Challenges
authors: Nadia Khiadani
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Smart grids (SGs), as a data-driven layer on top of traditional power grids, increase efficiency, reliability, and stability by processing information collected from various parts of the power system. However, the complex structure, huge volume, and diversity of data generated in these systems have made the use of traditional data processing methods inefficient. As a result, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) methods and algorithms for processing, forecasting and managing smart grids is essential. This research examines various applications of AI in key areas of SGs, including fault diagnosis, grid load forecasting, system stability assessment, and cybersecurity. It also discusses the technical and practical challenges of applying AI methods in complex and dynamic smart grid environments. This research shows that integrating AI with SG infrastructures, in addition to improving efficiency, increases the flexibility and resilience of these systems.

title: Research on Optimization of Instrument Settings and Logical Improvements in the Secondary Loop Sampling System
authors: Xuecui Ma, Yulei Liu, Min Gao, Qingfu Hao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The purpose of this article is to design a scheme that can realize the display and alarm of signals from different pipelines, meeting the requirements for the display and alarm of signals from different pipelines. In the secondary loop sampling system of a nuclear power plant, a shared analytical instrument is set up for different sampling pipelines. When switching sampling pipelines, the local control system issues a switch signal, which serves as the condition for setting the alarm and display logic, and performs logical operations, screen display, and alarms in the DCS control system. Through this method, it can effectively solve the problems of false alarms and misdisplay caused by the sharing of the same instrument by different sampling pipelines in the power plant, eliminating safety hazards in plant operation. At the same time, this design scheme not only applies to conventional detection, display, and alarm issues but also applies to situations where different pipelines operating under different conditions have the same signals. The innovation of this paper lies in achieving the safe and effective operation of the process system through this design scheme with reduced investment, which has practical economic benefits and market prospects.

title: Optimization of a Medium-Power Therapeutic Ultrasonic Generator for Bone Consolidation
authors: E. C. Barroso, A. J. Cañas, E. M. Hernandez, J. E. R Herrera, M. F. Pintor, L. L. Salas, A. Vera, N. V. Sanchez
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Ultrasound (UT) is widely used in medical applications due to its non-invasive nature and biological effectiveness, particularly at medium power levels for promoting bone consolidation and tissue healing. This work presents the design and implementation of a 1 MHz medium-power ultrasonic generator operating at 1–3W/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, composed of an ultrasonic transducer, an analog module, and a digital control module. The transducer is based on a PZT-4 piezoelectric ceramic (25.00–30.00 mm diameter, 2.00 mm thickness). Experimental analysis established that the operating temperature must remain below 40 °C to prevent tissue damage. The analog module ensured proper signal sampling, phase matching, and transmission, while the digital module, based on an ESP32 microcontroller with a touchscreen interface, enabled efficient system control. These results demonstrate the feasibility of implementing a medium-power therapeutic ultrasound system for bone consolidation within the framework of the 2025 SUTBIO project.

title: A Three-Stage Framework for Analyzing Residential Electricity Consumption Behaviors
authors: Mingshan Wu, Lurui Fang, Yimeng Wang, Xiaoyang Chen, Eng Gee Lim
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Complex residential electricity consumption behaviors (RECB) pose challenges to power grid operation and sustainable energy optimization. Existing studies on RECB have limitations, including subjective feature extraction for RECB classification, overemphasis on electricity consumption while ignoring pattern diversity, and insufficient consideration of the combined effects of meteorological conditions, tariff types, and wealth status. This research adopts a three-stage analytical framework to quantify RECB diversity under the interaction of these three factors, including Weather Pattern K-Means Clustering, LSTM-based RECB Classification, and Behavioral Diversity Quantification with Shannon Entropy. A dataset of 927 London households, containing 2013 load profiles, wealth status, and tariff types, is used to validate the proposed method. The results show that dynamic tariffs drive RECB convergence, this convergence is amplified by high radiation and high wind speed, and households with lower wealth status exhibit the lowest RECB diversity. These findings provide theoretical support for RECB research and practical guidance for power grid operation, demand-side management optimization, and energy policy formulation.

title: Variance-Guided Adaptive DWT-DCT Image Steganography for Defense and UAV Applications
authors: Gatram Sravan Kumar, Mitu Baral, Mettu Bhavya Nayana Reddy, Kamalakanta Sethi, G Rama Mohan Babu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The secure and secret transmission of visual data is a very important need in recent military and monitoring systems, especially when it comes to the use of UAVs and scouting devices. Image steganography is an efficient technique for hiding classified data inside common images, thereby preventing the detection of covert communication. In this paper, an adaptive transformdomain image steganography method is proposed for defenseoriented applications. The approach combines Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) to perform embedding in high-frequency wavelet sub-bands, while secret surveillance images are embedded into mid-frequency DCT coefficients to maintain a balance between invisibility, robustness, and embedding efficiency. The proposed method employs variance-guided adaptive payload control, allowing the embedding strength to adjust according to local image texture characteristics. Extensive experiments conducted on standard benchmark images and UAV surveillance data evaluate the performance using metrics such as Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), and resistance to common image processing attacks. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms conventional spatial-domain, transformdomain, and hybrid steganography techniques. These findings confirm the effectiveness of the proposed system for secure and covert image transmission in military and surveillance operations.

title: DORA: Dataflow-Instruction Orchestration Architecture for DNN Acceleration
authors: Xingzhen Chen, Zhuoping Yang, Jinming Zhuang, Shixin Ji, Sarah Schultz, Zheng Dong, Weisong Shi, Peipei Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Modern DNN workloads are increasingly diverse in operation types, tensor shapes, and execution dependencies, making it difficult for customized accelerators to sustain high hardware efficiency across models. We propose DORA, an instruction-based overlay architecture as well as a compilation framework that explicitly describes dataflow via a proposed ISA, enabling fine-grained control of data movement, computation, and synchronization at the layer level.

title: Work in Progress: A Quasi-Experimental Study of Technology-Enhanced Learning with Computer Simulation and Animation
authors: Ning Fang
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Computer simulation and animation (CSA) has received growing attention and wide application in STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics) education. This Work in Progress study aims to assess the effectiveness of an interactive set of CSA learning modules we recently developed to enhance student learning in an undergraduate engineering dynamics course, with a particular focus on particle dynamics. A quasi-experimental quantitative research study was conducted, involving pre- and post-tests administered during treatment and control sessions within an engineering dynamics course. The results show that overall, the CSA learning modules improved student learning gains by up to 30% in the treatment session compared to the control session. Students reported having positive experiences with CSA. It is concluded that CSA can serve as an effective pedagogical tool to supplement classroom lectures and enhance student learning in engineering dynamics.

title: Dual-Branch Multi-Task Transfer Learning for Short-Term PV Forecasting in Smart Grid-Integrated EV Charging Stations Under Data Scarcity
authors: Iman Soltani, Amirmohammad Molaei, Alireza Ebrahimi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The integration of photovoltaic (PV) systems into smart grid-connected electric vehicle (EV) charging stations requires accurate short-term forecasting to ensure reliable energy management and grid stability. However, newly deployed charging infrastructures often lack sufficient historical power data, limiting the effectiveness of conventional data-driven forecasting models. This study presents a dual-branch multi-task transfer learning framework specifically designed for hourly PV power forecasting under data-scarce conditions in smart grid-integrated EV fast-charging stations. The proposed approach combines meteorological and temporal features in one branch while processing global horizontal irradiance (GHI) in a separate branch, with active power as the primary prediction target and GHI as an auxiliary task. Transfer learning is employed by pretraining models on five years of meteorological and GHI data (2019–2023) before fine-tuning on a limited 4.5-month active power dataset (September 2024–February 2025) from a real operational site in Urmia, Iran. Extensive experiments on CNN, LSTM, and CNN-LSTM architectures demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms both simple transfer learning and from-scratch training, achieving test MAE improvements of 32–36% for LSTM, 23–38% for CNN, and 28–39% for CNN-LSTM. Ablation studies reveal that multi-task learning with optimal auxiliary task weighting (λ = 0.25–0.5) significantly enhances generalization and reduces prediction bias, particularly for daily peaks and ramp transitions. The framework offers a scalable and robust solution for renewable-powered charging infrastructures in smart grids, supporting optimized EV charging coordination, demand-response strategies, and grid decarbonization under limited data availability.

title: First Steps Towards Automated Grading of Handwritten Short Answers with LLMs
authors: Nina Yarovenko-Kuzminykh, Martín Llamas-Nistal, Manuel Caeiro-Rodríguez, Fernando-Ariel Mikic-Fonte
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper is a work-in-progress study that explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) can be used to improve automated short-answer grading (ASAG) in handwritten exams. A hybrid e-assessment approach that combines Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) and LLM-based grading for Spanish short-answer questions is presented. Initial experiments show that LLMs can accurately transcribe and understand handwritten responses. Commercial models performed best overall, but open-source options also showed promising results. Overall, the findings suggest that this first experience with LLMs could make grading more automated and scalable, helping educators manage assessments more effectively in digital and blended learning environments.

title: Artificial Intelligence-Driven Wind Power Frequency Regulation Control Technolegies and Applications
authors: Yaoxiang Chen, Xiaotong Zhang, Xin Xia, Huanxuan Yang, Can Wang, Keke Du, Tao Zhou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper presents a systematic review of artificial intelligence (AI)-driven control technologies for wind power frequency regulation, addressing the critical challenge of grid stability amid increasing wind energy integration. As wind power's share grows, the declining capacity of conventional units reduces system inertia and frequency regulation reserves, while the inherent intermittency of wind power complicates control. Traditional techniques often lack the adaptability for precise, real-time adjustment. The study first delineates the fundamentals of wind power frequency regulation, including primary and secondary control strategies, and maximum power point tracking (MPPT). It then comprehensively analyzes the application of AI algorithms, categorizing them into three domains: deep learning for wind speed prediction and direct parameter control, reinforcement learning for adaptive system optimization based on environmental feedback, and hybrid intelligent algorithms that synergize deep and reinforcement learning for enhanced perception and decision-making. The analysis projects future research directions, emphasizing model simplification, robustness under extreme weather, and the potential of online large-model systems leveraging advanced communication technologies. The review concludes that AI algorithms significantly improve the stability, precision, and intelligence of wind power frequency regulation, with hybrid algorithms representing a prominent trend for future power systems.

title: Finite Element Simulation (FEM) of Piezoelectric Micromachined Ultrasound Transducers (PMUT)
authors: Eduardo Moreno, Ricardo Gonzalez, Wagner Coelho, Roberto Giachetta, Jorge F. Cruza, Nuria Barniol
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: The development of 3D Finite Element Method (FEM) models for Piezoelectric Micromachined Ultrasound Transducer (PMUT) arrays, operating at approximately 200 kHz and 6 MHz , is presented. The objective is to evaluate its physical behavior with FEM models. Using COMSOL 6.2, single-element and array 7x7 configurations were simulated to evaluate vibration modes, electrical impedance, and ultrasonic radiation. The methodology involved was done assuming a low mechanical quality (Q) factor. Additionally, radiation steering was successfully demonstrated in low-frequency arrays, while high-frequency simulations in fluorinert identified a pseudo pulse-echo performance. These models provide a framework for designing next-generation ultrasonic devices for medical and industrial applications. This work is co-funded by the European Union under the Listen2Future Project (L2F).

title: Hybrid Methodology for the Design and Construction of an Aircraft for University Competitions
authors: Gloria Anaid González Sarmiento, Luis Mauro Ortega González, Andrés Alberto García-León
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Engineering students at Tecnológico de Monterrey, Campus Estado de México, frequently encounter difficulties in planning and executing the design and construction of ultralight aircraft prototypes, mainly due to limited project management training. These challenges hinder project completion on time and reduce opportunities for essential flight testing prior to international competitions. This study proposes the integration of project management methodologies-specifically the predictive framework of the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK) and the agile SCRUM approach-to improve organization, coordination and time management within student teams participating in the SAE Aero Design competition. By applying these methodologies to the design-build-test cycle, teams are expected to improve efficiency, ensure safety, and increase competitiveness in national and international aerospace design events.

title: Optimization Scheduling of Integrated Energy Systems Considering Flexibility and Demand Response
authors: Jinfeng Wang, Yaobin Wang, Zhaoyuan Zhang, Yuhui Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale grid connection of renewable energy, the uncertainty of its output and the volatility of load have intensified the energy fluctuations within the IES (Integrated Energy System), making flexibility gradually become an important indicator that must be paid attention to in IES operation. Enhancing the flexibility of IES can not only effectively alleviate the adverse impacts caused by the fluctuations of renewable energy but also enhance the stability and reliability of the overall IES. In this thesis, taking system flexibility as the breakthrough point, various flexibility resources in IES are mined, and a multi-objective optimal scheduling model for IES considering flexibility and demand response is constructed, comprehensively considering the economy and flexibility of the system. The model takes minimizing the system operation cost and maximizing the system flexibility index as the optimization objectives. Simulation is carried out under system constraint conditions, and the scheduling results are analyzed. Through the comparison of scheduling results in different scenarios, on the premise of balancing economy and flexibility, it is verified that demand response, as a flexibility resource, can effectively reduce operation costs and improve system flexibility.

title: Immersive Learning in Mathematics: Student Perceptions on the Use of Virtual Reality in the Classroom
authors: Peña-Becerril Maritza, Guadarrama-Cruz Eric, Jaimes-Vera Gonzalo, Becerril-Guadarrama Hannah N
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The design of an activity based on the constructivist approach is described, using the GeoGebra Mixed Reality virtual reality (VR) tool. The specialized literature indicates that VR stands out for generating immersive and interactive experiences for students, improving their understanding of disciplinary concepts by interacting with abstract objects and ideas in a three-dimensional environment. The activity was implemented in a multivariable calculus course with 75 university students in Mexico. The guiding question of this research is: What is the impact of using virtual reality in multivariable calculus classes from the students' perspective? The results show that students recognized that VR helped them better understand and reinforce the topics covered in class. The design of the activity under a constructivist approach proved to be an effective strategy, as it encourages the development of spatial reasoning and creative thinking.

title: Workshop: Responsible Artificial Intelligence in Higher Education: From Principles to Practice
authors: Martín Llamas Nistal, Usman Naeem, Lei Zhang, Luis Felipe Zapata Rivera
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The aim of this workshop is to introduce educators, researchers, and practitioners to the upcoming IEEE P3722 standard: Guide for Responsible Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Higher Education. The goals of this standard are to: 1.Increase awareness of responsible AI use in teaching, learning, and student support, 2.Provide hands-on guidance on implementing transparent and ethical AI practices, 3.Foster dialogue across academia, industry, and policy stakeholders. The workshop directly aligns with EDUNINE's mission of advancing engineering education through innovation, equity, and global collaboration. By equipping participants with practical strategies for responsible AI integration, the session contributes to EDUNINE's goal of enhancing the quality and impact of engineering education worldwide.

title: Towards Effective Sampling of SMT Solutions
authors: Shuangyu Lyu, Chuan Luo, Ruizhi Shi, Wei Wu, Chanjuan Liu, Chunming Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering
abstract: This work focuses on effectively generating diverse solutions for satisfiability modulo theories (SMT) formulas, targeting the theories of bit-vectors, arrays, and uninterpreted functions, which is a critical task in software and hardware testing. Generating diverse SMT solutions helps uncover faults and detect safety violations during verification and testing. This gives rise to the SMT sampling problem, whose goal is to construct a diverse solution set that achieves comprehensive coverage of the constraint space. While high coverage is crucial for exploring system behaviors, the number of generated solutions should also be kept small in software engineering scenarios. Since each SMT solution typically corresponds to a test case or test input, generating fewer solutions for the same coverage directly reduces testing time and resource consumption, thereby improving testing efficiency. In this work, we introduce PanSampler, a novel SMT sampler that achieves high coverage with a small number of solutions. PanSampler incorporates a new diversity-aware SMT algorithm and a post-sampling optimization strategy, which together enhance its practical performance. It iteratively samples solutions, evaluates candidates, and employs local search to refine solutions, ensuring high coverage with a small number of samples. Extensive experiments on practical benchmarks demonstrate that PanSampler exhibits a significantly stronger capability to reach high target coverage, while requiring fewer solutions than state-of-the-art samplers to achieve the same coverage level. Furthermore, our empirical evaluation on practical subjects, which are collected from real-world software systems, shows that PanSampler achieves stronger fault detection capability. Moreover, when reaching the same fault detection effectiveness as the competitors, PanSampler greatly reduces the number of required solutions, thereby improving testing efficiency. Our evaluations also validate the effectiveness of PanSampler’s core components. PanSampler represents a significant advancement in SMT sampling for software engineering, contributing to the cost reduction of software testing and hardware verification.

title: A Theoretical Characterization of the Good Properties of Extremely Randomized Trees for Random Forest-Distance Computation
authors: Manuele Bicego, Ferdinando Cicalese
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Ensembles of Extremely Randomized Trees (ERT), initially introduced for supervised scenarios, have been shown to represent surprisingly effective models also in unsupervised contexts, for example, for the detection of anomalies (i.e., Isolation Forests) or for defining Random Forest-distances. In this paper, we focus on this last field, providing a theoretical characterization to support the excellent empirical behaviors shown by ERTs in the definition of RF-distances. We start by assuming the existence, in a given domain, of a proper representation, i.e., a vectorial representation fulfilling the Compactness Hypothesis given by Arkadev and Braverman in 1967. Then, given the “true” distance between a pair of objects, we show that it is possible to derive a bound on the approximation guaranteed by an ERT-based RF-distance with respect to such true measure. In words, we show that it is possible to compute a constant $c$ such that, if a pair of objects are $\epsilon$-close in the true distance, then, with high probability, they are $(c \cdot \epsilon )$-close also in the ERT-based RF-distance.

title: High-Fidelity Forward-Transmission Vibration Sensing Using Dual-Sensor Beamforming
authors: Jian Fang, Yaowen Li, Wataru Kohno, Ting Wang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: While distributed acoustic sensing based on Rayleigh backscattering has been widely studied and deployed across various use cases, its adaptation in existing telecom infrastructure is fundamentally limited by inline optical amplifiers which block the backscattering light. Recently, vibration sensing based on forward transmission has emerged as a promising alternative, offering wider frequency response, higher signal-to-noise ratio, longer sensing distance, and compatibility with inline amplifiers. However, forward-transmission approach faces the challenge in signal identification and reconstruction when multiple vibration events and interference occur along the fiber simultaneously. To address this issue, we propose an equivalent dual-sensor phased array model to the bidirectional forward-transmission fiber sensing, which reformulates the problems in fiber sensing into the tasks in array processing. We introduce two beamforming techniques, including generalized sidelobe canceller (GSC) and null steering beamformer (NSBF) to reconstruct the signal of interest while suppressing noise and interference. We demonstrate the effectiveness of dual-sensor beamforming over 100km fiber experiment without amplifiers, achieving over 20 dB suppression of stationary interference using GSC and 18 dB jamming rejection using NSBF. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of high-fidelity signal recovery in forward-transmission sensing under strong noise and interference. This work bridges the gap between fiber sensing and adaptive array processing, opening new directions for blind source separation and integrated communication-sensing systems.

title: Toward Intelligent Earth Observation: Hierarchical Feature Fusion and a Drone Dataset for UAV-Based Fire Detection
authors: Sufyan Danish, Samee Ullah Khan, L. Minh Dang, Hyoung-Kyu Song, Hyeonjoon Moon
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In UAV-based monitoring and remote sensing, early fire detection remains challenging due to the complex appearance, scale variation, and spatial ambiguity of fire scenes. Existing deep networks often rely heavily on feature maps from preceding layers, which can weaken fine-grained spatial representation and reduce detection accuracy, particularly for small, distant, or irregular fire regions. To address these limitations, we propose a Hierarchical Feature Fusion (HFF) framework that captures fire patterns across multiple scales. The proposed framework integrates Discriminative Fire Features (DFF) with a Fire-Guided Attention (FGA) module to refine hierarchical representations and improve sensitivity to varying fire intensities, distances, and scene conditions. In addition, to address the limited diversity of existing datasets, we introduce a new Drone Fire (DF) dataset containing a broad range of fire scenarios, geographic settings, and environmental conditions, while reducing class imbalance. Extensive experiments on four datasets, namely DF, FD, FLAME, and DSFD, show that HFF achieves accuracies of 89.16%, 97.52%, 97.50%, and 96.00%, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework provides reliable and robust performance across diverse UAV-based fire monitoring scenarios, making it well-suited for remote sensing applications in fire surveillance and disaster management.

title: Selective Noise Suppression and Discriminative Mutual Interaction for Robust Audio-Visual Segmentation
authors: Kai Peng, Yunzhe Shen, Miao Zhang, Leiye Liu, Yidong Han, Wei Ji, Jingjing Li, Yongri Piao, Huchuan Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: The ability to capture and segment sounding objects in dynamic visual scenes is crucial for the development of Audio-Visual Segmentation (AVS) tasks. While significant progress has been made in this area, the interaction between audio and visual modalities still requires further exploration. In this work, we aim to answer the following questions: How can a model effectively suppress audio noise while enhancing relevant audio information? How can we achieve discriminative interaction between the audio and visual modalities? To this end, we propose SDAVS, equipped with the Selective Noise-Resilient Processor (SNRP) module and the Discriminative Audio-Visual Mutual Fusion (DAMF) strategy. The proposed SNRP mitigates audio noise interference by selectively emphasizing relevant auditory cues, while DAMF ensures more consistent audio-visual representations. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark AVS datasets, especially in multi-source and complex scenes.

title: Comparative Analysis of CNN Architectures for High-Accuracy Subtype Classification of Renal Cell Carcinoma using CECT
authors: Ramanath Kini M G, Paul K Laju, Vikas Bhat, Ananthakrishna T, Prakashini K, Anitha H
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Engineering in Medicine and Biology
abstract: Goal: Objective: Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC) is a heterogeneous cancer with multiple histological subtypes that require individualized clinical management. Once implemented, timely and appropriate subtype delineation from imaging data can lead to improved diagnostic accuracy and intervention decisions. Methods: Study compares 3 convolutional neural networks (CNN) architectures, AlexNet, GoogleNet, VGG16, Resnet18 and Densenet to determine which approach has the best performance for RCC subtype classification of Contrast-Enhanced computed tomography (CECT) images. The original dataset of 590 images from 90 patients was augmented using anatomically constrained augmentation (rotation ±5° and 2D scaling) to generate a total of 3469 unique images, with allocation of training, validation, and testing sets was 70:15:15, respectively. Each architecture was fine tuned to employ an optimized learning rate, dropout regularization, and L2 penalties to minimize overfitting. Results: The quantitative metrics for each architecture, displayed in Table IV, show that VGG16 achieved the maximum level of 99.21% accuracy but demonstrated some levels of overfitting behavior at the lower learning rates. GoogleNet achieved commendable accuracy levels of 97.67% accuracy after epoch optimization, but performance levels were highly sensitive across the learning rate. AlexNet, in comparison demonstrated the most stable convergence rates and achieved strong levels of generalization within all three large learning rates, achieving levels of 98.84% accuracy, and processing times were far lower than for either of the other networks. Conclusion: this work demonstrates that GoogleNet is a reliable and cost efficient implementation of a CNN for a clinical setting that enables rapid accurate clinical decision making from imaging data for the specific subtype of RCC. Significance: These findings reinforce the potential of tailored deep learning frameworks for early RCC subtype classification and encourage further validation on larger, multi-institutional datasets to enhance clinical applicability.

title: In-Depth Cluster Conflict-Based Search for Multi-Agent Path Finding
authors: Jaeho Lee, Junwoo Park, Byeong-Min Jeong, Dae-Sung Jang, Han-Lim Choi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper addresses a variant of the conflict-based search (CBS) algorithm for multi-agent path finding, presenting an effective cluster reasoning heuristic that searches for and detects conflicts involving more than two agents. The proposed in-depth cluster reasoning technique systematically alters the reference agent when computing the heuristic cost-to-go, and this yields a tighter estimate. It then balances the complexity of expanding CBS nodes against that of locating clusters by systematically scheduling the associated parameters. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm successfully detects clusters that state-of-the-art algorithms cannot identify and produces solutions for various extreme scenarios that existing algorithms cannot solve.

title: Data Compression with Stochastic Codes
authors: Gergely Flamich, Deniz Gündüz
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: Machine learning has had a major impact on data compression over the last decade and opened up many new theoretical and applied fields of inquiry. This paper describes one such direction – relative entropy coding – which focuses on constructing stochastic codes, mainly as an alternative to quantisation and entropy coding in lossy source coding. Our primary aim is to provide a broad overview of the topic, with an emphasis on the computational and practical aspects currently missing from the literature. Our goal is threefold: for the curious reader, we aim to provide an intuitive picture of the field and convince them that relative entropy coding is a simple yet exciting emerging field in data compression research. For a reader interested in applied research on lossy data compression, we provide an account of the most salient contemporary applications. Finally, for the reader who has heard of relative entropy coding but has never been quite sure what it is or how the algorithms fit together, we hope to illustrate how simple and elegant the underlying constructions are.

title: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters publication
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters

title: A Comparison Benchmark for Distributed Hybrid MPC Control Methods: Distributed Vehicle Platooning
authors: Samuel Mallick, Azita Dabiri, Bart De Schutter
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Distributed model predictive control (MPC) is currently being investigated as a solution to the important control challenge presented by networks of hybrid dynamical systems, for which the computational complexity is often prohibitive. However, a benchmark problem for comparing distributed hybrid MPC solutions is absent from the literature.We propose distributed control of a platoon of autonomous vehicles as a comparison benchmark problem. The problem provides a complex and adaptable case study, upon which existing and future approaches to distributed MPC for hybrid systems can be evaluated. Two hybrid modeling frameworks are presented for the vehicle dynamics. Five hybrid MPC controllers are then evaluated and extensively assessed on the fleet of vehicles. Finally, we comment on the need for new efficient and high performing distributed MPC schemes for hybrid systems.

title: A New Family of Buck–Boost DC–DC Converters Derived from Modified Boost and ZETA Topologies for EV Charger Applications
authors: Rambabu Kallelapu, Sankar Peddapati
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: In this article, proposes a new family of non-isolated bidirectional DC-DC converter with the features of a quadratic buck-boost voltage gain, continuous input, and continuous output currents, which can be employed in EV charger applications, via., batteries or super-capacitors. The proposed family of bidirectional converters combines the advantages of high voltage gain, wide voltage range, low voltage stress across power switches, and less current ripples at the input and output sides. A comprehensive investigation of the steady-state and dynamic modeling analyses is presented for both discharge and charge modes. Furthermore, the continuous nature of currents at the input and output sides of the proposed converters enhances the battery's lifetime and reduces the size of the DC-link capacitor. A 300 W, 50 kHz laboratory prototype of the proposed EV charger is developed, with real-time experiments confirming the theoretical findings. Furthermore, a comparative analysis with recent bidirectional converters across several performance indices confirms that the proposed family of DC-DC converter topology delivers enhanced performance, including a higher effective index, lower switch-voltage stress, reduced current ripple, and notable peak efficiencies.
