title: TimeLiteNet: Empowering Pre-training-Free Small-Sample State-of-Health Prediction of Lithium-Ion Batteries
authors: Yandan Tan, Chong Ma, Yubing Bao, Yuxin Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Traditional small-sample learning for lithium-ion battery state-of-health (SOH) prediction relies on pre-trained models transferred from large-scale datasets, which suffer from negative transfer due to domain mismatch and face challenges in acquiring sufficient labelled data for pre-training. To address these limitations, we propose TimeLiteNet, a novel deep learning framework for SOH estimation under small-sample training conditions, without requiring any pre-training. Within TimeLiteNet, a residual temporal convolution module (ResTCM) captures long-term temporal dependencies in aging trajectories using dilated causal convolutions. Residual connections are further incorporated to ensure stable gradient propagation in low-data regimes. Complementing this, a hierarchical differential learning (HDL) structure amplifies subtle yet critical information via multi-level differentiation, significantly enhancing sensitivity to key health indicators. Additionally, we also introduce an enhanced incremental capacity feature generation method by exploiting fine-grained charging voltage dynamics. Ablation studies demonstrate that the residual mechanism reduces root mean square error (RMSE) by over 47%, while the HDL contributes to more than 45% RMSE reduction in low-correlation regimes. Experiments show that TimeLiteNet achieves superior generalization across multiple tasks using only 10% of the available training data, consistently outperforming advanced methods. The results underscore the efficacy of TimeLiteNet as a data-efficient solution for SOH prediction in battery management scenarios.

title: AIDLC–FMEA: Explainability-Integrated Lifecycle Risk Assessment for AI Governance
authors: Chin-Pei He, Yu-Ru Pan, Kuo-Chung Chu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The growing deployment of AI-enabled systems in safety- and decision-critical contexts has exposed limitations in existing governance and risk assessment approaches, particularly regarding lifecycle alignment, interpretability, and decision support. This paper presents AIDLC-FMEA, a generalizable and explainability-aware governance risk analysis framework that extends Failure Mode and Effects Analysis to support structured examination of AI governance across the development lifecycle. The framework formulates governance risk as a phase-coupled aggregation problem and incorporates explainability as a sensitivity-based risk amplification mechanism within a lifecycle mapping structure. An Extended Governance Risk Priority Number is defined to characterize phase-level governance-related risk, which is subsequently aggregated into bounded system-level compliance indices to support comparative analysis and interpretive benchmarking in relation to selected governance frameworks. The framework is evaluated through formal analysis of key model properties, simulation-based sensitivity analysis, and illustrative reconstruction of post-2020 AI incident cases. These analyses illustrate how lifecycle-aware risk aggregation and explainability-related sensitivity contribute to differentiated governance risk profiles across heterogeneous AI systems, in ways that can be interpreted in relation to selected governance concepts reflected in ISO/IEC 42001. Overall, AIDLC-FMEA is designed to support governance risk diagnosis and comparative analysis, rather than prediction or certification, and provides an interpretable and computationally tractable analytical tool for enterprise-level AI governance and managerial risk oversight.

title: High-Reliable and Power-Aware RISC-V Hardware-Software Co-design for Mission-Critical UAV
authors: Omkar Shirasgaonkar, Rahul Ratnakumar, Ashwin R Rao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Reliable operation of processors with stringent power and weight requirements continues to be a major challenge to the Unmanned Aerial Systems. The paper is a proposal to implement an adaptive execution-unit-based Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) architecture to a 32-bit in-order RISC-V processor to operate on mission-critical drone platforms, using two proven UAV power models- Eman and Eldusoky. The software used on the soft-core is determined by the real-time environment which is the one that triggers which model to run TMR mode. The proposed architecture is also a selective triplication, unlike conventional full-core redundancy methods that impose a significant area and power overhead, which selectively triplicates only the execution unit, which is one of the most fault-sensitive parts of the processor pipeline. Transient faults are covered by a lightweight majority voting mechanism before they spread to the architectural state. In order to enhance energy efficiency, an Adaptive Majority Voting Unit (AMVU) is proposed to dynamically regulate the activation of redundancy depending on the system conditions like the available battery energy and mission requirements. The architecture is synthesized with an ASIC design flow till GDS-II using Cadence EDA, and assessed with analytical modeling using Simulink, MATLAB 2025. Experimentation has shown that the fault masking capability is enhanced significantly with much lower hardware overhead than traditional full-pipeline redundancy methods, keeping a firm grip on the real-time power efficiency for the UAV to complete its mission.

title: Selection, Aggregation, and Enhancement: Trajectory Consistent Diffusion Model for Image Super-Resolution
authors: Detian Huang, Yaohui Guo, Yuting Huang, Luanyuan Dai, Fei Shen, Huanqiang Zeng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Diffusion models have shown strong promise for image super-resolution (ISR). However, current approaches often underuse pretrained diffusion backbones and lack constraints on the sampling trajectory, which degrades structural consistency and fine details. For that, we introduce the trajectory consistent diffusion model (TCDM) for super-resolution, which jointly optimizes the sampling process through lightweight components and inference-time strategies while keeping the diffusion backbone frozen, yielding high-fidelity, detail-rich reconstructions. First, we propose a dynamic semantic selection (DSS) mechanism that records early intermediates, matches them to upsampled low-resolution features, and reconditions sampling with the best match to reduce the mismatch between conditioning and noise scale. Next, we design a cross-step aggregation guidance (CAG) strategy that aggregates features from the current state with the selected intermediate to enforce trajectory-level consistency in noise prediction. Finally, we present a plug-and-play frequency enhancement adapter (FE-Adapter) that injects different frequency-domain cues into the encoder during training, strengthening high-frequency perception while preserving global structures. Extensive experiments on multiple ISR benchmarks show that TCDM achieves strong structural fidelity and competitive no-reference perceptual quality, offering a favorable fidelity–perception trade-off.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Optimal Dispatch of Integrated Electricity and Heat Systems
authors: Xiaoyu Xin, Qiu-Wei Wu, Guocheng Song, Yang Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy, integrated electricity and heat systems exhibit strong multi-energy coupling and significantly heightened operational uncertainty, posing increasingly severe challenges to dynamic security operation. Conventional model-based optimization methods often rely on accurate system modeling and forecasting, which limits their adaptability and robustness in practical applications. To address the aforementioned issues, this paper investigates the optimal dispatch problem of integrated electricity and heat systems and establishes a centralized dispatch framework based on deep reinforcement learning. Within the proposed unified modeling framework, the coupling characteristics between the electricity and heat subsystems are explicitly considered, and coordinated multi-energy management is achieved through a single-agent proximal policy optimization algorithm.

title: XenoCipher: A Secure Key Exchange and Encryption Pipeline for Resource-Constrained Devices
authors: Alok Ranjan, Vivek S A, Subramanyam C V, Sujal Talekar, Sumit S Kadwadkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: In the current modern world due to the rise of Internet of Things there is an increase in the use of connected devices, such as smartwatches, smart thermometer, constantly sense and collect telemetry data from their environment and may send the collected data to a remote server located in the cloud for analytical processing. The data being sent has to be protected using an suitable cryptographic encryption/decryption system. However the challenges faced in the current systems are; traditional encryption algorithms like AES, RSA, ECC are not suitable for encrypting data in these devices because the devices have limited computational resources and these algorithms were designed for powerful systems such as pc, laptop, smartphones etc. Also current literature mainly focuses on fixed mode encryption for IoT, there is no runtime adaptability introduced in generating ciphers, most of the lightweight ciphers have single layered and static pipeline which is vulnerable to cyber attacks. Therefore in our quest to find solutions for these problems Xenocipher was developed. Xenocipher is a secure, lightweight, multilayered cryptographic system that operates at bit/stream level. It combines three lightweight symmetric algorithms in a layered fashion to generate strong ciphers, they are LFSR(Linear Feedback shift register) for pseudorandom stream generation, Chaotic map for entropy and non linearity and bit level dynamic transposition using grid for diffusion. Along with this core pipeline the system also provides an adaptive switching feature called “Zero Trust Mode” which aids in detecting threat pattern and dynamically switches to one of the five encryption recipes based on the live heuristic feedback. The system was developed and tested on a ESP32 microcontroller and C++ server at local. It achieved a median encryption time of 52.5 ms for every 106 byte packet. The ram used during the encryption process was 12 KB out of 512 KB. Entire module occupied 180 KB of the flash and around 22% of the cpu load.

title: The Future of Traffic Control Systems Evaluation of Expert Interviews in the Czech Republic and Germany
authors: Benjamin Gabber, JirÍ Růžička, Eva Hajčiarová, Felix Rudolph, Tomáš Tichý, JiřÍ Brož
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Traffic lights are a key tool to ensure safe and smooth traffic control in smart cities. Due to the complexity of the urban traffic situations, including different needs for different road users, dense development, and special constraints, the traffic light design process is not trivial. Modern trends in the field of traffic light control systems (TLS) primarily aim at greater adaptability, the use of predictive algorithms and the integration of Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS) communication, while maintaining comprehensibility and user-friendliness for all traffic participants. One of the key issues in European R&D is sustainable transport, as efforts to reduce emissions and energy consumption are becoming increasingly important. As part of the first phase of the international TransUrban project, a research and analysis of current TLS in Germany and the Czech Republic was carried out. Through qualitative interviews with leading experts in the fields of traffic engineering and C-ITS, the key current and future challenges of these systems were examined and assessed, with an emphasis on sustainability aspects. The research questions focused on the development of new control systems and on the increasing importance of prioritizing certain road users at traffic lights Experts suggest that, whilst the current TLS is functioning well, further improvements are needed in managing complex networks and in data-driven control. Differences emerge in evaluation approaches and preferred applications, whereas both countries share common views on key development directions and the relevance of cooperative and data-based solutions.

title: Stiffness regional changes of the biceps femoris long head after Nordic Hamstring Exercise
authors: Gabriel Pina De Carvalho, Thiago Torres Da Matta, Liliam Fernandes De Oliveira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Hamstring injuries, particularly involving the long head of the biceps femoris (BFlh), are prevalent in high-intensity sports and frequently occur in the proximal region. The Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) is commonly implemented for injury prevention; however, its acute regional effects on muscle mechanical properties remain insufficiently understood. This study aimed to investigate acute regional changes in muscle stiffness and thickness of the BFlh following a single NHE session using shear wave elastography (SWE). Twenty-one resistance-trained men performed four sets of six eccentric repetitions of the NHE. Muscle shear modulus and thickness were assessed in the proximal and distal regions of the BFlh before exercising, immediately after exercise, and 15 min post-exercise. Two-way ANOVA test with repeated measures and Bonferroni's test was applied to analyze values over time. A significant reduction in Young’s modulus was observed exclusively in the proximal region immediately after exercise (≈ 12.9%, p < 0.05), whereas no significant changes were detected at other time points or in the distal region. Muscle thickness remained unchanged across all conditions. These findings demonstrate that the NHE induces an acute, region-specific decrease in BFlh stiffness, potentially reflecting early exercise-induced muscle damage mechanisms. SWE appears to be a sensitive and noninvasive method for detecting early alterations in muscle mechanical properties, with relevant applications in sports injury prevention and training load management.

title: Active Directory-Inspired Identity and Access Management Framework for Secure Connected Vehicle Ecosystems
authors: Mahdi Seyfipoor, Mohammad Mahdi Eskandari, Siamak Mohammadi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs) rely on continuous cloud–edge–vehicle communication, making identity and access management a critical security challenge. Traditional solutions such as PKI or blockchain-based authentication provide integrity but lack a unified, hierarchical trust model. This paper introduces the Vehicle Directory Service (VDS), a lightweight, Active Directory-inspired framework for secure credential issuance and authentication in connected vehicle ecosystems. VDS extends the Kerberos ticket-based mechanism to a distributed Cloud–Edge–Vehicle hierarchy, enabling short-lived, context-bound credentials with low computation and latency overhead. Experimental evaluation on a SUMO–NS3 testbed demonstrates that VDS achieves 96.7% authentication success rate, reduces authentication latency by 68%, and maintains less than 8% CPU utilization, outperforming existing centralized and blockchain-based models. The proposed framework establishes a scalable, low-latency, and secure identity foundation for next-generation intelligent transportation networks.

title: Comparative Analysis of Multispectral Sensors (MicaSense RedEdge-MX, FLIR Vue Pro R, Parrot Sequoia) and Image Processing for Crop Health Assessment
authors: Alisa Mașnic, Arina Moisei
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Multispectral images captured by drones represent a crucial source of information for assessing plant health, chlorophyll content, water stress, and crop productivity in precision agriculture. The quality of these data is closely linked to the performance of the multispectral sensors employed, including parameters such as spectral sensitivity, radiometric calibration, spatial resolution, and stability under variable lighting conditions. Data obtained from these sensors are processed using vegetation indices, which provide clear insights into plant condition and vigor. The objective of this project is to conduct a comparative evaluation of the MicaSense RedEdge-MX, FLIR Vue Pro R, and Parrot Sequoia sensors in collecting data over the same agricultural plots, as well as to calibrate the images to produce radiometrically and spectrally standardized datasets, enabling the automation of crop monitoring processes.

title: Day-Ahead Energy-Reserve Joint Optimization Considering Bidirectional Power Imbalance Risks
authors: Hongjiang Wu, Xiaofu Xiong
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: High renewable penetration brings heterogeneous bidirectional power-imbalance risks to power systems, including deficit risk under N-1 contingencies and surplus risk in extreme renewable generation scenarios. This paper proposes a day-ahead energy-reserve joint optimization framework to coordinately hedge both risks. Conditional kernel density estimation is used to derive layered reserve boundaries for routine fluctuations and tail-surplus exposure. An N-1 worst-case feasibility-checking mechanism is embedded to ensure contingency robustness, while renewable stations are modeled as downward reserve providers to balance contracted regulation and out-of-contract curtailment. To improve tractability, an MW-enhanced multi-cut Benders decomposition is developed. Simulations on a provincial-scale system show that the proposed method reduces the worst-case contingency cost and expected surplus-handling cost, while also relieving process regulation stress and alleviating congestion on key transmission corridors.

title: Bridging Human-Centric Lighting and Visible Light Communications: A Review of Principles, Technologies and Applications in Tourism and Related Fields
authors: Casandra-Mariana Mănica, Alin-Mihai Căilean, Cătălin Beguni, Eduard Zadobrischi, Sebastian-Andrei Avătămăniței, Gabriela Țigu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper provides an analysis of Human-Centric Lighting (HCL), emphasizing that this concept represents a natural evolution of traditional lighting systems. HCL proposes dynamic illumination adapted to the moment of day, user activities and, most importantly user preferences, having an important role not only in comfort but also in health. In tourism and hospitality, HCL has applications in hotels, workspaces and public areas, contributing to the well-being of both tourists and employees. This paper also explores the integration of HCL with Visible Light Communication (VLC) technology. As both concepts rely on LED-based lighting infrastructure, their integration is straightforward and enables new opportunities. Therefore, the bridging of HCL and VLC represents a major paradigm shift that integrates biological, technological, and experiential aspects, having strong potential for the development of intelligent environments. In this context, this paper also discusses some of the aspects associated with HCL–VLC integration, highlighting that this integration can be achieved in a relatively straightforward manner, although certain design challenges remain.

title: Low-Carbon Economic Dispatch of Virtual Power Plants Considering Tiered Carbon Trading and Renewable Energy Uncertainty
authors: Lixia Zhou, Xuhao Li, Yiming Xue, Yachao Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the continuous increase in the penetration of renewable energy in power systems, the randomness and volatility of their output pose significant challenges to the optimal dispatch of virtual power plants (VPPs). Against the background of the carbon peak and carbon neutrality goals, this paper proposes a low-carbon economic dispatch model for VPPs considering a tiered carbon trading mechanism and the uncertainty of wind and photovoltaic (PV) power output. Firstly, a VPP structure containing multi-energy equipment such as gas turbines (GTs), wind turbines (WT), photovoltaic units, electric boilers (EBs), absorption chillers (ACs), and energy storage systems (ESSs) is constructed. Secondly, Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) is introduced to characterize the operational risks brought by wind and PV uncertainty, and a tiered carbon trading cost model is established to more realistically reflect carbon market behavior. On this basis, with the objective of maximizing the net profit of the VPP, a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) model is constructed, comprehensively considering electrical, thermal, and cooling power balance and various operational constraints. Comparative analysis through simulation examples shows that the proposed model outperforms traditional dispatch methods in terms of improving economic benefits, promoting renewable energy consumption, and reducing carbon emissions, verifying its effectiveness and practicality.

title: A Hybrid Deep Domain Adaptation Framework for Cross-Domain Demand Response Potential Prediction
authors: Ya-Juan Fu, Zi-Ye Wu, Xiao Yang, Xiao-Ming Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate prediction of air-conditioning demand response potential (DRP) is critical for intelligent energy management and flexible load scheduling. However, the significant variability in user behavior, regional environments, and limited data availability often hinder generalization across users or domains. To address these challenges, we propose HybridCLT_DA, a novel hybrid artificial intelligence framework that integrates convolutional neural networks (CNN), long short-term memory (LSTM), and temporal convolutional networks (TCN) to comprehensively capture both local and global temporal patterns. Furthermore, we incorporate a deep domain adaptation strategy by jointly optimizing Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and Correlation Alignment (CORAL) losses, enabling robust feature transfer across heterogeneous user domains. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that our model significantly outperforms traditional and single-architecture baselines, reducing MAE, RMSE, and MAPE by notable margins. The HybridCLT_DA framework offers a scalable and generalizable solution for cross-user DRP prediction in smart grid applications and provides a robust solution to facilitate efficient demand response management in real-world smart grid applications.

title: Performance Evaluation of SDN-Driven AQM Schemes using Open vSwitch for Delay Reduction
authors: Azmat Ali Shah, Siva Chandrasekaran
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) applications demand ultra-low latency and high reliability to support the mission-critical monitoring and control. The default First-In-First-Out (PFIFO) queuing mechanism in Open vSwitch (OVS) suffers from bufferbloat during congestion, resulting in excessive delay and jitter that degrades system performance. This study evaluates Software-Defined Networking (SDN)-controlled Active Queue Management (AQM) in OVS, with a focus on reducing delay for IIoT traffic. Three AQM algorithms, Random Early Detection (RED), Proportional Integral controller Enhanced (PIE), and Dual Queue Proportional Integral Enhanced (DualPI2) were integrated into OVS and compared to the PFIFO baseline using a physical SDN testbed. The evaluation considered round-trip time (RTT), jitter, packet loss, and throughput under both congested and uncongested network conditions. The results indicate that RED produced high packet loss (13%) and elevated average queueing delay (38 milliseconds). In contrast, PIE and DualPI2 maintained near-baseline RTT (2 milliseconds), sub-millisecond jitter, negligible packet loss, and full link utilization. This work provides the first comparative evaluation of RED, PIE, and DualPI2 integrated into Open vSwitch (OVS) under SDN control, demonstrating that software-based AQM can substantially reduce delay and enhance reliability in IIoT networks without the need for specialized hardware.

title: Air Quality Prediction Using Machine Learning Models on IoT Sensor Data
authors: Fatemeh Ensafdoust, S. N. TermehMousavi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Air quality is a critical determinant of human health, influenced by complex, nonlinear interactions among multiple pollutants and environmental factors. Leveraging a real-time IoT sensor network across India, this study develops and evaluates machine learning models for accurate Air Quality Index (AQI) prediction. The dataset, encompassing concentrations of PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₂.₅</inf>, PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₁₀</inf>, NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₂</inf>, SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₂</inf>, CO, and O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₃</inf> from over 100 stations, underwent rigorous preprocessing, including missing value handling, outlier mitigation, temporal feature extraction, and standardization. Four models (Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Stacking Ensemble) were trained and assessed using an 80/20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation. Results indicate that Linear Regression fails to capture the inherent nonlinearity (MAE=81.53, RMSE=104.64, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.027). In contrast, ensemble methods achieve near-perfect performance. The Stacking ensemble demonstrated competitive results (MAE=1.89, RMSE=3.61, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">²</sup>=0.999), while Random Forest showed superior stability with the lowest RMSE (3.44) and minimal tuning requirements. Cross-validation confirmed model robustness, with all ensemble methods achieving R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">²</sup> > 0.99. This work establishes a validated benchmark for model selection, emphasizing the importance of algorithm choice and practical constraints in IoT-driven AQI estimation. Future directions include integrating meteorological variables, exploring temporal forecasting, and extending model deployment to edge devices. The implementation code for this study is publicly available on GitHub [16].

title: AI-driven diagnostic system for brain tumor classification: a CNN multi-class classification with cross-dataset validation
authors: Alesea Morev, Alexandru Lavric, Anastasia Elinețchi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Early detection of brain tumors is critical for effective treatment and improved long-term patient outcomes. We developed a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based artificial intelligence model to serve as a diagnostic tool for classifying brain MRI images. We aimed to classify three primary brain tumor types, meningioma, glioma, and pituitary tumor, as well as healthy brain tissue. The optimization technique used in our model is Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum (SGDM). To evaluate the model, we tested its performance on 2 external validation datasets, and the accuracy achieved was 98.61% and 97.33%, ensuring a high level of performance in detecting and classifying brain tumors.

title: ChatGPT as Metacognitive Scaffolding in Physics Instruction: Experimental Evidence on Kinematics Learning
authors: Samuel Tomas, Sandy Salazar, Miel Gomez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The present study evaluates the effects of pedagogically guided use of ChatGPT in university-level kinematics learning and the development of metacognitive abilities in engineering students. An experimental design with random assignment was applied to two groups (Control and ChatGPT) solely differentiated by access to the artificial intelligence assistant. Both groups were to solve equivalent kinematics problems evaluated by performance tests (pretest and posttest) and a metacognitive rubric based on written reflections. The data was analyzed by Welch's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex>-tests and an ANCOVA model to control the initial level of familiarity with AI. The results demonstrated a positive and statistically significative effect favoring the ChatGPT group (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$g=1.30$</tex>), demonstrating a conceptual and metacognitive gain. The experimental group reflections revealed deeper planning, monitoring and verification processes. The findings suggest that the structured integration of ChatGPT can function as metacognitive scaffolding, strengthening both conceptual comprehension and scientific self-regulation.

title: Teaching Informed Neural Networks Modeling Using FrED
authors: Pedro Ponce, Brian Anthony, Russel Bradley, Juana Isabel Méndez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: In industrial settings, three modeling paradigms, white-box, gray-box, and black-box, are used for prediction, monitoring, classification, and performance assessment. With the rise of AI, black-box (data-driven) models are popular, but their accuracy can suffer with noisy, scarce, or incomplete data. To address this, informed neural networks (physics-informed/hybrid models) combine data with first-principles knowledge to enhance robustness, generalization, and physical plausibility. As hybrid modeling grows in importance, it is vital to teach these methods accessibly. This paper introduces the Fiber Extrusion Device (FrED) as an educational platform demonstrating informed neural networks in manufacturing. Students use FrED to connect models with real data, train hybrid models, and analyze how physics priors and data-driven residuals complement each other amid noise and data limits.

title: Revolutionizing Education Through Gamification and AI: Strategies to Reduce Disruptive Behavior
authors: Fredy Rivera, Josseline Mendoza, Carol Guapacasa, Julio Montesdeoca
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Education faces significant challenges in managing disruptive behaviors among primary school students. Gamification and Artificial Intelligence (AI) offer innovative strategies to address these challenges by creating personalized and engaging learning experiences. This study presents a novel approach that integrates AI and gamification to generate adaptive educational content tailored to individual student needs. An experimental design employing pre- and post-tests was implemented to evaluate the system's effectiveness. Results indicate a significant reduction in disruptive behaviors, improved classroom environment, enhanced student motivation, and increased engagement. Additionally, students demonstrated better emotional regulation and attention. The findings highlight the critical role of teacher training and adaptability in implementing these technologies successfully. Overall, the integration of AI and gamification shows considerable potential for supporting students' social, emotional, and academic development. This research underscores the practical implications of using AI-driven gamified interventions in education and suggests directions for future studies, emphasizing sustainability, replicability, and the promotion of inclusive learning environments.

title: Machine Learning Model for Forecasting Material Costs in School Construction Projects
authors: Warren Panizales, Herminiño Lagunzad, Napoleon Solo Dela Cruz, Carl Edgar Ambray
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: This study develops and evaluates a transparent baseline for early construction cost estimation using Linear Regression on a dataset of 178 projects. To address right-skew and heteroskedasticity in costs, the model is trained on log1p(Total_Project_Cost) and coupled with a reproducible pipeline: unit/currency harmonization, inflation adjustment to a base year, one-hot encoding for categorical attributes (e.g., school level, structural/project type, location), and lightweight feature engineering (e.g., area-per-classroom, footprint = area/floors, and floors×area). Performance is assessed using leakage-safe 5-fold out-of-fold (OOF) predictions, yielding an MAE of ₱21.84M, an RMSE of ₱33.09M, and an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.915, representing a substantial improvement over naïve OLS on raw cost (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.189). Diagnostics indicate that project scale, particularly Total_Floor_Area_m2, is the dominant driver; additional variables, such as the number of floors/classrooms, mainly capture density effects once area is controlled. VIF ≈ 1 across features indicates negligible multicollinearity, supporting the stability and interpretability of the coefficients. While typical OOF errors fall in the ±5–15% band, occasional outliers (~20–25%) suggest unmodeled premiums/discounts (e.g., site constraints, procurement route, award-month price dynamics). Overall, the pipeline provides an auditable, deployment-ready baseline suitable for conceptual budgeting, with clear upgrade paths. It adds duration, procurement type, month-level indices, and site-complexity flags to further tighten tail errors without sacrificing interpretability.

title: Systematic Literature Review on Wearable Devices as a Gateway for Cyberattacks
authors: Madilaine Claire B. Nacianceno, Aaron John C. Alegre, Carl Joseph R. David, Jhamil G. Gutierrez, Jorelle P. Jarabelo
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Information and Network Technologies (ICINT)
abstract: Wearables are increasingly common in daily lives; especially healthcare, however they face security challenges due to their connections to multiple networks. This paper reviews research on the security weaknesses of wearables within digital systems, following Kitchenham’s evidence-based guidelines and using the SALSA framework to ensure a reliable methodology. Of the 104 papers found, 85 met the predetermined quality assessment criteria and were included in the review. Using descriptive statistics and thematic analysis, the study classified wearable devices, attack types, and security solutions. Results show that devices with low processing power and Bluetooth Low Energy are especially vulnerable, posing a greater risk to system security. Although promising solutions such as lightweight cryptography, federated learning, and anomaly detection exist, they still face scalability and efficiency challenges. The paper highlights the urgent need to update security strategies to effectively support the growing use of wearables within current resource constraints and regulatory frameworks.

title: Research on HVDC Frequency Regulation Strategy for Multi-Area Asynchronous Interconnected Systems Based on FKC
authors: Tao Hu, Lei Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Aiming at the characteristics of multi-channel cross-diffusion of disturbances and multi- HVDC coupling in multi-area asynchronous interconnected systems, as well as issues in existing multi-area frequency regulation such as poor synchronization due to reliance on droop control and idle frequency regulation reserves in undisturbed areas, this paper breaks through the limitation of FKC (Frequency Keeping Control) being only applied to two-area scenarios. It proposes a three-area adaptation scheme of "Global Frequency Deviation Integrated by External Equivalent Frequency + Allocation of Frequency Regulation Responsibility Based on Installed Capacity Weight." Its core logic is: capturing multi-area dynamics through external equivalent frequency and matching frequency regulation responsibility with installed capacity weight to achieve multi-area frequency regulation. An HVDC three-area asynchronous interconnected simulation model is built based on CloudPSS, and the frequency regulation performance of the proposed scheme is verified through typical three-area fault scenarios, compared with traditional control methods. The results show that the improved FKC strategy achieves superior frequency regulation performance, even enabling quasi-synchronization of multi-area frequencies; simultaneously, the utilization rate of frequency regulation reserve capacity in undisturbed areas is significantly improved, markedly outperforming traditional control methods. This scheme provides a more practical path for frequency regulation optimization in multi-area asynchronous interconnected systems.

title: Real-time Soft-sensing Method of Blade Average Wind Speed Under Full Wind Conditions Based on Wind Turbine Mechanism Model
authors: Zhikai Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate wind speed measurement is essential for wind turbines. Existing methods face issues like blade occlusion, high costs, historical data dependence, or low accuracy. This paper proposes a real-time soft sensing method for blade average wind speed under full wind conditions based on the wind turbine mechanism model. Using the manufacturer’s power coefficient table and real-time measurable variables (rotor speed, electromagnetic power, pitch angle), it infers the average wind speed without additional hardware. Simulation with actual wind farm data shows 99.98% of data points have an absolute error <0.05 m/s and 98.38% a relative error <0.2% (down to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> order), verifying its effectiveness.

title: Analysis of the Temperature Pattern of the Temporomandibular Joint and Previous Temporal Muscle as a Function of Acquisition Distance Using Infrared Thermography
authors: A.C.R.Silva, S.F. Raymundo, W.C.A. Pereira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: The temporomandibular joint is formed by structures that work together to perform mouth movements. Thermography captures the infrared radiation emitted by the structure under analysis, recording temperature patterns. This pattern is a function of the structure and the distance between the device and the target and may be related to musculoskeletal conditions in Temporomandibular Disorders (TMD). It is important to find an optimal distance to avoid noise interference in the collection of thermograms for a more precise and accurate system. This study aimed to evaluate the effect of two distances on the detection of temperatures by infrared thermography in the evaluation of the temporomandibular joint and previous temporal muscle. Thermograms were captured with a FLIR E40 camera at distances of 50 and 100 cm which were established through a pilot study using a calibrated thermal phantom, with five repetitions for each region of interest. The results showed that the acquisition distance affects thermographic measurements in the four evaluated regions. The 50-cm distance for image acquisition proved to be reliable for verifying the small temperature variations in the TMJ structures.

title: Enhanced Detection of Subtle RTA Tool Differences via Multiple Layer Rs Measurements
authors: Cheng Kangjian, Li Yong, Huang Jingyan, Lew Wen Siang
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper presents a case study demonstrating subtle performance variations between Rapid Thermal Anneal (RTA) tools. While no difference was observed using conventional implant and spike anneal resistance (Rs) setup, a multilayer enhanced Rs methodology reveals an approximately 37.2% post-epi Rs difference between two RTA tools. The results are validated by spreading resistance profiling (SRP). This new methodology can be deployed as an inline monitor to improve setup detection sensitivity and minimize wafer exposure. Such analysis is critical for advanced semiconductor technology, which is sensitive to doping profiles, crystal lattice defects and epitaxial growth.

title: TPACK Competencies of University Professors in Times of Artificial Intelligence
authors: Nicolas Sumba-Nacipucha, Roberto López-Chila, Francisco Ganga-Contreras, Armenio Pérez Martinez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The objective of this study was to diagnose teaching competencies within the framework of the TPACK model among university professors in Ecuador. A validated questionnaire was administered that measures the dimensions of technological knowledge (TK), pedagogical knowledge (PK), and content knowledge (CK), as well as their intersections (PCK, TCK, TPK, and TPACK). The research was conducted using a quantitative, non-experimental, cross-sectional approach with a descriptive and inferential scope. The sample consisted of 391 professors, and the data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and nonparametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis and Mann-Whitney U tests). The results showed high levels of CK and PK, while the dimensions related to TK and their combinations presented greater weaknesses. Significant differences were identified by gender and age, but not by type of institution or in the overall level of TPACK. It is concluded that, although there is a solid pedagogical and disciplinary foundation, it is necessary to strengthen technological integration through continuing education strategies that address the deficiencies of university professors in Ecuador.

title: ImageHD: Energy-Efficient On-Device Continual Learning of Visual Representations via Hyperdimensional Computing
authors: Jebacyril Arockiaraj, Dhruv Parikh, Viktor Prasanna
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: On-device continual learning (CL) enables edge devices to adapt to non-stationary data streams without offline retraining, which is critical for real-time edge AI. However, most existing CL methods rely on backpropagation-based updates or exemplar-heavy classifiers, incurring high compute, memory, and latency overheads that hinder deployment on resource-constrained devices. Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) offers an alternative by enabling fast, non-iterative online updates. When paired with a lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN) feature extractor, HDC supports efficient on-device adaptation with strong visual representations. Despite this progress, prior HDC-based continual learning systems typically employ multi-tier memory hierarchies and complex cluster management, which complicate deployment on resource-constrained hardware platforms.In this paper, we propose ImageHD, an FPGA accelerator for on-device continual learning of visual data based on HDC, designed to address these limitations. ImageHD targets streaming continual learning under strict latency and on-chip memory constraints, without expensive iterative optimization. At the algorithmic level, we introduce a hardware-optimized continual learning method that bounds total class exemplars through a unified exemplar memory and a hardware-efficient cluster merging strategy, while integrating a quantized CNN front-end to reduce edge deployment overhead without sacrificing accuracy. At the system level, ImageHD is realized as a streaming dataflow architecture on the AMD Zynq ZCU104 FPGA, integrating hyperdimensional encoding, similarity search, and bounded cluster management using word-packed binary hypervector representations to enable massively parallel bitwise operations within tight on-chip resource budgets. Experimental results on the CORe50 dataset demonstrate up to 40.4× (4.84×) speedups and 383× (105.1×) energy efficiency gains over optimized CPU (GPU) baselines, establishing HDC-enabled continual learning as a practical foundation for real-time, on-device lifelong learning systems.

title: Rayleigh Signature Spectral Stitching for Distributed Fiber Optic Sensors Based on Coherent Optical Time-Domain Reflectometry
authors: Vahid Sharif, Mikel Sagues, Alayn Loayssa
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: We demonstrate an interrogation technique for distributed fiber-optic sensors based on Rayleigh backscattering that bridges the gap between high-sensitivity dynamic vibration sensing and wide-range static monitoring of strain or temperature. The technique builds on our previous development of a method to measure the so-called Rayleigh signature (RS) in coherent optical time-domain reflectometry (C-OTDR) setups. While this approach enables high-resolution, long-range measurements of high-frequency vibrations, in practice the retrieved RS is band-limited by the probe waveform bandwidth, which constrains the measurable strain/temperature excursion. In this work, we propose a spectral stitching method that reconstructs a broadband RS reference map by combining multiple band-limited RS measurements acquired during an initial calibration stage at different probe center frequencies. Neighboring RS segments are aligned using their spectral overlap and stitched into a single broadband reference. After this one-time calibration, dynamic sensing can proceed at a fixed probe wavelength: each measured RS is matched to the spectrally stitched reference to estimate the induced frequency shift. We experimentally demonstrate the method in a pulse-compression C-OTDR system over 25 km with 1 m spatial resolution, reconstructing a 30 GHz stitched reference RS that supports large temperature/strain excursions and enables measurements acquired days after calibration. We further demonstrate long-term dynamic strain sensing with fast 757.57 Hz vibrations superimposed on slow environmental drift, achieving a strain sensitivity of 0.8 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rm{n}\varepsilon /\sqrt{\rm{Hz}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and highlighting the robustness of the proposed absolute-referencing approach compared with continuously updated reference schemes.

title: Game-Driven Adaptive Workload Distribution for Consumer Vehicular Mobile Edge Computing
authors: Jieming Zhou, Xiaolong Xu, Muhammad Bilal, Guangming Cui, Shucun Fu
publish: IEEE Consumer Electronics Magazine
abstract: The rapid evolution of intelligent transportation system (ITS) and connected vehicles is driving increasing demand for reliable computing support for consumer-oriented vehicular applications. Although mobile edge computing (MEC) enables vehicles to distribute workload to nearby infrastructure, dynamic traffic conditions often cause spatiotemporal workload imbalance across edge servers, resulting in degraded system performance. To address these challenges, this article proposes a Game-Driven Three-Tier Adaptive Workload Distribution method (GD-TAWD) for vehicular MEC environments. The framework coordinates vehicles, base stations (BSs), and an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) to support adaptive workload distribution. The problem is formulated as a potential game in which vehicles act as autonomous agents that update their computation strategies using lightweight local feedback. Each vehicle can dynamically choose among local execution, BS offloading, inter-BS migration, or UAV-assisted execution. Simulation experiments based on an urban traffic scenario demonstrate that GD-TAWD can reduce system computation overhead and improve workload balancing across heterogeneous edge resources compared with baseline approaches. The results highlight the potential of game-driven collaborative intelligence for scalable consumer-oriented vehicular edge computing systems.

title: TinyML for Transportation Systems: Enabling Smart Mobility
authors: Siva Sai, Kunjan Shah, Granth Jain, Vinay Chamola
publish: IEEE Consumer Electronics Magazine
abstract: Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) is an innovative technology paradigm that allows Machine Learning (ML) inference on resource-constrained microcontrollers. This paper surveys TinyML for transportation systems. Eight application areas from the literature are covered: (i) intrusion detection for in-vehicle networks and EV-charging interfaces, (ii) safety monitoring (alcohol impairment, drowsiness, falls), (iii) vehicle perception in embedded vision, (iv) environmental impact estimation (fuel type classification, CO2 soft sensors), (v) vehicle diagnostics for automotive and rail systems, (vi) driver behavior analytics, (vii) automotive enhancements, and (viii) traffic control with adaptive signal timing. We highlight representative deployments on MCUs (Microcontroller Units) across these domains and present their quantization choices, memory footprints, and real-time performance. Furthermore, we present the limitations of TinyML, including hardware resource constraints, framework fragmentation, data scarcity, robustness–accuracy trade-offs, integration challenges, constraints on online learning, and application-specific challenges. TinyML is a promising enabler of responsive, private, and affordable technology for transportation intelligence, but progress requires standardized toolchains and benchmarks, richer open datasets, robustness methods for non-stationary environments, and lightweight on-device adaptation.

title: Attention-Guided and Role-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Multi-AUV Counter-Game
authors: Wenhao Gan, Kai Guo, Lei Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: This article proposes an attention-guided, role-aware multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) scheme to enhance collaborative decision-making among autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) in the counter-game (CG). First, a customized multi-AUV CG model is developed, incorporating AUV-specific constraints. Ashared reward alignment mechanism is introduced to synchronize individual and team objectives, promoting cooperation and enabling the emergence of heterogeneous behaviors. Second, a hybrid decision architecture integrating soft graph attention, recurrent structures, and contrastive role encoding is proposed to enhance AUV tactical cognition and context-aware responsiveness, while ensuring extension and adaptability. Finally, building on reward-guided optimization, a role-driven contrastive learning objective is introduced to maximize mutual information between individual behaviors and role representations, fostering heterogeneous coordination and diverse policy development. Comparative studies and multiscale games confirm the scheme’s superiority in adaptability and coordination over existing baselines. The learned policies exhibit heterogeneous behaviors, such as focused fire and decoy tactics, which improve mission efficiency and safety. Model performance analysis and lake trials further validate its applicability. The related experimental videos are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://sjtu-mirus.github.io/MIRUS.github.io/research/counter-game</uri>

title: A Ku-Band Liquid-Crystal-Reconfigurable Fabry–Pérot Cavity Antenna with Nine-State Discrete Two-Dimensional Beam Steering
authors: Hsi-Hsir Chou, Yin-Cheng Wu, T.D. Wilkinson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a Ku-band nematic liquid-crystal (LC) reconfigurable Fabry–Pérot cavity antenna (FPCA) with nine-state discrete two-dimensional beam-steering capability. The proposed antenna consists of a slot-coupled microstrip patch radiator and a 6 × 6 LC-based partially reflective surface (PRS) array. The PRS is partitioned into four independently biased regions, whose voltage-controlled reflection phase and magnitude responses introduce controllable field asymmetry in the cavity and thereby steer the main beam. In contrast to conventional fixed FPC antennas and more complex actively switched reconfigurable designs, the proposed approach enables discrete two-dimensional beam steering using a simple biasing architecture. A prototype operating at 14.25 GHz is fabricated and experimentally characterized. Measured results verify beam switching toward nine azimuthal directions (φ = 0°, 45°, …, 315°), with a maximum deflection angle of ±10°. The antenna achieves a peak realized gain of 10.6 dBi, while the gain variation among all beam states remains within 1.21 dB. These results demonstrate that the proposed LC-reconfigurable FPCA provides a practical and low-complexity solution for beam-switching applications in Ku-band high-gain wireless systems.

title: Digital Triplet for Developing Future Reliable 6G Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems: Case Studies from Teleoperation to Platooning
authors: Hendrik Schippers, Niklas A. Wagner, Lennart Reiher, Harun Teper, Lukas Ostendorf, Jian-Jia Chen, Lutz Eckstein, Christian Wietfeld
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Future Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITSs) will impose stringent requirements on mobile networks in terms of latency, data rate, and reliability. These requirements must be met in rapidly changing wireless channel conditions. Hence, their fulfillment requires rigorous and reproducible testing environments that enable a seamless transition from simulation to real-world deployment. However, existing testbeds and simulation environments either lack physical realism or fail to scale cost-effectively, hindering comprehensive C-ITS evaluation. To overcome these limitations, we implement the concept of a Digital Triplet, comprising three complementary layers: a full-scale real-world test field, a co-simulation environment for integrating additional simulated entities, and a scaled-down replica of the test field. This architecture enables reliable, reproducible, and scalable testing of future 6G-enabled C-ITS applications across different levels of abstraction. We demonstrate the applicability of the Digital Triplet through multiple case studies. For remote driving, we deploy and optimize a low-latency video streaming system and simulate a mmWave communication channel incorporating Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RISs), and achieve up to a 74% reduced median Glass-To-Glass (G2G) latency compared to related work. Moreover, emulating real-world vehicle localization in the scaled test field enables the implementation of cross-domain Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC)-based vehicular platooning. Finally, we evaluate the NMPC platooning controller in both decentralized (on-vehicle) and centralized (edge cloud) configurations. The results highlight the strong potential of the Digital Triplet to accelerate the development and validation of C-ITS applications within future 6G networks.

title: A New Integrated Sensing and Backscatter Communication System Based on Phaseless Extended Rytov Approximation
authors: Dingfei Ma, Min Yang, Junxiang Yang, Kaixu Wang, Min Wang, Yi Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper proposes a novel integrated sensing and backscatter communication framework based on inverse scattering methodology utilizing the extended Rytov approximation (xRA), which can operate without the need for explicit phase information. The proposed system uniquely employs phaseless received signal strength indicator (RSSI) measurements combined with dynamic tag impedance modulation to decompose RSSI data into structural and antenna mode components. This decomposition not only facilitates tag identification but also enables robust differentiation between metallic and dielectric objects with varying permittivities through xRA integration. Furthermore, the extracted antenna mode RSSI component provides essential channel state information (CSI) for backscatter communication, significantly mitigating interference from environmental objects and enhancing backscatter communication reliability. To further improve spectral efficiency and bit error rate (BER) performance, we introduce and adapt the joint permutation, group, and antenna index spatial modulation (JPGA-ISM) scheme, which transmits additional information through multi-dimensional indexing while employing low-order pulse amplitude modulation (PAM) and secondary pulse amplitude modulation (SPAM) constellations to maximize minimum Euclidean distance (MED). Comprehensive three-dimensional electromagnetic simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed system across diverse scenarios, including single and multiple tags in the presence of various metallic and dielectric objects. It is demonstrated that the proposed system successfully detects all tags, reliably distinguishes between metallic and dielectric materials with varying permittivities, and effectively identifies individual tags even in complex environments. In addition, performance analysis of the JPGA-ISM scheme demonstrates close agreement between simulated BER and theoretical upper bounds in high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regions, and confirms its superior performance compared to alternative spatial modulation techniques under equivalent spectral efficiency. Unlike phase-coherent backscatter systems, the proposed phaseless approach eliminates the need for phase synchronization across receivers, significantly simplifying hardware requirements. These findings underscore the significant potential of combining advanced inverse scattering and phaseless techniques with efficient spatial modulation schemes, revealing a promising direction for the development of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems in next-generation wireless applications.

title: Adaptive Point-Prompt Tuning: Fine-Tuning Heterogeneous Foundation Models for 3D Point Cloud Analysis_supp1-3697442.pdf
authors: Hui Huang
publish: N/A
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies for foundation models in 1D textual and 2D visual analysis have demonstrated remarkable efficacy. However, due to the scarcity of point cloud data, pre training large 3D models remains a challenging task. While many efforts have been made to apply pre-trained visual models to 3D domains through “high-to-low” mapping, these approaches often lead to the loss of spatial geometries and lack a generalizable framework for adapting any modality to 3D. This paper, therefore, attempts to directly leverage point features to calibrate the heterogeneous foundation model of any modality for 3D point cloud analysis. Specifically, we propose the Adaptive Point-Prompt Tuning (APPT) method, which fine-tunes pre-trained models with a modest number of parameters, enabling direct point cloud processing without heterogeneous mappings. We convert raw point clouds into point embeddings by aggregating local geometry to capture spatial features followed by linear layers to ensure seamless utilization of frozen pre-trained models. Given the inherent disorder of point clouds, in contrast to the structured nature of images and language, we employ a permutation-invariant feature to capture the relative positions of point embeddings, thereby obtaining point tokens enriched with location information to optimize self-attention mechanisms. To calibrate self-attention across source domains of any modality to 3D and reduce computational overhead, we introduce a prompt generator that shares weights with the point embedding module, dynamically producing point-prompts without adding additional parameters. These prompts are then concatenated into a frozen foundation model, providing rich global structural information and compensating for the lack of structural context in the heterogeneous data. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that our APPT is effective for various downstream tasks in point cloud analysis while achieving high efficiency by fine-tuning only 3.8%ofthetrainable parameters. The source code and additional details are available at https://github.com/wish254/APPT.

title: CNLTrans: A Cluster-Wise Non-Local Transformer for Self-Supervised Deep Clustering of Large Hyperspectral Images
authors: Nan Huang, Tong Wu, Qichao Liu, Pengfei Liu, Kang Ni, Zhizhong Zheng, Liang Xiao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have recently demonstrated remarkable advantages in hyperspectral image (HSI) clustering by effectively capturing global contextual dependencies and complex spectral–spatial relationships. However, the quadratic computational complexity and considerable memory requirements of self-attention in ViTs pose significant challenges for large HSI clustering and limit scalability in practical applications. Moreover, we observe that global queries generally treat all tokens equally, whereas in clustering tasks tokens contribute unequally to cluster assignment and separation. To address these issues, we propose a cluster- wise non-local Transformer framework for self-supervised deep clustering (CNLTrans), which integrates multi-scale convolutional neural networks and a cluster- wise non-local Transformer into a unified architecture. Specifically, we design a cluster- wise non-local Transformer module that incorporates both pixel-to-cluster and cluster-to-cluster attention mechanisms over sparsity-routed cluster representatives, enabling the modeling of fine-grained non-local and coarse-grained global dependencies with reduced computational cost. In addition, a self-supervised module is introduced to facilitate end-to-end optimization and enhance feature discriminability for clustering tasks. Extensive experiments on benchmark HSI datasets demonstrate that the proposed CNLTrans achieves state-of-the-art clustering performance compared with representative clustering methods.

title: Design of a Wafer Contactless Transportation System Using Deformable Thin Film of Air_supp1-3697454.mp4
authors: Sifeng He
publish: N/A
abstract: To prevent fragile silicon wafers from being scratched or contaminated by mechanical contact during semiconductor manufacturing, as commonly occurs in conventional handling methods such as robotic grippers, contactless transportation systems are expected to eliminate direct physical interaction. Hence, this article presents the development of a wafer contactless transportation system capable of both levitation and active transportation, enabled by a vacuum-preloaded thin film of air. A compact thin air film generation system is first designed, featuring a pneumatic pathway that maintains stable vacuum power regardless of wafer presence. In addition, the mechanical structure of the system is designed to be compliant, allowing it to modify the local air film thickness to create a traction force on the wafer. To achieve actuation, a compact hybrid reluctance actuator is integrated, providing precise control of the compliant structure within a limited working space. Next, the system is evaluated by both numerical simulations and experimental tests. The results confirm that the proposed contactless system integrated with active vibration isolation can achieve contactless wafer transportation with a steady state accuracy of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{\pm}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> 2.5 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m.

title: Integrating Big Data and Virtual Metrology: Autoencoder-Driven Robust Etch Endpoint Detection
authors: Yi-Rong Chou, Guan-Ming Huang, Kai-Yu Kang, Cheng-Ying Wu
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Accurate etch endpoint detection is vital in semiconductor manufacturing to maintain device quality and yield. However, mixed runs in the same chamber can degrade chamber condition, causing slot effects that shift EPD (End Point Detection) signal patterns and lead to process instability. This study proposes an unsupervised Autoencoder-based approach to detect these shifts by analyzing reconstruction errors of EPD signals. Without requiring labeled data, the method identifies subtle anomalies and process disturbances in real time. Enhanced with advanced signal processing, it improves fault slot effect, stabilizes the etching process, and reduces wafer scrap. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of AI-driven analytics in optimizing semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Challenges in Selecting Technical Words When Implementing Assessments for Generative Artificial Intelligence Literacy
authors: Diego Cheuquepán-Maldonado, Luz Chourio-Acevedo, Rodrigo Islas, Valentina Ayala-Catalán, Cristina Villa-Torrano
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence literacy has become a key competency in higher education. This requires students to use precise technical language. Previous studies have shown that most of the specialized terminology in the field of computing comes from English loanwords. Furthermore, these terms are often preferred over their Spanish equivalents. However, it is unknown whether linguistic familiarity equates to conceptual understanding. Moreover, no studies address this issue in the context of generative artificial intelligence literacy. This paper presents the results of a survey administered to a sample of 40 higher education students in Chile. The instrument assesses the level of knowledge of twenty anglicisms on generative artificial intelligence. In addition, it explores the respondents' choices regarding the anglicism and its translation. The findings show an inverse relationship in the choice process, the more familiar a student is with an English term, the less likely they are to prefer its Spanish translation.

title: Capacity Planning of a Wind-PV-Storage-Hydrogen-to-Methanol Coupled System via an Improved Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Binbin Zhong, Mingwei Peng, Chenghong Liang, Jun Bai, Zishan Zhao, Yongqi Teng
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the dual-carbon goals, volatility in power systems with high renewable penetration has become more pronounced. Since standalone energy storage cannot effectively smooth wind and PV fluctuations, this paper proposes a wind — PV-hydrogen-to-methanol coupled system that integrates electrochemical storage with hydrogen storage. First, the operating characteristics of each component are modeled and an operating strategy is developed. Second, a hybrid energy storage system (HESS) capacity planning model is established to maximize lifecycle net revenue. To improve solution accuracy, an improved particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm with a mutation mechanism is applied. It achieves faster solution speed and higher accuracy, while effectively preventing local optimality. Finally, simulations using measured data from eastern China show that the proposed configuration reduces wind and PV curtailment and load interruption, while improving economic performance and system stability.

title: ML and Cloud-Based Turbulence Prediction Model for Aviation Safety
authors: Ananya Karn, Brijmohan Lal Sahu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Turbulence is a leading unpredictable hazard in aviation and often remains undetected until an aircraft is already experiencing destabilizing atmospheric conditions. Traditional sources such as onboard weather radar, Pilot Reports (PIREPs), and Air Traffic Control (ATC) advisories are largely reactive and are less effective against Clear-Air Turbulence (CAT). This problem is addressed using a Machine Learning (ML)-based turbulence severity classifier with a Random Forest (RF) algorithm to predict three operationally relevant levels: Low, Moderate, and Severe. The trained model is integrated into an Amazon Web Services (AWS)-based inference service using an Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)hosted Docker container with model artifacts stored in S3. The designed system supports real-time API-based inference, and a browser interface demonstrates operational usage. Full realtime ingestion and automated alerting pipelines are outlined as future work. The results suggest practical potential for improving passenger safety and airline operations. Unlike prior turbulence-prediction studies that remain offline, this work includes a cloud-deployable prototype using Docker and AWS, demonstrating a practical pathway toward operational realtime turbulence alerts.

title: Human-Machine Interface Design Implications for Fuel Cell Range-Extended Motorcycles
authors: J. Svoboda, P. Toman, P. Bouchner, U. Michel-Schneider, D. Poliaček, P. Horák
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: The decarbonisation of urban mobility is accelerating the development of alternative propulsion technologies for light vehicles, including hydrogen fuel cell systems. In powered two-wheelers, hybrid battery-hydrogen architectures can extend operational range while preserving the advantages of battery-electric mobility. However, such multi-source energy systems introduce additional complexity that must be effectively communicated through the vehicle's human-machine interface (HMI). This study investigates rider perception, trust, and visual attention when interacting with an HMI designed for a hydrogen fuel cell range-extended motorcycle. The evaluation was conducted using a high-fidelity motorcycle riding simulator combined with eye-tracking measurements and user experience questionnaires. Results show that riders predominantly prioritize battery-derived range information, while hydrogen indicators attract attention mainly during propulsion transitions. Participants reported high trust in the displayed information and positive usability ratings. The study provides experimental eye-tracking evidence on how riders interpret multi-source energy information and highlights the importance of clear visual hierarchy and transition signalling in HMI design for hybrid electric-hydrogen motorcycles.

title: An Efficient Fault Tolerance Approach for CNNs based on Selective TMR and Pruning
authors: Farzin Sharifzadeh Javidi, Setareh Ahsaei, Mohsen Raji
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable performance in a wide range of vision tasks. However, deploying them in safety-critical systems introduces concerns regarding their vulnerability to hardware faults, particularly soft errors. Although traditional soft error mitigation techniques offer strong protection, they often come at the cost of high memory overhead, making them unsuitable for resource-limited embedded platforms. In this paper, we present a novel efficient fault tolerance framework that improves soft error resilience in CNNs without incurring any memory overhead. The proposed method takes advantage of the singular value decomposition (SVD) technique to find the importance of the filters in the feature extractor component of a CNN. Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) is then applied selectively to the top-ranked filters to ensure efficient protection where it is most needed. To mitigate the overhead introduced by TMR, pruning is applied to offset the overhead created by the TMR. This cycle is repeated iteratively until the desired level of fault tolerance is reached. Finally, an additional pruning step is performed to remove any remaining redundant weights while further reducing the potential fault sites. Empirical results for ResNet-50 and VGG11, evaluated with the CIFAR-100 dataset, demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly enhances fault tolerance while maintaining model accuracy and achieving zero memory overhead. The results demonstrate that the proposed method can protect the VGG11 model at a bit error rate of 5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup> achieving over 50% fault tolerance enhancement, while reducing memory overhead to 45%, compared to 100% or more in traditional selective TMR methods.

title: Work in Progress: Integrating Active Learning and Emerging AI Agent Support in a Blended First-Year Engineering Course
authors: Hugo G. Espinosa, Sue Whale, Krishnasamy T. Selvan
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This work-in-progress paper reports on the early implementation of a blended and flipped learning approach in a first-year Electronic Engineering course, introduced to modernize the learning experience and increase student engagement. Core concepts are delivered through pre-recorded videos, while workshop sessions incorporate active learning through multi-level problem sets and real-time interactive activities that promote discussion, participation, and peer engagement. These activities aim to make problem solving more dynamic by encouraging students to share their reasoning. The course uses a continuous assessment model comprising quizzes and laboratory activities, removing the traditional final exam. Laboratories provide hands-on opportunities to reinforce weekly theoretical content. An emerging AI agent was incorporated to offer content support and respond to course-related queries. Preliminary survey responses indicate that students find the developing structure more engaging than previous formats. Ongoing work aims to refine the intervention, examine patterns of AI adoption, and evaluate its educational impact as implementation continues.

title:
Switching Impulse Surface Flashover Characteristics of Insulators in C

                    F

                    Gas Mixtures

abstract: C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</inf>N/CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> gas mixture is a promising alternative to SF<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf>, and the surface flashover voltage of insulators under switching impulse voltage is an important indicator for evaluating its insulation performance. However, relevant reports are currently lacking for C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</inf>N/CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. In this paper, the surface flashover characteristics of insulators in C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</inf>N/CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> gas mixtures under positive and negative switching impulse voltages are investigated, with a constraint of -25°C. The results show that at 0.8 MPa, the surface insulation strength of the 6% C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</inf>N / 94% CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> gas mixture can reach 97% of that of SF<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf> at 0.5 MPa.
