title: Design and Implementation of SoM-Based Coded CSS Transceiver
authors: Vu N. An, Huynh P. M. Cuong
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LPWANs) have been extensively employed in Internet of Things (IoT) systems. Among LPWAN techniques, LoRa modulation is notable for its low power consumption and long-range capability, enabled by the high receiver sensitivity. As a proprietary technique of Semtech, LoRa lacks a formally published mathematical description of its modulation and demodulation principles. It has, however, been disclosed that LoRa is based on chirp spread spectrum (CSS) modulation. Although several studies have investigated and partially decoded this technique, comprehensive CSS transceiver implementations on system-on-module (SoM) platforms remain scarce. The SoM platform provides software flexibility during system development and reduces hardware design and integration effort through its integrated processor and field-programmable gate array (FPGA) resources. This work presents the design and implementation of a coded CSS transceiver on a SoM comprising the AMD Zynq-7000 system-on-chip (SoC) and the ADI AD9361 radio frequency integrated circuit (RFIC). The low-latency interface between the SoC’s FPGA and the RFIC enables the realization of the physical (PHY) layer, while a lightweight program executed on the SoC’s ARM processor serves as the application (APP) layer handling message transmission and reception. Furthermore, a radio frequency (RF) device driver is integrated into the system to simplify the configuration of the AD9361 chip. The proposed design’s performance is evaluated in the additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel, demonstrating reliable demodulation and high decoding efficiency even in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. The proposed design leverages the reconfigurability of the SoM platform, providing essential PHY-layer primitives that serve as a versatile foundation for future implementations of CSS-based LPWAN protocols.

title: Compact Broadband Dual-Polarized Dielectric Resonator Antenna Array For 5G Low Band Massive MIMO IoT and ISAC Applications
authors: Ahmed El Yousfi, Kerlos Atia Abdalmalak, Abdenasser Lamkaddem, Bruno Biscontini, Daniel Segovia Vargas
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper proposes a compact array based on a compact DRA element [1] for massive MIMO IoT and ISAC applications at the low band. The DRA element is implemented with a small inter-element distance in the vertical plane (0.3λ) to form three subarrays of 1 × 4 elements. With such a small distance, the coupling increases and theoretically, the vertical half-power beamwidth can’t attain an average value of 33º. Therefore, to decrease the vertical half-power beamwidth (HPBW) and enhance the gain, three loaded conducting walls above the entire array, along with four vertical conducting side walls, are used. Moreover, the proposed array can steer the beam over a wide angle ranging from -40º to 40º with a gain variation of 2.4 dB. The measured results show a wideband (|S11|≤ -10 dB) of almost 13% ranging from 720 to 820 MHz, port isolation in each subarray of more than 15 dB, isolation between subarrays of more than 17 dB, a gain of 9.5 dBi, an average vertical HPBW of 33º, and good radiation patterns. The diversity parameters (ECC, DG) are calculated and demonstrate good performance in the operating band. The proposed antenna array is well-suited for providing wide coverage and supporting a large number of IoT connections.

title: A 2-Vpp 106-dB SFDR Artifact-Tolerant Gm-C CT-DSM ADC with Residue-Compression and Gm-Boosting Technique
authors: Chen Wang, Wenshuo Li, Yuanqi Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This brief presents a high-linearity, artifact-tolerant, fourth-order G<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>–C continuous-time ΔΣ modulator (CT-DSMS) for neural recording. To address the transconductor nonlinearity limitations inherent to conventional G<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>–C CT-DSMs, a combined input–feedback linearity enhancement strategy is introduced. A passive single-bit, four-tap FIR-based low-pass filter DAC is employed to attenuate the input–feedback residue by more than 40×, leveraging the intrinsic linearity of single-bit quantization. In parallel, a G<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</sub>-boosted differential difference amplifier with source degeneration is utilized to extend the linear operating range of the constructor, providing a 4–6× improvement relative to conventional implementations. Operating at a 10-MHz sampling frequency with a 50 kHz signal bandwidth, the proposed modulator achieves a 94.0 dB dynamic range (DR), 91.2 dB peak SNDR, and 106.0 dB SFDR under a 2-V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">pp</sub> input, corresponding to a FoM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SNDR</sub>of 170.2 dB. The modulator further demonstrates a high common-mode rejection ratio of 84 dB. Together with its wide linear input range, these attributes enable direct digitization of tens-of-μV-level biopotentials in the presence of volt-level artifacts, making the architecture well suited to buffer-less, high-impedance biopotential interfaces for neural recording.

title: An Efficient and Reliable Agent-Based System for Clinical Data Governance
authors: Kaiyin Zhou, Xiangling Fu, Chenwei Yan, Shaohui Liu, Xinxin You, Mengqiang Zhou, Xinhui Lv, Ji Wu, Xien Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Clinical data governance is the cornerstone of reliable intelligent healthcare systems. However, real-world clinical records frequently suffer from complex data quality issues that demand high semantic fidelity and processing efficiency to resolve. Existing section identification and fragmented standardization methods either fail to address these intricate anomalies or inadvertently sacrifice semantic integrity. Meanwhile, directly deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for this task as free-form text generators introduces hallucinations and computational bottlenecks. To bridge these gaps, we propose GovernAgent, an LLM-driven framework that overcomes these limitations through two core designs. First, inspired by the intrinsic structure of clinical records, our approach introduces a hierarchical governance mechanism. By employing cascading Note- and Section-Level agents, it constrains the governance space in a top-down manner, systematically disentangling these anomalies into resolvable, multi-level quality issues. Second, the framework employs a Constrained Action Planning mechanism. By restricting the LLM to a hybrid “Copy-Generate” action space rather than free-text generation, it maximizes original text reuse, thereby mitigating hallucinations, guaranteeing medical provenance, and ensuring high efficiency. Evaluations on real-world hospital datasets demonstrate that GovernAgent improves governance accuracy and efficiency, minimizes hallucinations, demonstrates high practical adaptability, and empowers downstream clinical applications. Code: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/kaiyinzhou/GovernAgent</uri>
