Notably, the proposed approach maintains high efficacy despite the lack of annotated data and the presence of unexpected faults.

title: MultiMoE: Multimodal Mixture of Experts Adapter for Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images
authors: Ziyue Qi, Qirui Guo, Libo Wang, Yicong Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Multimodal remote sensing image semantic segmentation has benefited remarkably from recent progress in deep learning. However, most existing methods treat heterogeneous modalities with excessive uniformity, failing to fully exploit their complementary features. Furthermore, the absence of visual prior knowledge limits their generalization capabilities across diverse scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose a novel multimodal fine-tuning framework based on vision foundation models, namely MultiMoE. Specifically, we select the vision foundation model (DINOv3) as the frozen encoder and develop lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) adapters on the encoder to transfer general visual knowledge to task-specific semantic contexts. These MoE-based adapters dynamically route samples to dedicated expert pools, thereby processing heterogeneous modalities distinctly and promoting complementary feature representation. Extensive experiments on two widely-used benchmark datasets (ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam) demonstrate the competitive performance of our MultiMoE compared to state-of-the-art methods.

title: On the Impact of Dataset Quality in Multi-View BEV Mapping
authors: Alaa Eddine Ben Zekri, Reza Bahmanyar, Houda Chaabouni-Chouayakh
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Bird's Eye View (BEV) mapping from multi-camera systems has emerged as an important form of ground-level remote sensing for constructing geospatial representations in autonomous navigation and urban analytics. Although recent advances in deep learning architectures have expanded the capabilities of BEV mapping, the impact of data-quality factors on mapping performance remains insufficiently explored. In this work, we investigate how fundamental dataset characteristics such as spatial coverage, ground sampling resolution, annotation quality, and cross-domain differences impact BEV map accuracy, boundary fidelity, and robustness. Using the nuScenes dataset as a primary case study, we systematically vary these data-quality attributes and quantify their effects using geospatial accuracy metrics. To ensure the generality of our findings, all experiments are conducted using two architecturally distinct models, BEVCar and SimpleBEV, and the observed trends are consistent across both. Our results show that reducing BEV map coverage consistently improves accuracy by limiting predictions to visible regions. Additionally, we found that resolution and class granularity jointly affect boundary quality; finer annotations at higher resolutions enhance precision for small structures. Once the previous quality factors were evaluated, we created a synthetic dataset under conditions similar to nuScenes to assess generalization under domain shifts and evaluate cross-domain performance between real and synthetic environments. We highlighted that such data augmentation reduces the tendency to hallucinate unseen objects. By quantifying the data-quality drivers of mapping performance, this work offers general insights for the design, curation, and quality assessment of AI-based remote sensing datasets used for geospatial mapping and perception, which are essential for sustainable mobility and resilient urban planning.

title: Optimal Home Energy Management System for a Residential Grid-Connected Microgrid Using Improved Slime Mold Algorithm
authors: Syuan-Yi Chen, Faa-Jeng Lin, Pin-Jung Chen, Chi-Cheng Chen, Chih-Ting Wu
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: The current research introduces a home energy management system (HEMS) utilizing an improved slime mold algorithm (ISMA) for the efficient energy management of a residential grid-connected microgrid. The studied microgrid comprises a photovoltaic system (PVS), a battery storage system (BSS), two DC–DC converters, a DC–AC voltage source inverter (VSI), and varying electricity loads. The proposed ISMA-based HEMS optimizes the power flow ratio among the PVS, BSS, and the utility grid, taking into account factors including load demand, battery’s state of charge (SOC), PVS power, time-of-use (TOU) electricity rates, feed-in tariffs, and the operational cost of the BSS to minimize overall electricity costs. To achieve the control object, the system incorporates advanced power flow control techniques for the VSI and parallel converter system within the microgrid. The performance of the ISMA-based HEMS is evaluated through experimental validation on a practical microgrid platform. The findings demonstrate that the HEMS using ISMA outperforms conventional rule-based control strategy in terms of electricity cost reduction.

title: On the Consistency Between Subjective and Objective Evaluation for Speech Enhancement Under low-SNR Drone Noise
authors: Dmitrii Mukhutdinov, Ashish Alex, Andrea Cavallaro, Lin Wang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Speech enhancement in the presence of strong drone noise at extremely low signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) remains challenging, and conventional objective metrics may fail to accurately reflect perceived speech quality. In this paper, we evaluate several deep learning-based speech enhancement algorithms under severe drone-noise conditions using a diverse set of objective metrics, including signal fidelity, intelligibility, and perceptual quality measures. The results reveal substantial inconsistencies among metrics, leading to conflicting conclusions regarding algorithm performance. To address this issue, we conduct a controlled subjective listening experiment under extremely low-SNR conditions to assess the perceptual relevance of these metrics. The consistency analysis shows that learning-based perceptual metrics (e.g., NISQA) and intelligibility-oriented measures (e.g., ESTOI) achieve the highest agreement with subjective judgments, followed by SI-SDR, while traditional quality metrics such as PESQ exhibit weaker agreement. These findings highlight the limitations of conventional metrics in extremely low-SNR conditions and provide guidance for selecting perceptually meaningful evaluation criteria in drone audition.

title: Editorial
authors: Zhizhang Chen, Christian Waldschmidt, Jeffrey A. Nanzer, Joseph C. Bardin, Steven M. Bowers
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters

title: Distributed Nash Equilibrium Seeking with Linear Convergence Rate for Constrained Multi-Cluster Aggregative Games over Digraphs
authors: Jingzhao Zhao, Tian Lu, Hongzhe Liu, Zaijun Wu, Wenwu Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper studies the distributed Nash equilibrium (NE) seeking problem for constrained multi-cluster aggregative games over digraphs, where the closed convex set constraints are involved. In the considered game, the players within the same cluster collaboratively minimize the collective cost within locally closed convex sets regardless of the interests of other clusters, where the local objective functions depend not only on local decisions but also on a global aggregative variable. A distributed algorithm is designed to seek the NE based on distributed aggregate estimation and gradient tracking. Specially, the eigenvector learning method is employed to address the information asymmetry caused by unbalanced digraphs. Moreover, the method of feasible direction is incorporated into the proposed algorithm to handle the involved closed convex set constraints. Under certain conditions, it is proved that the proposed algorithm achieves a linear convergence rate. Finally, the proposed algorithm is applied in a numerical simulation associated with the Energy Internet System to verify its effectiveness.

title: A Low-Rank Perspective on Similarity Matrix Completion
authors: Changyi Ma, Runsheng Yu, Xiao Chen, Youzhi Zhang, Zhen Lei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: In real-world information retrieval scenarios, addressing data incompleteness is crucial for providing accurate similarity scores and ensuring reliable results for downstream tasks. Previous Similarity Matrix Completion (SMC) methods aim to estimate a similarity matrix that exhibits positive semi-definiteness (PSD) from an inaccurate one. However, these methods are inadequate in cases where the initial similarity matrix <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$S^{0}$</tex-math></inline-formula> exhibits PSD. In this paper, we propose a novel SMC framework that simultaneously explores the symmetric, positive semi-definiteness (PSD), and low-rank properties to provide an accurate and efficient solution for similarity search tasks. Specifically, we exploit the low-rank property and introduce an efficient specialized Cholesky factorization (CF) technique into the conventional SMC framework, which is implemented via a regularizer. It improves computational efficiency by learning a smaller factorized matrix instead of the entire similarity matrix. Moreover, to enhance the optimality guarantees of SMC, we introduce a novel lower-rank matrix property and design two corresponding regularizers. Building upon these meticulous designs, our novel SMC framework, for the first time, ensures both efficiency and accuracy with theoretical guarantees. Consequently, we propose two novel algorithms, i.e., SMCFN/SMCRN, to implement the SMC framework. Theoretical analysis verifies the effectiveness and fast convergence speed of SMCFN/SMCRN. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets validate our theoretical analysis: SMCFN/SMCRN achieves up to 42% lower RMSE (e.g., 0.34 vs. 0.59 on ImageNet) and 15% higher Recall than state-of-the-art baselines, while being the most efficient among all baseline methods.

title: Towards Intelligent Operations in Energy Harvesting Cognitive Low-Power Wireless Networks: A Survey
authors: Nada Abdel Khalek, Walaa Hamouda, Amr Youssef
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is increasingly evolving into a more intelligent and environmentally sustainable ecosystem. However, as IoT systems proliferate, they face persistent challenges, including limited spectrum and energy resources. These challenges can be mitigated by integrating cognitive radio (CR) technology and energy harvesting (EH) techniques. CR enables wireless devices to increase their spectral resources by opportunistically utilizing licensed spectrum bands, while EH allows energy-constrained devices to become self-sustaining and extend their operational lifetime. The increasing diversity of wireless devices and data, combined with rapidly changing wireless environments and unpredictable energy availability, presents significant challenges. As a result, traditional optimization-based approaches often fall short. The advent of artificial intelligence (AI) offers a promising alternative, enabling low-power wireless (LPW) networks to become self-managing, self-sustaining, and adaptive to changing conditions through data-driven methods. However, existing surveys rarely address cognitive LPW networks in depth and often overlook the role of AI in enabling EH and smart operations. This survey fills that gap by reviewing state-of-the-art AI approaches that address key challenges in EH-enabled cognitive LPW systems, including power allocation, resource optimization, and task scheduling. We review key advancements in cognitive LPW systems, such as IoT, backscattering networks, multi-hop and relay-assisted networks, and multiple access networks. Finally, we highlight key insights from the literature and outline promising directions for future research on cognitive LPW systems that enable smart, green, scalable, and secure deployments.

title: Study of Foreign Object Detection Techniques in Power Transmission Lines Using YOLOv13
authors: Xia Wang, Wei Jiang, Yulong Li, Yun Su, Zhinan Wang, Sheng Huang, Qingxue Liu, Zhiyu Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the challenges of low detection accuracy for small and occluded targets, significant environmental interference, and complex backgrounds in transmission line inspections, which are further exacerbated by the scarcity of specialized datasets, this study proposes an enhanced detection method based on YOLOv13. First, MobileOne is employed as the backbone to maintain robust feature extraction while significantly optimizing the model structure. Second, WTConv is integrated into the neck’s feature fusion layers to expand the receptive field, specifically enhancing the recognition of small and occluded foreign objects in complex scenarios. Third, the MECA attention mechanism is embedded in key layers to strengthen the focus on foreign object features and suppress background noise, effectively preventing information loss. Experimental results show that the improved model achieves 4.8 GFLOPs of computational complexity and 1.67M parameters, and its real-time deployment performance on the UAV end-side outperforms mainstream models. Specifically, the model reaches a recall (R) of 0.942, which is 1.9% higher than the baseline YOLOv13. Ablation studies further confirm that MobileOne, WTConv, and MECA contribute 1.0%, 0.2%, and 1.5% to the recall improvement, respectively— verifying the effectiveness of the proposed model in overcoming the bottlenecks of transmission line foreign object detection.

title: ReCoVLM: A Reconfigurable FPGA–GPU Co-Design for Edge Vision-Language Inference
authors: Jingyu Wang, Yongjiang Xue, Kailai Zhuang, Mingze Sun, Qingzeng Song
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in open-world understanding and interaction. However, edge deployment remains challenging due to strict constraints on power, latency, reliability, and privacy requirements. The VLM inference pipeline typically consists of three stages: visual encoding, cross-modal prefill, and autoregressive decoding. These stages exhibit distinct characteristics regarding operator types, parallel granularity, and memory access patterns. Consequently, a single homogeneous edge device (e.g., CPU, GPU, NPU, or FPGA) often fails to achieve simultaneous optimality in performance and energy efficiency across the entire pipeline.To address this, we propose a configurable FPGA–GPU heterogeneous collaborative system tailored for edge VLM inference. Leveraging specific hardware strengths, we map the compute-intensive visual encoding and cross-modal Prefill stages to the GPU, while offloading the bandwidth-sensitive autoregressive decode stage to the FPGA. This workload-aware mapping results in improved energy efficiency. To mitigate edge resource constraints, we designed a model compression method for edge GPUs and FPGAs. By compressing the model parameters to a range acceptable by the hardware and using only 5% of visual tokens, we achieved 88.9% of the baseline performance. On the FPGA, we implement a pipelined decoder that efficiently utilizes DDR bandwidth and propose an efficient deployment method for MoE (Mixture of Experts). Including aligned block layout of expert weights, operator fusion, and on-chip caching strategies to reduce bandwidth fluctuations caused by weight switching and improve throughput stability, enables the effective bandwidth utilization rate to reach 87.2%. Experimental results on a heterogeneous platform comprising a Jetson Orin Nano (8GB) and a Xilinx VU9P FPGA demonstrate an end-to-end inference throughput of 18.1 tokens/s. Compared to an NVIDIA RTX 4090 baseline, our system achieves a 2.67× improvement in energy efficiency.

title: Adaptive CSI-SLFN-DDPG-Inspired Framework for Net Load Forecasting in High-Renewable Energy Systems
authors: Gin-Ni Kuo, Rua-Huan Tsaih
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The integration of high‑penetration renewable energy sources (RES) has introduced strong nonlinearity and volatility into power‑system net‑load forecasting, challenging both traditional statistical models and over‑parameterized deep learning architectures. This paper proposes a CSI‑SLFN‑DDPG‑inspired framework that combines a dynamically sizing single‑hidden‑layer feedforward network (CSI‑SLFN) with a lightweight, DDPG‑inspired heuristic controller for continuous hyperparameter adaptation. CSI performs structural adaptation via Cramming (capacity expansion) and Integrating (importance‑based pruning), while the controller adjusts learning rate and L2 regularization based on validation improvement and a normalized train–validation gap, without relying on a full reinforcement‑learning agent.Evaluated on a highly nonlinear Irish net‑load dataset (EirGrid 2016–2020, 43,848 hourly samples, high wind penetration), using a 31‑feature engineering pipeline that captures RES‑driven dynamics, the framework is benchmarked against eight baselines including ARIMA, Ridge regression, SVR, Random Forest, standard SLFNs, an LSTM‑style network, and a Transformer‑style network. Random Forest attains the lowest overall error (RMSE = 9.60 MW, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9998), while the proposed CSI‑SLFN‑DDPG-Inspired model achieves the best performance among neural models with RMSE = 48.77 MW, MAE = 39.12 MW, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9955, and MAPE = 10.15%, using only 2,728 parameters and 88 hidden nodes. This represents a substantial reduction in model size and training time compared to deep sequence models, while retaining near state‑of‑the‑art neural accuracy.The contributions are threefold: (1) a two‑tier adaptive design that separates structural adaptation (CSI‑based Cramming/Integrating) from parametric adaptation (DDPG‑inspired heuristic control); (2) a RES‑aware feature engineering pipeline tailored to high‑renewable net‑load forecasting; and (3) empirical evidence that distilled DDPG heuristics can provide robust, validation‑responsive hyperparameter control without the complexity of full deep reinforcement learning. The resulting lightweight and interpretable framework offers a practical alternative to heavy deep architectures for operational Transmission System Operator (TSO) forecasting and forms a strong neural baseline for future comparisons against full RL‑based CSI‑SLFN‑DDPG models.

title: KD-OCT: Efficient Knowledge Distillation for Clinical-Grade Retinal OCT Classification
authors: Erfan Nourbakhsh, Nasrin Sanjari, Ali Nourbakhsh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Age-related macular degeneration and choroidal neovascularization are widespread ocular conditions that can severely reduce vision and are prevalent globally. OCT imaging is essential for spotting early changes and helping doctors determine the best treatment approach. Despite the strong results achieved by architectures such as ConvNeXtV2-Large in classifying retinal conditions, their substantial computational requirements limit their feasibility in clinical environments. To address this, we introduce KD-OCT, a knowledge distillation framework designed to transfer the diagnostic capabilities of a high-capacity ConvNeXtV2-Large model—trained using advanced augmentation techniques, focal loss, and stochastic weight averaging—to a lightweight EfficientNet-B2 student model. This framework employs a joint loss function that harmonizes soft-label supervision from the teacher with the true class labels, optimizing the student model for accuracy and efficiency. Using patient-level splits for cross-validation on the NEH and UCSD OCT datasets, KD-OCT maintains near-teacher performance and achieves large reductions in both model footprint and inference time. This method achieves high accuracy without heavy computation, making it well-suited for real-time AMD screening in clinics with limited computing capacity. The full implementation is provided at https://github.com/erfan-nourbakhsh/KD-OCT.

title: A Review on Optimization of Dynamic Electricity Price-Responsive Charging Load Driven by Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yudong Pu, Xinyi Zeng, Qiyu Wang, Yixin Tang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the global ownership of electric vehicles (EVs) projected to exceed 300 million by 2030 [1], their high-power and spatiotemporally random charging loads pose severe challenges to the stability of power grids, and traditional optimization methods struggle to address the problems of high-dimensional state space and multi-scale decision-making. This paper systematically elaborates on the research progress of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) in the collaborative optimization of dynamic electricity price-responsive charging loads [42][46][47]. By constructing a dynamic coupling model of charging load and electricity price, the nonlinear impacts of three types of electricity price mechanisms (time-of-use, real-time, and demand response incentive) on user behavior are analyzed in depth. Typical algorithms such as DQN, PPO and SAC are sorted out, together with their adaptive improvements in the energy field, including the processing of high-dimensional states by Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) or Graph Neural Networks (GNN) [22] and privacy protection by federated learning [21]. At the application level, the TD3/DDPG strategy on the user side can reduce charging costs and cut peak loads [29][30]; aggregators participate in the electricity market relying on virtual power plants and achieve a significant increase in revenue; on the grid side, the combination with graph neural networks reduces the voltage deviation rate [22][37] and the curtailment rate of wind and solar energy [41]; after the introduction of Nash equilibrium and blockchain into multi-agent games [39][40], regional carbon emissions are reduced and the peak-valley difference is narrowed. However, issues such as strategy coordination dilemmas, V2G technical bottlenecks [30][33], insufficient cross-domain adaptability of models, and a single incentive mechanism still restrict large-scale implementation. Future research should focus on multi-agent federated reinforcement learning [21], transportation-energy cross-domain coordination, trusted privacy protection, and policy-technology-market coupling simulation, so as to promote the evolution of DRL from theoretical verification to engineering application, and provide theoretical support and technical paths for building a safe, efficient and low-carbon smart grid.

title: ALMA: A Multimodal Companion Powered by Large Language Models for Alzheimer’s Reminiscence Therapy
authors: Mihai-Emanuel Spiță, Ioana-Veronica Vavrici, Evelina Miron
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Social isolation is an important factor in accelerating cognitive decline for patients with Alzheimer’s Disease, yet conventional digital interfaces remain inaccessible due to motor and sensory barriers. This paper presents ALMA, an empathetic multimodal companion designed to mediate the relationship between patients and caregivers through a natural Voice User Interface. The system integrates a dual architecture: a physical Totem interface for the patient, which leverages Generative AI for digital reminiscence therapy and emotional support, and a dedicated family application for managing digital memories. Our implementation utilizes Large Language Models optimized for low-latency interaction, facilitating fluid conversation. Preliminary results indicate that removing tactile interaction significantly reduces the user’s cognitive load, facilitating consistent mental stimulation and mitigating the feeling of loneliness.

title: Evaluation of a Cabin Design Using Virtual Reality
authors: M. Cenkner, N. Tylová, U. Michel-Schneider, I. Melnyk, P. Bouchner
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Traditionally, operator cabin design is evaluated using physical mock-ups and subjective feedback based on user impressions and preferences. However, this approach is timeconsuming, costly, and lacks objective metrics for systematic comparison. Virtual reality (VR), combined with physical simulator hardware, enables immersive real-time testing of cabin layouts during simulated crane operation. Modern VR headsets track head position and orientation within the virtual scene, allowing estimation of the operator's viewing direction. Using this approach, we conducted a pilot study to analyze the distribution of operator gaze and map the operator's field of view for two different crane cabin layouts. The results introduce gaze-based metrics that enable the identification of operator visual behavior patterns and provide a basis for systematic comparison of cabin layouts.

title: Optimization of Accident Operation Strategy for Nuclear Power Plants with Passive Shutdown Seal
authors: Chen Sun, Min Li, Yumei Chen, Rushi Fu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This article studies the adaptability of accident operation Strategy following the introduction of Passive Shutdown Seal (PSDS) technology in CPR1000 nuclear power plants. By systematically analyzing the passive characteristics of PSDS and the modification schemes of its supporting process systems, the study evaluates the impact of this technology on unit control under Station Blackout (SBO) conditions. Key influencing factors are identified, including triggering conditions for PSDS and adjustments to the operational logic of the hydrostatic test pumps. The research proposes optimization solutions for accident operation Strategy, including manually starting the hydrostatic test pump under SBO conditions, cool-down two units with shared hydrostatic test pump and intervention Strategy when exceeding the left boundary of the natural circulation (P, T) diagram which constrains the reactor operating. Through thermal-hydraulic and critical safety analysis verification, the optimized Strategy can effectively control the pressure and temperature of the primary circuit, ensuring reliable sealing of the PSDS and safely cool down of the units, significantly enhancing the safety operation capability of the CPR1000 nuclear power plant under extreme accident conditions.

title: Research on Construction Plan and Key Technologies of Provincial Energy and Carbon Service Platform
authors: Jian Geng, Yulong Jin, Shuheng Zhao, Meng Guo, Yiduo Xiong, Bokang Zou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To achieve the dual carbon goals, energy serves as the primary battlefield, electricity acts as the main force, electricity-carbon synergy represents the key focus, and industrial parks stand as the optimal testing ground. The establishment of a provincial-level energy-carbon service platform serves as a critical foundation for achieving accurate regional carbon emission measurement and dynamic carbon flow monitoring, providing unified data support for scientific decision-making under the dual carbon goals. At the same time, it acts as a central management mechanism for coordinating multiple stakeholders—including parks, enterprises, and power grids—in their participation in the green transition. This effectively promotes the integrated and synergistic development of clean energy and carbon management, ultimately contributing to the development of zero-carbon parks in China.

title: Thermal Safety of Conventional Focused Ultrasound for Retinal Neurostimulation and Retina/Lens Heating Assessment via Duty-Cycle Parametric Study
authors: Alejandro Rodríguez Peña, Arturo Vera Hernández, Eduardo Moreno Hernández, Mario Ibrahín Gutiérrez Velasco
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LIPUS) has been proposed as a noninvasive therapeutic stimulation modality for retinal neuroprotection, yet its translation requires rigorous quantification of intraocular heating in thermally sensitive structures. A 2D axisymmetric thermoacoustic eye model was implemented including temperature-dependent perfusion in the retina and choroid and zero perfusion in avascular domains such as the crystalline lens and ocular humors. Heating in the retina and crystalline lens was evaluated for three exposure levels (Grades 1, 2, and 5) during a continuous 600 s (10 min) therapy window followed by a post-exposure cooling period to characterize thermal relaxation. Under the baseline regimen, peak temperatures increased with ultrasound exposure level yet remained below 41 °C even at Grade 5, with faster and higher heating in the retina than in the crystalline lens despite the use of a focused ultrasound field targeting the retina. Across the duty-cycle sweep, peak temperature increased markedly with active-time fraction, demonstrating that anterior structures can experience clinically relevant heating despite not being the intended focal target. Overall, the results indicate that thermal risk—interpreted here as proximity to conservative temperature-based safety limits for ocular tissues—is governed by exposure dosing and tissue-specific heat dissipation rather than by focalization alone, motivating ocular-specific focusing strategies that confine energy deposition to retinal regions of interest while minimizing incidental exposure, particularly in the crystalline lens.

title: Evaluation of Traditional and Deep Learning-Based Video Compression Techniques for Surveillance Systems
authors: Soheib Hadi, Asadollah Shahbahrami, Hossien Azgomi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The rapid growth of surveillance systems has generated vast amounts of video data, requiring efficient compression for transmission and storage. This study compares traditional codecs such as H.264/AVC and H.265/HEVC with deep learning–based methods, including DVC, RLVC, and GAN-based models, for surveillance video compression, evaluating their performance in terms of bitrate efficiency, perceptual quality (PSNR and SSIM), and computational complexity. Experiments on the PETS2009 and CAVIAR datasets show that deep learning codecs achieve, on average, 1.5–2 dB higher PSNR and up to 0.03 improvement in SSIM compared with H.265 at equivalent bitrates. However, these gains are accompanied by a three- to fivefold increase in computational cost, which may limit their suitability for certain real-time or resource-constrained applications. While traditional codecs remain more practical for large-scale, low-latency surveillance systems, deep learning methods offer superior perceptual quality and rate–distortion efficiency at low bitrates. These results suggest that hybrid frameworks combining both approaches could provide balanced solutions for future surveillance video compression.

title: Unsupervised Hyperspectral Image Anomaly Detection using 3D Convolutional Autoencoders
authors: Damoon Alemzadeh, Saman Mohammadi Raouf, Maryam Amirmazlaghani
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Hyperspectral imaging, due to its ability to capture spatial and spectral information with high precision, is an important tool for detecting anomalies in complex scenes. The main objective of this research is to propose an effective approach for anomaly detection with high accuracy and reduced error in hyperspectral data. In this study, an approach based on autoencoder and a three-dimensional convolutional neural network is presented. By utilizing spectral dimensionality reduction through Principal Component Analysis and selecting normal background regions using classical methods, the proposed model reconstructs the data and extracts anomalies. The experimental results on real San Diego datasets demonstrated that the proposed method is capable of detecting anomalies with high accuracy and good stability. Furthermore, comparison with several well-known methods in this field showed that the proposed approach outperforms many of them. In particular, the AUC metric on the two subsets AVIRIS-1 and AVIRIS-2 of the main San Diego dataset were 0.9912 and 0.9518, respectively, indicating the high accuracy and efficiency of the model in anomaly detection.

title: Research on coordinated planning and scheduling optimization of regulating energy in 'wind-solar-water-storage' base under high proportion of new energy access
authors: Ting Pan, Qiao Zhao, Dongjing Zou, Jianzan Yang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To reasonably plan hydropower expansion and pumped storage configuration, this paper constructs a two-layer optimization model based on a Stackelberg game to achieve joint optimization of capacity planning and scheduling coordination. The upper-level model aims to minimize investment cost and determines the expansion scale of hydropower sites and the installed capacity of pumped storage stations. The lower-level model performs operational scheduling optimization under the given capacity scheme from the upper level, with the objective of maximizing wind and solar consumption and generating the corresponding scheduling strategy. Through interactive iteration between the upper and lower levels, an effective trade-off between investment cost and consumption targets is achieved. Finally, taking an integrated basin in Southwest China as an example, simulation experiments and comparative analysis are conducted to verify that the proposed method has strong application potential in improving wind and solar absorption, enhancing regulation flexibility, and increasing economic benefits. The study provides reference suggestions and theoretical insights for hydropower expansion and pumped storage capacity allocation at the basin scale.

title: The Proactive Detection of Student Disengagement in E-Learning Environments via Action Prediction
authors: Rohini Vedam
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Ever since the COVID-19 pandemic, the popularity of e-learning has skyrocketed. A significant challenge facing teachers online is low student engagement, a problem that has been addressed through action recognition techniques. However, these are lagging indicators that only detect after students have already disengaged, allowing little time for teacher intervention. This research develops a novel model that uses action prediction to proactively detect student disengagement. A Sequence-to-Sequence Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Seq2Seq bi-LSTM) model that predicts the future engagement level was built. When presented with only 70 percent of a video it is able to predict the next engagement level with an accuracy of 85 percent. In addition, this paper introduces the first dataset designed for student engagement prediction models. The results demonstrate that action prediction is a better alternative to action recognition for engagement monitoring and should be further researched.

title: Study of Charging Induced Attraction Phenomenon in Cobalt Silicide Defects
authors: Sutanto Leonardo, Wong Tung Sheng, Tay Ching Leon, Prahalathan Kaushik Ram, Li Xiqiang, Lee Chin San
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper investigates the detrimental effects of surface charging induced by Cobalt silicide (CoSi<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) processes that involve spin processing, such as the Deionized Water (DIW) Scrubber rinse process. Due to high-speed rotation involved in the spin-cleaning process, high surface charge is accumulated on the wafer. This poses adverse effects on the wafer, such as charge-induced defectivity which is further studied in this paper. A technique used to prevent charge build-up is through the injection of Carbon Dioxide (CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) into the Ultra-pure water (UPW), which reduces its resistivity and enables the static charges to be safely dissipated. An experiment was conducted to understand the effects of surface charging on the wafer at different CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> injection levels. It was found that below a certain threshold level of CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> injection, an undesirable phenomenon was observed with the formation of a unique wafer defect, characterized by localized Cobalt (Co) defect on Cobalt Silicide (CoSi<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) regions. The failure mechanism is unraveled in this paper as a charge-induced particle attraction phenomenon. This emphasizes the critical role of surface potential control in eliminating charge-induced defects in advanced silicide processes.

title: Smart Solutions for CMP Scratch Control
authors: Xinqiao Dong, Russell McCabe, Vivek Pragada, Jeremy Lovell, Abbas Arab, Yenebeb Worku, Subhadeep Mukherjee, Jian Li, Yi Yang, Jing Nie
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Chemical Mechanical Polishing (CMP) is essential for global planarization in advanced semiconductor manufacturing, where its performance directly impacts device yield and reliability. Yet, scratch defects formed during CMP continue to limit yield and compromise device integrity, especially as device features shrink and integration schemes grow more complex. These scratches are typically caused by large particles dragged across the wafer surface particles that originate from interactions between the polishing pad, slurry, wafer, and conditioner. Common particle sources range from pad debris created during conditioning, to abrasive-slurry agglomerates, to residues from consumables and process byproducts, all of which are extremely sensitive to consumable choices and process settings.This work presents a comprehensive, system-level framework for CMP scratch mitigation across oxide, metal, nitride, polysilicon, and wafer-reclaim processes. We systematically address the full particle lifecycle from generation and transport to removal through coordinated optimization of consumables, process parameters, and tool hardware. Strategies include enhanced slurry filtration, optimized pad conditioning, implementation of a Less-Deposition-Less-Polish (LDLP) scheme, upstream process tuning, and hardware modifications such as high-pressure micro jet (HPMJ) cleaning.Experimental validation across multiple CMP modules using inline defect monitoring and macro inspection demonstrates substantial and sustained reduction in scratch-related defects. The proposed framework achieved significant improvement in scratch performance while maintaining required material removal rates for advanced technology nodes. These results establish a scalable and cost-effective methodology for CMP defect control, enabling improved process stability, yield enhancement, and device reliability in next-generation semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Improving Public Transport in the Calarasi and Olt Valley Regions of Romania
authors: Stefan Popa, Eduard - Antonio Surdoaia, Tomas Horak, Miroslav Svitek
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: This paper evaluates the feasibility of On-Demand Public Transport (ODPT) in two contrasting Romanian regions: the Olt Valley, a congested, linear mobility corridor, and the Călărași-Lehliu Gară lowland region. A macroscopic modelling framework is developed using census-based trip generation, behavioral aggregation through an elasticity-based function, and a corrected gravity model to preserve long-distance socioeconomic interactions. The model estimates ODPT demand, accessibility, cycle structure, and fleet requirements for each hub. Results show that ODPT demand is highest in the Olt Valley due to strong regional attractors, tourism and congestion along DN7, while Călărași exhibits lower but more uniformly distributed demand. The combined use of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{h}}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda$</tex> captures the distinct spatial and behavioral dynamics of both regions, demonstrating ODPT's capacity to provide flexible, efficient mobility where fixed-route transport is unsuitable. The findings support ODPT as a viable solution for improving rural and peri-urban accessibility.

title: Uncovering Urban Mobility Signatures: A Multi-Facility Analysis of Parking Behavior
authors: Ondrej Pribyl, Roman Dostal
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: This paper presents a novel data-driven framework for identifying and quantifying urban mobility signatures using long-term parking transaction data. Leveraging a longitudinal dataset spanning 97 months and over 221,000 observations across 222 parking installations, we move beyond aggregate occupancy metrics to uncover latent behavioral archetypes. We employ an unsupervised watershed transform to segment the arrival-duration density space, successfully isolating seven distinct mobility signatures ranging from high-turnover utility visits to multi-day vehicle storage. These discovered behaviors serve as dependent variables in a Multinomial Logit (MNL) model designed to evaluate policy sensitivity. Our results demonstrate that pricing acts as a significant structural filter, with a strong negative coefficient (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta=-1.1215, p<0.001$</tex>) for workday commuting, effectively shifting facility composition toward highervelocity urban utility. Furthermore, the model reveals a critical dependency on infrastructure, where proximity to the public transport backbone significantly suppresses long-term vehicle idling (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta=1.0526$</tex> for transit distance). This research provides a robust, GDPR-compliant methodology for urban planners to transition from reactive monitoring to proactive, signature-based policy design, enabling more efficient utilization of existing urban infrastructure without intrusive tracking technologies.

title: Synergy of Semantic Modeling and Multi-Agent Systems for Resilient Smart City Utility Networks
authors: Leonard Walletzký, Miroslav Svítek, Sergei Kozhevnikov, Zuzana Hruška
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Bridging the gap between conceptual service design and technical execution remains a critical challenge for Smart City resilience. This research presents a novel integration of the 4DocMod semantic framework with Multi-Agent Technology (MAT) for autonomous utility network management. Using the “Diamond See” methodology, we deconstruct technical ontologies into dynamic semantic maps. The proposed framework enables “paradigmatic resilience,” allowing AI agents to switch operational logics-from economic optimization to crisis survival-via a “Context Bridge.” This approach ensures systemic stability in complex urban ecosystems without requiring modifications to the agents' core programming.

title: Exploring NCAE Cyber Competencies Under ABET Student Outcomes Framework
authors: Erald Troja, Joan E. DeBello
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: There has been a well-documented shortage of Cybersecurity professionals in the United States. This deficiency has triggered a well-funded National Cybersecurity education effort both from the part of the National Security Agency (NSA) and Department of Defense (DoD). The most successful Cybersecurity educational movement is highlighted by the NSA through the creation of the National Centers of Academic Excellence (NCAE) in Cybersecurity. There are currently over 400 such designated NCAEs in the United States and all of them have excellent programs to train the next generation of Cybersecurity professionals. Recent changes in the NCAE re-designation criteria revolve around building and assessing Cybersecurity competencies which can be thought of as the ability of a student to perform a task within the context of a work role. In this paper, we explore some efficient ideas and methods in order to allow Cybersecurity NCAEs, who also hold an ABET accreditation in Cybersecurity, to be able to build and assess Cybersecurity competencies in a manner that can be seamlessly used towards ABET program assessment. We hope that NCAEvalidated Cybersecurity program coordinators/directors whose program is also ABET-accredited will benefit the most from the ideas and practices shared on this paper.

title: A Unified Wafer Anomaly Detection and Segmentation
authors: Shih-Chih Lin, Jenq-Neng Hwang, Shang-Hong Lai
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: We present UniWADS, a unified framework for wafer anomaly detection and segmentation in semiconductor high-volume manufacturing (HVM). Existing automated optical inspection (AOI) pipelines rely on hand-crafted rules, defect-specific thresholds, or class-specific training, limiting scalability across products and process conditions. UniWADS addresses this by performing image-level anomaly detection and pixel-level localization within a single reconstruction-based architecture.UniWADS follows a constrained reconstruction principle: robust anomaly detection requires both stable token correspondence and controlled decoder capacity. It combines a frozen EfficientNet-B4 encoder, a lightweight SpatialAdapter decoder with full softmax attention, a zero-parameter Noisy Bottleneck, and a hard cosine reconstruction objective to suppress identity mapping and amplify defect-sensitive discrepancies.We evaluate UniWADS on Texture-AD Wafer and MIIC/IC microscopy datasets. Ablations show that softmax attention is more reliable than linear and hybrid variants, while the Noisy Bottleneck and hard cosine loss play complementary roles: the former improves localization stability, and the latter enhances image-level separability. On Texture-AD Wafer, the best configuration achieves 82.46% pixel-AUROC and 58.36% AUPRO.These results demonstrate that constrained reconstruction provides a robust and deployment-friendly solution for unified wafer anomaly inspection. More fundamentally, we show that reconstruction-based anomaly detection is governed by a capacity–correspondence trade-off: limiting reconstruction capacity while preserving token correspondence is key to separating normal and anomalous patterns. The code is available at https://github.com/leolin65/UniWADS.git.

title: Scholar Augment: An End-to-End Multi-LLM Platform for Accelerating Systematic Literature Reviews and Semantic Corpus Analysis
authors: Mihai-Emanuel Spiță, Ovidiu-Andrei Schipor
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Systematic literature reviews and large-scale qualitative document analysis are foundational to academic progress in fields ranging from Computer Science to the social sciences. However, the process is fundamentally constrained by the time-intensive, laborious, and error-prone nature of manual data extraction. This bottleneck not only slows the pace of research but also limits the scope and scale of questions that can be feasibly investigated. Existing digital tools offer partial solutions but fail to address the core challenge holistically. Standard reference managers excel at citation management but are content-agnostic, while Qualitative Data Analysis Software (QDAS) still relies on extensive manual coding.This paper introduces Scholar Augment, a novel, user-centric web platform designed to bridge this methodological gap. We present its architecture and a new framework for researcher-AI collaboration that accelerates systematic data extraction while keeping the researcher in full control. Scholar Augment introduces a unified pipeline that automates the entire SLR lifecycle: from AI-driven Boolean search string generation and automated PDF retrieval (via Unpaywall), to structured data extraction and semantic corpus interaction. Leveraging a provider-agnostic Multi-LLM architecture and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) backed by a vector database, the platform enables researchers to generate custom analytical frameworks. The platform incorporates a persistent database and post-processing tools for data standardization. A case study analyzing 592 academic articles demonstrates that Scholar Augment reduced data extraction time by approximately 99.32%, offering a new paradigm for efficient, scalable, and rigorous document analysis. To demonstrate real-world viability, the platform has been deployed as a public web service (https://starlit-brioche-15a023.netlify.app) and is currently actively used by an initial cohort of researchers, offering a new scalable paradigm for rigorous document analysis

title: Work in Progress: A Novel Implementation of a Remote Laboratory Applying Kolb's Experiential Learning Theory and IEEE 1876-2019 Standard
authors: Miguel Shiniti Aguena, Javier Solis-Lastra, Anarosa Alves Franco Brandão, Bruno de Carvalho Albertini
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Immediate monitoring of student activities and performance is essential for learning. Thus, remote laboratories can become not only a technological tool but also a pedagogical tool when they integrate strategies that ensure learning. This study proposes to implement a novel remote laboratory for engineering education, applying Kolb's experiential learning theory and the IEEE 1876-2019 standard, which will not only guarantee learning, but also have the potential to be expanded to other types of laboratories, as well as make it possible to take advantage of the data collected to determine student engagement, behavior, and performance. The development will be open source, allowing the source code to be altered and adapted to meet the needs of users, thereby ensuring universal access to quality education, a requirement of Sustainable Development Goal 4. Likewise, the impact of remote laboratories on teaching is expected to be evaluated both quantitatively and qualitatively in the future.

title: Impact of Ultra Ethernet Transport on Co-Tenant Latency in Reconfigurable Data Center NICs: A Simulation Study
authors: Vyshnavi Alladi, Ronald Sass
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: While most hyperscalers now offer reconfigurable computing options, these resources typically remain isolated in tiers separate from standard CPU and GPU offerings. Beyond the security concerns, a more immediate practical hurdle to deeper integration is the challenge of sharing a single network port between reconfigurable accelerators and standard CPU-based tenants. Ultra Ethernet (UE) is an emerging open standard designed to integrate AI and High-Performance Computing (HPC) features—such as low latency and scalable collectives—directly into commodity 200G+ Ethernet infrastructure. This work presents a simulation-based analysis of a reconfigurable Network Interface Card (NIC) modified to prioritize UET egress traffic, specifically evaluating the latency penalties imposed on non-UET tenants. Proving that such resource sharing is viable would allow the reconfigurable tier to evolve from a niche specialty into a mainstream component of cloud architecture. This work presents a simulation-based analysis of a reconfigurable Network Interface Card (NIC) modified to prioritize UET egress traffic, evaluating the additional latency imposed on non-UET tenants. A key barrier to hyperscaler adoption is the uncertainty of whether Ultra Ethernet Transport (UET)-enabled tenants impact co-located ordinary tenants on shared nodes. Results show that average latency increases by less than 2% (under 2 ns absolute at 200 Gbps), a threshold we argue is inconsequential. These findings support the incremental adoption of UET in hyperscaler environments and justify further investment in full FPGA-based UET NIC implementations.

title: A Hybrid Model for Regional Electricity Price Forecasting Based on TimesNet-DWT-PA
authors: Zhiyuan Chen, Ping Wang, Tao Qian, Qinran Hu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In modern electricity markets, the electricity price is a core variable for resource allocation and dispatch optimization; accurate forecasting is crucial for secure grid operation and fair energy trading. Electricity price series typically exhibit periodicity, regional heterogeneity, non-stationarity, and multi-scale dynamics, which challenges traditional machine-learning models in both representation and generalization. To address this issue, this paper proposes a new forecasting architecture, TimesNet-DWT-PA, which integrates discrete wavelet transform, a multi-scale convolutional structure, and a period-attention mechanism. The model uses DWT to identify dominant periodic components, employs the Inception structure to capture local multi-scale variations, and introduces multi-head attention along the period dimension to model long-range dependencies within each period. Experiments on multi-region electricity price forecasting show that the proposed model consistently outperforms representative baselines, demonstrating improved robustness and generalization under complex periodic structures and regional heterogeneity. The proposed approach provides a new paradigm for periodic time-series modeling and offers promising engineering applicability.

title: Looking for an even better fit? A Personalized Incremental Reinforcement Learning Multimodal Object Referencing Framework
authors: Amr Gomaa, Kiran Gani, Michael Feld, Antonio Krüger
publish: ACM Transactions on Interactive Intelligent Systems
abstract: None

title: Identifying Opportunities for Faculty and Staff Optimization and Resource Reduction Through AI Integration
authors: Khaled Shaaban
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The traditional model of higher education is based on faculty and support staff responsible for instruction, research, service, and administration. This model is facing an unprecedented challenge from fast advancements in Artificial Intelligence (AI). This work examines the potential for AI to streamline and potentially optimize a wide range of faculty and staff roles within the academic field. Different AI applications across various academic functions are presented, encompassing areas from core teaching and learning, such as automated course instruction, creation, assessment, and curriculum design to student support systems like AI-powered tutoring. The analysis also extends to administrative functions, research and grant administration, and strategic institutional planning. These applications demonstrate that AI can transform the faculty and staff from a broad-based workforce into a more specialized one. While this transition promises significant gains in operational efficiency, scalability, and cost reduction, it also raises profound questions regarding the reduction of faculty and staff positions, educational quality, the preservation of academic mentorship, and the ethical implementation of automated systems. This work concludes that a strategic integration of AI is inevitable. The challenge for university leadership is to manage this transition responsibly and preserve the core values of academia.

title: Methodologies and Approaches for Developing Computer-Based Mathematics Learning Games: a Systematic Literature Review
authors: José Vitor Ramos de Lima, Péricles de Lima Sobreira, Franck Gilbert René Bellemain, Karim El Guemhioui
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This article presents a Systematic Literature Review aimed at mapping and discussing about methodologies used in the development of computational games for Mathematics Education, highlighting trends, patterns, and gaps in recent literature. The review considered publications from 2019 to 2024, retrieved exclusively from the IEEE Xplore database, using a search strategy structured around three conceptual blocks: game development, mathematics, and methodological processes. After applying inclusion and exclusion criteria, 11 studies composed the final analytical research corpus. The findings reveal a diversity of methods, frameworks, and design approaches, but also recurrent weaknesses, such as incomplete descriptions of development processes, limited integration between pedagogical and computational dimensions, and a predominance of technically driven solutions with insufficient educational grounding. The discussion identifies structural gaps in the documentation of design methodologies and emphasizes the need for more consistent models that articulate pedagogical and technological principles. Overall, this review contributes to mapping the state of the art and offers insights for future research on the development of educational games for Mathematics Education.

title: Process Optimization to improve Breakdown Voltage and Metal Leakage in Dual MIM Capacitors
authors: Jia Shern Heah, Tubati Sricharan, Rajkumar Thiruchelvam
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper studies the critical factors affecting dual metal-insulator-metal (DMIM) early breakdown and metal shorts. Through detailed evaluation, we identified the manufacturing hurdles and process controls required to overcome this issue, including metal etch process optimization, metal annealing and metal etch chamber seasoning. With these optimizations, good breakdown voltage with low metal leakage were achieved. Our findings establish a viable methodology for the fabrication of high-performance DMIM capacitors, demonstrating enhanced electrical characteristics and process compatibility. This work lays the groundwork for overcoming integration-related barriers, thereby enabling future innovations in advanced semiconductor applications.

title: Topology Planning Strategy for Distribution Networks Based on Fully Controllable Network Clusters
authors: Siyu Ye, Xiafei Tang, Linfeng Tan, Yixian Dai, Ye Cai
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Voltage deviations have been observed to increase with the growing penetration of distributed photovoltaic systems in distribution grids. This paper proposes a distribution network topology planning strategy based on fully controllable clusters. Firstly, a weighted directed graph based on the electrical distance is constructed to represent the distribution grid. Then, the Kuhn-Munkres algorithm is employed to determine the minimum set of driving nodes, thereby enabling the network to be decomposed into multiple fully controllable clusters. Secondly, a mixed-integer optimization model is constructed with the decision to build interconnection lines between driving nodes taken as the core binary decision variable. An optimal trade-off between system voltage deviation and investment cost is achieved. In addition, a multi-stage greedy algorithm is employed to effectively solve the proposed model. Finally, a modified IEEE 123-node system is simulated. It is demonstrated that adding a few critical interconnection lines can significantly improve the system voltage distribution. The proposed strategy provides a feasible, cost-effective planning framework for distributed voltage control in distribution networks with high PV penetration.

title: A Conceptual AI Framework for Personalized Learning in Engineering Education
authors: Abhilash Deori
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping higher education by enabling instruction to be tailored to individual learners at scale. This paper proposes a conceptual framework for implementing AI-enabled personalized learning in engineering education. The framework integrates adaptive learning algorithms, intelligent tutoring systems, real-time learning analytics, and generative AI tools into a cohesive architecture that personalizes content and support for each student. We review recent literature on AI in education to ground our design in evidence and identify critical considerations for implementation. Key design factors - including scalability, the evolving role of instructors, ethical data use, and equity of access - are analyzed to ensure the framework supports effective and inclusive learning. We clarify the contributions of this work relative to prior studies and avoid unsupported assumptions by citing empirical findings. The discussion addresses practical limitations and challenges (e.g., infrastructure needs, teacher training, algorithmic bias) and outlines future work. This study contributes a structured approach for leveraging AI to enhance engineering and higher education, with the goal of fostering more engaging, adaptive, and equitable learning environments.

title: Prototyping an AI-Based Waste Monitoring and Salubrization Route Optimization System
authors: Trohin Vlad, Dorogan Andrei
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: The paper presents the development and experimental validation of a functional prototype for automated waste monitoring and dynamic salubrization route optimization. The system employs an IP camera to periodically capture static images of waste containers at predefined intervals. Each image is processed using a vision-based analysis module that estimates container fill levels using discrete values of 0, 50, 80 or 100 percent. The average fill level is computed, and a binary decision rule is applied: when the average is greater than or equal to 50 percent, collection is required; otherwise, the status remains normal. The results are stored in a CSV log and displayed through an automatically generated web-based dashboard using red and green status indicators. When the threshold condition is met, the system automatically invokes the Google Routes API to generate an optimized collection route, which is opened in Google Maps. Experimental validation using two real containers demonstrated correct status classification and successful end-to-end route triggering, confirming the operational feasibility of the proposed system.

title: Assessing the Impact of User-Side Demand Response Identification Errors on Microgrid Scheduling
authors: Xidong Zheng, Jingzheng Li, Yuwei Yang, Siyuan Chen, Kehan Wang, Tao Jin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Microgrid dispatch increasingly relies on demand response (DR) to handle renewable uncertainty. However, DR participation is often inferred from data and subject to identification errors. Thus, a three-stage framework is proposed to assess how such errors propagate from user side identification to microgrid (MG) dispatch. Correlated DR scenarios are synthesized via Monte Carlo sampling and reduced by K-means clustering. An improved sparrow search algorithm tunes a least squares support vector machine classifier (ISSA-LSSVM) for DR identification, and a demand response identification deviation rate (DRID) is defined to evaluate dispatch deviations under imperfect identification. In an industrial-park MG case, the classifier achieves about 94% accuracy. A 0.005% aggregate load deviation leads to amplified deviations of 0.144% in power sales, 0.332% in CHP outputs, and about 0.056% in operational cost. These results highlight the sensitivity of MG dispatch to user-side DR identification errors and encourage incorporating identification reliability into source-network-load dispatch.

title: Spectral F-Test to Assess Cortical Activity During Virtual-Reality Rehab Card Game
authors: Mariana Souza Pinto, Eric Kauati Saito, Maurício Cagy
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) video games have been increasingly used in physical therapy and are recognized for enhancing motivation, accessibility, and effectiveness in rehabilitation. These technologies promote motor learning and neuroplasticity through real-time feedback. This study aims to investigate the effects of VR gaming on cortical activity using electroencephalography (EEG). Eleven healthy young volunteers participated in this study, and the EEG signals were collected during VR simulation of a card game. Signals were pre-processed and then analyzed using the Spectral F-Test (SFT), which identified power reduction in the EEG at F3, F4, and Oz, mainly in the beta and gamma bands. The results suggest distinct modulations of cortical activity according to the difficulty of the task. SFT indicated a reduction in EEG power spectral density in the F3 and F4 electrode regions compared with the P3, P4, Cz, and Oz electrode regions (other regions of the international 10-20 system were not analyzed). The study demonstrated that the card game promoted changes in EEG power in different cortical regions, highlighting its potential as a neurorehabilitation tool for stimulating brain plasticity and functional recovery.

title: Ionic Reflection-Induced Profile Evolution in 3D NAND Etching: Mechanisms and Optimization Strategies
authors: Siddharth Mohanty, Pranay Arvind Patil, Om Maheshwari, Nihar R. Mohapatra
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This work presents a simulation based study of ionic reflections in Oxide-Nitride-Oxide (ONO) stack plasma etching for high aspect ratio 3D NAND structures. The model captures plasma-surface interactions and the angular distribution of reflected ions. Results show that higher substrate bias power reduces ion reflections, while optimized mask tapering increases etch depth by approximately 31%. Surface charging further lowers reflections from approximately 1.3% to 0.7%. The study identifies substrate bias power, mask geometry, reflected ion angular spread, and surface charging as key levers for minimizing reflection-induced non-uniformities in 3D NAND etching.

title: Elimination of First Wafer Effect in Plasma Etch Processes
authors: Jeff J. Ye, Raviteja Pagadala, Fei Lu, Allan Fang, William Simpson
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: The first wafer effect is a common issue in plasma etch processes in high-volume manufacturing (HVM), affecting yield, defectivity, and product quality in memory devices. In various processes, we encountered severe first wafer effects that caused significant yield loss. In this paper, we report a newly developed best known method (BKM) for waferless chamber warm-up by adding process purge gases combined with a two-step pre-heating process. With implementation of this BKM, the first wafer effect was successfully eliminated, resolving major yield loss issues across multiple processes.

title: Impact of Forming Gas Treatment on Vtlin Modulation in Asymmetric NMOS Devices
authors: Bryan Wong, Moh Moh, Huang Daiyu
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This work presents a systematic investigation of the impact of forming-gas treatment on the linear-region threshold voltage (VTLIN) of asymmetric NMOS devices. Electrical characterization demonstrates that N<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> exposure induces a significantly larger VTLIN increase compared with conventional charging, thermal, and oxidation treatments. A structured design-of-experiments (DOE) further identifies forming-gas exposure duration as the dominant factor governing VTLIN modulation, while plasma power, chamber pressure, and gas flow rate exhibit minimal influence. Time-of-Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) measurements confirm enhanced hydrogen incorporation within implanted regions, indicating strong interactions between hydrogen species and phosphorus dopants. These results support a mechanism in which hydrogen passivation preferentially deactivates dopants in the lightly doped drain (LDD) region of asymmetric NMOS devices, increasing the effective channel barrier and shifting VTLIN. The findings provide important process insights for threshold-voltage engineering in high-voltage asymmetric NMOS technologies.

title: Déjà Vu Packing: Optimizing FPGA Logic Clustering Runtime via Pattern Memoization
authors: Milo Liebster, Amin Mohaghegh, Andrew Boutros
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Implementing a digital circuit on a field-programmable gate array (FPGA) fabric requires clustering technology-mapped netlist primitives into coarser-granularity blocks that can be directly mapped to the physical resources available on the FPGA fabric. As the internal architecture of FPGA logic blocks (LBs) has grown in complexity, with sophisticated logic elements (LEs) and highly irregular local interconnect, this packing problem has become significantly more challenging. To ensure the feasibility of intracluster routing, the computer-aided design (CAD) tools must solve a costly multi-source multi-sink routing problem for each candidate cluster. In this paper, we first show that such packing legality checks consume a significant portion of the CAD flow runtime for LB architectures with complex LEs and local routing structures resembling modern commercial FPGAs. We demonstrate that the packing stage constitutes 58% and 94% of the entire Versatile Place and Route (VPR) flow runtime on average when mapping a wide variety of benchmarks to the AMD 7-series-like and Altera Stratix 10–like VTR architecture captures, respectively. By analyzing the packing algorithm behavior, we observe that a significant fraction of the attempted packed clusters are repetitions of a much smaller number of packing patterns, and therefore many of the packing legality checks are redundant and could be skipped. To this end, we introduce our Déjà Vu packing approach, which leverages a novel packing signature tree data structure that enables efficient identification of recurring packing patterns and memoization of their legality check outcomes. Our approach speeds up the packing runtime by up to 13.4× and 29.3×, with an average of 3.7× and 6.9×, across the evaluated benchmarks on the 7-series and Stratix 10 architecture captures. These packing runtime gains result in a significant 1.6× and 5.3× average reduction in end-to-end VPR runtime, while maintaining quality of results.

title: Robust Trajectory Tracking and Muscle Force Estimation in a 2-DOF Musculoskeletal Arm Using Adaptive Integral Terminal Sliding Mode Control
authors: Erfan Sedaghat, Seyyed Arash Haghpanah
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Musculoskeletal modeling of the human arm plays a vital role not only in understanding joint dynamics and estimating internal muscle forces but also in developing advanced control strategies for assistive, rehabilitation, and bio-inspired robotic systems. This study presents a comprehensive simulation framework for a two-degree-of-freedom musculoskeletal arm model operating in the transverse plane. The system is driven by six musculotendon actuators whose force distribution is obtained through a static Lagrangian-based optimization approach. An Adaptive Integral Terminal Sliding Mode Controller (AITSMC) is proposed to robustly track a desired trajectory in the presence of dynamic uncertainties and disturbances. The controller is designed to emulate key features of human motor regulation by ensuring fast finite-time convergence of joint angles without requiring precise knowledge of system parameters. Following successful trajectory tracking, muscle forces are computed to identify potential overload in specific muscles, which is relevant for both ergonomic evaluation and robot-assisted rehabilitation planning. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed AITSMC in achieving accurate path tracking and realistic muscle force estimation, making the framework applicable to rehabilitation robotics and bio-inspired control research.

title: A Three-Loop Cooperative Control Strategy with CCS-MPCC for Bipolar DC Converter
authors: Xin Shen, Yajun Zhao, Yikun Tian, Feilong Jiang, Peng Xue, Feifei Bu, Haihong Qin, Wenxin Huang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Bipolar DC systems have attracted much attention for their ability to provide multiple voltage levels and high power supply reliability. The three-phase voltage source converter (VSC) topology with a coupled ground inductor can suppress midpoint potential fluctuation caused by load imbalance without adding an additional voltage balancer. However, conventional dual-loop proportional-integral (PI) control exhibits slow dynamic response and entails complex parameter tuning, making it difficult to realize both fast AC current tracking and precise DC voltage balancing simultaneously. To address this, this paper proposes a three-loop cooperative control architecture: the voltage outer loop adopts PI control to ensure the stability of the total DC bus voltage; the current inner loop adopts the continuous control set model predictive current control (CCS-MPCC) to realize the fast and accurate tracking of the AC side current; and a zero-sequence control loop balances the positive and negative bus voltages by regulating the ground inductor current. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly enhances the dynamic response of the bipolar DC converter under load transients and improves midpoint potential balancing performance.

title: Research and Application on Non-Disassembly Program Modification and Efficient Maintenance Methods for the Electronic Equipments Based on CAN Bus
authors: Ding Jie, Fang Ling, Han Zhuwen, Zheng Jiaming, Xie Jin, Li Zhiwei
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: During the commissioning phase or maintenance process after a failure, program modifications are often required to test the functions and performance of marine electronic equipment. When commissioning or maintaining marine electronic equipment, technicians often have to remove the equipment from its complex installation location on the ship and open the device casing to use a simulator for program burning. This process is not only time-consuming and labor-intensive, but may also cause physical damage to the equipment due to frequent disassembly, further affecting the stability and service life of the equipment. This paper proposes a method for program modification and efficient maintenance of electronic equipment without disassembly, based on the CAN bus. This method enables program modification for marine electronic equipment during commissioning or maintenance without the need for disassembly, facilitating maintenance and debugging, and saving time and labor costs.

title: WD-STGNN: A Wind Direction-Aware Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Network for Short-term Wind Power Cluster Forecasting
authors: Zheyong Jiang, Yu Dong, Jinxing Che, Qingmei Tan
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is essential for renewable energy integration and grid stability. Traditional methods often neglect the spatial dependencies among wind turbines caused by wind propagation. This paper proposes WD-STGNN, a Wind Direction-Aware Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Network that explicitly models directional wind propagation patterns. Unlike conventional graph neural network with static spatial graphs, WD-STGNN constructs dynamic graphs based on real-time wind direction alignment and distance-based weighting, capturing the directional nature of wind energy transfer. The model combines Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) for temporal feature extraction and Graph Attention Network (GAT) for spatial dependency modeling. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that WD-STGNN achieves superior forecasting accuracy compared to baseline methods, with significant improvements across multiple forecasting horizons.

title: Privacy-Preserving Unlinkable Identity Management on Blockchain for e-Health Records
authors: Vanga Odelu, Goutham Reddy Alavalapati, K. G. Raghavendra Narayan
publish: Proceedings of the 8th ACM International Symposium on Blockchain and Secure Critical Infrastructure
abstract: None

title: Dual-Mode Perovskite/GaN Tandem LEDs With Interlayer-Enabled Voltage-Dependent Color Tuning
authors: Yunfang Tong, Hao Min, Ting Zhi, Zhe Zhuang, Zili Xie, Rongrong Dou, Tao Tao, Bin Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: Metal halide perovskites have emerged as promising candidates for light-emitting diodes (LEDs) owing to their superior optoelectronic properties and solution processability. However, the fabrication of perovskite-based multicolor or white LEDs remains challenging due to the difficulty in depositing multiple emissive layers without damaging underlying films. Here, we demonstrate a hybrid Pe-GaN tandem LED by vertically integrating a solution-processed perovskite LED (PeLED) with a commercial GaN LED using an indium tin oxide (ITO) interconnecting layer. A two-step annealing strategy is employed to optimize the perovskite film quality, yielding significantly enhanced photoluminescence and improved film morphology. Benefiting from the optimized perovskite layer, the PeLED unit achieves substantially enhanced electroluminescence (EL) performance, with a maximum external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 10.2% and a high luminance of 91,233cd <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathrm {m}}^{-{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.
