Furthermore, by employing two different interconnecting layer structures, we achieve voltage-controlled color tunability with distinct spectral evolution behaviors. By changing the interconnecting layer from ITO to SnO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> or Ag/SnO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, we transition from independent unit control to series-connected color tuning. This work introduces interlayer design as a new degree of freedom for expanding the color palette of Pe-GaN tandem LEDs.

title: SwapController: Towards Improving Identity and Attribute Control for Diffusion-Based Face Swapping
authors: Fengyuan Liu, Lingyun Yu, Quanwei Yang, Runxin Liu, Yongdong Zhang, Hongtao Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Face swapping efforts strive to achieve high-fidelity and well-controlled generation effects. Owing to the remarkable generative capabilities, diffusion models deliver promising high-fidelity solutions. However, their intrinsic stochastic properties complicate the accurate modeling of facial representations, introducing new challenges for identity and attribute consistency of the generated faces. In this paper, we introduce a novel diffusion-based face-swapping framework, named SwapController, which achieves high-fidelity generation via careful facial identity and attribute modeling. Specifically, our facial modeling mainly involves facial structure and facial texture. For structure modeling, 3D facial priors are leveraged to provide explicit structure supervision, enabling accurate head structure control. On this basis, two novel components are proposed to deeply mine facial textural representations from identity and attribute aspects. To enhance identity control, multi-grained source identity embeddings are obtained from various functional encoders to convey critical global identity and fine-grained identity details. To improve attribute modeling, identity-shifted attribute embeddings are derived by applying identity modulation to the most salient textural attribute features of the target face. Moreover, in line with the diffusion denoising characteristics, a timestep-aware identity optimization objective is introduced to optimize identity consistency guidelines and overall fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our SwapController in generating identity-consistent portrait images while faithfully preserving target attributes, which obtains a 98.32 ID Retrieval, exceeding the SOTA DiffSFSR by 7.32 $\uparrow$. Codes will be available at https://github.com/tyrink/SwapController.

title: IBM Power11 Processor
authors: William J. Starke, José E. Moreira
publish: IEEE Micro
abstract: The IBM Power11 processor extends the system architecture introduced in Power10 with a focus on memory-centric design and system-level scalability. While maintaining a largely compatible core microarchitecture and programming model, Power11 delivers substantial improvements in memory bandwidth, capacity, and system integration. Enhancements to the Open Memory Interface (OMI) enable up to 3× higher memory bandwidth and 2× increased capacity per socket, while improved coherence and interconnect bandwidth support more efficient scaling across larger configurations. These advances reflect a broader shift in computer architecture, in which performance is increasingly limited by data movement rather than raw compute capability. By combining high-performance cores with an enhanced memory subsystem and system architecture, Power11 supports a new class of enterprise and AI-oriented systems optimized for the emerging memory-centric era of computing.

title: A Foot Resistive Force Model for Legged Locomotion on Muddy Terrains_supp1-3694687.pdf
authors: Jingang Yi
publish: N/A
abstract: Legged robots face significant challenges in moving and navigating on deformable and highly yielding terrain, such as mud. We present a resistive force model for legged foot-mud interactions. The model captures rheological behaviors, such as visco-elasticity, thixotropy of the mud suspension, and retractive suction. One attractive property of this new model lies in its effective, uniform formulation to provide underlying physical interpretation and accurate resistive force predictions. We further take advantage of the resistive force model to design a new morphing robotic foot for effective and efficient legged locomotion. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the force model and the results demonstrate that the morphing foot enhances not only the locomotion mobility in terms of reducing sinking/suction and preventing robot appendage entrapment, but also energy-efficiency of walking in mud. The new resistive force model can be further used to develop data-driven simulation and locomotion control of legged robots on mud.

title: DAS 2026 Commentary
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)

title: EU-Transformer: An Enhanced U-shape Transformer via Texture Retention for SAR Image Denoising
authors: Shuaiqi Liu, Shengye Wang, Yonglu Zhou, Qi Hu, Bing Li, Yudong Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) inherently suffers from speckle noise attributable to its distinct imaging mechanism, imposing significant challenges on the extraction of valid information from SAR images. Consequently, speckle suppression is essential in the processing of SAR images. Current deep learning-based SAR image denoising algorithms struggle to achieve an optimal balance between speckle reduction and texture retention. Regarding the above issue, this article proposes EU-Transformer, an enhanced U-shape Transformer via texture retention for SAR image denoising. Specifically, during the encoding phase, the EU-Transformer employs a multi-scale enhanced Transformer block (METB) to extract hierarchical features from the noisy image, thereby capturing multi-scale representations. Subsequently, a bottleneck layer is utilized to further distill deep features. In the decoding phase, to fully exploit both local and global image information, this article constructs a dual-branched enhanced Transformer block (DETB) to progressively reconstruct deep features. Furthermore, to better preserve image textures, a texture retention block (TRB) is proposed to enhance feature transmission during the skip-connection stage. Finally, the EU-Transformer generates the denoised output through global residual learning. Experimental findings demonstrate that the proposed EU-Transformer outperforms current top-performing denoising algorithms in both quantitative and qualitative analyses. The source code is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/cvmdsp/EU-Transformer</uri>.

title: High-Speed White-Light Interferometry with Expanded Dynamic Range Enabled by the Optical Vernier Effect
authors: Paulo Robalinho, Vinícius Piaia, António B. Lobo Ribeiro, Susana Silva, Orlando Frazão
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: White-light interferometry (WLI) enables the optical interrogation of interferometric sensors with different optical path lengths. However, its implementation using all-optical fiber technology is challenging when the interrogation dynamic range of approximately 1000 μm or higher is required at sampling frequencies around or above 750 Hz. This paper presents an integration of the WLI technique with the optical Vernier effect (WLIVE), allowing an effectively expanded interrogation dynamic range to be achieved. To accomplish this, the optical interrogator is composed of two interferometers. The measurement window is determined by one interferometer operating at a high-frequency oscillation, while the second interferometer, characterized by a low-frequency oscillation, provides the tuning mechanism (tuning filter). The operation relies on the analysis of the carrier or envelope signal, which results from the beating between the interferometric sensor cavity and the tunable filter, when the sensor optical path difference (OPD) exceeds the physical limit of the measurement band. The experimental results confirm the repeatability of the system, achieving an average experimental error of 0.29 ± 0.07%, as well as an expansion of the interrogation dynamic range from 180 ± 2 μm to 1352 ± 2 μm. These results demonstrate the feasibility of WLIVE combination to achieve high sampling frequencies and large interferometric dynamic range, while simultaneously reducing the complexity of sensor cavity fabrication.

title: Band-edge slow-light modulation in thin-film lithium niobate
authors: Shufeng Li, Shiyao Deng, Weikang Xu, Yang Li, Ziliang Fang, Xiao Ma, Fangheng Fu, Xu Ouyang, Yuming Wei, Tiefeng Yang, Heyuan Guan, Huihui Lu
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) electro-optic modulators are a promising platform for high-speed communications, optical computing, and programmable photonics. Slow-light engineering offers an attractive route to enhance electro-optic interaction and reduce device footprint. However, in compact TFLN modulators, slow-light enhancement aggravates microwave–optical velocity mismatch and optical modal mismatch, limiting electro-optic efficiency while increasing reflection and insertion loss. Here, we realize band-edge slow-light modulation in TFLN using a Mach–Zehnder interferometer with identical slow-light gratings in both arms. Co-tapered transition sections at the two ends of the slow-light region enable near-adiabatic mode evolution and suppress parasitic reflection and excess loss, while T-shaped capacitively loaded electrodes increase the microwave effective index and improve electro-optic velocity matching. The device exhibits a pronounced stopband near 1511.8 nm with an extinction ratio of 27 dB. Near the stopband edge, it achieves a minimum VπL of 0.56 V·cm, together with a stopband tuning efficiency of 8.7 pm/V under single-arm DC bias. These results establish band-edge slow light as a promising design strategy for electro-optic modulation in TFLN, opening new opportunities for scalable and functional integrated photonic systems.

title: A Systematic Evaluation Framework on Emotion Inference for Large Language Models Using Appraisal Theory
authors: Tobias Thejll-Madsen, Nutchanon Yongsatianchot, Stacy Marsella
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: We propose and implement a systematic evaluation framework to assess Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to infer emotions through the lens of Appraisal Theory of Emotion. Our framework generates thousands of story vignettes across six domains ranging from sports to social interactions that can be systematically modified using emotion-relevant features derived from appraisal theory. We first validated these vignettes through human participant responses, which served as validation for the models' responses. Our evaluation included four LLMs: GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Llama 3.1 405B. Our results show that all tested LLMs exhibit strong correlations with human responses in five of the six domains, demonstrating sensitivity to emotion-relevant features that align with both theoretical predictions and human ratings. Notably, Claude Sonnet and Llama perform consistently across all domains, while GPT-4o and Gemini Pro diverged from human ratings in one domain, assigning implausible emotional intensities. These inconsistencies suggest that surface-level features can override theoretical manipulations, leading to inconsistent emotion ratings even when models demonstrate robust performance in other domains. Our findings highlight the general alignment between LLMs and human emotional inference when appraisal features are explicitly manipulated, while also demonstrating the necessity of systematic testing to identify domain-specific vulnerabilities. This work provides a framework and recommendations for such systematic evaluations.

title: DOGT: Double-Order Graph Transformers With Adaptive Node-Group Learning
authors: Yupei Zhang, Xian Sheng, Mengfei Liu, Ruotong Zhao, Xuequn Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Graph transformers (GTs) have recently attracted considerable attention for graph representation learning (GRL). However, the existing methods often neglect hyper-order structures that arise from implicit node-groups within graphs. More critically, many approaches construct hyper-order structures by using predefined heuristics or clustering algorithms, resulting in a disjointness between hypergraph construction and downstream optimization objectives. To this end, this article proposes double-order GTs (DOGT), a novel framework that dynamically incorporates hyper-order features into graph representations through adaptive node-group (ANG) learning. Specifically, DOGT uses a learnable discriminative mask matrix to infer hyper-order edges, i.e., groups of nodes, followed by building a hyper-order graph (HOG). HOGs are then extracted from the HOG using a new double-order attention (DoA) integrated with a graph neural network (GNN), and meanwhile, raw-order graph features are obtained from another separate GNN branch. Finally, DOGT creates a feature pyramid from the double-order graphs, comprising both the GNN-based and attention-derived representations, to achieve the fused graph representation. This study contributes via ANG, adaptively seeking hyper-order edges instead of using traditional predefined rules, and DoA, computing weights along the transformation from raw-order to HOGs rather than just one of them. Extensive experiments on real-world graph datasets manifest that DOGT not only achieves rapid convergence but also outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on graph-level classification and regression tasks.

title: Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation for Emotion Recognition_supp1-3695876.pdf
authors: Tianyi Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Emotional states manifest as coordinated and heterogeneous physiological responses across central and autonomic systems, posing a fundamental challenge for multimodal representation learning in affective computing. Learning such joint dynamics is further complicated by scarce and subjective affective annotations, motivating the use of self-supervised learning (SSL). However, most existing SSL approaches rely on pairwise alignment objectives, which are insufficient to characterize the joint dependencies among more than two modalities and fail to capture higher-order interactions arising from coordinated brain-autonomic responses. To move beyond this limitation, we propose Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation (MFMC), a principled SSL framework that maximizes higher-order multimodal dependence via a Dual Total Correlation (DTC) objective. By deriving a tight sandwich bound and optimizing it with a functional maximum correlation analysis (FMCA)-based trace surrogate, MFMC directly captures joint multimodal interactions without resorting to pairwise contrastive losses. Experiments on three public affective computing benchmarks demonstrate that MFMC consistently achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance under both subject-dependent and subject-independent protocols, highlighting its robustness to inter-subject variability. In particular, MFMC achieves substantial gains on CEAP-360VR, improving subject-dependent accuracy from 78.9% to 86.8% and subject-independent accuracy from 27.5% to 33.1% using the EDA signal alone, and remains highly competitive within 0.8 percentage points of the best-performing method on the most challenging EEG subject-independent split of MAHNOB-HCI. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/DY9910/MFMC</uri>.

title: Projeto e simulação de um controlador fuzzy para sistemas de FES-Cycling aplicados à reabilitação motora
authors: Gabriel Mendanha Da Costa, Guillermo Alvarez Bestard, Claudia Ochoa-Diaz
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)-assisted cycling is a promising rehabilitation modality for individuals with spinal cord injury, but closed-loop control remains challenging due to neuromuscular nonlinearities and stimulation-induced fatigue. This paper presents a simulated fuzzy logic controller for cadence tracking in an FES-Cycling loop, derived from an ongoing hybrid fuzzy-PID control architecture proposed for the open platform Bem-Te-Vi. In the simulation model, the fuzzy controller uses cadence error and error variation as linguistic inputs and outputs incremental adjustments of stimulation current amplitude, which is accumulated over time to form the applied amplitude. A first-order approximation is adopted for the muscle-bike-patient dynamics. Results indicate a smooth, well-damped cadence response, reaching the reference in the order of tens of seconds and stabilizing without aggressive transients, supporting the feasibility of fuzzy modulation as a safe and adaptive strategy prior to experimental validation.

title: Modelos de Aprendizado de Máquina aplicados à Previsão de Readmissões Hospitalares em Pacientes com Diabetes Mellitus
authors: Jéssica De Andrade Gomes, Renan Moritz VR Almeida, Núbia Karla De Oliveira Almeida
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Hospital readmission is a relevant indicator of healthcare quality, particularly among patients with chronic diseases such as diabetes mellitus. This study evaluates the performance of Random Forest and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) models for predicting hospital readmissions using a public dataset from Kaggle, comprising over 100,000 hospital admissions recorded between 1999 and 2008 in the United States. Data preprocessing steps included handling missing values, standardizing continuous variables, encoding categorical features, and splitting the dataset into training and test sets. Model performance was assessed using commonly adopted classification metrics, including Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Balanced Accuracy, and the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC). Both models demonstrated limited predictive performance, with AUC values close to 0.60, indicating modest discriminative ability. The Random Forest model presented more balanced results across the evaluated metrics, whereas XGBoost achieved higher sensitivity after decision threshold optimization, at the expense of reduced specificity. Overall, the findings highlight the challenges of predicting hospital readmissions using secondary administrative data and suggest restricted applicability of these models for clinical decision support in the evaluated context.

title: Machine Learning Enabled Non-Invasive Techniques for Early Detection of Chronic Compartment Syndrome to Prevent Limb Amputation-A Systematic and Critical Exploration
authors: Pruthvi MR, Gangothri Sanil, Savitha CK, Prajna MR, B Jaishma Kumari
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Compartment Syndrome is a serious condition that can damage muscles and tissues if it is not identified early. In severe cases, it may even lead to amputation. Current diagnosis methods, mainly invasive intracompartmental pressure (ICP) measurement, have a few drawbacks such as patient discomfort, delayed identification, and difficulty in continuous monitoring. This article reviews different Machine Learning (ML) techniques that have been developed for the early and non-invasive detection of compartment syndrome.Around 40 research papers published between 2015 and 2025 were studied by comparing the datasets used, preprocessing techniques, ML algorithms, validation methods, interpretability, and practical clinical usage. Traditional ML models like Support Vector Machine and Random Forest showed accuracy levels between 68% and 94%, while deep learning approaches using multiple physiological parameters achieved more than 90% accuracy in some studies.The review also points out a few important challenges such as limited availability of quality datasets, lack of proper real-time clinical testing, and very little use of Explainable AI (XAI) techniques. Overall, the study shows that ML-based systems can play an important role in improving the early diagnosis of Chronic compartment syndrome. However, more work is needed to make these systems more reliable, explainable, and suitable for real-world clinical use.

title: Agentic AI to Reconfigure Low-Altitude Non-Terrestrial Networks: Convergence of Collaborative Communication, Sensing and Control
authors: Yang Cao, Shao-Yu Lien, Zheng Chang, Xiaohu Tang, Li-Chun Wang
publish: IEEE Network
abstract: To support emerging low-altitude economic activities such as urban monitoring, unmanned aerial vehicles acting as non-terrestrial access points (NT-APs) play a pivot role in providing high data-rate communication and high-resolution sensing services in non-terrestrial networks (NTNs). To tackle optimization for matching spatial-temporally varying resources and demands, NT-APs can utilize artificial intelligence (AI) models to enable near-real-time decision-making. Nevertheless, due to the partial observability, those NT-APs as AI-agents struggle to actively perform long-horizon adaptability to the dynamic environment. Toward optimizing the overall performance, the agentic AI technology with coordination to AI-agents in different levels is a promising remedy to orchestrate communication, sensing services and resource control models. To this end, we propose a multi-AI-agent coordinated architecture to empower intelligent resource optimization in cell-free communication and sensing (C&S) services, in which the C&S service clustering and resource allocation are tackled by AI-agents at central processing units and NT-APs, respectively. By leveraging multi-agent rollout training and cross-task information exchange, AI-agents can tackle individual sub-problems such as trajectory design and beam management in a nearly-stationary environment. To enable continuous policy improvement, model-based planning is conducted using online-collected sensing results of unexplored environments. Finally, simulations on the obstacle-avoidance task of air traffic management demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed design over the centralized evolutionary algorithm and centralized-training-and-decentralized-execution deep reinforcement learning (DRL).

title: Analysis of Edge Degradation Induced Characteristics in Amorphous-Alloy Axial-Modular Flux-Switching Permanent Magnet Machine
authors: Lujia Xie, Jiamu Liu, Wei Hua, Peng Su, Yongjian Li, Biao Zhang, Yiwei Wang, Zhongyuan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This paper proposes an Edge-degradation-dependent nonlinear subdomain (EDNS) model for amorphous-alloy axial-modular flux-switching permanent-magnet (AAM-FSPM) machines. An equivalent edge degradation layer (EEDL) method is investigated to evaluate the degradation-induced magnetic reluctance in the magnetic circuit. The EDNS model is established by coupling a nonlinear magnetic circuit (NMC) model with a nonlinear subdomain (NS) model that accounts for edge-degradation effects, through which degraded equivalent surface currents are derived to represent the complex degraded magnetic properties of the amorphous-alloy (AA) cores. To further examine the relationship between the AA core dimensions and edge degradation, a large-size AAM-FSPM machine is designed, which is less affected by the edge degradation effect. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed model is verified through comparisons with FEA and experimental results.

title: Interval State Estimation for LPV Systems Based on Ellipsotope
authors: Yan Wu, Weixin Han
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a two-step interval state estimation framework for discrete-time linear parameter-varying (LPV) systems subject to mixed unknown-but-bounded (UBB) uncertainties. First, a stabilizing LPV observer is utilized to generate a stable nominal center trajectory. Second, the estimation error bounds are propagated using ellipsotope set arithmetic, which flexibly unites the geometric advantages of ellipsoids and zonotopes to tightly enclose the reachable set of the error dynamics under mixed-norm constraints. To ensure computational tractability during the set propagation, order reduction strategies are employed. Finally, the feasibility and performance of the introduced method are validated through simulation on a longitudinal LPV model of a Boeing 747 aircraft.

title: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters publication information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters

title: Electrical Behavior at Cryogenic Temperature of the Radiation-Tolerant Hourglass MOS Transistor
authors: Carlos Alfredo Pelcastre Ortega, Mónico Linares Aranda, Daniel Ferrusca Rodríguez, Ismael Martínez Ramos, Ignacio Juárez Ramírez
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: This article presents experimental Id-Vgs measurements and an analysis of the electrical characteristics of a MOS (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) transistor featuring a novel gate geometry referred to as the hourglass shape. This new transistor was designed to increase total ionizing radiation (TID) tolerance without modifying the manufacturing process. Experiments were conducted using a test chip with individual transistors, under temperature sweeps down to 3.6 K. Key performance metrics—including threshold voltage, subthreshold swing, and zero temperature coefficient (ZTC)—were compared against those of a conventional rectangular transistor used as a reference and a diamond transistor with similar geometry, and fabricated on the same chip. The experimental results show that the hourglass-shaped transistor presents a 27.17% reduction in normalized power consumption compared to the rectangular transistor, while the reduction is 3.61% compared to the diamond-shaped transistor. Additionally, the hourglass transistor presents a normalized increment of 115% and 10.4% in current difference with respect to the rectangular and diamond transistor, respectively across the evaluated temperature range. This study highlights the improved current efficiency of the hourglass design while maintaining lower power consumption under cryogenic conditions. The enhanced thermal stability, the stability of threshold voltage (Vth) and subthreshold swing (SS) shifts and energy efficiency of the proposed device across a wide temperature range are also shown.

title: Characterization of Electrooculography Signals for Diabetic Retinopathy Based on Fractal Algorithms Using a Low-Cost Device
authors: Franco López Sánchez, Dulce Martinez-Peon, Miguel Reyes González, Elsa Michell Contró Esparza, Rene Sanjuan Galindo, Pedro Rosales Gutierrez, José Váldes-Rayón, Mariela Flores Martinez, Jairo Sánchez Uriegas
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy is a disease that, if left untreated, can lead to blindness. The typical techniques for making a diagnosis are based on images; however, if the patient has an obstruction to the fundus, it is difficult to detect the stage of the damage. Electrooculography has been shown to detect this disease in a non-invasive and less costly manner. In this paper, we present a design for a low-cost EOG device, incorporating a light stimulus to elicit a response in the cones. The response to this stimulus can reflect damage at different stages of diabetic retinopathy. We did a test on a healthy subject using the light stimulus and recording the EOG signal with the circuit design. Finally, we apply the Hurst exponent for characterization, obtaining 0.7569 ± 0.0219 for the dark response and 0.8407 ± 0.0026. for the light response. Using the designed low-cost device, it is expected to repeat the experiment on diagnosed patients to compare their responses.

title: A Bloch-Sphere Geometric State Representation for Constraint-Preserving Distributed Coordination in Inverter-Dominated Microgrids
authors: Jingwei Guo, Jie Yu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Distributed secondary control in inverter-dominated microgrids is challenged by multi-variable coupling among frequency, voltage, and power, as well as heterogeneous feasibility limits of inverter-based resources. Conventional Euclidean consensus schemes typically enforce feasibility via channel-wise saturation or heuristic clipping, which may distort regulation directions and degrade performance under near-boundary operating conditions. This paper proposes a Bloch-sphere-inspired geometric coordination framework that represents a coupled frequency–voltage regulation direction by a bounded spherical state with an auxiliary slack coordinate. A projection-based Laplacian interaction is designed on the sphere to guarantee positive invariance of the coordination state and to promote geometric alignment over a connected communication graph, which supports a consistent planar regulation direction for actively participating nodes. Physical feasibility of inverter commands is ensured at the execution layer by computing a locally feasible step size along the agreed direction, yielding heterogeneous participation induced by inverter-specific constraints. Case studies on an islanded inverter-dominated microgrid demonstrate improved transient regulation, substantially reduced infeasible-command tendency under near-boundary overload, and enhanced robustness under cross-coupled regulation compared with a conventional dual-channel consensus baseline.

title: Real-Time Plasma Etch Rate Control Using Noise-Augmented Neural Networks and Evolutionary Optimization
authors: Prakash R. Kota, Rohan P. Kota
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Accurate etch rate control is essential in semiconductor plasma etching to ensure dimensional fidelity, process stability, and yield. This paper presents a machine learning framework for real-time etch rate optimization, combining a neural network surrogate model with a differential evolution (DE) optimizer. The surrogate was trained on physics-inspired synthetic data that explicitly incorporates random noise and batch-level drift, emulating realistic semiconductor fabrication facility (fab) conditions. Seven process parameters were considered: RF power, bias voltage, chamber pressure, three etchant gas flows, and wafer temperature. After scaling and truncation of unrealistic outliers, the resulting dataset yielded a surrogate with high predictive fidelity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.990 on test data, RMSE = 19.8 Å/min). Coupled with DE, the surrogate was transformed into a tuning engine that identified feasible input settings to achieve specified etch-rate targets. Benchmark tests showed that a lightweight configuration (PopSize = 1, MaxIter = 10) completed optimization in under 2 seconds on a CPU-only platform, with absolute errors typically below ±10 Å/min. Higher-precision configurations reduced errors to sub-angstrom levels at the expense of runtime. These results demonstrate that the framework balances speed, accuracy, and flexibility, providing a scalable pathway for integration with fab-level control systems. While validated with synthetic data, the modular architecture supports retraining on empirical datasets and is generalizable to other unit processes, offering a step toward intelligent, closed-loop manufacturing.

title: Pure Functional Programming in Python
authors: Rajshekhar Sunderraman
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The functional programming paradigm has a long and storied history, with its beginnings in the Lambda Calculus. In recent decades, it has been shown that robust software can be highly effectively produced by pure functional languages such as Haskell, due to its statically typed nature and strong type inference mechanisms built into the language. Desirable functional features are included in almost all programming languages these days. In this paper, a subset of Python is introduced, using which pure functional ideas such as immutability, pure functions with no side effects, and stateless programming can be learned by students. Bugs can be reduced, performance can be improved, and code can be made more maintainable and scalable by this approach. It is strongly felt that familiarity with pure functional programming is needed by students in computing, and it is argued that this can be taught within introductory programming courses that are taught in Python.

title: A Review of Research on Distribution Network Topology Identification
authors: Ran Chen, Peng Zhang, Wei Bao, Yuze Li, Chengzhi Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Distribution network topology identification is crucial for power system analysis and serves as a prerequisite for applications such as power flow calculation, network reconfiguration, and optimized dispatch. This paper reviews existing distribution network topology identification techniques, categorizing them into two main types: the first comprises mechanism-based optimization approaches, including matrix methods, linear programming, and clustering analysis; the second encompasses data-driven techniques, focusing on deep learning-based topology identification. Finally, it provides a comparative analysis of existing algorithms and outlines future research directions.

title: Research on Game Model and Optimization Technology of Microgrid Cluster Resource Scheduling Based on Virtual Operators
authors: Shiwei Li, Xuli Wang, Hui Zhang, Ru Ling
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the transformation of energy structure toward low-carbon, the coordinated scheduling of microgrid clusters has become a key means to improve the consumption of renewable energy and the reliability of the power grid. However, individual interest differences among microgrids pose dual challenges to both efficiency and fairness in market-based resource scheduling. To address this, this paper proposes a master-slave game optimization model based on a Virtual Operator (VO), constructing a three-layer interactive framework of "distribution network – virtual operator – microgrid," and designing a market-oriented trading mechanism for Electricity Consumption Rights (ECR) and Electricity Generation Rights (EGR). The dynamic interaction between the virtual operator and the microgrids is characterized through a Stackelberg game. In the upper level, the virtual operator dynamically sets prices to maximize comprehensive revenue, while in the lower level, each microgrid optimizes its generation-consumption strategy based on price signals to minimize operating costs. The dual-layer model is solved using the particle swarm optimization algorithm and the fmincon function, with the Shapley value method introduced to ensure a fair distribution of cooperative benefits. Simulation results show that, compared with non-cooperative scheduling, the proposed method increases the overall operating revenue of the microgrid cluster by approximately 8% and reduces scheduling deviation by about 42.2%, effectively enhancing the economic efficiency and operational stability of the system. Meanwhile, the allocation mechanism based on the Shapley value ensures that the benefits of each microgrid are higher than those under independent operation, thereby promoting cooperation among microgrids and providing theoretical and technical support for the market-oriented operation of microgrid clusters.

title: Simulation Research on Two-Terminal Voltage and Current Method of Short Circuit Fault Location for Long Distance Power Cable
authors: Chunming Wei, Bo Zhu, Chao Xu, Long Tan, Zhongzhi Qu, Jizheng Yang, Siming Gao, Ruihui Han
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The long-distance power cable features a cross-linked metal sheath. This design effectively eliminates induced voltage on the sheath but creates significant challenges for locating cable insulation faults. This paper proposes a two-terminal voltage and current measurement method, which detects instantaneous voltage and current at both cable ends and uses the known cable length during normal operation. Using these parameters, the wave impedance and propagation coefficient can be calculated. The short-circuit fault location is then determined by substituting these values into the calculation formula. The principle, formula derivation, and theoretical verification are described. MATLAB was used to simulate an equivalent short circuit for a three-phase cable. Simulation results for wave impedance and propagation coefficient are accurate and align more closely with actual values than traditional methods. The calculated short-circuit fault distance closely matches real values and can locate various short-circuit fault distances. The method is minimally affected by synchronous errors and frequency fluctuations in the power system. This paper demonstrates the validity and feasibility of the two-terminal voltage and current measurement method for any cable insulation fault connection. This method is significant for ensuring cable operation safety.

title: An Optimized Hybrid Learning Model for Degradation Prediction of Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells
authors: Bochen Li, Yicheng Liu, Hao Chen, Chao Jia
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) are increasingly deployed in modern power systems as clean and efficient energy conversion devices. They undergo performance degradation over time, which poses a critical challenge to their reliability and operational lifespan. This paper proposes a predictive framework that integrates a hybrid deep learning model with Northern Goshawk Optimization (NGO) for accurate degradation prediction of PEMFCs. The proposed model combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) for local feature extraction, a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM) for capturing temporal dependencies, and an attention mechanism to emphasize critical degradation patterns. The NGO algorithm is designed to optimize key hyperparameters through its efficient global search capability, thereby enhancing the model’s predictive performance. The effectiveness of the proposed model is quantitatively assessed using multiple evaluation indicators. The comparative results demonstrate that the proposed model significantly outperforms the baseline CNN-BiLSTM-Attention and BiLSTM models. By enabling accurate degradation prediction of PEMFCs, this study facilitates proactive maintenance strategies and supports enhanced operational reliability of PEMFCs.

title: Global-Local Contrastive Graph Transformer for Node Classification
authors: Gaichao Li, Jinsong Chen, Kun He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Graph Transformers have shown promising performance in node classification tasks. Rather than using the entire graph as input, recent approaches focus on constructing token sequences for target nodes, enhancing scalability and reducing noise in large graphs. However, these token sequence-based graph Transformers have three key limitations: (1) limited ability to capture global information while missing fine-grained insights from distant but relevant nodes; (2) insufficient consideration of the interplay between topology and attributes in local context sampling; (3) potential interference caused by mixing different token types within the same sequence. To address these challenges, we propose a novel global-local contrastive graph Transformer termed GLFormer, which learns node representations more comprehensively by integrating global and local graph information. From the global perspective, we leverage graph hierarchies to build layer-wise global sequences that capture coarse-to-fine global information. From the local perspective, we construct local sequences by sampling nodes from a weighted graph derived from attribute similarity, effectively preserving both topological and attributed information. Additionally, we introduce a global-local alignment loss, which aligns node representations learned from different token sequences, further aiding the expressiveness of target node representation. Empirical results demonstrate that GLFormer consistently outperforms mainstream baselines across 13 widely-used graph benchmarks.

title: Mad or Impossible to be Mad? Rethinking Load Manipulation Threats in Renewable-Integrated Power Grids and Defenses
authors: Zhouhao Ji, Kaikai Pan, Xiaoyu Ji, Wenyuan Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The increasing penetration of renewable distributed energy resources (DERs) in power grids is reshaping its security. While DERs expand the attack surface, they also provide enhanced protection and flexibility. This motivates us to reconsider the security landscape of the DER-dominated grid under a novel threat called manipulation of load and renewables (ToLaR). It represents a two-sided threat that extends beyond conventional MadIoT by jointly leveraging load-side and DER-side dynamics. To evaluate the threat, we develop an analyzer in which we propose an event-triggered model to characterize the attack variability (e.g., attack sequence, grid evolution) and incorporate it into state-of-the-art simulation tools. Evaluations on six benchmark systems and a physical microgrid testbed demonstrate that the DER-dominated grid shows longer collapse latency and smaller transient fluctuations under ToLaR. However, ToLaR achieves comparable cascading impacts with only 10% of the attack cost required by MadIoT, and by manipulating the fast frequency response (FFR) of energy storage systems, the transient grid frequency can be driven down to 53 Hz, triggering severe instability. These results reveal that although DER-dominated grids exhibit improved resilience, their control mechanisms (e.g., FFR) introduce new high-impact vulnerabilities. We aim to raise awareness of emerging security challenges in renewable-integrated power grids as they shape new attack vectors and complicate defense strategies. Finally, we propose countermeasures from the perspectives of the power grid, the network system, and attack detection.

title: Optical Degradation of 845 nm VCSELs with Different Oxide Apertures for Silicon Photonics
authors: Michele Zenari, Matteo Buffolo, Filippo Perale, Carlo De Santi, Jeroen Goyvaerts, Alexander Grabowski, Johan Gustavsson, Roel Baets, Günther Roelkens, Gaudenzio Meneghesso, Enrico Zanoni, Matteo Meneghini
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: In this work, we analyzed the optical degradation of 845 nm VCSELs designed for silicon photonics (SiPh) applications as a function of the oxide aperture. First, we investigated the optical degradation in relation to the stress current. The experimental results showed that devices with a larger oxide aperture exhibit better reliability. From the analysis of the degradation kinetics, we found that the current at which the highly accelerated degradation process occurs depends inversely on the aperture radius. This result was explained by showing that devices with a larger aperture have lower thermal impedance, and therefore operate at lower internal temperatures at similar bias points. This interpretation is supported by isothermal constant current stress tests, which show that when the same internal temperature is maintained during ageing, for all VCSEL geometries under study the onset of degradation occurs at the same time. The equivalent activation energy of the degradation process was found to fall between 0.43 and 0.68 eV. Our analysis demonstrated that using a larger aperture can improve device reliability.

title: Fully Distributed Fog Load Balancing with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Maad Ebrahim, Abdelhakim Hafid
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Distributed fog computing environments demand efficient resource management to support real-time Internet of Things (IoT) applications. This paper proposes a fully distributed load-balancing framework based on multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), where independent agents learn to manage heterogeneous fog resources without centralized control or inter-agent coordination. The agents jointly optimize a global objective that minimizes workload waiting delay (reduces overall fog queue accumulation) while ensuring fair resource utilization. We evaluated agents’ dynamic adaptation to unpredictable load bursts through transfer learning in simulated fog environments with heterogeneous, unbalanced, and geographically distributed fog nodes. Compared to centralized RL, learning localized policies within smaller collaboration regions allows our distributed agents to achieve superior performance (up to 70.1% reduction in average waiting delay), reduces state-action space, accelerates convergence (6× faster), and scales efficiently as the network grows. In addition, we analyze the impact of realistic interval-based state observation (using a protocol like Gossip) to evaluate the trade-off between performance and practical deployment constraints. Compared to the unrealistic assumption of real-time state availability before every decision, a Gossip interval of 3 seconds in our simulations reduces the state observation overhead by a factor of 9.5×, ensuring the solution is viable for real-world deployment.

title: Integrating Earth Observation and Digital Tools for Agricultural Mapping and Monitoring: The Mozambique Case Study
authors: Claudia Paris, Rolf A. de By, Robert Ohuru, Mehmet Furkan Çelik, Florian J. Ellsäßer, Gabriel Tseng, Yawen Zhang, Sosdito Estevão Mananze, Lourenço Manuel, Jawoo Koo, Andrew Nelson
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate and timely agricultural data are vital in low- and middle-income countries for effective national planning, informed food policy decisions, and progress toward achieving sustainable development goal (SDG) 2 (zero hunger). Annual field surveys are costly, time-consuming, hampered by weak infrastructure, and vulnerable to extreme weather. This article presents the architecture developed in the statistics from space project, which integrates Earth observation (EO) data, digital tools for field-data collection, and a cloud-based aggregation and visualization platform to propose a cost-effective solution for efficient agricultural monitoring. The workflow was tested in Mozambique and includes: first, an EO-based sampling-frame methodology for heterogeneous agro-landscapes, second, an open-source smartphone application for efficient, geo-referenced field data collection, third, a data-aggregation and visualization system for real-time quality monitoring and national-level dissemination, and fourth, automated methods for EO-based crop type mapping. The project delivered a comprehensive framework for integrating EO and digital technologies into efficient and scalable workflows. High-resolution crop type maps (openly accessible) and statistics were generated for the main crops in three pilot provinces, Gaza, Manica, and Zambezia, for the 2024/25 season.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Prediction Method for Manufacturing Tolerances in Multilayer Circuit
authors: Weihong Liu, Shuai Zhang, Zhiyuan Qu, Miao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: Manufacturing tolerances (MTs) have a significant impact on the transmission characteristics of millimeter-wave circuits. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to obtain a cost-effective strategy for predicting MTs. This letter proposes a method that combines the vector network analyzer (VNA) with machine learning (ML) to estimate the MTs in multilayer liquid crystal polymer (LCP) circuits. First, an artificial neural network (ANN) surrogate model is trained to map geometric parameters to electromagnetic (EM) responses using a sampled dataset. Subsequently, the S-parameters measured by the VNA are used as the target function of the genetic algorithm (GA) to inversely determine the MTs of fabricated circuit while ensuring its structural integrity. Experimental validation demonstrates that the prediction percentage error of key geometric parameters is below 10%.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)

title: IFCoT: Insights Fusion Chain of Thought for Multimodal Reasoning in LLMs
authors: Jiaxiang Liu, Jiawei Du, Tianxiang Hu, Linjie Tong, Yang Feng, Joey Tianyi Zhou, Mingkun Xu, Zuozhu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Multimodal reasoning akin to human capability has been a long-term goal in AI for decades. Existing works typically address reasoning tasks using enhanced large language models (LLMs) with multi-step reasoning (e.g., Chain-of-Thought, CoT) along a single reasoning path, which either relies on labor-intensive annotations or remains vulnerable to data bias and hallucination. In this paper, we propose Insights Fusion CoT (IFCoT), a novel multimodal reasoning framework that fuses insights from three distinct yet complementary thinking paths: the forward path, the reverse path, and the data-driven path. The forward and reverse paths aim to generate multi-step rationales with LLMs inspired by human reasoning strategies in cognitive psychology. In particular, the forward path generates rationales by directly inferring the answer based on question descriptions, while the reverse path generates rationales by indirectly eliminating wrong answers. The reasoning capability is further enhanced via the data-driven path, which is supervised with annotated rationales. IFCoT synergistically integrates knowledge from LLMs and data from annotated rationales, representing a novel paradigm for multimodal reasoning. Extensive experiments and theoretical analysis show that IFCoT can not only outperform state-of-the-art methods substantially, but also surpass human-level performance on a variety of multimodal visual question-answering tasks, suggesting the great potential of LLMs for machine reasoning. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/JXLiu-AI/IFCoT.

title: Joint Semantic and Coded Generation for Conditional Latent Coding
authors: Siqi Wu, Weiming Chen, Yinda Chen, Dong Liu, K. C. Ho, Zhihai He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Deep image compression and text-to-image generation represent two distinct paradigms in visual representation learning: one focuses on coded representations, while the other emphasizes semantic representations. This paper bridges this gap within a unified framework that integrates image generation and compression. We propose Joint Semantic and Coded Generation for Conditional Latent Coding (JSCCL), which combines minimal coded features with semantic descriptions from vision-language models to guide a controllable rectified flow model. This joint guidance enables high-fidelity reference generation capturing both semantic coherence and structural details. Through iterative alignment in the latent domain, we synthesize conditional priors for efficient transformer-based encoding and decoding. Experimental results on Kodak, CLIC, and Tecnick datasets demonstrate up to 15.8% BD-rate improvement over VVC with only 0.01 bpp overhead. Unlike existing generative compression methods approximating inputs solely at the semantic level, our approach achieves both semantic understanding and pixel-level reconstruction.

title: Nonlinear System Identification Through Amplitude-Phase-Frequency Characterization
authors: Xiaojing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: Based on expanded memory polynomial (EMP) and orthogonal EMP (OEMP), this paper addresses the nonlinear system identification (NSI) problem by introducing an amplitudephase- frequency (APF) characterization framework to enable performance measurability, monotonicity, and optimality. APF characteristic functions (CFs) are defined to better capture the nonlinear distortions arising from both the amplitude and phase of the input signal. It is shown that an APF CF can be expressed as a weighted sum of characteristic basis functions (CBFs) or their orthogonal counterparts (OCBFs), with the associated CBF or OCBF frequency responses directly linked to the EMP or OEMP coefficients. As a result, NSI can be carried out by alternatively estimating the CBF or OCBF frequency responses. The paper also presents detailed estimation methods employing frequency modulated continuous wave (FMCW) test signals and analyzes different mean-squared-error (MSE) measurement strategies. Simulation and experimental results using a 73.5 GHz carrier frequency and 2.125 GHz bandwidth millimeter wave communication system validate the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed technique.

title: On the Uncertainty Quantification Ability of Tabular Foundation Models
authors: Tyler R. Johnson, Kian Ben-Jacob, Nima Negarandeh, Oriol Vendrell-Gallart, Ramin Bostanabad
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: Foundation models (FMs) have achieved substantial success in generalizing across tasks without problem-specific training or fine-tuning. However, many critical applications in mechanics and computational science require not only accurate predictions but also reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ). Herein we investigate the UQ capabilities of tabular FMs in regression tasks through a comprehensive empirical study comparing Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TabPFN) against Gaussian processes (GPs). We systematically evaluate these two methods across a host of regression problems with varying complexity, dataset sizes, and input dimensionalities. We use a default setting to build all the GPs and for a fair comparison against TabPFN v2.5. Our findings highlight an important trade-off between explicit and learned priors: while TabPFN achieves highly competitive performance for complex, high-dimensional problems with sufficient data, GPs often provide superior predictive accuracy and UQ in data-scarce settings. Moreover, when the chosen kernel constitutes a good prior for the underlying function, GP performance can substantially exceed that of TabPFN. Our results can be reproduced from <ext-link ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://github.com/kianswarehouse/GPvsPFN" xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/kianswarehouse/GPvsPFN</ext-link>.
