title: Generative AI in FDM and FEM Numerical Methods: Prompting, Validation, and Competency-Based Learning in Engineering Education
authors: Adrian Isrrael Tec Chim, José M. Nieto-Jalil, Jorge Alvarez, David I. Ibarra-Zarate, Juan Manuel Martínez Huerta
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The teaching of numerical methods is essential in engineering, yet their complexity and the heterogeneity of learning curves hinder mastery, particularly when comparing the Finite Element Method (FEM) with the Finite Difference Method (FDM). To address this challenge, we integrated generative artificial intelligence (AI) as a pedagogical scaffold in a MATLABbased course with two equivalent groups: FDM (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{2 8}$</tex>) and FEM (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{n}=30$</tex>), both under the same AI usage protocol and assessment criteria. Over five weeks, we measured accuracy (L2 error), efficiency (time), prompting quality, robustness, documentation, transfer, and AI hallucinations. Mixed-model analyses showed significant reductions in error (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta^{2}=0.17, \mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>) and time (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta^{2}=0.14, p<0.001$</tex>); slopes were steeper for FDM, while FEM exhibited consistent improvements under higher cognitive load. Prompting quality emerged as a predictor of performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\rho=-0.41, \quad p<0.05)$</tex>. The competency radar revealed complementary profiles; heatmaps showed decreasing hallucination frequency through verification and validation; and the forest plot identified prompting (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\beta} \approx-\mathbf{0. 2 8}$</tex>) and systematic validation (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{\approx} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{0. 2 2}$</tex>) as key factors. We conclude that generative AI, when responsibly applied, accelerates, stabilizes, and diversifies numerical learning, strengthens the instructor's role, and offers a replicable model aligned with Tec21/ABET frameworks and SDG 4: Quality Education.

title: Lightweight Landmark-Based Gesture Recognition System for Romanian Sign Language
authors: Andrei Enachi, Corneliu Octavian Turcu, George Culea, Dragos Alexandru Andrioaia, Andrei Gabriel Ungureanu, Bogdan Constantin Sghera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents a lightweight computer vision system designed for real-time recognition of hand gestures in Romanian Sign Language. The proposed approach focuses on extracting spatial hand landmarks using the MediaPipe framework and converting them into normalized feature vectors suitable for gesture classification. Unlike traditional image-based gesture recognition methods that rely on raw input data, the proposed system operates on a compact representation consisting of 21 hand landmarks, reducing computational complexity while preserving gesture information. A custom gesture dataset was collected using a standard RGB camera under different illumination conditions and signer variations. Multiple gestures were recorded representing commonly used signs with different hand shapes and configurations. The gesture classification stage is implemented using neural network-based models trained on hand landmark feature vectors. Experimental results indicate that the proposed system achieves high recognition accuracy while maintaining real-time performance. The obtained results suggest the feasibility of lightweight landmark-based gesture recognition systems for assistive communication applications.

title: A Spatiotemporal Graph Autoencoder for Early Freeway Traffic Anomaly Detection
authors: Aminreza Najafinia, Zahed Rahmati
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Freeway traffic anomalies, such as accidents or sudden slowdowns, require rapid detection to improve safety and reduce congestion. This study uses a graph-based method to detect lane-level traffic anomalies on freeways at an early stage of development. Using a new high-resolution lane-level traffic dataset with sensor data and delayed incident reports, we model the freeway network as a spatiotemporal graph in which nodes represent lanes, and edges capture spatial and temporal dependencies. We adopted the FT-AED baseline graph topology and centered the novelty on a GTG-TGCN autoencoder that models spatiotemporal sequences to detect deviations from normal traffic patterns. Experiments on real-world data show that modeling the spatial relationships between lanes improves the detection performance, whereas ignoring them reduces the accuracy. Compared with prior architectures on the FT-AED benchmark, our model increases AUC by about 3% and achieves more stable lead times with lower delay variability. The proposed model detects approximately 75% of recorded crashes and flags incidents over 10 min earlier than official reports, demonstrating the potential of graph-based models for proactive traffic-incident management.

title: Practical Solutions for Integrating Generative AI into Modern Engineering Education
authors: Khaled Shaaban
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The integration of generative AI (GenAI) is rapidly transforming pedagogical practices in engineering education. This work explores practical innovative applications where GenAI moves beyond a tool to become an asset in the engineering classroom. Different distinct use-cases are discussed, organized into four primary categories: personalized teaching assistant, interactive problem generator, code explanation and debugging partner, and simulator of professional engineering scenarios. These methods demonstrate that GenAI can significantly enhance personalized learning, foster critical thinking and algorithmic reasoning, and bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application. While acknowledging challenges such as academic integrity and model hallucinations, these methods suggest that the strategic implementation of GenAI holds the potential to create more dynamic, efficient, and deeply engaging educational experiences, ultimately preparing students for the complexities of modern engineering practice. This integration also empowers instructors to focus on advanced concepts and critical thinking, fundamentally enhancing how future engineers are educated.

title: Impact of Tool Front End Airflow Dynamics on Outgassing Defects Affecting Product yield in Advanced Semiconductor Fabrication
authors: Varun Subramanian, Pete Pusamlidlert, Thien Lai, Kenneth Tinsley
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Airborne Molecular Contaminants (AMCs) pose considerable challenges in modern semiconductor manufacturing, particularly as technological advancements move towards the nanoscale. This paper presents a case study examining the effects of tool front-end airflow dynamics on Airborne Molecular Contaminants (AMCs) and their subsequent impact on the yield of 20nm DRAM products during the contact etch process. Yield impact was observed on atypical wafer slot positions, which correlated with Front Opening Unified Pod (FOUP) loadport locations and tool front-end airflow variations. Identifying this unique failure mode led to targeted preventive actions, such as tool configuration optimization and improved maintenance procedures. An automated AMC sampling and inline SPC chart monitoring method was developed to enable real-time AMC detection.

title: Evaluating User Experience and Cognitive Load in AR-supported STEM Education
authors: Andrei Braicov, Tatiana Veverița, Mihail Terenti, Ivan Budanaev
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Augmented reality can create interactive and immersive STEM learning experiences. This study explored user experience and cognitive load using the Merge EDU platform, which employs a tangible Merge Cube to manipulate 3D objects. An exploratory study involved 13 middle school students and 11 STEM teachers performing AR-based tasks. Participants completed the NASA-TLX and UMUX questionnaires and participated in informal interviews. Results show that students experienced low TLX and high usability, while teachers reported higher workload and lower usability. A negative correlation between TLX and UX was observed for students, but not for teachers. Qualitative feedback indicated that students valued the interactive and exploratory aspects of the tool, whereas teachers focused on pedagogical integration and practical classroom use. The findings suggest that AR applications can effectively engage students and support learning with minimal cognitive effort, while teachers may benefit from additional training to optimize their experience.

title: A Multimodal Deep Learning Framework for Continuous Grasp Score Prediction from 2D Images
authors: Shayan Ahmadi, Amir Arsalan Nematollahi, Mehdi Tale Masouleh, Ahmad Kalhor
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Predicting the physical stability of a robotic grasp from 2D visual data is a significant challenge. This paper presents a deep learning framework to regress a continuous grasp quality score by processing both visual and geometric data. A multimodal network, featuring a pre-trained ResNet-18 backbone and a dedicated MLP for encoding grasp parameters, is trained on a diverse subset of Dex-Net dataset where grasp quality is quantified by object stability. The grasp candidates for this dataset were generated using a geometric-based algorithm. To ensure robustness to label noise and effectively leverage transfer learning, a two-stage co-teaching training regimen is utilized. While the model achieves a Mean Absolute Error of 0.0922, its practical utility is best demonstrated through a classification-style analysis. By discretizing scores into operational categories, the model achieves a tolerant accuracy of 89% on an unseen test set. The model’s effectiveness and successful sim-to-real transfer are validated on a physical Delta parallel robot, where predicted scores correlate strongly with real-world grasp outcomes.

title: Implementation of Securing Text Data Using Steganography and Cryptographic Techniques
authors: Sukshma Shetty, Sowmya K, Shreejith D Shetty, Sunil, Vinay Poojari S, Deepak Shenoy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Contemporary conditions call for an effective means of protecting sensitive text information from being intercepted and subsequently analyzed due to the increasingly heavy use of digital communications. Primarily, the efficiency of implementing only one security mechanism of encryption or steganographic embedding often becomes poor under modern network surveillance tools and steganalysis software and techniques. This article proposes the concept of the two-layer security scheme utilizing AES-256 encryption, SHA-256 for generating the key, and the BiLSB image embedding method. In the first layer of security, the provided passphrase is hashed to produce a 256 -bit key via the SHA-256 hashing function, coupled with AES-256 encryption of the text that would render it unreadable even if intercepted by an intruder. Afterwards, an encrypted data output is subsequently hidden inside an image file by employing the concept of mindless two-bit LSB replacement, thus ensuring that the image is remaining untouched with no apparent changes. A prototype is developed with the objective of verifying the efficiency of the encryption and key procedures and image quality. It was found that due to distinct initialization vectors, there is always a different output from all encryption routines with the; hence, it limits the risk for pattern-based attacks. Incorrect keys, modified ciphertext, and malformed input data have been consistently rejected by the system; integrity safeguards are very strong. The Bi-LSB method also maintained the visual quality of the carrier images while secretly concealing the encrypted text. Security analysis indicated protection levels of about 95% for AES-256, 90% for bcrypt, 85% for SHA-256, and 80% for Bi-LSB can be attained. In general, the combined framework provides a practical and covert approach for transmitting sensitive text in environments where confidentiality and invisibility are critical.

title: Educational Innovation Challenges in the EU-BEGP Consortium: Challenge-Based Learning Experiences in Bolivia, Ecuador, Guatemala Y Perú
authors: Agatha Clarice da Silva Ovando, Claudia María Taracena Parada, Eliana Acurio, Juan M. Rodriguez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper examines the design and early implementation of Challenge-Based Learning (CBL) across the Erasmus+ EU-BEGP consortium in Bolivia, Ecuador, Guatemala, and Peru. Building on the EXPLORE Energy Digital Academy (EEDA) and the Learnify platform, partners co-created modular, Creative Commons resources to support flipped classrooms, remote labs, and peer-reviewed quality assurance. We integrate CBL with Design Thinking, SCAMPER, and Six Thinking Hats to guide students from empathetic problem framing to prototyping and dissemination. Nine locally grounded challenges, spanning electric last-mile logistics, self-sustaining communities, renewable energy design, and sustainable ephemeral construction, connect curricula to real stakeholders and the SDGs. We report consortium-level outputs (shared modules, planners, and QIP reviews) and four illustrative cases that demonstrate gains in technical competence, teamwork, and reflective practice. Findings highlight how a lightweight, modular ecosystem can align transnational actors while preserving local relevance, offering a replicable pathway for modernizing energy-and-sustainability education in Latin America, with measurable learning improvements.

title: Design of an IDS for Detection of Adversarial Attacks in IoT Using Machine Learning
authors: Sachit Kumar Nayak, Ashalata Panigrahi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: As the Internet of Things (IoT) expands, the security of IoT networks has becoming more challenging. To address the security issues faced by the IoT network this work proposes metaheuristic optimization techniques namely, Elephant herding optimization (EHO) and Sequential Hybrid of Elephant Herding Optimization and Simulated Annealing (EHO+SA) to select an optimal subset of features that maximize a specific objective function. We use oversampling technique to avoid over-fitting and under-fitting issues that results in biased classification. Two classification techniques namely, deep forest (DF) and deep neural network (DNN) have been implemented for multiclass and binary classification. For evaluation of the model CIC-IoT 2023 dataset is used. The proposed approach reports Oversampling + EHO + DF + Brute-force attack achieves highest accuracy of 0.9999, precision of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 0}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F}$</tex>-value of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 9 9 7}$</tex> and lowest FAR of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0}$</tex>. Oversampling + EHO+SA + DF + DoS reports highest recall of 1.0. Oversampling + EHO + SA + DF + DoS reported highest NPV of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 0}$</tex> and lowest FNR of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0}$</tex>. For binary classification oversampling + EHO+ Deep Forest reports highest accuracy of 0.9944, recall of 0.9962, precision of 0.9977, specificity of 0.9672, F-value of 0.9969, and lowest FAR of 0.0328.

title: Diluted N2O SAB to stabilize P+ Polysilicon Resistor Performance
authors: Chiam Chek Chou, Ker Peng Lau, Kenneth Chih Yuang Mah, Adukkadukkam Dineshan, Chuah Koh, Jun Xie
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In this report, we address the instability and fluctuating sheet resistance (Rs) of unsilicided P+ polysilicon resistors. Through a detailed study of the salicide block (SAB) film properties—identified as a major contributor to this instability— we established a strong correlation between the film's refractive index (RI) and the Rs of the resistors. Experiments were conducted using PECVD SAB oxide deposition, focusing on process optimization to adjust deposition conditions and film characteristics. As a result, we achieved significantly narrower Rs distribution, with overall lot-to-lot variation reduced by 80%. The underlying mechanism contributing to this improvement is also discussed.

title: Multi-Physics Modeling and Cross-Scale Nonlinear Dynamic Response Quantification of Multi-Energy Coupling Systems for Green Power Chemical Industrial Park
authors: Chenglong Wang, Shibo Wang, Nan Wang, Peng Yu, Shumin Sun, Yan Cheng
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Green power chemical industrial parks represent a critical scenario for high-proportion renewable energy consumption and low-carbon transformation in high-emission industries. Addressing challenges in the park's multi-energy coupling system — encompassing wind/PV power, electrolytic hydrogen production, and chemical processes, such as random fluctuations at the source end, step disturbances at the load end, and strong cross-scale coupling between electricity, heat, and gas, this study conducts modeling and dynamic response quantification research. A nonlinear state-space model incorporating PEM electrolyzer polarization, thermal equilibrium, and multi-energy-flow interactions was constructed to analyze multi-timescale coupling mechanisms. A disturbance quantification method based on dynamic sensitivity and normalized variance ratio was proposed. Simulations demonstrate the model's capability to reproduce millisecond-scale electrical, second-scale transport, and minute-scale thermal evolution. The system exhibits a "low-pass filtering" effect due to the integrative effects of thermal inertia and gas storage. However, it exhibits inertial delay in responding to chemical load steps, with pressure recovery taking minutes. This research provides support for the safe, stable operation and flexible resource allocation of industrial parks.

title: Ultrasound-based Detection of Polycystic Ovary Morphology using YOLO26 on Custom Datasets
authors: Marinela Leampar, Iulian B. Ciocoiu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS), the leading hormonal imbalance in women of childbearing age, is commonly diagnosed through manual ultrasound interpretation, a process prone to operator-dependent variability. This paper presents an automated approach for detecting polycystic ovary morphology (PCOM) using YOLO26 architecture, trained on a custom dataset of 4071 clinically validated ultrasound images. Among the evaluated variants, YOLO26s achieved the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 0.9314, an F1-score of 0.8596, and a ROC-AUC of 0.9812. A comparative analysis against prior YOLO models showed that YOLO26s outperforms those, while an ablation study revealed an optimal input resolution of 416×416 pixels. The results show that YOLO26 is a promising and reliable tool for automated PCOM detection and could help support more consistent PCOS screening in clinical practice.

title: Research Trends in Cloud Computing-Based Signal Processing: A Bibliometric Analysis
authors: Kamyab Azizi, Fatemeh Aminizade, Elaheh Shafiei, Hasti Mirshams, Hossein Shahbodaghkhan, Mohammad Hossein Alizadeh Roknabadi, Farzaneh Mohammadi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Cloud computing now enables simultaneous, scalable, and cost-efficient signal analysis. To map progress in this interdisciplinary area, this study aims to conduct a comprehensive bibliometric analysis to explore research trends, collaboration networks, main contributors, and interdisciplinary and key research themes in cloud computing-based signal processing. A total of 5,269 published papers were extracted from the Web of Science database and analyzed using VOSviewer with specific thresholds for keyword co-occurrence and author collaboration. The study utilizes a quantitative bibliometric methodology to map the field's transition from traditional signal processing to AI-driven cloud architectures. The results show a growing trend in publications, as there has been a notable increase since 2010, with peaks in 2022 and 2024. Based on keyword co-occurrence analysis, core research themes are (i) remote sensing and atmospheric themes; (ii) algorithmic and learning-based processing; (iii) network and communication optimization; and (iv) IoT and data management. The results also reveal a varied distribution of contributors, including leading institutions such as the Jet Propulsion Laboratory and pioneering countries such as China and the United States. In addition, Electrical and Electronic Engineering is the key research category, and IEEE plays a main role in publishing papers in this field. Future research can be directed by considering the emerging trends revealed in this bibliometric analysis.

title: Multi-Task Transformer Network for Active Power Support Capability Mapping of Wind Turbine
authors: Shenkai Pan, Bingtuan Gao, Hairong Luo, Xutao Li, Yong Ren
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of wind power, accurately assessing the active power support capability of wind turbines is crucial for grid stability management. However, these quantities depend nonlinearly on multiple operational and control parameters, making their online estimation challenging. This paper proposes a multi-task Transformer network that maps five key inputs—wind speed, pitch angle, rotor speed, inertia time constant, and droop coefficient—to two critical outputs: active power reserve capability and active power regulation rate. The model treats inputs as a sequence, applies learnable positional encoding, and employs a class token for global feature aggregation, followed by a Transformer encoder with multi-head self-attention to capture high-order feature interactions. Two regression heads jointly estimate both targets within a multi-task learning framework. Using a synthetically generated dataset of 20,000 samples based on realistic turbine parameters, the proposed model achieves capability RMSE of 0.021 and rate RMSE of 0.0008, outperforming MLP, CNN, and single-task Transformer. Ablation studies reveal that removing the auxiliary task increases rate RMSE to 0.20, while omitting the class token or positional encoding degrades capability RMSE to 0.045 and 0.0043, respectively, validating the contribution of each component. The proposed model provides an accurate and interpretable tool for real-time assessment of wind turbine active support capabilities.

title: New "Array-Only SRAM" (AOS) Test Structure for Yield Learning
authors: Matthew Deming, Carl Radens, Alberto Cestero, Steve Lucarini, Chris Sheraw, Julien Frougier, Zhigang Song, Steve Crown, Qianheng Du, Michael Gribelyuk, James Demarest, Victor Chan, Chris Murphy, Sean Teehan, Ishtiaq Ahsan
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Functional SRAM arrays are frequently used as yield learning vehicles for semiconductor technology development [1]. However, the complexity of the peripheral circuitry in a traditional SRAM (e.g. address decoder, write driver, sense amp, IO circuits, redundancy control) may limit bitcell array access. When peripheral circuity fails, it restricts the ability to extract and analyze array bit-fail maps, thereby preventing electrical fault isolation, failure analysis, and technology yield learning. In this paper, we present an SRAM test structure called "Array-Only SRAM" (AOS) which does not utilize peripheral circuitry for use as an early technology yield learning vehicle.

title: Analysis of Ultra-Low-Frequency Oscillation in Generating Units Induced by Stochastic Fluctuations of Wind Power Output
authors: Yixin Xian, Fengfeng Zhou, Qihui Yan, Wenlong Lu, Yiping Yu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The output of adjacent high-penetration wind power exhibits significant stochastic fluctuations, with power variations predominantly concentrated in the ultra-low-frequency band, thereby posing a risk of inducing general forced ULFOs in hydropower units. This paper first elucidates the mechanism of ULFO in hydropower units, detailing its frequency characteristics and primary influencing factors. It demonstrates that the coupling of governor system parameters—specifically proportional and integral gains—results in inherent ULFO modes, which can be triggered under specific operating conditions. Secondly, the frequency-domain characteristics of stochastic wind power fluctuations are analyzed, followed by an investigation into the mechanism of general forced ULFOs. A frequency-domain derivation verifies that stochastic disturbances can excite the system's weak damping modes, thereby providing a theoretical basis for the induction of ULFOs in hydropower systems by wind power fluctuations. Finally, the general forced ULFO phenomenon is verified through simulations based on a transmission model of an integrated hydro-wind base. The results indicate that stochastic fluctuations in wind power output consistently excite the system's ULFO modes, with the response being most pronounced near the system's resonant frequency.

title: Extending Transportation Digital twins With Behavioral Dynamics: Linking Traffic Operations and Activity-Based Demand
authors: Milad Ziya, Ondřej Přibyl
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Digital-twin (DT) platforms are increasingly deployed in transport systems to support real-time monitoring and smart-city mobility decision support through a continuous alignment between observed data and simulation, forming a Digital Shadow. However, a critical gap exists in the evaluation of mobility policies: the inference step required to interpret discrepancies between the physical transport system and its virtual replica. This paper proposes a dual-loop DT architecture that decouples a fast operational loop (Digital Shadow) for state estimation and dynamic traffic assignment (DTA) from a slow learning loop (Travel Behavior DT) for strategic demand adaptation. Central to this framework is an interpretation/inference layer that performs evidence-driven discrepancy diagnosis to disambiguate supply-side disruptions from demand-side behavioral shifts using state/LOS signatures. The behavioral loop integrates multi-source streams such as GPS traces, smartcard records, and app logs to update activity-based demand representations via accessibility/utility metrics (logsums) and MATSim-compatible plan adaptation. Furthermore, the architecture incorporates a Decision Support System (DSS) that accounts for traveler perception and response to information and incentives. This blueprint provides a defensible and socially responsive framework for smart-city mobility governance and the evaluation of complex mobility policies within digital-twin environments.

title: From Enrollment to Persistence Intention: Factors Shaping Engineering Students' Online Experience
authors: Edgardo Fuentes-Caceres, Maria Elena Truyol, Monica Quezada-Espinoza
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This study examines predictors of students' Behavioral Intention (BI) to enroll and continue in a fully online Computer Engineering program in Chile using a UTAUT2based survey <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{N}=65)$</tex>. Analyses used descriptive statistics, Spearman correlations, Mann-Whitney U tests, and multiple regression with diagnostic checks and BCa bootstrap confidence intervals (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5, 0 0 0}$</tex> resamples). Perceptions were generally high. BI correlated most strongly with Habit, Performance Expectancy, and Facilitating Conditions. In the baseline UTAUT model, Performance Expectancy was the only reliable unique predictor of BI. In an alternative specification including Habit and Attitude, model fit improved modestly and Habit showed the largest point estimate, though bootstrap intervals indicated a marginal unique effect. Exploratory subgroup comparisons suggested higher Social Influence and Facilitating Conditions among students financing their studies with their own income. Implications include making task value explicit, providing early visible support, and using predictable course routines.

title: Study on Combined Measurement of Leakage Current and Partial Discharge in DC XLPE Insulated Cables
authors: Tianyu Yang, Hong Zhou, Jialiang Yuan, Shuhan Wang, Yihan Zhou, Zhe Zheng
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This study investigates the leakage current and partial discharge characteristics of a cross-linked polyethylene DC cable with a micro-void defect through combined experimental measurements. A stepwise voltage method was applied under both positive and negative polarities, and the leakage current and PD signals were simultaneously recorded under high-temperature and constant-temperature conditions. The results show that the leakage current exhibits a transition from linear to nonlinear growth around 45 kV. In the low-field region, conduction is dominated by ohmic behavior, while in the high-field region, nonlinear mechanisms including Schottky injection, the Poole–Frenkel effect, and space-charge-limited conduction jointly contribute to the rapid increase. Regarding PD characteristics, the discharge frequency increases significantly with rising voltage, whereas the discharge magnitude remains within 30–40 pC, indicating insensitivity to voltage. Both leakage current and PD exhibit clear polarity dependence: under negative polarity, higher leakage current and discharge frequency are observed, along with a lower inception voltage. Correlation analysis further reveals a strong positive relationship between leakage current and both discharge frequency and total discharge charge, highlighting the tight coupling between conduction and PD processes.

title: Design and Implementation of a 20kW Three-Phase Bridge Rectifier Utilizing Paralleled Discrete SiC MOSFETs
authors: Zhuwen Han, Jie Ding, Ling Fang, Jiaming Zheng, Haokai Zhao, Jin Xie
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a 20kW three-phase full-bridge bridge rectifier based on paralleled discrete SiC MOSFETs. To overcome the challenges of current imbalance and parasitic oscillations inherent in high-frequency (48 kHz) paralleled SiC devices, a systematic hardware design methodology is proposed. This includes a stringent threshold voltage V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gs(th)</inf> screening process, symmetrical PCB layout optimization, and an independent gate-drive resistor configuration. Furthermore, a common heat sink strategy is employed to leverage the positive temperature coefficient of SiC devices for self-balanced thermal distribution. Experimental results on a 20kW prototype demonstrate nearunity power factor and high-quality input current waveforms, proving that the proposed discrete paralleling approach is a cost-effective and reliable solution for high-power charging and discharging applications.

title: The Challenge of Improving Critical and Scientific Thinking in Engineering Students Through RAG Systems Based on Validated Scientific Corpora
authors: Mariana Olivares Avalos, Ricardo Chaparro-Sánchez, Alexandro Escudero-Nahón
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Developing critical and scientific thinking in engineering students remains a persistent challenge in higher education. Traditional teaching practices in mathematics and physics often prioritize procedural knowledge over analytical reasoning, leading to limited reflection, superficial understanding, and low levels of engagement. This research-in-progress doctoral study explores the potential of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems-artificial intelligence models supported by validated scientific corpora-to enhance critical and scientific thinking in firstand second-year engineering students. Based on constructivist and deep learning theories, the study proposes a mixed-method quasi-experimental design that integrates technological mediation, ethical literacy, and active learning strategies. This paper presents the theoretical framework, research design, preliminary observations from pilot experiences, detailed evaluation instruments, strategies for monitoring AI biases and hallucinations, and expected contributions. The study aims to provide an empirical basis and ethical framework for the integration of generative AI in engineering education.

title: Electric Field Distribution and Partial Discharge Characteristics of Electron Gun Under DC Voltage
authors: Daifang Zhang, Zhiwei Guo, Zizhao Jia
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The electron gun is the core equipment of the electron beam melting system, and its insulation performance is directly related to the safe and stable operation of the system. However, there are few studies on the partial discharge characteristics of the electron gun under DC voltage. A finite - element simulation software was used to build an electric field simulation model of the electron gun. The electric field distribution of the high - voltage insulation components of the electron gun under typical operating conditions was obtained, and the weak points of the high - voltage insulation components of the electron gun were identified. In addition, a partial discharge test measurement of the electron gun under DC voltage was carried out through the established partial discharge measurement system. The partial discharge inception voltage and the discharge process spectrum of the electron gun device under different DC high - voltage input modes were obtained, which laid a foundation for the improvement of the insulation performance of the electron gun.

title: SACVD BPSG Tungsten Extrusion Elimination
authors: Giam Xiong Feng, Lau Ker Peng, Mah Kenneth Chih Yuang, Koh Chuah, Xie Jun
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Sub-atmospheric chemical vapor deposition (SACVD) borophosphosilicate glass (BPSG) films remain essential in pre-metal dielectric (PMD) fabrication yet are susceptible to tungsten (W) extrusion defects when precursor delivery instabilities create localized dopant non-uniformities. In this report, we present a process recipe optimization for SACVD BPSG, incorporating process engineering (PE) charting to monitor injection valve (IV) performance and short-loop wafer testing for early defect detection. This approach enables effective control of boron (B) and phosphorus (P) concentration and profile, thereby mitigating W-extrusion defects and ensuring BPSG film integrity and process reliability at PMD module.

title: Safety Assessment of Electromagnetic Effects for UHV Transmission Lines
authors: Haotian Wang, Tianyu Xie, Minghui Sun, Yong Zhu, Jinwenxi Zhao, Chao Liu, Linjun Zhang, Sida Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper conducts a systematic safety assessment of electromagnetic effects for UHV same-tower double-circuit transmission lines, aiming to address the demand for refined quantitative evaluation of human electromagnetic exposure under the UHV power frequency electromagnetic environment. Finite element models of the UHV transmission lines and a refined human body model with a layered head structure are established via COMSOL Multiphysics. The power frequency magnetic field effects, electric field effects, and the influence of the typical arms-raised operation posture on human exposure characteristics are quantitatively analyzed, with all results strictly benchmarked against the ICNIRP public exposure limits. The results show that all human electromagnetic exposure parameters under different working conditions are far below the corresponding safety limits with sufficient safety margins. This research provides reliable quantitative data and theoretical support for the electromagnetic environment safety assessment of UHV transmission lines.

title: Detection of Coordinated Social Media Manipulation using Temporal Network Analysis
authors: Ramya D, Vishali M, Dhora M, Meena R, Bowyashree K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Social media platforms have been more frequently targeted by a concerted effort in manipulation campaign in order to influence opinions and misinform as well as artificially amplify stories. Current detection methods tend to use network characteristics or content information that are static and do not detect the dynamic and cooperative behavior of coordinated inauthenticity. This paper suggests a framework based on temporal network analysis of identifying coordinated social media manipulation through modeling user interactions as time-evolving graphs. The framework combines temporal edge-construction, synchronized activity-detection, and community-level coordination measures in order to find collections of accounts that perform in a coordinated fashion. Two publicly available data sets of social media containing confirmed manipulation campaigns and organic interactions with users are experimented with. The proposed approach has a F1-score of 0.91, precision of 0.93 and recall of 0.89, which is 8-14 times higher than the state-of-the-art baseline methods such as static graph clustering, bot-detection models and content-based classifiers. The analysis of temporal motifs shows that the coordinated manipulation accounts have a much higher interaction synchrony and repeated patterns of activation than genuine users. The proposed framework also shows a high level of generalization across platforms, and performance deterioration under 4% when tested on unseen data. The findings affirm that use of temporal dynamics is important in order to detect coordination manipulation campaigns. The suggested strategy is a platform-agnostic early warning of organized influence operations, and the proposed strategy can be applied practically to reduce misinformation, control platforms, and build digital trust.

title: Layout-Derived Features for Chip-Level Misalignment Correction in DRAM
authors: HyeJin Kim, SoYoung Kim
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: As dynamic random access memory technology scales, chip-level interlayer misalignment has become a critical manufacturability issue, increasingly dominated by layout-dependent design-induced variation. Conventional correction methods rely mainly on wafer-level characteristics and simple coordinate-based models, which limits robustness under sparse and noisy metrology coverage. This paper presents a layout-aware regression framework for correcting chip-level interlayer misalignment by representing design-induced variation through physically interpretable features derived from chip layout. The proposed approach predicts correction values at unmeasured chip locations using limited metrology data while reducing the influence of wafer- and lot-level variation. Validation on real dynamic random access memory products demonstrates that the proposed Random Forest-based framework achieves 71-78% reduction in chip-level interlayer misalignment while reducing correction time compared with conventional methods. These results indicate that physically interpretable layout-derived features enables practical and manufacturing-ready solutions for chip-level interlayer misalignment correction.

title: GNN Enhanced Binary Loop Detection for NOMA-AFDM
authors: Qingyu Li, Yusha Liu, Guanghui Liu, Dan Tian, Fuchen Xu, Chengxiang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) achieves full diversity but faces multiple-access challenges due to signal dispersion. To address this issue, we propose a power domain non-orthogonal multiple access AFDM (PD-NOMA-AFDM) system, which enables parallel transmission of multi-user signals on the same resource block through power-domain multiplexing. Furthermore, we design a binary-loop maximal ratio combining-message passing (BLMM)-based successive interference cancellation (SIC) scheme. Specifically, the inner loop fully leverages the sparsity of the AFDM equivalent channel to effectively eliminate inter-symbol interference and achieve reliable initial symbol estimation; the outer loop iteratively updates extrinsic information to compensate for performance degradation caused by banded-matrix approximation. We prove the convergence of the inner loop to the MMSE fixed point and the local convergence of the outer loop. Subsequently, by combining Lipschitz continuity and perturbation theory, we demonstrate the convergence of the overall BLMM detector to a neighborhood of the exact fixed point. The pairwise error probability analysis is then used to characterize its diversity gain and performance gap to maximum likelihood (ML) detection. To further narrow this gap, a graph neural network (GNN) is incorporated into the BLMM multi-user detection framework. This approach dynamically captures the multi-user interference (MUI) characteristics through node message interactions, thereby improving the accuracy of the approximate a posteriori probability distribution. Simulation results show that the proposed BLMM-GNN achieves near-ML performance with strong robustness.

title: EMC Society Governors-at-Large Candidate Petition Form
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Electromagnetic Compatibility Magazine
abstract: Candidate nominations are now being accepted for the 2026 IEEE Annual Elections ballot for Governor-at-Large positions on the IEEE EMC Society Board of Governors (BoG) for a three-year term, beginning January 1, 2027. In accordance with the bylaws, nominations may be made by the Nominations and Appointments (N&A) Committee or by petition by individual voting members.

title: Moving-Discretized-Control-Set Model Predictive Control for Quad-Port Magnetic Network Energy Routers in DC-Microgrids
authors: Zheng Yin, Fujin Deng, Yeyuan Xie, Yun Yang, Xiaojun Deng, Cristian Garcia, Sergio Vazquez, Jose Rodriguez
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The quad-port magnetic network energy router (QPMNER) is a promising solution for energy dispatch in DC microgrids with its high efficiency and flexible power control capability. For QP-MNER systems, effective multi-port power control is the core premise to ensure reliable and stable operation, while the inherent nonlinear power coupling between ports has become a key bottleneck restricting its control performance. To solve this problem, this paper proposes a moving-discretized-control-set model predictive control (MDCS-MPC) strategy for QP-MNER. The proposed MDCS-MPC achieves independent power regulation for each port by designing adaptive candidate phase-shift angles and predicting the corresponding port voltages and currents, which effectively coordinates the power flows of the whole system and greatly improves the dynamic performance. The feasibility and superiority of the proposed MDCS-MPC are fully verified by experimental tests based on a QP-MNER hardware prototype.

title: A Systematic Analysis of Oversampling and Classic Machine Learning Pipelines for Multilingual Offensive Language Detection in Resource-Constrained Scenarios
authors: Avital Rozenthal, Avigail Stekel, Yaakov HaCohen-Kerner
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: While recent advances in Natural Language Processing (NLP) are increasingly dominated by large-scale deep learning (DL), the detection of offensive language in real-world applications still relies heavily on classical machine learning (ML) approaches due to computational constraints and the need for interpretability. Despite the critical importance of identifying harmful content, existing studies typically focus either on single-language settings or on computationally intensive models, leaving a significant gap in systematic, cross-lingual evaluations of lightweight detection pipelines under resource-constrained conditions. This study addresses this gap by presenting a large-scale benchmark and evaluation of classical ML-based classification pipelines across 15 datasets covering 6 languages. Our findings yield two major insights. First, widely adopted preprocessing techniques often provide minimal performance gains, and in several cases may even degrade detection accuracy. Second, oversampling emerges as the most consistently effective strategy for boosting performance, improving F1-scores significantly in the highly imbalanced datasets typical of toxic content moderation. Overall, this work challenges conventional assumptions in NLP pipeline design and provides practical, evidence-based recommendations for building efficient and robust multilingual offensive language detection systems under real-world constraints.

title: Low-Elevation Source Pattern Effects in Evaporation Duct Propagation and Their Low-Dimensional Representation_supp1-3701727.pdf
authors: Yuqiang Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: The transmitting source in evaporation duct propagation modeling is often approximated by an ideal Gaussian pattern, but the influence of fine low-elevation radiation-pattern details near the horizon remains insufficiently quantified. This letter investigates this effect using a three-level source model composed of an ideal Gaussian source, an engineering reference source, and a low-elevation perturbed source. Parabolic equation simulations are performed in range-independent and range-dependent evaporation duct environments to compare source-induced propagation differences and their relative importance with respect to duct parameter uncertainty. The results show that the discrepancy between the ideal Gaussian and engineering reference sources is limited, whereas subdegree low-elevation perturbations can produce pronounced propagation loss variations. Although duct uncertainty remains the dominant contributor, source uncertainty accounts for 29.7%-36.4% of the full field error variance and 25.2%-32.9% of the receiver-height error variance. A two-metric coupling representation further characterizes the source-induced errors with coefficients of determination of 0.934-0.969.

title: Integrated GaAs Fiber-Optic Fabry-Perot Sensor for Temperature and Pressure Simultaneous Measurement_supp1-3699716.pdf
authors: Pinggang Jia
publish: N/A
abstract: Achieving simultaneous temperature and pressure measurement is critical for accurate monitoring in complex environments. However, conventional integrated sensors typically suffer from spatial offsets between sensing elements, leading to inevitable thermal gradient errors during dynamic temperature variations. To overcome this limitation, we propose a true in-situ, zero-offset gallium arsenide fiber-optic sensor for simultaneous dual-parameter measurement. The sensor features a specifically designed mesa-diaphragm architecture, integrating a pressure-sensitive Fabry-Perot cavity with the temperature-sensitive bandedge absorption of GaAs at the exact same spatial location. A novel two-stage wet etching process and homologous GaAs wafer bonding technique are developed to ensure high-quality optical interference fringes and thermomechanical reliability. Experimental characterization demonstrates excellent performance. The sensor exhibits a temperature fitting error of 0.61% F.S. and a pressure fitting error of 0.75% F.S., with rigorous GUM-based expanded measurement uncertainties (k = 2.26) evaluated to be 2.85 °C and 18.6 kPa. Notably, within the 50–200 °C range, the zero-offset design enables precise dynamic thermal compensation, achieving decoupled pressure and temperature measurement accuracies of 0.90% F.S. and 0.35% F.S., respectively. This firmly demonstrates its superior capabilities in eliminating cross-sensitivity and realizing simultaneous temperature and pressure testing.

title: NeuroLens: A Holistic Visual Analytics System for Exploring Brain Networks across Scales_supp1-3701404.mp4
authors: Jun Tao
publish: N/A
abstract: Identifying biomarkers from human brain networks is critical in early detection of neurological disorders and understanding disease mechanisms. Existing visual analytics approaches show a remarkable ability to assist experts in discovering and validating biomarkers through exploration. However, these approaches often focus only on the diffusion features of fiber bundles and evaluate individual bundles and regions separately. This may hinder their ability to accurately represent the complex brain network for investigation from various perspectives. In this paper, we present NeuroLens, a visual analytics system that integrates comprehensive information across multiple levels, including fiber bundles, local regions and entire brain networks. Specifically, to model the bundles more precisely, we enhance the features by incorporating the joint distribution of geometric features. The bundle information is further aggregated to form representations at the region and the brain level using an attention-based graph neural network. The region-level representation describes complex structures involving multiple bundles, and the brain-level representation enables comparisons between subjects and groups. The NeuroLens interface enables comparative exploration of this multi-level and multi-faceted information. To verify the findings during exploration, NeuroLens leverages the large language model to query related information from existing literature. We collaborate with domain experts to examine the effectiveness of NeuroLens. Their exploration, findings, and feedback are discussed
