title: An Integrated Energy System Optimization Model Based on Soft Actor-Critic Deep Reinforcement Learning and Stepped Carbon Trading
authors: Bolin Zhao, Zhiwen Zhao, Yilu Wang, Jingzheng Li, Dawei Yu, Tao Jin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the continuous development of integrated energy systems, the requirements for their economy and low carbon performance are increasing day by day. This paper proposes an integrated energy system optimization model based on the soft actor-critic algorithm and stepped carbon trading, which flexibly solves the problems of uncertainties in wind and solar energy and various loads in integrated energy systems, and realizes the optimal decision-making in different scenarios. Firstly, a multi-energy complementary integrated energy system model was constructed, and the objective function and constraint conditions of the model were determined. Secondly, the established model is placed as the environment into the deep reinforcement learning framework, and the action space, state space and reward function of the agent are set. Through the continuous interaction and adaptive learning between the agent and the system, the output of each device within the system is continuously optimized, and finally the optimal configuration is obtained. Finally, through simulation verification, it can be known that this algorithm can effectively reduce the operating cost and carbon emissions of the integrated energy system, and has both economic and environmental benefits.

title: Research on the Integration of High-Speed Rail Mobility Hub Functions Based on an International Expert Survey
authors: Vladimír Kremlík, Miroslav Svítek, Peter Vittek, Miroslav Haltuf
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: High-speed rail (HSR) stations are increasingly expected to operate as mobility hubs that integrate multimodal transport, urban functions, digital services and low-carbon energy. This paper proposes a modular functional architecture for Mobility Hubs intended for future Czech HSR terminals. The approach combines a structured literature review with an international questionnaire survey (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 1}$</tex> items) administered to infrastructure managers and station operators, complemented by a comparative assessment of ten operating hubs in Europe and Asia. Results indicate that multimodal integration and passenger services are widely implemented, whereas energy autonomy, alternative-fuel infrastructure aligned with the Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR), and cybersecurity governance remain uneven. Based on observed practices, we synthesize a reference set of functional modules covering rail operations and passenger flow management, multimodal interchange and parking, digital passenger services and Mobility as a Service (MaaS), safety and cyber protection, energy management and low-emission supply options (including fuel cells), universal accessibility for Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM), and mixed-use urban integration and governance. The architecture supports staged implementation and scalability and provides a basis for requirements definition and cross-case benchmarking of future HSR Mobility Hubs.

title: Optimal Day-Ahead Dispatch for Distribution Networks Leveraging Spatio-temporal Load Flexibility from 5G Base Stations
authors: Yuanshi Zhang, Fengtao Li, Gang Ma, Bokang Zou, Yulong Jin, Weiqi Pan, Rushuai Han
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The widespread deployment of 5G networks has positioned base stations as primary energy consumers. Their integrated backup battery systems and shiftable traffic loads, however, offer significant potential for spatiotemporal load regulation. To address the lack of clear strategies for coordinating large-scale 5G base station clusters with the power distribution grid—specifically, the challenge of balancing grid operational benefits with communication performance—this paper makes three key contributions. First, we propose a hierarchical scheduling architecture for the backup energy storage of 5G base stations. Second, we develop a two-stage coordinated optimization framework. The first stage performs spatial load migration to enable deep sleep modes for under-utilized base stations, thereby reducing the network's energy consumption. The second stage then leverages the liberated battery capacity and predicted load profiles to formulate a temporally refined charging/discharging schedule for the distribution grid, guided by dynamic safety margins. Finally, a co-simulation study using an IEEE 33-node distribution system and a base station cluster validates the proposed strategy. The results demonstrate that this dual-scale (spatial and temporal) coordination strategy can significantly improve system-wide economic efficiency.

title: Heterogeneous Relation-Aware GNN for Optimal Multi-Energy Flow in Integrated Energy Systems
authors: Xiaoqin Wu, Yaoqin Chen, Lu Shao, Yameng Dai, Wei Fu, Xiaoqiang Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The Optimal Multi-Energy Flow (OMEF) problem is fundamental to the economic and secure operation of Integrated Energy Systems (IES). However, the complex coupling of heterogeneous energy domains—electricity, gas, and heat—renders traditional iterative numerical solvers computationally prohibitive for real-time dispatch, especially under the uncertainty of renewable energy. Existing data-driven approaches often struggle to handle the distinct physical laws of different energy networks and lack guarantees for physical feasibility. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Physics-Informed Heterogeneous Relation-Aware Graph Neural Network (RA-GNN) for real-time OMEF solving. We model the IES as a heterogeneous graph and design a relation-aware message passing mechanism to decouple the learning of electrical, gas, and thermal dynamics. Furthermore, a physics-informed unsupervised training strategy is developed, which embeds Kirchhoff’s laws, fluid dynamics equations, and operational constraints directly into the loss function, eliminating the dependency on labeled data. Extensive case studies on both small-scale and large-scale IES benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a computation speedup of over 200 times compared to conventional solvers while maintaining high solution optimality and strictly satisfying physical constraints.

title: Lightweight Queueing Abstraction for Rapid Simulation and Automated Tuning of Input-Dependent Streaming Pipelines on FPGAs
authors: Shashank Obla, Bin Li, James C. Hoe
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: The programmability of FPGAs enables designs to be tuned to specific deployment and use-cases. This capability is critical for input-dependent streaming pipelines, whose optimal configuration varies not only across resource limits and performance targets, but also with the data being processed. However, existing methods fail to scale for large, real-world designs with long workloads. To address this challenge, we propose RapidQ, a performance model that allows designers to capture the dataflow of their streaming pipeline as a queueing system, enabling fast case-by-case re-tuning for resource efficiency. Unburdened by the functional details of the design, a trace model drives a fast queueing simulation that can predict the performance of the pipeline across various buffer sizes and module throughput configurations without repeating full functional simulations. Our simulator is over 7x faster than the state-of-the-art and yields up to 42% resource savings for real-world workloads.

title: Triangulated Authentic Competency Assessment in a Four-Campus Evaluation Center
authors: Jose M. Nieto-Jalil, Juan-Gabino Diaz-Martinez, Juan Fernando Piñal-Montezuma, Luis C. Félix-Herrán, Karla B. Coyote Aguirre, Atoany N. Fierro-Radilla
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: We introduce an institution-scale educational innovation: a standardized Evaluation Center deployed across four campuses (Cuernavaca, Toluca, Tampico, Sonora Norte) in synchronous hybrid mode, simulating professional recruitment and performance. Multi-campus teams collaborated via Zoom with external evaluators. The study integrates triangulation framework (students, employers, faculty) and a PRE/POST design with explicitly defined constructs to estimate perceived change and convergent validity. Participants included students (PRE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n=56$</tex>; POST <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n=44$</tex>), 5 employers, and 11 faculty; internal consistency was high (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=0.96$</tex> PRE; 0.93 POST). POST results indicated utility 8.8/10, realism 8.6/10, confidence gained 8.4/10; <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 4 \%}$</tex> recommended continuation. Cross-campus deployment with shared governance and calibrated rubrics under a common operational protocol enabled comparable metrics, detection of local gaps, and immediate formative feedback. Triangulation confirmed alignment with professional competencies: employers rated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9-10 / 10$</tex> on industry relevance, while faculty stressed curricular applicability. The primary contribution is the integration of professional simulation, longitudinal evidence, and inter-campus governance into a replicable mechanism for program-outcomes assurance and accreditation readiness, supported by openly accessible and anonymized assessment instruments. Novelty lies in combining PRE/POST evidence, actor triangulation, and hybrid synchronous operation, thus underpinning external validity. This work demonstrates that authentic, multi-campus performance assessment can simultaneously scale, maintain fidelity, and enhance employability while preserving ethical data practices and methodological transparency.

title: From Zdiby to Prague: Designing Tram Logistics for Alza
authors: Matej Harant, Matyas Sedivy, Tomas Horak, Miroslav Svitek
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Urban logistics faces increasing pressure to reduce emissions and traffic congestion while maintaining high service levels for e-commerce. This paper develops a general framework for evaluating cargo-tram integration and applies it to a Prague case study linking the planned Zdiby extension with four major Alza branches. The routing problem is formulated on a directed tram graph and solved by a state-extended Dijkstra's algorithm with turn penalties and stop-visitation states. A second screening step evaluates night-time timetable compatibility on shared passenger infrastructure. For the Prague case, the feasible loop serves Vltavská, Dejvická, Anděl and Pankrác, departs at 03:10, and returns to Zdiby at 06:21. Under the assumed vehicle layout, one tram can transport up to 19 roll cages, corresponding to an upper bound of 4.75 t or approximately 1,710 parcels per trip. The results confirm technical feasibility for night operation, while also identifying noise, timetable coordination, infrastructure access, and demand uncertainty as key implementation limits.

title: Boosting Academic Success in Engineering Through Gamified Mentoring with Advanced Digital Tools
authors: Carracedo-Navarro Juan José, López Cavazos Lucio, Maldonado-Guevara Blanca I
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper presents a mentoring program designed to support engineering students with low motivation or failing grades in the Electrical Systems Analysis course. The program integrates Arduino, sensors, Tinkercad, and basic artificial intelligence (AI) concepts within a gamified learning environment to enhance student engagement and reduce dropout rates during the first academic year. It was implemented at a university with students from several engineering programs, using an experimental group that participated in the mentoring program and a control group. Atrisk students engaged in hands-on activities such as constructing electrical circuits and measuring wheel angular velocity using Hall effect sensors. A quantitative analysis revealed statistically significant improvements in four of seven activities and in final course grades, accompanied by a 0% dropout rate in the experimental group compared to 7.7% in the control group. suggesting that the integration of technology, AI, and gamification into mentoring supports learning and retention.

title: Impact of the COIL Methodology on Teacher Development: Benefits and Challenges
authors: Víctor R. Campos-García, Alan Miralrio, Maritza Peña-Becerril
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Teachers identify the primary motivation for implementing Collaborative Online International Learning (COIL) as the benefits it provides to students. Key challenges include managing differences in time zones, academic calendars, students' motivation, and course topics, with particular emphasis on aligning subjects across disciplines. The icebreaker phase is regarded as a crucial element of the collaboration, receiving a high relevance rating of 4.64 on a five-point Likert scale. Participation in COIL initiatives fosters the development of both pedagogical and personal competencies, notably in leadership, communication, empathy, and flexibility. Overall, COIL collaborations demonstrate the intellectual engagement of educators and their commitment to advancing innovative and globally oriented teaching practices.

title: Hierarchical Coordinated Optimization Scheduling for Energy Interaction Among Multiple Microgrids
authors: Ben Wang, Qingshan Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: This paper proposes a coordinated optimization framework for energy interconnections in multi-microgrid systems. To overcome the limited flexibility and insufficient coordination capability of isolated microgrids, a hierarchical scheduling architecture is developed that integrates microgrid– grid interaction, inter-microgrid power exchange, and intra-microgrid resource dispatch. A unified optimization model is formulated to minimize total operating costs, including fuel, operation and maintenance, and power exchange costs, subject to multi-microgrid operational constraints. To support scalable and cooperative implementation, a distributed ADMM-based solution method is adopted. Case studies on a three-microgrid interconnected system demonstrate that the proposed strategy enhances renewable-energy utilization, improves internal energy sharing, and reduces overall operating costs. Comparative results further show that the coordinated scheduling strategy significantly outperforms isolated microgrid operation in terms of economic efficiency and reduced main-grid dependence. The results verify the effectiveness of the proposed framework for coordinated and economically efficient operation of future multi-microgrid integrated energy systems.

title: Power System Dynamic Component Modeling via Physics-Constrained Predictor-Corrector Neural Differential Algebraic Equations with Soft Continuity
authors: Yu Xia, Weiyong Yang, Shengtao Yang, Bin Zhang, Baohua Sun, Henggui Han
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: stability analysis is essential for secure power system operation. Large disturbances introduce topology changes and lead to highly nonlinear differential-algebraic equations, posing challenges for data-driven dynamic modeling. Conventional neural ordinary differential equation methods often suffer from trajectory drift and error accumulation during long-horizon integration. To address this issue, this paper proposes a physics-constrained predictor-corrector neural surrogate model. A self-attention predictor estimates system states near disturbance events and provides reliable initialization for multiple shooting training. A soft continuity constraint is further introduced to enforce trajectory consistency across shooting segments and mitigate error propagation. In addition, a physics-informed derivative loss guides the learned dynamics to remain consistent with the underlying physical vector field. Experiments on the standard IEEE 39-bus system demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively stabilizes long-horizon trajectory prediction while achieving reliable macroscopic trajectory reconstruction and significantly minimizing the prediction deviations of both states and algebraic currents.

title: Teacher Professional Development for Responsible AI and Digital Competencies in Higher Education
authors: Roberto López-Chila, Nicolás Sumba-Nacipucha, Jorge Cueva-Estrada, Joe Llerena-Izquierdo
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This research designs and preliminarily validates a concise professional development (PD) plan to integrate responsible AI into higher education teaching while strengthening teacher digital competency. Our measure of faculty self-efficacy with a 12-item scale (n=100; Cronbach's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=0.959$</tex>) and found moderate to high readiness, with higher scores in resource curation and communication and lower scores in accessibility/privacy and learning analytics. The validity of nine content PD modules, judged by an expert panel (k=5), was strong (S-CVI/Ave =0.956 for relevance; 0.911 for clarity). Semistructured interviews with five experts converged on promptinformed design, authentic assessment with rubrics, privacy/data protection, transparency/authorship, and bias verification. We interpret these findings into deployable micro courses (12-16 h) with implementation resources (prompt guides, accessibility and privacy checklists, verification workflows, rubric packs, analytics starter dashboards). The study provides a feasible, evidenceinformed path to align PD with trustworthy AI principles and identifies priority areas for institutional support and future evaluation.

title: Engineering a Patient-Level Cost-Sensitive Framework for Temporal Machine Learning in Early Sepsis Detection
authors: Marcos Luiz Cliton Bezerra, Joaquim M. Maia, Fabio A. Motta
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Early sepsis detection in Intensive Care Units (ICUs) remains challenging because physiological data are irregular and decisions must be made at the patient level. We propose a unified patient-level cost-sensitive framework for early sepsis alerting, with false negatives penalized more heavily than false positives (FN_COST = 10; FP_COST = 1). Using the PhysioNet Challenge 2019 dataset (20,346 patients; 790,534 hourly records), we trained temporal Logistic Regression (LR) and Random Forest (RF) models with 24-hour sliding windows and a 24-hour prediction horizon, as well as Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models on padded multivariate sequences. Inputs included routinely monitored ICU variables and simple derived indicators. Alert thresholds were calibrated on the validation set by minimizing patient-level clinical costs, and test performance was assessed using patient recall, patient precision, clinical costs, and median lead times. Temporal RF achieved the best overall performance (patient recall = 0.893; cost = 561; median lead time = 25.5 h), outperforming LR (0.836;613;24.0 h), while GRU and LSTM yielded substantially higher costs (2,837 and 2,827) and lower recall. These findings suggest that clinically grounded patient-level decision design may be more important than architectural complexity for early sepsis alerting.

title: Input-Parallel CSC-MMC DC Transformer Startup and Steady-State Operation Strategies
authors: Jie Li, Fucheng Sun, Dong Han, Shuai Wang, Zhexu Dong, Yilu Zhou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: High-step-ratio DC transformers are core equipment for new energy DC collection and transmission. Constructing a CSC-MMC DC transformer by hybrid connecting a controllable current source converter (CSC) and a modular multilevel converter (MMC) can achieve highly controllable commutation, high-power density design, and efficient power transfer. The input parallel design on the low-voltage side enables high current carrying capacity. This paper focuses on the Input-Parallel CSC-MMC DC transformer. Firstly, a steady-state control strategy for the Input-Parallel CSC-MMC DC transformer is established to realize its stable control. Then, the rationality and construction method of the input parallel design are discussed. Secondly, for new energy DC collection scenarios, a startup control strategy is designed to charge the MMC side sub-modules and achieve smooth power rise. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed topology and control strategies is verified through PSCAD/EMTDC simulations.

title: Research on Coordinated Optimal Configuration of Source-Grid-Load-Storage in Green Power Parks for Frequency Security
authors: Chenglong Wang, Song Yang, Shibo Wang, Nan Wang, Jiawei Xing, Yishu Kong
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Under the background of "double carbon", the anti-disturbance ability of a high proportion of renewable energy penetration scenarios including green power parks is weak, and one of the biggest challenges it faces is frequency stability. The traditional energy storage configuration only considers the operation economy, and ignores the frequency security risk under extreme working conditions. In this paper, a mixed integer linear programming strategy is used to solve the capacity configuration problem considering frequency Security constraints. Numerical examples show that considering frequency Security constraints not only ensures the economy of cooperative optimal configuration, but also significantly improves the frequency stability of the system.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence and Chatbots: Profiles and Uses in Chilean Distance Education
authors: Emanuel Arredondo González, Rodrigo Prestes Machado, Carlos Alario-Hoyos, Priscila Ávila Hormazábal
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The emergence of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) opens new opportunities in higher education by enabling the personalization of students' learning experiences. Within this context, this study examines how students in distance higher education use a chatbot and explores its metacognitive implications, particularly temporal spacing as a learning strategy. The research was conducted at Iplacex, Chile's largest distance-learning institution, analyzing 10,490 chatbot conversations from 4,276 students during the first academic term of 2025. Two complementary studies were conducted. Study 1 analyzed the temporal structure of interactions, revealing that most conversations were brief and concentrated within short study sessions, with limited evidence of spaced use; women engaged more frequently in extended interactions. Study 2 applied Latent Class Analysis and multinomial regression, identifying three user profiles: administrative-oriented, content-focused, and low-interaction users. Overall, the findings indicate that the chatbot primarily supports immediate, task-oriented needs while also serving differentiated functions related to cognitive regulation, highlighting the need for pedagogical guidance to promote reflective use.

title: Recovering Speech: Language Study Restoration
authors: Michael Dorin, Mariana Moyano, Mario Chong
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Urarina is an endangered language spoken by a small indigenous community in Northern Peru. In 1987, a research team from the University of San Marcos in Lima, Peru, conducted a documentation study to help preserve the language, producing thousands of handwritten lexicon cards and cassette recordings of native speakers. Decades later, these materials had deteriorated significantly, making them difficult to use in contemporary linguistic or educational research. Therefore, this project aims to digitize and restore these artifacts to inform both modern linguistic analysis and future educational initiatives. By converting handwritten notes into a structured database and enhancing analog audio recordings, the project provides invaluable data to support national educational program proposals and to lay the groundwork for integrating cultural knowledge with emerging AI technologies. Beyond safeguarding the materials themselves, this work highlights the role of engineering, particularly AI, in revitalizing pre-digital studies and expanding around endangered-language preservation. The methodology and results presented here aim to encourage similar restoration efforts and to contribute to the conservation and potential pedagogical use of endangered languages.

title: Joint Spot-Peak Market Clearing Strategy Accounting for Wind and Solar Uncertainty
authors: Siyuan Liu, Weifang Lin, Yongzheng Gu, Haoran Sun, Tiankun Wang, Huaqing Zhao, Jiangtao Zhu, Mingyue Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Under the dual carbon goals, the increasing penetration of wind and photovoltaic generation has significantly intensified the peak regulation pressure on power systems. At the same time, conventional thermal power units face a conflict between flexibility enhancement and low carbon operation, while the existing electricity spot market and peak regulation ancillary service market still lack a coordinated clearing mechanism for renewable uncertainty and low carbon flexible resources. To address this issue, this paper proposes a joint clearing method for carbon capture power plants participating in both the spot market and the peak regulation ancillary service market under uncertain wind and photovoltaic output. First, Latin hypercube sampling and K means clustering are combined to generate and reduce renewable output scenarios, thereby preserving the fluctuation characteristics of wind and photovoltaic generation while reducing the computational complexity of the optimization problem. Then, within a two stage stochastic optimization framework, a joint clearing model is developed by considering the flexible decoupling characteristics between power generation and carbon capture in carbon capture power plants. The model incorporates day ahead security constrained unit commitment and real time economic dispatch to achieve coordinated optimization of electric energy and peak regulation resources. Finally, case studies on a modified IEEE 39 bus system are conducted, and the proposed method is compared with the independent clearing mode of the spot market and the peak regulation ancillary service market. The results show that the proposed method reduces the wind curtailment rate from 8.26% to 4.05% and the photovoltaic curtailment rate from 24.21% to 19.45%. In addition, it increases the revenues of wind farms, photovoltaic stations, and thermal power units, while improving the overall economic performance and coordinated operation of the system. The proposed method enhances the capability of the joint clearing mechanism to accommodate renewable uncertainty and provides support for the market participation of carbon capture power plants and the coordinated operation of spot and peak regulation markets.

title: Control Strategy of the Energy Storage System to Provide the Auxiliary Frequency Regulation Service
authors: Hainan Liu, Zhixue Yuan, Hongting Yu, Qi Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Oriented an energy storage system (ESS), a grid-forming (GFM) control architecture according to dynamics of DC capacitor is presented in this paper. The auxiliary inertia support and droop control loops are given to provide the frequency regulation function. To reveal the dynamic interaction, a small-signal impedance model of ESS converter is established. The stability margin quantification index for GFM ESS is presented. Effects of grid stiffness, inertial support, droop control coefficient on the ESS’s stability is researched. Research results show the GFM ESS has good grid adaptability and can operate stably in both stiff and weak grid environments. It is still able to work stably as the inertia support coefficient and droop control coefficient vary. At last, simulation verification of the ESS’s control strategy is conducted on the PASCAD/EMTDC platform.

title: Performance Evaluation of OTFS and OFDM Technologies in High Mobility Scenarios
authors: Lucian Ştefan, Partemie-Marian Mutescu, Razvan Marius Mihai, Alexandru Lavric
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Highly mobile wireless scenarios for future 6G systems, such as high-speed trains, unmanned aerial vehicle links and low-earth orbit satellite communications, create challenging Doppler conditions that expose the limitations of Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM). In these scenarios, loss of subcarrier orthogonality causes intercarrier interference and degrades bit error rate (BER). This article compares the performance of OFDM and Orthogonal Time Frequency Space (OTFS) in four mobility scenarios: urban, highway, high-speed train, and extreme mobility. The evaluation measures BER as a function of signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) for four receivers: single-tap OFDM, symbol-PIC OFDM, time-LMMSE OTFS, and MP OTFS. The results show that performance differences are minimal in low-mobility scenarios, but become more pronounced as Doppler effects increase. In high and extreme mobility scenarios, OTFS-MP achieves a lower BER than single-tap OFDM. The OTFS time-LMMSE detector also outperforms the OFDM symbol-PIC detector, indicating that the main advantage of OTFS is its delay-Doppler domain representation rather than detector complexity. The results suggest that OTFS is a promising candidate waveform for high mobility 6G communications and integrated sensor and communication systems.

title: Enhancing Cultural Heritage Narratives with On-the-Fly Mixed Reality Visualizations
authors: Cosmin-Mihai Pascariu, Radu-Daniel Vatavu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: We present a mixed reality system that complements cultural heritage narrations with on-the-fly 3D visualizations. Our implementation for the HoloLens head-mounted display automatically extracts information about narrated objects and applies Shap-E to generate corresponding visualizations, which are rendered within the user’s field of view as the narration progresses. Unlike other systems that require explicit user input, our system operates passively for laid-back consumption of visually enhanced narrations. In an evaluation involving a 23-minute cultural heritage podcast, our system generated a total of 88 visual augmentations, approximately one at every 15 seconds. Based on our results, we outline future work opportunities in automatic augmentation of audio narrations with visual representations toward novel mixed reality experiences for cultural heritage.

title: Stressmonitoring System in a Mobile Application for Medical Science Students
authors: José Emiliano Calderón Gurubel, Daniel Ponce Naranjo, Adriana Denisse López Andrade, Javier Eduardo Martínez Giorgetti, Jessica Alejandra Ruiz-Ramirez, Mauricio Adolfo Ramírez-Moreno
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Goal: This study presents the development and implementation of a mobile application designed to monitor stress in medical students using a biometric wearable device, IoT technologies, and Machine Learning algorithms. The application captures real-time physiological data, including heart rate, heart rate variability, and electrodermal activity, utilizing these parameters to predict stress levels. Data is collected using Empatica E4 wristband to then be processed by a Python-based server using signal processing techniques, which is then fed into a predictive model to classify and provide real-time stress levels into three categories: no stress, moderate stress, and high stress. Two machine learning models were employed: Standard Random Forest and Balanced Random Forest. These models achieved accuracies of 91.18% and 88.57%, respectively, demonstrating reliable classification of stress levels. Additionally, the application offers stress management tools such as articles, podcasts, and meditation guides to enhance the well-being and academic performance of medical students. The system's effectiveness was validated through a trial involving two medical science students during their clinical shifts, showcasing its potential to bolster mental health support in clinical settings.

title: Process and Hardware Optimizations For Aluminum Interconnect Etch Defectivity Improvement
authors: Cheryl Chow, Katsuhiro Kono, Midori Murakami, Ivan Chen, Jack Hsiao, Henry Jiang
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Aluminum (Al) etch is one of the critical backend-of-line (BEOL) metallization processes in dynamic random access memory (DRAM) manufacturing. Al etch profile and sidewall polymer passivation impact defectivity-related yield loss and backend quality and reliability. This paper presents a case study on 1β nm DRAM Al interconnect metal etch process, describing how reliability and yield can be improved by implementation of a new process with appropriate polymeric gas selection and optimized polymeric gas ratios, and adoption of new hardware that is designed to improve defectivity margin. This is important because as the dimension of devices scale down, the process margin between Al metal line over etch and under etch shrinks, with over etch causing irregular notches or pits (mouse bite defects) along the bottom of the metal lines resulting in backend reliability issues, while under etch (blocked etch) causes metal line shorts resulting in yield loss; balance between the over etch and under etch margin becomes critical. These process and hardware improvements have successfully eliminated mouse bite defects and reduced blocked etch defects by at least 80%.

title: Haptics: Adding the Sense of Touch to Feel the Magnetic Force in Undergrad Physics Courses
authors: Victor Robledo-Rella, Luis Neri, Rosa M.G. Garcia-Castelan, Julieta Noguez, Andres Gonzalez-Nucamendi
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This contribution extends a previous paper that aimed to measure the impact of visuo-haptic simulators (VHS) in education. The students used Falcon haptic devices in the classroom with a 3D joystick that allows them to feel the force exerted on a simulated conducting wire immersed in an external magnetic field. A Pre-test/post-test approach was used with experimental and control groups <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(N_{\text {total }}=236\right)$</tex>, yielding a relative learning gain difference <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta g=0.16$</tex> between the experimental group (which used the haptics) and the control group (which did not). A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex>-value of 0.045 for the paired sample indicates that the results are trustworthy at the 95 % confidence level. A perception questionnaire administered to the experimental students shows that they consider that using the VHS enhanced their understanding of course concepts and, consequently, their problemsolving abilities. These results indicate that incorporating external stimulus through touch may increase the students' learning potential.

title: Accelerating XPS Metrology with AI to Enhance Throughput in High-Volume Semiconductor Manufacturing
authors: Parikshit Jain, Dmitry Kislitsyn, Heath Pois, Paul Isbester, Mark Klare, Mitch Shiver, Prakhar Soni, Wei Ti Lee, Ravi Dey, Aarthi Sundaram, Ganesh Subramanian, Mark Duggan
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Metrology in high-volume semiconductor manufacturing is increasingly constrained by the need for nanometer-level precision, extensive sampling, and the growing complexity of device architectures. As the global demand for semiconductors accelerates, there is an urgent need for scalable solutions that enhance measurement efficiency and throughput from manufacturing facilities without compromising on measurement integrity. In this study, we present results from an AI-driven in-line metrology framework developed as part of this work, which leverages big data analytics and machine-learning to significantly reduce measurement time while maintaining accuracy, precision, and sensitivity. By integrating AI with established film analysis algorithms, the system performs spectral reconstruction to compensate for signal degradation caused by reduced acquisition times. It is trained on large-scale historical datasets, enabling reliable performance in real-world production environments. Experimental evaluations conducted in a high-volume manufacturing setting at a GlobalFoundries facility demonstrate up to a twofold increase in metrology throughput, while remaining within bounds to ensure measurement integrity in terms of accuracy, precision, and sensitivity. This advancement offers a scalable and cost-effective approach to accelerate XPS metrology, with the potential to support next-generation fabrication demands.

title: Performance Evaluation of ZTNA for Hybrid Academic Networks
authors: Valentina Tirsu, Lilia Sava, Vladimir Mirovski, Cristin Djanoslavschii
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: The accelerated digital transformation of university laboratories has led to the development of hybrid infrastructures integrating on-premise resources, virtual machines, IoT devices, and cloud-based educational services. Traditional Virtual Private Network (VPN) solutions provide encrypted remote access but rely on an implicit trust model that may expose extended network segments after authentication. In contrast, Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) enforces continuous verification and fine-grained access control based on identity and contextual attributes. This paper analyzes the performance impact of deploying a ZTNA architecture in a hybrid academic environment. A reference architectural model is proposed, integrating Identity and Access Management (IAM), Policy Decision Point (PDP), Policy Enforcement Point (PEP), and micro-segmentation mechanisms. The authentication process is analytically modeled using queueing theory (M/M/1 and M/M/c), and the total latency is formulated as the sum of authentication, policy evaluation, secure channel establishment, and resource access components. The analytical model is validated through stochastic discrete-event simulations for 50–1000 concurrent users and compared with a traditional VPN architecture. Results indicate a moderate and predictable latency increase under ZTNA, minimal impact on throughput, and superior scalability under high-load conditions. The proposed framework provides a quantitative basis for evaluating the security–performance trade-off in hybrid academic infrastructures.

title: A Data-driven Approach to Industrial Process Analysis and Quality Assurance in Steel Manufacturing Company Using Machine Learning Techniques
authors: Akram Nakhaei, Iraj Mahdavi, Ali Taheri-Ahangar, Babak Fayyaz-Shahandashty, Nazila Hasanzadeh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The steel industry is a cornerstone of modern infrastructure, with reinforcement bars (rebars) playing a crucial role in ensuring structural integrity in construction. Maintaining the quality of steel rebars requires precise control over mechanical properties such as yield and final stress. While effective, traditional quality control methods are often time-consuming and costly, creating a need for machine learning (ML) approaches to optimize production processes. This study applies ML models to predict the final and yield stress of steel rebars, identify key influencing factors, and enhance quality control. A dataset of 200 steel rebar samples was preprocessed using data normalization, outlier detection and treatment, and data augmentation. Feature selection was performed using the Mutual Information Bottleneck method, with models trained on subsets containing the top 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of ranked features. Neural Networks (NN), Random Forest (RF), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) models were evaluated using performance metrics such as RMSE, MAE, and R-squared. RF consistently outperformed other models, achieving the best results with the top 50% of features for final stress prediction and all features for yield stress prediction. Feature importance analysis revealed that diameter, ivory, and furnace temperature were the most influential factors. The findings emphasize the importance of ML-driven interpretability in industrial quality control and propose a robust framework for optimizing steel manufacturing processes.

title: RL-MEL: Reinforcement-Learning-Guided Multi-Task Evolutionary Learning for High-Dimensional Feature Selection
authors: Malihe Danesh, Mohammad Hassan Ghafourian
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: High-dimensional feature selection continues to represent one of the core challenges in machine learning, particularly in domains where the number of features dramatically exceeds the number of samples. Although particle swarm optimization (PSO) and other evolutionary algorithms have demonstrated promise in addressing these difficulties, they still suffer from premature convergence, limited scalability, and inefficient exploitation of accumulated knowledge. To overcome these limitations, this study introduces RL-MEL, a reinforcement-learning-guided extension of the Multi-task Evolutionary Learning (MEL) framework. RL-MEL replaces the heuristic accuracy-difference-based weight updating strategy of MEL with a reinforcement-learning agent capable of making adaptive, reward-driven decisions regarding feature importance. In RL-MEL, the population is divided into two cooperative subpopulations, where the first performs global exploration through an enhanced PSO mechanism, and the second carries out exploitation based on an evolving feature-importance distribution produced by the reinforcement-learning policy. The integration of reinforcement learning enables more stable knowledge transfer across tasks, reduces the influence of noisy accuracy fluctuations, and results in more discriminative and long-term-effective weight assignments. Experiments conducted on twenty-two high-dimensional benchmark datasets demonstrate that RL-MEL improves classification accuracy, identifies more compact feature subsets, and achieves more robust convergence behavior in comparison with twenty-four state-of-the-art evolutionary methods. The results confirm that incorporating reinforcement signals into evolutionary multitasking provides significant advantages for large-scale feature selection.

title: Heart Disease Prediction: A Novel Approach Using Mutual Information and Deep Learning
authors: Ahmad Azarnik, Arash Khosravi, Arash Moradi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Heart Disease (HD) is a serious clinical syndrome caused by the stenosis or occlusion of coronary arteries, leading to insufficient blood supply and manifestations like disturbance of sleep and abnormal heart rhythms. Detection in early stages should be done to prevent death due to HD covering about 18 million deaths annually (WHO). Traditional diagnosis involving techniques like angiography, although highly specific, is costly and invasive. This research introduces a new HD prediction method based on an improved well-known dataset and advanced machine learning techniques. Class imbalance is resolved using the synthetic minority over-sampling technique (SMOTE) that generates synthetic samples to ensure model generalizability. The crux of our method is feature selection by mutual information and building a deep learning model. We evaluate feature sets of varying sizes to identify the most informative inputs. The deep learning algorithm employs dense layers with dropout to minimize overfitting, and its performance is evaluated in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, f1-score, and roc-auc over a number of training iterations. The results present a consistent growth in predictive power with the inclusion of more features up to a level of accuracy of 0.94. Our research exemplifies the excellent performance of our approach in enhancing HD diagnosis versus traditional approaches and against new recent related researches. Our approach provides an effective way for the prediction of heart disease that leads to improved diagnostics and patient care.

title: Reconfigurable Computing Challenge: Real-Time Graph Neural Networks for Online Event Selection in Big Science
authors: Marc Neu, Frank Baptist, Thomas Lobmaier, Fabio Papagno, Torben Ferber, Jürgen Becker
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Graph neural networks are increasingly adopted in trigger systems for collider experiments, where strict latency and throughput constraints render deployment on embedded platforms challenging. As detectors move towards higher granularity, the number of inputs per inference increase and FPGA-only solutions face resource bottlenecks. This work presents an end-to-end demonstrator for the real-time deployment of a dynamic Graph Neural Network for the Belle II electromagnetic calorimeter hardware trigger on the AMD Versal VCK190, leveraging both FPGA fabric and AI Engine tiles. We develop a Python-based semi-automated design flow covering operator fusion, partitioning, mapping, spatial parallelization, and kernel-level optimization. Our design achieves a throughput of 2.94 million events per second at an end-to-end latency of 7.15 µs. Compared to the FPGA-only baseline, this represents a 53% throughput improvement while reducing DSP utilization from 99% to 19% at 29% AI Engine tile utilization. To validate the deployment, an interactive visualization pipeline enables real-time monitoring of inference results on the physical demonstrator.

title: Multi-Time-Scale Optimal Dispatch and Mobile Resource Co-Configuration for Park Microgrids under Extreme Disaster Scenarios
authors: Zhewen Li, Yihui Luo, Tao Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Driven by the "dual carbon" goals, renewable energy represented by wind and solar power continues to increase its penetration in park microgrids, while frequent extreme weather disasters pose severe challenges to the safe and stable operation of microgrids. Traditional microgrid optimal dispatch mostly adopts a single time scale, making it difficult to balance the differential requirements between fast response of energy storage and spatiotemporal configuration of mobile resources; existing studies also lack collaborative optimization methods for emergency resources such as mobile energy storage (MES) and backup generators (BG). To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-time-scale optimal dispatch and mobile resource co-configuration method for park microgrids under extreme disaster scenarios.

title: Accuracy in Infrared Overlay Metrology
authors: Maxim Vanrusselt, Shuo Kang, Joeri De Vos, Anne-Laure Charley, Gerald Beyer, Tom Depuydt, Walter Zorbach, Markus Wimplinger, Frank Bögelsack, Sebastian Köhl
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In 3D integration, particularly in wafer-to-wafer (W2W) hybrid bonding, achieving tight on-product overlay (OPO) control is increasingly critical as contact pitches are scaling down to 200 nm and beyond. To ensure reliable electrical connection, the total measurement uncertainty (TMU) of the associated overlay metrology must remain below 5 nm [1], [2]. Infrared overlay (IR OVL) metrology, which enables through-silicon measurements, faces unique challenges compared to traditional image-based overlay (IBO) techniques. This paper evaluates the TMU and trueness of state-of-the-art IR OVL tools and correlates their performance with IBO measurements conducted after removing the backside silicon of the bonded test vehicles, providing insight into the reliability of IR-based metrology in advanced bonding applications.

title: Mapping Technological Dimensions of XR-based Cognitive Augmentation Systems
authors: Mihail Terenti, Laura-Bianca Bilius, Andrei Braicov, Tatiana Veverița, Ilona Popovici, Radu-Daniel Vatavu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Extended Reality (XR) systems enable immersive interaction in hybrid physical-virtual environments, delivering new perceptual and cognitive experiences compared to traditional digital applications. However, research in this area remains fragmented due to the diversity of XR devices and interaction modalities underlying these experiences. This paper analyzes a corpus of twenty-seven scientific articles on XR-based cognitive augmentation through the lens of a framework involving device type and output modality. By using clustering analysis, we identify specific patterns in how these dimensions have been combined in prior XR systems designed for human cognitive augmentation. Our results reveal a strong dominance of head-mounted displays for visual output, while audio and haptics remain underexplored, a finding that reveals new opportunities for future research in this space.

title: Smart Mobility in the Institutional Trap: Analysis of Non-technological Implementation Barriers in Czech Public Administration
authors: Luke Bojčev, Radomíra Jordová
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: While the technological maturity of Smart Mobility solutions has accelerated rapidly, their effective deployment in the public sector remains quite stagnant. This paper challenges the prevailing technocratic narrative by arguing that the primary hurdles to Smart City development are not technological or financial but deeply institutional. Drawing on a unique qualitative dataset of semi structured interviews with city and regional representatives in the Czech Republic, we identify a systemic Institutional Trap. Our findings reveal that a culture of defensive procurement, political discontinuity, and organisational fragmentation (resortism) actively neutralises innovation. We demonstrate that current subsidy mechanisms, biased towards Capital Expenditure, create an Investment Trap that fosters unsustainable projects and deepens vendor lock-in. The study concludes that without a fundamental software upgrade of public administration specifically regarding human capacity and legislative agility the hardware of smart mobility will remain functionally unutilised.

title: An Adaptive NFT Fractionalization Framework with Rights Segregation and Dynamic Rights Management

title: A Software-based Fault Tolerance Mechanism for Matrix Multiplication Operations in Tensor Cores
authors: Robert Limas Sierra, Juan-David Guerrero-Balaguera, Josie E. Rodriguez Condia, Matteo Sonza Reorda
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract:
1Modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) are increasingly employed to enhance the performance of algorithms across scientific and machine learning domains. Given the importance of General Matrix Multiplication (GEMM) operations, GPUs feature specialized in-chip accelerators, such as Tensor Cores (TCUs), to speed them up. High-Performance Computing (HPC) and safety-critical sectors (e.g., automotive, space, and autonomous robotics) impose severe constraints concerning not only energy consumption, performance, and area but also reliability. Faults arising from advanced semiconductor technologies or sustained HPC workloads can silently propagate, potentially leading to catastrophic failures.

This work introduces a hardware-aware, software-based fault tolerance method to enhance the resilience of GEMM operations on TCUs. By leveraging TCU architecture and parallel operation distribution, the method enables efficient fault detection and mitigation. It utilizes redundant executions on TCU arithmetic cores (Dot-Product Units) to detect and, if required, correct fault effects. The method’s flexibility supports online detection and correction of transient and permanent hardware faults in TCU’s arithmetic units. Experimental results on real GPUs show the proposed mechanism introduces minimal and constant memory overhead and a negligible performance overhead (up to 1.13 times) across operand sizes. Thus, this solution offers an effective and complementary hardening strategy for TCU operations.

title: Effects of the Stress Between Substrate and GeTe Films in Phase-Change Material RF Switches_supp1-3698813.docx
authors: Libin Gao
publish: N/A
abstract: An RF switch based on germanium telluride (GeTe) as a phase-change material (PCM) has gained widespread application. The advancement in switch performance is attributed to the improvement in the quality of GeTe thin films and the realization of efficient heat transfer within the device. This study investigated the interfacial stress between different substrates and GeTe, and explored the effects of stress on the performance of the top heater structure switch devices. The results indicate that GeTe thin films grown on Sapphire substrates exhibit the highest quality. The film roughness measures 1.74 nm, with a low sheet resistance of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$23.8~\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula>/sq. The measured residual stress of the film is only 127.1 MPa. The top heater structure switch devices fabricated on Sapphire exhibit superior overall performance compared to those fabricated on other substrates. The switching pulse is 17.5 V, 130 ns, and the insertion loss is −0.57 dB@20 GHz. Among the two-port switches of traditional structure, the device fabricated on the silicon carbide (SiC) exhibits superior performance, with a switching pulse of 4.2 V, 200 ns, and an insertion loss of −1.62 dB@20 GHz. Overall, selecting suitable substrates and novel device structures is an effective approach to achieving high-quality films and enhancing switching performance.

title: Domain Adaptation for IMU Data to Enhance Objective Assessment of Friedreich Ataxia
authors: Minh Tran, Kanishka Ranaweera, Thang Ngo, Pubudu N. Pathirana, Sarah C. Milne, Malcolm Horne, Martin B. Delatycki, Louise A. Corben
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Friedreich Ataxia (FRDA) is an uncommon progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the nervous system that primarily affects motor coordination. Measuring disease severity is crucial, as it allows clinicians to monitor disease progression and make appropriate treatment decisions. By using inertial measurement units (IMUs) to capture the motion of individuals with FRDA, it has been possible to develop algorithms that objectively assess motor impairment. These studies have primarily employed conventional machine learning (ML) methods but have been limited by small datasets, which is an inherent challenge in researching rare diseases. To address this, we propose a robust deep learning framework that integrates heterogeneous data sources to enhance model performance. Our approach leverages a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture to automatically learn high-level representations from raw IMU signals, minimizing reliance on manual feature engineering. Central to our method is a two-stage training strategy that incorporates domain adversarial learning, enabling knowledge transfer between two IMU-based assessment tools: the Ataxia Instrumented Measures cup (AIM-C) and spoon (AIM-S). This strategy enhances learning from each device by exploiting shared underlying representations. The effectiveness of the framework was validated by comparing the coefficient of determination between clinical scores (the modified Friedreich's Ataxia Rating Scale) and model outputs from both standard CNNs and the proposed two-stage training approach. For the standard CNN models, the coefficients of determination were 0.53 for AIM-C and 0.46 for AIM-S. With the proposed two-stage training, these increased to 0.62 and 0.49, respectively, demonstrating clear performance gains through cross-device knowledge transfer.

title: Optically Tunable Fiber-Tip Nano-Optomechanical Resonator for Mass Sensing
authors: Yanping Chen, Qiao Zhou, Yu Liu, Mengqiang Zou
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Nanomechanical resonators operating at high frequencies are well-suited for demanding applications, particularly in the realm of ultrasensitive mass and force detection. For these applications, a continuous tunable frequency with a linear wide, linear range is highly desired. However, conventional electrically tunable methods have limitations of complex design, nonlinearity, and pull-in instability. The present work demonstrated a fiber-tip nano-optomechanical resonator that can be tuned using an all-optical method. This all-optical tuning method is realized through changing the laser power, which is fast and convenient. The nano-optomechanical resonator fabricated at the fiber tip is further used for mass sensing. Its fabrication process includes transferring multilayer graphene to the end of a hollow-core optical fiber segment that is fusion-spliced to a single-mode fiber, and then processing the suspended graphene membrane using a focused ion beam (FIB) to create a trampoline-type structure. The proposed optically tuned fiber-tip mass sensor has an all-optical-fiber integrated structure and thus is compact. Small masses are detected by monitoring changes in the nanomechanical resonator's resonance frequency. The mass detection of the nano-optomechanical resonator is demonstrated by depositing Au atoms on the surface of the trampoline-type multilayer graphene (MLG) film. This miniature, optically tunable, all-fiber mass sensor exhibited a resolution of 2.381 fg within its linear operating range at room temperature. The results show that the optically tunable trampoline-type MLG resonator has enormous potential in ultrasensitive detection for biosensing and gas sensing.

title: Field Performance Analysis of Bifacial Photovoltaic Modules Using Ground Reflection Spectra and View Factor Models
authors: Lei Wang, Xin Huang, Xuandong Xiong, Zhen Zhang, Jiayang Qian, Xinyue Cao, Xiang'an Zeng, Yanfang Zhou, Peng Quan
publish: IEEE Journal of Photovoltaics
abstract: The field performance of bifacial photovoltaic (PV) modules is influenced by both the ground reflection spectrum and the irradiance distribution on the backside surface. The reflection spectrum is dependent on various factors, including air mass and ground surface type, which complicates its evaluation. In this article, a simplified yet accurate reflection spectrum model based on readily available meteorological parameters is proposed and introduced to calculate the reflected solar spectrum for engineering applications. The impact of the reflection spectrum on the performance of bifacial modules is analyzed and quantified using the spectrum mismatch factor, which measures the degree of deviation between the actual and standard spectra. In addition, a calculation method for the output power of the module is introduced, incorporating the view factor model for backside irradiance. Experimental designs conducted on grassland, soil, and concrete demonstrate the accuracy and applicability of both the reflection spectrum model and the power calculation method. The results indicate that in all three scenarios, the reflection model demonstrates high accuracy, with an average error within 10%. After correction with the spectral mismatch factor, the average accuracy of total power prediction for bifacial modules in three scenarios increased from 91.81% to 96.41%; compared with the traditional fixed broadband reflectance method, the relative errors of power prediction in the three scenarios were reduced from 14.75%, 14.68%, and 5.21% to 2.67%, 1.90%, and 1.44%, significantly improving prediction accuracy and enhancing the fault diagnosis capability in intelligent operation and maintenance of PV systems.

title: GRU-Enhanced Whale-Optimization-Based Channel Parameter Tracking for Joint Communication and Positioning Systems
authors: Zhiwei Liang, Wei Wang, Ting Sun, P. Takis Mathiopoulos, Yisheng An
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Distance-based localization using Time-of-Arrival (ToA) estimation is fundamentally constrained by the accuracy of channel parameter tracking in non-stationary, high-mobility environments. In such scenarios, the estimation landscape becomes highly non-convex and multi-modal, rendering conventional gradient-driven trackers inadequate. This paper proposes a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)-enhanced Whale Optimization-based Tracking Algorithm (WoTA), a robust framework that synergistically fuses meta-heuristic global optimization with recursive filtering. Specifically, an Opposition-based Learning Whale Optimization Algorithm (OBL-WOA) is integrated to adaptively explore the complex cost function surface, effectively initializing the tracker and preventing local optima entrapment in multi-modal distributions. To overcome the limitations of manual parameter tuning in traditional WOA and Kalman Filters (KF), a GRU-based adaptive mechanism is introduced. This mechanism learns the temporal dependencies of optimization residuals to dynamically recalibrate the filter’s noise covariance and optimization hyperparameters, thereby enhancing resilience against model uncertainties. By coupling OBL-WOA’s global exploration with the KF’s exploitation of temporal kinematics, WoTA achieves smoothed and precise state transitions. Comprehensive simulations and real-world channel sounding measurements demonstrate that WoTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, such as SAGE and KEST. These results demonstrate that the proposed integration of heuristic exploration and recursive refinement offers a scalable and accurate framework for channel parameter tracking in dynamic multipath environments, thereby supporting reliable 6G wireless systems.

title:
Geometry-Dependent Current Injection Mechanisms and Contact Length Scaling in p-Type WSe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Field-Effect Transistors
authors: Szu-Huan Hsu, Yiming Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This work investigates the impact of source/drain (S/D) contact geometry on carrier injection mechanisms and contact scalability in p-type monolayer (ML) WSe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> field-effect transistors (FETs). Density functional theory (DFT) is employed to analyze Pd–WSe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> interfaces with three geometries: top, top+edge, and C-type contacts. The results indicate that the C-type contact exhibits a reduced van der Waals (vdW) gap, stronger metal–semiconductor orbital hybridization, and a lower effective tunneling barrier. These interfacial properties are incorporated into calibrated three-dimensional (3D) device simulations to evaluate device-level transport and contact length (L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</sub>) scaling behavior. Compared with the top and top+edge contacts, the C-type configuration achieves ~20% higher on-state current (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</sub>) under identical bias conditions. Furthermore, a shorter transfer length (L<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">T</sub>) and reduced contact resistance (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</sub>) are obtained, resulting in improved immunity to aggressive LC scaling. The results identify contact geometry as an important physical factor governing injection and scalability in p-type 2D FETs, and demonstrate the potential of C-type contacts for enabling high-performance and scalable WSe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> devices.

title: Hall Sensor Zero-Position Compensation Algorithm for PMaSynRM Under Field Oriented Control Scheme
authors: Shenghui Deng, Yan Wang, Xiaojun Lv, Xianqing Wu, Wenqi Lu, Junjie Wang, Keye Xiao, Zhichang Liu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Permanent-magnet-assisted synchronous reluctance motors (PMaSynRMs) with ferrite permanent magnets (PMs) provide a cost-effective solution for electric vehicle applications while maintaining competitive power density. Meanwhile, low-cost Hall sensors have attracted increasing attention in field-oriented control (FOC) systems. However, existing Hall-based algorithms are mainly developed for permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) and are not directly applicable to PMaSynRMs. In PMaSynRMs, armature reaction under load conditions significantly affects the air-gap magnetic field distribution, leading to a current-dependent deviation in the electrical zero-position of Hall signals. This degrades rotor field-oriented accuracy and may affect system stability. Focusing on the characteristics of this type of machine, this paper investigates the physical mechanism of the Hall zero-position shift phenomenon. By comparing the Hall signal triggering characteristics between PMaSynRMs and PMSMs, and deriving an analytical model of the shift angle based on electromagnetic theory, the deviation originates from air-gap flux distortion induced by armature reaction. Based on this, an offline identification-based Hall zero-position compensation method is proposed within a FOC scheme incorporating maximum torque per ampere (MTPA) control strategy, for real-time correction of rotor position estimation. Experimental validation is conducted on a 1.5-kW PMaSynRM prototype platform. The results show that the proposed method effectively reduces rotor position estimation errors caused by the air-gap magnetic field distortion. Within the tested load range from no-load to twice the rated load (6.4N·m), the compensated drive system maintains stable operation under all tested operating conditions. The experimental results further indicate the existence of the Hall zero-position shift phenomenon in PMaSynRMs and demonstrate that it can be mitigated using the proposed offline identification-based compensation strategy within the investigated operating conditions, providing a reference approach for further research on Hall sensors in FOC systems for PMaSynRMs.

title: Advancements in Millimeter wave and Sub-THz Flexible Devices for Industrial Applications: From Sensors to Intelligent Surfaces
authors: Rasoul Ebrahimzadeh, Suren Gigoyan, Gaozhi Xiao, Stephen Lang, Milad Entezami, Seyed Ali Hosseini Farahabadi, Mohammad-Reza Nezhad-Ahmadi
publish: IEEE Journal of Radio Frequency Identification
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of a scalable manufacturing framework for advanced flexible radio-frequency (RF) devices based on screen-printing technology. The study systematically evaluates the electrical and electromagnetic performance of multiple conformal components spanning microwave to sub-terahertz frequency ranges. First, a low-loss flexible microstrip transmission line (FML) is designed and experimentally characterized from 1 GHz to 20 GHz under various bending conditions. The results demonstrate minimal degradation in S-parameters, with phase variation remaining below 10° at a 45° bend, confirming its suitability for high-speed digital and analog interconnects. Building upon this platform, a whispering gallery mode (WGM) resonator-based sensor is developed for liquid characterization, specifically for monitoring alcohol concentration in water. The sensor maintains stable operation under mechanical deformation, highlighting its applicability in confined and dynamically varying environments. Notably, this work demonstrates that phase variation provides a highly sensitive and robust sensing metric, offering improved immunity to environmental noise compared to conventional amplitude- or frequency-based approaches. Furthermore, the proposed fabrication process is extended to millimeter-wave applications through the realization of an ultrathin dual-polarized conformal transmissive band-stop frequency selective surface (FSS) operating at 79.25 GHz and 84 GHz, as well as reflective intelligent surfaces (RIS) at 30 GHz and 60 GHz enabling high-gain beam and beam redirection for enhancing signal-to-noise-ratio and field of view (FoV) in wireless communications. Furthermore, the FSS maintains functionality under extreme bending conditions up to 80°. Ultimately, to assess scalability toward higher frequencies, dielectric properties of candidate substrates are characterized using vector network analyzer (VNA)-based quasi-optical and terahertz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) methods. The simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and versatility of the proposed screen-printing process make it a promising candidate for next-generation flexible communication, sensing, and intelligent surface applications.

title: End-to-End Analog Edge AI Architecture For Scalable Energy-Efficient On-Chip Learning
authors: Mohamed Watfa, Alberto Garcia-Ortiz, Gilles Sassatelli
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
abstract: Equilibrium Propagation (EP) offers an attractive learning framework for analog and mixed-signal neural hardware by expressing learning as a perturbation of physical equilibrium dynamics. However, practical deployment has been limited by sensitivity to voltage variations, device non-idealities, and learning rules that are poorly matched to circuit constraints. As a result, existing EP hardware demonstrations remain difficult to scale beyond small or idealized settings. This work presents an end-to-end mixed-signal learning architecture based on EP and a hardware-driven analysis of update rules, batching, and gradient quantization under realistic analog and memristive conditions. A systematic evaluation shows that several commonly used EP formulations fail to provide stable learning when circuit effects are considered. To address this, a quantized reformulation derived from the EP adjoint update is introduced and implemented using switched-capacitor charge-domain accumulation, enabling intrinsic batching and low-precision operation. SPICE-level simulations on Iris and MNIST demonstrate stable convergence with 2–4 bit gradients and consistent performance gains over prior hardware-oriented EP implementations, indicating that scalable on-chip EP training is achievable under realistic hardware constraints.

title: DRFMamba: Multi-focus Microscopic Image Registration Fusion Based on Vision Mamba
authors: Jie Song, Xinyu Xie, Qiuhui Yang, Benjamin Koon Kei Ng, Wei Ke, Tong Tong, Yue Sun, Zitong Yu, Tao Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Microscopic images provide detailed views of microstructures. They enable the observation and analysis of small biological samples, including their morphology and dynamics. However, these images often suffer from multi-focus and misalignment issues. To obtain clear and accurate images, registration and fusion are essential preprocessing steps. Currently, no unified network exists for multi-focus microscopic image registration and fusion, posing a significant challenge in extracting useful information from misaligned images. To address this challenge, we propose a high-performance model, Deep Registration Fusion Mamba (DRFMamba). The framework is divided into two stages: affine registration and fusion. In the first stage, to handle global rigid misalignment, we introduce the Deep Learning Vision Mamba Registration (DLVR) network for coarse affine registration of multi-focus microscopic images. In the DLVR network, a Linear Deformable Convolutional Vision Mamba (LDCVmamba) block is designed to capture both local and global displacement and deformation features from misaligned image pairs. This module enhances geometric disparity perception in in-focus regions while suppressing defocus interference, thereby improving affine registration accuracy. In the Deep Learning Vision Mamba Fusion (DLVF) network, a Laplacian Pyramid enhances multi-scale high-frequency representations, while the Vision Mamba (Vim) captures global structural features. Moreover, a Bidirectional Adaptive Cross-Alignment Fusion (ACAF) module achieves pixel-level feature alignment between image pairs, enabling bidirectional exchange of complementary information and adaptive fusion, further enhancing overall fusion performance. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that DRFMamba exhibits strong registration and fusion capabilities on multi-focus image datasets. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/JIESONG9812/DRFMamba</uri>.
