title: Modelling Bicycle Rider Behavior in a Smart City
authors: Leon J.M. Rothkrantz, Siska Fitrianie
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Delft is a student city with many daily cyclists traveling between the train station and the university campus. As the city works toward becoming an eco-city, it is important to understand how bicycle traffic works in order to design safer and better bike paths. Most current traffic models are based on cars and do not fit cyclist behavior, especially during busy hours. The results show that bicycle traffic is highly dynamic, characterized by fluctuating densities and short but intense congestion waves during rush hours, often triggered by train arrivals and lecture schedules. A distinct behavioral category, termed the “hasty cyclist,” was identified. These cyclists tend to prioritize speed over safety, frequently overtaking others, forming informal lanes, and occasionally ignoring traffic signals. Questionnaire analysis suggests that this behavior is influenced by both situational factors such as time pressure and individual traits, rather than personality alone. Overall, the study highlights fundamental differences between bicycle and car traffic, including flexible lane formation and highly variable speeds. It also introduces an initial behavioral framework for cyclist modeling. The findings underline the need for dedicated bicycle traffic models to support safer infrastructure design and sustainable urban mobility in the city.

title: Level of Confidence in Using an Android Robot as an Assistant in Robotics Microclasses
authors: Jose Luis Ordoñez-Avila, Maria Elena Perdomo, José Fernando Ibarra Dubón, Karben Selim Mejía López, Jesús Humberto Rosales Agüero, Isabel Mendez
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This study evaluates the effectiveness of ADAI, an interactive android-based teaching assistant, in fostering trust and reducing mistrust among engineering students during micro-classes. ADAI integrates computer vision, natural language processing, and speech synthesis to deliver adaptive explanations, detect student participation, and administer assessments via QR codes. A mixed-methods approach assessed trust and mistrust after student interaction with the system, using validated instruments focused on reliability, safety, and perceived risk. Statistical analyses included Shapiro-Wilk tests, Pearson correlations, and paired-samples t-tests. Results showed significant correlations between trust and mistrust dimensions, indicating interdependence between these perceptions. Paired-samples t-tests revealed significantly higher trust than mistrust scores, reflecting a generally positive reception of ADAI. Although exposure to robotics slightly increased perceived mistrust compared to non-exposure, trust levels remained stable, and no gender-based differences were observed. Overall, the findings suggest that ADAI supports interactive learning while maintaining positive perceptions of reliability and safety, highlighting its potential for enhancing engagement and trust in engineering education.

title: A Novel Trace Transfer Learning Framework to Improve Dataset Quality and Reduce Modeling Effort
authors: Xu Han, Alexander Suer, Marcella Miller, Xiaodong Jia, James Moyne, Jimmy Iskandar, Fei Li, Arvind Shankar Raman
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Semiconductor manufacturing processes exhibit high variability in sensor traces across chambers, recipes, tools, hindering the deployment of robust fault detection (FD) models and limiting performance in data-scarce contexts. This study proposes a Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)-based trace transfer learning (TL) framework that unifies heterogeneous trace data to enable model reuse and reduce modeling effort. The method features (1) a trace transfer learning framework with trace distribution adjustment, (2) a context similarity metric to assess transfer suitability, and (3) a feature boundary segmentation mechanism for refined transfer paths. Validated on a public metal-etch dataset, the proposed approach reduces the false positive rate from 0.682 to 0.009 and improves the area under the curve (AUC) to 0.973. With the proposed TL framework, the number of FD models required across diverse data contexts can be significantly reduced, thereby lowering modeling and maintenance effort without compromising model performance.

title: VITA: A Voice-based Intelligent Transformer-GMM Assessment Framework for Parkinson’s Disease Diagnosis
authors: Saghar Shafaati, Ziaeddin Beheshtifard
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Parkinson’s disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disease whose early diagnosis is critical for timely intervention. Traditional diagnoses rely on clinic motor assessments, and a delayed and wrong classification can therefore arise through overlaps in symptoms with neurological disease syndromes. In this work, a new AI-facilitated mixed model for PD diagnosing is proposed, VITA (Voice-based Intelligent Transformer-GMM Assessment), fusing Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for clustering with a probabilistic basis and deep learning with a Transformer for feature extraction and classification. The model was trained with two publicly available PD datasets, including 756 voice recordings and 252 speakers and 756 voice recordings and 252 speakers with Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS) for both overall and motor evaluations. The model proposed achieved a 90.18% accuracy and 92.91% F1-score, with robust performance with a range of evaluation measures. This framework introduces a nonintrusive, transparent, and scalable AI model for early PD diagnosis through complex preprocessing, dimensionality reduction, and XAI techniques. Involving interpretability with SHAP ensures model prediction transparency, enhancing trust in clinicians. All such observations reveal AI-powered PD diagnostics' capabilities in early detection, paving the way for future work in multimodal data fusion, real-time telemonitoring, and AI in clinic environments.

title: Research on Fast Identification Methods for Transformer Faults Based on Wavelet Transform
authors: Yaojun Zhang, Qiantong Ye, Tong Wu, Jiahao Lu, Xinxiang Lai, Sizhe Chen, Yuxin Yang, Zhiyong Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: When an external fault occurs outside the protection zone of a transformer, saturation of current transformers may lead to incorrect operation of the differential protection. To address this issue, this paper proposes a time-difference criterion based on wavelet analysis to distinguish between internal and external faults in transformer differential protection. First, the impact of current transformer saturation on transformer differential protection is analyzed. Second, the method of wavelet transform for signal singularity detection is investigated using the modulus maximum principle, and appropriate threshold rules are selected to denoise signals contaminated with noise. Third, the wavelet modulus maximum principle is applied to accurately determine both the fault inception time and the emergence time of the differential current. Finally, a novel criterion incorporating the negative-sequence component is proposed to prevent incorrect operation of the differential protection during the transition from an external to an internal fault. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: The Integration of Causal Information From Changing Operating Conditions Reduces the Robustness of Remaining Useful Life Prediction
authors: Yi Liu, Yao Ma, Yunhui Mei
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate remaining useful life (RUL) prediction of aluminum electrolytic capacitors is crucial for ensuring the reliability and safety of power electronic systems. However, existing mainstream data-driven methods, such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, inherently learn statistical correlations from historical data. This makes them susceptible to significant performance degradation when faced with dynamically changing operating conditions, as they fail to capture the underlying physical causal mechanisms. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a causal information-guided LSTM (C-LSTM) framework. This framework structurally integrates a predefined causal graph, encoding the physical degradation mechanisms of the capacitor, with a Graph Neural Network (GNN). At each time step, the GNN performs message passing on this causal graph to learn causally related feature representations; these representations are then fed into the LSTM network to model the temporal dynamics of the degradation process. Validation results on an accelerated aging experimental platform show that, especially under step-change stress conditions, C-LSTM significantly outperforms traditional LSTM and physics-informed neural network (PINN) baseline models in terms of prediction accuracy and robustness. Beyond prediction accuracy, the C-LSTM framework also possesses stronger interpretability and enables counterfactual analysis, providing a new technical approach for proactive maintenance and optimized operational decision-making.

title: Sub/super-synchronous Oscillation Suppression Strategy Based on Virtual Impedance Method
authors: Nan Wang, Wenlong Ren, Wanjie Li, Liucheng Hao, Lixin Zhang, Yuwen Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale development of new energy power systems, the widespread grid connection of direct-drive wind turbine generators has made the problem of sub/super synchronous oscillations increasingly prominent, seriously threatening the safe and stable operation of the power grid. Therefore, researching effective oscillation suppression strategies has become a current research hotspot and key demand in the field of new energy power. This paper focuses on the sub/super synchronous oscillation problems that are prone to occur during the operation of direct-drive wind power systems, deeply analyzes the mechanism and influencing factors of the oscillation, and then proposes a sub/super synchronous oscillation suppression control strategy based on virtual impedance. Finally, a simulation model of the direct-drive wind power system is built to verify the feasibility of the proposed control strategy. The results show that the designed suppression strategy can effectively suppress the sub/super synchronous oscillations on the side of new energy generating units under variable operating conditions.

title: Evaluating Barriers to Pan-European Traffic Data Exchange: Metadata, Structural Quality, and Consumer Usability and Accessibility
authors: Petr Bureš, Chrysostomos Mylonas, Maria Stavara, Jan Vlčinský, Apostolos Vouitsis, Evangelos Mitsakis
publish: 2026 Smart City Symposium Prague (SCSP)
abstract: Making traffic information accessible on a panEuropean scale presents significant challenges for data publishers and consumers. Our research evaluated the real-world experience of unprivileged data consumers accessing such information across Europe. We examined 31 National Access Points (NAPs), 39 catalogue records, and 42 distributions from 22 publishers, grading them on an A-F scale with a focus on Safety Related Traffic Information (SRTI). Results showed that while 21 records received B grades, half of distributions lacked proper DATEX II XML schemas, and many protocols exhibited implementation flaws in content encoding, authentication, and data validation. Location referencing frequently lacked crucial data for map visualization. Our findings confirm that while panEuropean traffic information exchange functions at a basic level, it faces substantial quality and accessibility barriers. Improvements require better publisher education and formal evaluation processes. Though focused on SRTI, our approach could enhance data consumer experience across all mobility services, including public transportation.

title: Impact of Grid-Forming Wind Turbine Penetration on the Dynamic Support Capabilities of Hybrid Direct-Drive Wind Farms
authors: Zhijie Zeng, Yajie Zhang, Shijie Fu, Zhixiang Zou, Kuosong Chen, Puyu Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Traditional wind turbines employing grid-following control strategies struggle to actively support the stable operation of new power systems, whereas wind turbines based on grid-forming control possess the capability for stable operation in weak grids. In future wind farm development, the hybrid operation of grid-following and grid-forming wind turbines presents a solution balancing economic and technical advantages. However, the optimal capacity share of grid-forming turbines within such hybrid farms lacks clear quantitative guidance. To address this, this paper establishes a detailed model of a direct-drive wind farm incorporating both grid-following and grid-forming controls based on the PSCAD/EMTDC simulation platform. It systematically investigates the impact of the grid-forming turbine capacity ratio on the dynamic performance of the hybrid wind farm. By establishing three typical test scenarios-grid frequency disturbance, single-phase-to-ground fault, and two-phase short-circuit fault-the voltage support and frequency regulation capabilities of the hybrid farm are compared and analyzed as the grid-forming proportion varies from 0% to 100%. Simulation results indicate that increasing the capacity share of grid-forming turbines significantly enhances the system's dynamic support capabilities, but this improvement exhibits diminishing returns. When the grid-forming capacity share reaches approximately 20%-30%, substantial performance gains can be achieved at a relatively lower cost. The insights revealed in this paper regarding the influence of the grid-forming turbine ratio on the voltage and frequency support capabilities of hybrid direct-drive wind farms can provide valuable design references for the capacity planning and economical configuration of practical hybrid wind farms.

title: Design and refinement of a non-contact capacitive sensor system for hand and wrist kinematics
authors: Humberto Alves Mesquita, Wanghley Soares Martins, Guillermo Alvarez Bestard, Fábio Henrique Monteiro Oliveira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: This work presents the design and hardware evolution of a non-contact capacitive sensing system for hand and wrist kinematic assessment in Parkinson’s disease. The system employs PS25454 EPIC capacitive sensors combined with a custom analog front-end to detect displacement variations without physical contact. The hardware architecture evolved through four development stages, including an initial feasibility PCB, Arduino Due and Arduino Mega acquisition platforms, and a final ESP32-based architecture incorporating wireless connectivity and embedded processing capabilities. The progressive hardware refinement improved signal stability, modularity, and system integration, demonstrating the feasibility of a low-cost, scalable, and non-invasive platform for objective motor monitoring and connected biomedical instrumentation.

title: Probabilistic Constraint Shutdown (PCS) Informer at Extreme Low Temperature for Wind Power Forecasting
authors: Shiyu Lin, Hongshan Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Extreme low temperature (ELT) may lead to large-scale wind turbine (WT) off-grid. Traditional power forecasting methods do not consider the ELT influence on WT, resulting in a large number of power shortages. A power forecasting method based on probabilistic constraint shutdown Informer under ELT is proposed. Firstly, an ELT shutdown event identification model of WT based on temperature change rate and temperature kernel multi-head probsparse self-attention is proposed. Secondly, the ELT shutdown probability of WT is embedded into the power forecasting model. The multi-task learning strategy is designed, and the accurate forecasting of wind power in ELT scene is realized by sharing the encoder structure. It is verified by the data of a 100MW wind farm in northern China. The results show that the forecasting MAE of the proposed method is 5.18MW in the ELT, which reduces the power shortage of 54.565MW compared with the traditional method.

title: A Multi-Period Coordinated Load Restoration Strategy Based on Electro-Hydro Mobile Energy Storage
authors: Fan Bu, Yujing Li, Zhisheng Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In order to realize the rapid recovery of important loads in urban distribution network (DN) under extreme events, this paper proposes a strategy based on vehicle-to-grid (V2G) to use electricity and hydrogen mobile resources to realize the load recovery of DN. Firstly, the fault information and available resources of the DN layer, the vehicle information with uncertainty and available energy of the traffic network layer were collected, and the path planning model and the load recovery model were built respectively. Secondly, considering the penalty items such as network loss and user driving cost, an integrated load recovery model with multi-period cooperation was established. Finally, a 34-node road network and an IEEE 69-node DN are used to verify the effectiveness of the proposed model.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Online Conformal State-of-Health Prediction via Adaptive Quantile Alignment for Reliable Energy Storage Operation
authors: Bo Peng, Lei Liu, Hongwei Zhao, Qiuju Chen, Bin Li, Long Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate state-of-health (SOH) prediction with reliable uncertainty quantification is essential for the safe and efficient operation of battery energy storage systems (BESS). However, existing methods often struggle with non-stationary degradation dynamics in real-world scenarios. This paper proposes an Adaptive Quantile Alignment (AQA) method for online conformal prediction of battery SOH, which dynamically recalibrates prediction intervals by leveraging local volatility patterns and empirical quantile information from recent observations. Specifically, we design a volatility-aware lookback mechanism that automatically adjusts the historical window length, combined with a regularization term that anchors the conformal threshold to a weighted estimate of recent score quantiles. Extensive experiments on two public lithium-ion battery datasets demonstrate that AQA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art online CP methods, achieving substantially narrower and more stable prediction intervals across multiple base forecasters while rigorously maintaining the target 90% coverage rate.

title: ForgeBench: A Machine Learning Benchmark Suite and Auto-Generation Framework for Next-Generation HLS Tools
authors: Andy Wanna, Hanqiu Chen, Cong Callie Hao
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) has gained traction in hardware design, yet two key limitations prevent it from becoming mainstream. ❶ First , existing HLS benchmarks are outdated and unrepresentative of modern machine learning (ML) workloads. Legacy suites such as MachSuite [1] and Rosetta [2] rely on outdated algorithms, while Rodinia [3] and Poly-Bench [4] were designed for GPUs and software compilers, respectively, and thus offer limited relevance to HLS evaluation. Even recent efforts like HLSyn [5] exclude modern architectures such as Large Language Models (LLMs). Meanwhile, the research community increasingly evaluates HLS tools using advanced ML models through ad-hoc, private implementations, making fair and reproducible comparisons impossible without a standardized, open-source benchmark. ❷ Second , current HLS tools are accelerator-oriented , targeting simple standalone functions and generating one-off accelerators via static dataflow analysis. However, modern ML hardware design is architecture-oriented , requiring general-purpose architectures with flexible dataflow control, multi-application mapping, and complex design hierarchies featuring shared module extraction and reuse. This mismatch between tools and design practice limits HLS productivity. To address these limitations, we propose ForgeBench , an ML-centric HLS benchmark suite and generation framework for next-generation HLS tool design and evaluation. Our key contributions are: (1) an open-source, extensible HLS design generation framework supporting easy integration of new designs and applications; (2) over 10,000 high-quality, diverse ML-representative HLS benchmarks that expose limitations of modern HLS frameworks on ML workloads; and (3) an architecture-oriented benchmark suite featuring pairs of HLS designs with manually implemented module-level reuse and mappings, serving as a baseline for evaluating future modular HLS tools. We demonstrate the diversity of our generated designs across latency and resource usage, and show the potential for module reuse within HLS designs. ForgeBench is open-sourced at https://github.com/hchen799/ForgeBench .

title: A Real-Time Drone Detection System Using Hybrid Deep Learning YCFANet
authors: Siba Sundar Das, Sai Krishna Beeraka, Charulata Palai, Pradeep Kumar Jena
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The timely detection of low-altitude aerial threats is a critical requirement in modern air-defense and security operations. Small and fast-moving unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), particularly drones, pose significant challenges to conventional surveillance systems due to their limited visual signatures, high mobility, and susceptibility to environmental variations. This paper presents YCFANet, a hybrid deep learning framework designed for real-time drone detection in UAV imagery, with a specific emphasis on airspace surveillance and early warning applications. The proposed framework is built upon a YOLOv8 backbone and integrates weather-aware data enhancement techniques to improve robustness under diverse illumination and atmospheric conditions. To strengthen the localization of small and dynamic aerial targets, the standard YOLO neck and detection head are replaced with a CBAM-enhanced SlimNeck and a multi-branch FRMHead, respectively. Additionally, an adaptive focal loss is employed to mitigate class imbalance and enhance detection reliability for drones compared to other airborne objects, including airplanes, helicopters, and birds. Experimental results demonstrate that YCFANet achieves a drone detection accuracy of 95.9 % and an overall average accuracy of 96.8 % across all classes, while sustaining a real-time processing speed of 24.7 frames per second. Both quantitative evaluations, including confusion matrices and precision-recall curves, and qualitative analyses confirm stable performance under occlusion, motion blur, and adverse weather conditions. These findings indicate that YCFANet can function effectively as an AI-based perception module for UAV-assisted aerial surveillance and defense-oriented threat monitoring systems.

title: Women in STEM: Fab Academy-Based Learning Between Latin America and Africa
authors: Pablo C. Herrera, Macarena Valenzuela-Zubiaur, Vaneza Caycho, Marilú Pineda-Gutierrez, Marcela Duharte
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: We analyzed the ecosystem of the global online STEM program Fab Academy, which graduated 1,697 students in 63 countries between 2009 and 2025 in the Northern (49%) and Southern (51%) Hemispheres. We characterized the profiles of female graduates from Latin America and Africa, regions that gave rise to the Fab Academy-based learning model but show a lower-than-average percentage of women. We identified differences in profiles linked to Architecture and the Built Environment, and Creative Arts (Latin America) and engineering (Africa), with STEM development in the academic context (Latin America) and the industrial context (Africa). Despite its challenges, Fab Lab-based learning demonstrates its impact on women linked to STEM with a model that continues to promote entrepreneurship and management by women in this globalized program.

title: Voltage Euclidean Distance and Convolutional Neural Network-Based Topology Identification for Distribution Networks
authors: Jian Li, Yao Man, Jiahong Xu, Fangyuan Si
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Traditional topology identification methods in distribution networks often suffer from limited accuracy and computational inefficiency, especially under frequent topology variation conditions. To overcome these limitations, a novel voltage Euclidean distance and convolutional neural network-based topology identification method for distribution networks is proposed. First, a Euclidean distance matrix derived from node voltage sequences is constructed to characterize the network topology. Then, a CNN-based classification model consisting of two convolutional layers and two pooling layers is developed. The model performs hierarchical feature extraction from large-scale measurement data and maps the extracted features to topology classes. A simulation dataset containing 15 representative topology classes is established based on the IEEE 33-node standard distribution test feeder. The dataset is used to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. The results demonstrate that under frequent topology variation conditions, the proposed model achieves an identification accuracy of 100%. The average inference time is 24.79 ms.

title: Multi-Time Scale Scheduling of Hybrid Energy Storage in High-PV Microgrids Under Stepped Carbon Trading
authors: Jiahao Zhai, Hongru Shi, Lijun Ma, Yan Ma, Bowen Tan, Keyu Wei
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address renewable intermittency and battery limitations, this paper proposes a multi-time scale optimal scheduling strategy for an electrical-hydrogen Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS). A stepped carbon trading mechanism, day-ahead economic dispatch, and MPC-based intra-day rolling optimization are established. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed HESS effectively absorbs surplus renewable energy. Compared with single battery schemes, the strategy reduces total operation cost by 16.7% while minimizing carbon emissions, verifying its superiority in high renewable penetration scenarios.

title: An Optimization Strategy for Frequency Regulation of a Dual Modal Cooperative Photovoltaic Generation System
authors: Dong Zhang, Kai Xie, ZhaoYi Jin, GenJun Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Driven by the global energy transition and China’s "dual carbon" targets, large scale grid integration of photovoltaic (PV) generation is exacerbating the problem of insufficient inertial support. Existing virtual inertia control (VIC) and droop control (DC) schemes—each designed in isolation, with limited parameter adaptability and inadequate consideration of AC–DC coupling— show degraded performance at high penetration levels, leading to sub synchronous oscillations, power overshoot, and even inverter tripping. To overcome sluggish dynamic response and poor multi scenario adaptability, this paper proposes an enhanced frequency regulation strategy that dynamically couples VIC with DC. Real time sensing of frequency deviation establishes an associative function between VIC gain and the deviation, enabling dynamic inertia tuning to curb the initial rate of change of frequency (RoCoF). Concurrently, DC parameters are linked to RoCoF so that adaptive gain adjustment reduces the maximum frequency deviation. Simulations confirm that the proposed method significantly outperforms conventional strategies in suppressing frequency dips and improving regulation accuracy, thereby offering an efficient solution for the stable operation of high penetration PV grids.

title: AccelOrb: FPGA Acceleration of Orb v2 for Fast Molecular Dynamics
authors: Sunjae Kim, Gwanhong Park, Jeawoo Lim, Faaiz Asim, Jongeun Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Machine learning force fields (MLFFs) offer high accuracy at reduced computational cost, but their repeated evaluation at every molecular dynamics (MD) timestep leads to prohibitive runtime, limiting simulations to short physical timescales. In this work, we accelerate Orb v2, a Graph Neural Network–based MLFF, by targeting its dominant Attention Interaction Network (AIN), which accounts for approximately 97% of inference time. We identify on-chip memory constraints and a latency-memory trade-off in block-based computation as the key challenges to efficient acceleration. To address these challenges, we propose AccelOrb, a memory-aware FPGA acceleration of AIN based on a single streaming kernel that employs a non-uniform tiling strategy to balance latency and memory footprint, and selectively maps parameters beyond BRAM. Implemented on a resource-constrained AMD Alveo U50 FPGA, our approach achieves 9.19× and 8.18× speedup over CPU and GPU baselines while operating within tight power and resource budgets.

title: Fault Analysis and Control Measures of Equipment in Electrochemical Energy Storage Power Stations
authors: Yifeng Qi, Xin Zhang, Gang Li, Zhentao Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: As a prominent representative of new energy storage technologies, electrochemical energy storage has experienced explosive growth in installed capacity in recent years due to its advantages of fast response speed and flexible configuration. It has become a core technological pathway to address the bottleneck of high-penetration renewable energy integration. However, with the continuous expansion of energy storage system scales, safety operation issues have become increasingly prominent, posing a critical challenge to the healthy development of the industry. Based on hands-on operational and maintenance practices, this paper systematically sorts out and summarizes the typical fault characteristics and control measures of large-scale electrochemical energy storage systems, focusing on battery operation mechanisms, fault response characteristics, diagnostic identification methods, and safety control strategies. Firstly, starting from the selection of electrochemical energy storage battery technology routes, it clarifies the basic electrochemical operating principles of lithium-ion batteries and analyzes the root causes of frequent failures. Secondly, based on the component composition of the energy storage battery system, it classifies common typical faults, analyzes the system's failure modes and potential failure risks, and proposes efficient response strategies during service. Finally, from the two dimensions of early warning monitoring methods and technical supervision systems, it reviews the current cutting-edge research progress in the field of electrochemical energy storage system safety and explores the feasibility of ensuring the safe and stable operation of energy storage power stations from a technical supervision perspective. The paper concludes by summarizing the main challenges and unresolved key issues in current research.

title: Contributions on the creation of a Forestry Digital Twin through procedural generation and multimodal data capture
authors: Vițega Mihai, Turcu Corneliu Octavian
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: In the context of global environmental awareness, a critical demand for new dedicated tools for forest monitoring and ecological conservation has emerged; however, the evolution of these technologies is significantly constrained by the chronic scarcity of high-fidelity, labeled datasets within complex, unstructured forest environments. This research paper proposes a robust methodology that leverages cutting-edge 3D graphics engines, specifically Unreal Engine 5, to synthesize a photorealistic Digital Twin framework for diverse forest ecosystems. By integrating procedural generation tools, this approach overcomes the logistical and financial limitations of manual data collection, facilitating the automated extraction of comprehensive multimodal datasets. These datasets, comprising high-resolution RGB imagery, depth maps, and pixel-accurate semantic segmentations, provide the requisite data for training deep learning architectures. Beyond data synthesis, the ultimate objective of this framework is to pioneer a paradigm of data-driven forest management. This platform enables the high-fidelity simulation of long-term dendrological growth, complex silvicultural interventions, and the multi-faceted impacts of climate change, all within a strictly controlled and repeatable virtual environment, thereby bridging the gap between theoretical ecology and actionable computational forestry.

title: AI-Based Resonance Avoidance in Military Vehicle Components
authors: Santosh Kumar Panda, Ratikanta Nayak
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Army vehicles operate in extreme environmental conditions, making them highly vulnerable to the risk of resonance in mechanical systems. The interaction of engine excitation frequencies and terraininduced accelerations with the system natural frequencies may result in resonance, causing amplified dynamic responses, increased material fatigue, degradation of sensing performance, and potential failure of critical components such as antenna support structures, gun barrels, and optical systems. Traditional passive vibration control techniques are inadequate for such applications due to their fixed tuning and inability to adapt to varying operational conditions. The objective of this paper is to propose a new system using Artificial Intelligence (AI) capable of not only pointing out but also countering a problem of resonance in a system. The proposed system makes use of Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC), which has been developed using Python's ˋscikit-fuzzyˋ library. The RPM value and vibration amplitude are selected as input variables to identify the danger of system resonance. A mathematical model of a representative vehicle component is used for the simulation of vibration behavior and identification of resonance danger zones. Simulation results show that the intelligent control system is able to detect an increase in the vibration level by avoiding resonance; this leads to improved durability of components and overall reliability in military applications.

title: LSTM Based Threat Intelligence Methods for Intrusion Classifications of DOS Attack in WSN
authors: Bipin Bihari Jayasingh, M Yogitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) are becoming more common in various applications such as environmental monitoring, military applications and health care networks. Because WSNs are often resource-constrained and operate over a channel of open communication, these networks have some significant security implications. Among these security implications, Denial of Service (DoS) attacks pose a significant threat to the availability of a network by disrupting normal operation and degrading data integrity. Effective cybersecurity strategies are necessary to defend against DoS attacks, and denial of service threat intelligence methods would require mechanisms that can discover attack signatures as well as anomalous behaviors in real-time. In this paper, we present an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) based on deep Learning which uses LSTM networks to detect and classify DoS attacks. The benefits of the LSTM framework are identified as the structures ability to depict or capture temporal patterns in network traffic sequences and gives the benefit of identifying complex and dynamically changing behavior patterns over time. Finally, By leveraging threat intelligence and implementing sound cybersecurity strategies, our system improves the defensive capability of the network to both sophisticated and adaptive attacks. The proposed IDS has been both trained and tested on the WSN-DS dataset which contains multiple attack classes including Normal, flooding, and TDMA attacks.

title: Android Application for Blood Flow Doppler Signal Processing
authors: Ahmed Mansur Graverán, Martín Fuentes Cruz, Eduardo Moreno Hernández, Fabián García Nocetti, Juan Prohias Martínez, Victoria Hernández Mederos, Jorge Estrada Sarlabous, Yanela Peguero Bringuez, Manuel Cruz Rodríguez
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: The principal objective of this investigation is to implement a portable and low-cost system for the real-time analysis of blood flow using mobile technology. The methodology employs a specialized application developed in Kotlin for the Android platform. This software captures audio signals from a continuous-wave Doppler ultrasound device and applies digital signal processing techniques, including high-pass filtering and the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), to generate high-resolution spectrograms. The results demonstrate that the application accurately calculates essential hemodynamic parameters, such as mean blood velocity, pulsatility index, and resistive index, directly on the mobile device. The principal conclusion is that this tool significantly advances the Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) model by providing a reliable and accessible solution for the early detection of subclinical atherosclerosis, particularly in environments with limited clinical resources.

title: Three-Way Conflict Resolution in Fuzzy Situation Tables With Nonlinear Conflict Functions
authors: Jing Liu, Mengjun Hu, Weiping Ding, Witold Pedrycz, Guangming Lang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: Three-way conflict analysis resolves real-world conflicts in two main steps: identifying the causes of conflict and designing feasible strategies to mitigate disputes. Existing approaches predominantly rely on linear conflict functions, which are insufficient for capturing nuanced variations in inter-agent relationships. Moreover, systematic development of feasible strategies has been limited. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a three-way conflict resolution model incorporating nonlinear conflict functions. Firstly, we introduce nonlinear conflict functions that accurately model the subtle fluctuations in conflict degrees. These functions are used to trisect both the set of agent pairs and the set of issues. We further propose a novel method to determine optimal trisections along with the corresponding optimal threshold pairs. Once the trisection phase is completed, we precisely identify the critical conflict issues that require resolution, where the parameter of conflict tolerance level is induced from the optimal issue trisection. Subsequently, conflict resolution of critical issues is formulated as an optimization problem, and two adjustment mechanisms are developed to derive distinct adjustment strategies. Finally, the applicability and superior performance of the proposed model, in terms of accurately identifying conflict issues and generating effective resolution strategies, are demonstrated through a commonly-used case study, a sensitivity evaluation, and a comparative analysis. The results confirm that our approach offers a flexible, efficient, and practical framework for three-way conflict analysis in complex decision-making contexts.

title: Model-Based and Data-Driven Bispectral Index Prediction under Combined Drug Infusion during General Anaesthesia
authors: Arya Shibu, M Pravika, C P Pramod, P K Preetha
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Depth of anaesthesia (DOA) monitoring is very crucial during surgical procedures to prevent complications like intraoperative awareness or excessive anaesthetic drug administration. The Bispectral Index (BIS), extracted from the electroencephalogram (EEG) signals, is a widely used indicator for monitoring the level of consciousness during general anaesthesia. However, a reliable DOA prediction remains challenging due to nonlinear drug dynamics and inter-patient variability. This study presents a comparative framework that evaluates classical pharmacokinetic–pharmacodynamic (PK–PD) models alongside data-driven machine learning (ML) models for BIS prediction. A real-world clinical dataset is analyzed using strict patientwise partitioning, for training and testing, ensuring subject independent validation. BIS values are predicted at multiple horizons of 1 second, 30 second, 60 second, and 90 second ahead using Nonlinear Autoregressive model with eXogenous inputs (NARX) of Random Forest (RF) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to examine short- and medium-term forecasting performance. Model performance is assessed using various performance indices, along with phase-wise evaluation across induction, maintenance, and recovery stages of anaesthesia. The results show that ML models, particularly LSTM networks, achieve lower prediction errors and a high correlation with measured BIS values. The findings demonstrate that ML models effectively capture temporal dynamics and inter-patient variability, highlighting their potential for enhanced anaesthesia monitoring and future closed-loop anaesthesia control systems.

title: Hybrid Consistency Policy: Decoupling Multi-Modal Diversity and Real-Time Efficiency in Robotic Manipulation_supp1-3701559.mp4
authors: Qianyou Zhao
publish: N/A
abstract: In visuomotor policy learning, diffusion-based imitation learning has become widely adopted for its ability to capture diverse behaviors. However, approaches built on ordinary and stochastic denoising processes struggle to jointly achieve fast sampling and strong multi-modality. To address these challenges, we propose the Hybrid Consistency Policy (HCP). HCP runs a short stochastic prefix up to an adaptive switch time, and then applies a one-step consistency jump to produce the final action. To align this one-jump generation, HCP performs time-varying consistency distillation that combines a trajectory-consistency objective to keep neighboring predictions coherent and a denoising-matching objective to improve local fidelity. In both simulation and on a real robot, HCP with 25 SDE steps plus one jump approaches the 80-step DDPM teacher in accuracy and mode coverage while significantly reducing latency. These results show that multi-modality does not require slow inference, and a switch time decouples mode retention from speed. It yields a practical accuracy–efficiency trade-off for robot policies. Project website: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://sites.google.com/view/hybrid-cp</uri>.

title: Simulated Investments: From the Related Problems of Planning Tall Buildings to the Definition of Design Processes
authors: Aaron Tobey
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing
abstract: Beginning in the late 1960s numerous mixed-use high-rise buildings such as Chicago’s John Hancock Center were constructed in US cities, employing novel externalized structural systems to create uninterrupted rentable spaces that could support a variety of interior configurations over time. Concurrently, large US architecture firms including Skidmore, Owings, & Merrill (SOM) and Caudill, Rowlett, Scott (CRS) began efforts to distill the design of tall office buildings into a series of related design problems that could drive computer simulations of a building’s performance as an investment. More than the individual computer programs or buildings, the most significant impact of these efforts was the impetus they provided for a transformation of design processes at large architectural firms, as well as the built environment generally, according to terms that might be amenable to computation. Drawing on primary sources from the period, this article outlines the foundations of this transformation in a network of collaborators and competitors, the legacies of this transformation in contemporary approaches to building information modeling, and how the practice of architecture and the urban fabric of the US have been shaped in the process.

title: Robust Semi-Supervised CT Radiomics for Lung Cancer Prognosis: Cost-Effective Learning with Limited Labels and SHAP Interpretation
authors: Mohammad R. Salmanpour, Amir Hossein Pouria, Sonya Falahati, Shahram Taeb, Somayeh Sadat Mehrnia, Mehdi Maghsudi, Ali Fathi Jouzdani, Mehrdad Oveisi, Ilker Hacihaliloglu, Arman Rahmim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: Computed tomography (CT) imaging is essential for lung cancer (LCa) management, while offering detailed visualization and valuable information for Artificial Intelligence-guided prognosis. Meanwhile, supervised learning (SL) models require extensive labeled data, limiting their real-world utility where annotations are scarce. Methods: We analyzed CT scans from 977 patients across 12 public/private datasets, extracting 1,218 radiomics features using Laplacian of Gaussian and wavelet filters via standardized PyRadiomics. Dimensionality was reduced using 56 feature selection and attribute extraction algorithms, and 27 classifiers were benchmarked. Semi-supervised learning (SSL) framework with pseudo-labeling utilized 478 unlabeled and 499 labeled cases. Model sensitivity was assessed in three scenarios: varying labeled data in SL, increasing unlabeled data in SSL, and jointly scaling both from 10% to 100%. SHapley Additive exPlanations analysis (SHAP)analysis explained and interpreted the top model predictions. Five-fold cross-validation and external testing (in two cohorts) were performed. Results: SSL outperformed SL across all metrics, improving overall survival prediction by up to 17%. The top SSL model (Feature Importance by Random Forest+XGBoost) achieved 0.90±0.01 accuracy in cross-validation and 0.88±0.01 externally. SHAP revealed enhanced feature discriminability in SSL and SL, particularly for class 1 (survival>4 years), and helped explain model decisions. SSL maintained strong performance even with only 10% labeled data. Both SSL-based scenarios demonstrated more stable performance compared to SL, with lower variance across external testing, emphasizing SSL's robustness and cost-efficiency. Conclusion: We propose an interpretable, cost-effective SSL framework for CT-based LCa survival prediction. Significance: It improves performance, generalizability, and clinical readiness via SHAP explainability and unlabeled data utilization.

title: A Biophysical Prior-Based Hierarchical Graph Pooling Strategy for Protein Function Prediction
authors: Yanbin Gu, Shiming Zhao, Lingzhi Liu, Yanrui Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Accurate prediction of protein function is fundamental to understanding biological systems. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated promise in this field; however, effectively aggregating residue-level features into discriminative global representations remains a core challenge. Existing graph pooling methods often rely on data-driven clustering, neglecting the inherent hierarchical organization of proteins, which may lead to the loss of critical structural information and limit their interpretability. To address this, we propose BioPhysPool, a biophysical prior-based hierarchical pooling strategy. Unlike traditional approaches, BioPhysPool utilizes secondary structure elements as a deterministic blueprint for graph coarsening, transforming fine-grained residue interaction networks into coarse-grained secondary structure networks without requiring computationally intensive assignment matrices. We further develop a hierarchical GNN architecture equipped with a cross-granularity attention fusion mechanism to adaptively integrate local chemical features with macro-topological patterns. Evaluations on rigorous benchmarks demonstrate that BioPhysPool outperforms different types of classical pooling methods in both binary and multi-class classification tasks. Furthermore, interpretability analysis reveals that attention allocation is primarily driven by structural context rather than amino acid type, with observed patterns consistent with known residue functional importance. These findings validate the effectiveness of embedding biophysical priors into the graph learning framework, offering a robust and interpretable solution for protein function prediction.

title: Real-Time Active Magnetic Compensation Based on Multipole Expansion for Magnetoencephalography under Arbitrary Motion
authors: Xinyu Cao, Tingyu Zhu, Shinichi Chikaki, Motofumi Fushimi, Masaki Sekino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Real-time active magnetic field compensation is essential for mobile precision measurements inside magnetically shielded rooms with inhomogeneous residual fields, particularly for applications such as moving magnetoencephalography (MEG) using optically pumped magnetometers (OPMs). Conventional field evaluation based on the Biot–Savart integration is computationally demanding for sensor motion. This paper presents a high-speed active magnetic field compensation method using a precomputed multipole expansion model of coil-generated fields, enabling rapid adaptation to arbitrary sensor motion without numerically re-evaluating the field. The system, comprising an eight-coil array mounted on a 50-cm cubic frame, achieved a feedback-control update time below 10 ms and maintained OPM operation during 360° rotations and 28 cm translations (56% of the frame side length). A shielding factor of 20.4 dB was achieved under stationary conditions, and at least 10.3 dB was maintained during continuous motion. Experimental validation using an MEG setup demonstrated reliable detection of the auditory evoked response component under both stationary and dynamic conditions, verifying the robust suppression of motion-induced field fluctuations. The proposed multipole-expansion-based real-time compensation provides a computationally efficient and motion-adaptive approach for magnetic field stabilization and offers a scalable solution for a wide range of mobile precision measurement systems.

title: A Resilient and Privacy-Preserving Cloud-Edge Distributed Method for Reliable Economic Dispatch in Cyber Physical Energy System
authors: Jun Yang, Yulong Zhao, Fengyuan Sun, Yantong Fan, Shunjiang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: With the rapid development of 5G and IoT technologies, privacy concerns caused by passive eavesdropping attacks have become increasingly prominent. Firstly, an economic dispatch model for information-energy systems considering the Cloud-Edge-Devices architecture is established, with refined modeling of energy transmission characteristics and losses. A KL-divergence-based differential privacy mechanism is designed for the data acquisition stage. By introducing Gaussian noise with standard deviation correlated to the marginal cost, the privacy of terminal devices during data collection and transmission is effectively preserved. Secondly, a privacy-preserving distributed consensus economic dispatch algorithm (PP-DCED) is proposed. Local secret functions are embedded into the multi-energy management controllers at the edge layer. Combined with a disturbance gain feedback mechanism and a Gaussian decaying noise-based iterative algorithm, the proposed method ensures privacy protection during data exchange among edge devices. Finally, a hierarchical privacy protection scheme is achieved across the Cloud-Edge-Devices architecture. Hardware-in-the-loop simulation results demonstrate that compared with traditional centralized methods, the proposed approach reduces the privacy disclosure risk by 32.5% and improves communication efficiency by 24.8% while maintaining a high convergence accuracy of 0.001. This confirms that the proposed method can effectively balance the trade-off between privacy requirements and economic dispatch performance in complex energy ecosystems.

title: Reliable Hard Negative Sampling for Implicit Collaborative Filtering
authors: Jiayi Wu, Zhengyu Wu, Xunkai Li, Shiyu Yang, Rong-Hua Li, Guoren Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Implicit collaborative filtering (CF) models are widely used in recommender systems due to their cost-effective data collection and broad applicability. Negative sampling methods play a crucial role in training implicit CF models. However, most of these methods ignore the false negative problem, and the remaining methods rely solely on statistical features to identify them. The integration of contextual information to address this issue remains unexplored. To fill this gap, we propose a novel sampling method called Reliable Hard Negative Sampling (RHNS). Our approach consists of two key components: a hard negative sampling module, which constructs hard negative samples through a controllable dimension-level fusion approach, and a reliable transformation module, which incorporates contextual information to transform hard negative samples, including those that may be false negatives, into more reliable hard negative samples. We provide theoretical insights showing that the hard negative sampling module tends to generate harder negative samples than existing hard negative sampling methods, while the reliable transformation module tends to mitigate false negative risk. Extensive experimental results on five real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of RHNS.

title: Does Source Data Harm Adaptation? A Mixed-Supervised Degradation-Aware Framework
authors: Hao Luo, Zhiqiang Tian, Shuo Wang, Kaibing Zhang, Juan Wang, Jue Jiang, Shaoyi Du
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: This paper studies two crucial undiscovered issues for semi-supervised domain adaptation (SSDA) that severely limit existing methods. Firstly, their development has been confined to the transductive semi-supervised learning paradigm, neglecting the potential of other paradigms to improve adaptation. Secondly, contrary to common assumption, increasing source domain training data often degrades performance, a phenomenon we term Source-Induced Degradation (SID). To address these issues, we propose a novel dictionary pair transfer learning method under a mixed-supervised learning paradigm (DPTL-Mixed). DPTL-Mixed is built upon the discriminative Dictionary Pair Learning (DPL) framework, leveraging its linear efficiency and representational power. Our framework consists of two complementary components: a supervised component (DPTL-Sup) and a semi-supervised component (DPTL-Semi). DPTL-Sup learns separate domain-specific analysis dictionaries for source and target data, enabling explicit re-weighting of domain contributions to mitigate SID. DPTL-Semi follows the traditional SSDA philosophy, incorporating distribution alignment via adaptive maximum mean discrepancy and manifold regularization. The final model fuses the outputs of both components, levering the complementary strengths of supervised and semi-supervised learning. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms other shallow competitors in 53 out of 60 SSDA tasks, validating its effectiveness in overcoming both identified issues.

title: Flexible Shape Formation Control for Quadrotor UAVs
authors: Emmanuel Cardenaz, José A. Guerrero, Gerardo Romero, Daniel Olivares, Youcef Mezouar
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this work, we address the problem of quadrotor flexible formation control. To this end, a control algorithm is proposed to achieve geometric platoon formations based exclusively on similarity transformations (rotation, translation, and scale) rather than rigid structures with fixed geometric references for the quadrotors. Firstly, the dynamic model of the quadrotor is established along with a nonlinear stabilizing control that uses saturated inputs. Then, we introduce the flexible formation behavior into the resulting quadrotor’s lateral and longitudinal subsystems using a control law that combines the transformation of a chain of integrators into a system described by a single integrator, and a shape optimization cost function. The optimization criterion consists of a geometric deformation measure term inspired by the Procrustes problem, which is a shape-alignment problem. Results are validated using the nonlinear quadrotor model in a simulated environment based on platforms such as ROS 2 and Gazebo, providing a realistic approximation to experiments with physical hardware.

title: Optimal Wind Farm Repowering under Uncertainty with Hydrogen Hybridization
authors: Ana Fernández-Guillamón, Isabel C. Gil-García, Rafael Zárate-Miñano, Miguel Cañas-Carretón, Miguel Carrión
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper addresses the optimal repowering of existing wind farms by integrating battery storage and green hydrogen production systems to enhance profitability and flexibility under market and resource uncertainty. A two-stage stochastic mixed-integer linear programming model is developed to jointly optimize wind turbine selection, battery sizing, and electrolyzer and hydrogen storage capacities. The model considers uncertainty in electricity prices, wind resource availability, and hydrogen prices through a scenario-based approach, and incorporates physical constraints such as turbine spacing and grid capacity. To ensure computational tractability, a chronological time-period clustering technique and an iterative technology-evaluation algorithm are applied. A case study of a wind farm in Spain demonstrates that hybridization with hydrogen increases expected annual profit by approximately 4%, while batteries remain uneconomical at current costs. Sensitivity analyses reveal that higher hydrogen prices significantly increase investments in electrolyzer capacity and hydrogen storage, highlighting the importance of supportive market conditions for the green hydrogen transition.

title: Deep Spatial—Spectral&mdash;Temporal Fusion with Transformers for Wheat Fractional Vegetation Cover Estimation_supp1-3701773.docx
authors: Songlin Yang
publish: N/A

title: A Systematic Review of UAS-based Remote Sensing for Ecosystem-based Disaster Risk Reduction: Applications, Policy Interfaces and Opportunities
authors: Monica Herrero-Huerta, Francesco Pirotti, Cosme Hernanz-Gilbert, Nuria Sanchez-Lopez
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) have rapidly evolved from high-resolution mapping tools to strategic enablers of ecosystem-based disaster risk reduction (Eco-DRR). This systematic review of 51 peer-reviewed studies assesses how UAS support hazard monitoring, ecosystem resilience evaluation, and implementation of nature-based solutions across forests, freshwater, agricultural, coastal, grassland, and urban ecosystems affected by wildfires, hydrologic and climatic hazards, landslides, and multi-hazards. Recent advances reveal a shift from RGB photogrammetry to multisensory approaches integrating multispectral, LiDAR, thermal, and emerging hyperspectral or SAR data, enhancing detection of pre-hazard stress signals, fuel-load dynamics, hydrological connectivity, and coastal stability. Machine learning techniques increasingly automate risk mapping and ecological indicator extraction, improving operational transferability. UAS-derived biophysical metrics are progressively linked to ecosystem functions contributing to risk reduction, such as carbon storage, hydrological regulation, heat mitigation, and erosion control. However, explicit ecosystem service framing remains limited and uneven across ecosystems, with critical coastal and deltaic areas still underrepresented despite high hazard exposure. Policy integration is partial: although findings align with the Sendai Framework, the UN 2030 Agenda, and the Horizon Europe framework, only <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx$</tex-math></inline-formula> 45% of studies indirectly reference these agendas. Three main gaps were identified: (1) strong geographic bias toward high-income regions, (2) limited multi-temporal monitoring of resilience trajectories, and (3) insufficient standardization for decision support. UAS hold exceptional potential for quantifying ecosystem condition and modeling hazard dynamics, but realizing their full Eco-DRR potential demands stronger science-policy integration, regional scaling, and reproducible analytical frameworks.

title: Exploring Metaverses: Acceptance of Spatial as an Immersive Learning Environment Among University Students in Mexico
authors: Iris Cristina Peláez-Sánchez, Gerardo Rocha-Feregrino, Leonardo David Glasserman-Morales
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: In the current digital education landscape, metaverses are emerging as innovative tools with the potential to revolutionize learning. Among these, the Spatial platform stands out for its unique features. This virtual environment allows users to interact through avatars within a three-dimensional universe, expanding opportunities for education beyond entertainment and socialization. This study explored the acceptance and usage of the Spatial platform in a private university in Mexico, involving 44 engineering students in a quasi-experimental study. Students used Spatial to supplement their learning, evaluating dimensions such as perceived usefulness, ease of use, enjoyment, and continued usage intention through an adapted questionnaire. The study's results demonstrated a higher acceptance of the Spatial platform among the group that used immersive virtual reality (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M=52.63$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SD}=8.469, ~\mathrm{V}=71.726$</tex>). However, these differences were not statistically significant compared to those using desktop virtual environments <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(M=51.08, S D=9.529, V=90.811)$</tex>. Both groups showed good acceptance of Spatial as an educational tool. Future research should expand to encompass larger and more diverse educational settings, thereby improving our understanding of student preferences and perceptions of these emerging technologies and guiding future academic implementations and technological developments.

title: Short-term Wind Power Forecasting Using Mutual Information Feature Selection and PSO-optimized GRU Network
authors: Yu Dong, Zheyong Jiang, Dingbo Tan, Qingmei Tan
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Against the backdrop of the global low-carbon energy transition, precise plays a vital role in grid robustness and efficiency exploitation of renewable energy. This study proposes a hybrid MI-PSO-GRU approach that combines Mutual Information (MI)-based feature selection with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network optimized by the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm. The methodology first employs MI to quantitatively analyze nonlinear relationships between meteorological variables and power output to construct an optimal feature set. Subsequently, the PSO algorithm is applied to the global optimization of GRU hyperparameters—such as hidden layer size and learning rate—mitigating the risks of manual tuning and local optima. Results on summer datasets demonstrate superior performance, achieving an RMSE of 6.9302, MAE of 3.9064, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.8957, indicating high consistency between forecasted and actual outputs. The proposed model substantially exceeds baseline models in forecasting accuracy and stability.

title: Workshop: AI Innovative Approach Assessment. Applied to the Programming Courses with JULIUS
authors: Julio Ricardo Martínez-Montezuma, Susana Eugenia Maya-Duran, Ana Luna, Mario Chong
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Assessment in programming courses represents a constant challenge, especially for young students without training in computer science. This workshop presents JULIUS, an intelligent assessment assistant that supports instructors in generating students' feedback on PSeInt algorithms. Through a structured methodology based on the “traffic light method,” JULIUS enables the analysis considering inputs, processes, and outputs before evaluating pseudocode, thereby reducing subjectivity, improving error traceability, and enhancing teacher efficiency in the classroom. The system generates personalized reports, group performance indicators, and identifies error patterns, facilitating precise pedagogical interventions. This approach fosters understanding of logical thinking and transforms assessment into a formative experience for students and instructors.

title: Cyber Takeover of UAS - Opportunities and Challenges
authors: Vipin Kumar Singh, Shubham Pandey, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) have penetrated both civil and military fields due to their flexibility, autonomy and cost effectiveness. However, this reliance has exposed UAS to sophisticated cyber takeover risks, where adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in communication links, navigation systems and control protocols. Recent conflicts demonstrate both the operational potential and the security challenges of UAS showcasing both autonomous capabilities and vulnerabilities to electronic interference. Additionally, widespread use of GPS jamming and spoofing on the battlefield has underscored the susceptibility of UAS to cyber-electronic disruption and hostile takeover attempts. These developments highlight opportunities for enhancing mission resilience through secure communications, autonomous decision algorithms and intrusion detection systems, alongside the challenge of defending against adversarial electronic warfare and software exploitation. This paper surveys these dual facets, drawing on recent conflict cases to review cyber takeover opportunities and challenges in modern UAS operations [1].

title: Strategic Environment and Geopolitical Shift - Artificial Intelligence, Cyber Resilience and the Architecture of Air and Missile Defence in 2026
authors: Rajendra Kumar Yadav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The international security environment of 2026 is shaped by a convergence of artificial intelligence (AI), cyber power, space systems, and the erosion of traditional deterrence frameworks. Military power is no longer defined primarily by platforms such as aircrafts, tanks, or warships, but by the ability to control data, algorithms, and digitally networked battlespaces. This transformation has profound implications for air and missile defence (AMD), where low-cost autonomous systems, cyber-enabled disruption, and precision-guided missiles now threaten even the most advanced forces. This paper integrates two analytical perspectives: first, the global shift from hybrid warfare to algorithmic warfare, and second, India's strategic response through Atmanirbhar Bharat, defence public-private partnerships (PPPs), and indigenous Integrated Air and Missile defence (IAMD). By examining initiatives such as Project Kusha, Mission Sudarshan Chakra, and Operation Abhyaas (2025), the paper argues that future deterrence will depend less on retaliatory firepower and more on technological sovereignty, system resilience, and civil–military integration. It proposes a “Whole-of-Nation Air Defence” framework in which algorithms, networks, industry, and society become as critical as missiles and radars.

title: A strategy for wind power interval prediction based on Parametric Distributions and Non-parametric Estimation
authors: Xingchao Zhang, Fan Guo, Xiaodong Yu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The fluctuation, intermittency and uncertainty of wind energy pose severe challenges to the grid-integrated operation of wind farms. Traditional point prediction of wind power suffers from the limitation that uncertainty can hardly be quantified. To address this issue, this paper innovatively proposes a multi-confidence interval prediction method that integrates parametric distributions and non-parametric estimation. Based on the prediction error of wind power point forecasting via neural networks, this method fits multiple probabilistic distribution models to generate prediction intervals under various confidence levels, and quantitatively evaluates the model performance by combining the interval width and the actual power out-of-limit situation. The core contribution of this paper lies in the comparative verification of multiple distributions, which breaks the limitation that a single estimation method can adapt to all scenarios. By selecting the optimal scheme according to practical conditions, the prediction interval width is significantly reduced on the premise of ensuring coverage accuracy. It provides a new paradigm for the uncertainty quantification of wind power and also offers more accurate technical references for power grid dispatching decisions.

title: Optimization of Frequency Stability Defense Configuration for Power Systems with High Penetration of Renewable Energy
authors: Jincheng Wu, Ying Huang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Addressing the frequency stability challenges in power systems with high penetration of renewable energy, this paper proposes an optimized configuration method for the three-line defense framework by coordinating "source-grid-load" resources. A frequency stability defense framework tailored to renewable energy characteristics is established. Furthermore, a coordinated optimization strategy for high-frequency generator tripping and low-frequency load shedding is introduced, incorporating rapid-action stages and reliable backup stages to prioritize renewable energy participation in frequency control. Simulation based on PSS/E is conducted using a renewable energy-rich regional power grid as a case study. Results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses frequency deviations, enabling rapid frequency recovery to a safe range under scenarios such as DC block faults and renewable energy disconnection, thereby proving its practical engineering applicability.

title: Short-Circuit Failure and Durability Analysis of Crimped IGBT
authors: Yue Wu, Yuebin Zhou, Liu Yang, Weihuang Huang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Press-pack IGBT (PPI) is a key component of flexible DC transmission equipment. Compared with traditional welded devices, it has advantages such as compact structure, failure in short-circuit mode, double-sided heat dissipation, and high reliability. Developing domestically-produced PPIs and widely applying them in converter valves and circuit breakers is of great significance for improving grid power levels and system reliability. This paper takes T0160NB45A-type single-chip crimped IGBT as the research object, systematically studies the unique short-circuit failure mechanism of PPI devices, and clarifies the correlation between uneven pressure and single-chip failure through controlled variable experiments. A short-circuit experimental platform matching the actual working conditions of flexible DC transmission was built, and related experiments were conducted to analyze the causes of device failure. Based on short-circuited chips, short-circuit withstand experiments were carried out, a quantitative three-stage aging model was established to describe the resistance evolution law of chips under short-circuit conditions, and the process leading to eventual open-circuit failure was revealed. The aging mechanism was analyzed in combination with energy spectrum analysis and scanning electron microscope (SEM) microstructure characterization of failure sites. All experiments were completed with multiple parallel samples to verify the universality of the results, which provides a theoretical reference for the reliable application of domestically-produced PPIs.

title: Comparative Reliability Study of 1.2 kV Planar and Trench SiC MOSFETs under 10,000-Hour HTRB Stress
authors: Yuncong Li, Zhizhe Wang, Shichen Su, Menglin Yan, Qiuzhen Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: The long-term reliability of silicon carbide (SiC) MOSFETs under combined high-temperature and reverse-bias stress is pivotal for their successful integration into high-power applications, such as electric vehicles and renewable energy systems. This paper presents a comprehensive 10,000-hour High Temperature Reverse Bias (HTRB) reliability assessment of three commercial 1.2 kV SiC MOSFET architectures: planar, asymmetric trench, and double-trench. Comparative tests were conducted at an ambient temperature of 150°C under three specific stress conditions: (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GS</inf> = -6 V, V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf>= 1320V), (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GS</inf>= 0V, V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf> =1440V) and (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GS</inf> = -6 V, V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf> = 1440V). Experimental results demonstrated that negative gate bias significantly accelerated parameter degradation. While the blocking leakage currents (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DSS</inf> and I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">GSS</inf>) exhibited negligible variations throughout the test, validating the inherent stability of the edge termination structures, the threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>) proved to be the most sensitive degradation indicator with strong structural dependence. Specifically, the double trench device exhibited a significant and continuous negative V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> shift (up to -5.3%), whereas the asymmetric-trench device demonstrated superior stability. Furthermore, an insensitivity of on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS(on)</inf>) to V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> degradation was observed in double-trench devices, where substantial V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> drift occurred without inducing noticeable changes in R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS(on)</inf>. These findings provide critical data for SiC MOSFET reliability evaluation and underscore the impact of negative gate bias on long-term gate oxide integrity.

title: Optical Aberration and Flux Uniformity Analysis of Heliostat Fields on Non-Planar Receivers
authors: Wei Zhao, Siyuan Zhang, Junfeng Zhang, Zekai Liang, Yue Zhao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Solar power tower plants employing cylindrical receivers have gained prominence due to their omnidirectional flux acceptance capability. However, conventional planar approximation models fail to accurately predict the energy flux distribution on curved receiver surfaces, particularly when large incidence angles couple with surface curvature to produce significant optical aberrations. This paper presents a comprehensive modeling methodology that integrates reverse ray-tracing with discrete grid unfolding techniques to characterize the flux distribution on non-planar receivers. The proposed approach incorporates the finite angular extent of the solar disk, accounting for the solar cone divergence of 9.3 mrad and the associated limb-darkening effect through a physically-based intensity distribution model. Simulation results demonstrate that as the slant range between heliostats and the receiver tower increases, the reflected spot undergoes a characteristic morphological transformation from a quasi-rectangular shape to a longitudinally elongated ellipse, a phenomenon attributable to off-axis astigmatism. The quantitative analysis reveals that heliostats positioned at distances exceeding 200 meters exhibit substantial spot spreading, with the longitudinal dimension expanding by factors of 2.5 to 3.0 compared to near-field heliostats. The superposition of multiple heliostat contributions produces a centrally peaked flux distribution with potential hotspot formation, where the peak-to-edge flux ratio can exceed 2:1. These findings provide theoretical foundations for receiver thermal management and inform the development of optimized aiming strategies to prevent localized overheating and material degradation in commercial solar tower installations.

title: Few-shot Fine-tuning of Large Vision Model for SEM Image Contour Extraction
authors: Sanghyun Choi, Keetae Kim, Jing Wang, Yuansheng Ma, Hawren Fang
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This work explores how to fine-tune (calibrate) a Large Vision Model (LVM) for accurate contour extraction of patterns in wafer Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images. Obtaining accurate pattern contours is important for metrology tasks such as optical proximity correction (OPC) calibration and verification. Although various LVMs have been published in academia that have strong zero-shot performance, their performance is often limited when presented with out-of-domain images like SEM images.
