By fine-tuning a segmentation model called Segment Anything Model (SAM), published by Meta, we show improved segmentations (contours) of SEM image patterns as compared to zero-shot segmentations.

title: Changes in Electroless Cu Film’s Properties Characterized by Physical & Electrical Methods
authors: Tatt Wai Wan, Chan Lik Tan, Kuan Yew Cheong, Wei Loon Chan, Siew Chun Low, Suzylawati Ismail, Sai Link Lee
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: The changes of properties and behavior of copper (Cu) films (deposited on tungsten (W) coated substrate) using fresh (FS) and end life (EL) electroless copper (EC) solution was investigated to understand the window of usage of the plating solutions before the byproducts in the solution contaminate the Cu films. The studies include the variation effect of Cu thickness, with the evaluation on annealing temperature at 300°C and 400°C, exploring the Cu grain re-arrangement trend of FS and EL samples. Physical characterization was performed by atomic force microscopy (AFM), nano-indentation, Micro-Vickers hardness and electron bean scattering diffraction (EBSD), while electrical characterization was conducted by four-points probes RI Kelvin sheet resistance tester. FS samples annealed at 300°C reveal an increasing trend in hardness, Young’s Modulus and grain size due to the dislocation buildup of the Cu grain. The hardness reduced at 400°C when internal stress is released due to grain re-arrangement at complete annealing temperature. However, a decreasing trend of hardness & Young’s Modulus at both temperatures is observed for EL films due to the increase of voids when temperature is elevated (outgassing byproduct trapped in the film). The hardness and Young’s Modulus of thinner Cu films are higher with no significant impact to surface roughness. The reduction of roughness at 400°C annealing temperature is observed for both FS & EL samples. It can be attributed to stress relaxation after annealing with the effective compressive stress converted to tensile stress. On the other hand, electrical characterization methods can be used to assess the adhesion, over-plated and filling properties of Cu films on structured wafers.

title: A RISC-V Pipeline Simulation Framework for Comparative Evaluation of nMPRA and nHSE Real-Time Microarchitectures
authors: Nicolai Iuga, Vasile Gheorghita Gaitan, Tudor Petrescu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: This paper presents a RISC-V pipeline simulation framework intended for the comparative evaluation of conventional pipelined execution and multi-context execution aligned with nMPRA (multi pipeline register architecture, where n is the degree of multiplication) and nHSE (hardware scheduler engine for n threads) real-time microarchitectural concepts. While instruction set architecture (ISA)-level simulators remain useful for functional validation, they provide limited visibility into pipeline behavior, context-switching penalties, and scheduler-driven execution effects. To address this limitation, the proposed framework combines an ISA reference mode with an explicit five-stage pipeline mode, enabling controlled comparison between functional execution and pipeline-level execution under the same workload conditions. The simulator supports scheduler-driven task activation and context switching, while enabling comparative evaluation of total cycles, retired instructions, pipeline stalls, context switches, and instructions per cycle (IPC). Representative experiments comparing ISA execution, standard pipelined execution, and an nMPRA-aligned configuration show that the framework can make switching-related timing effects directly observable. In particular, the results highlight the difference between a conventional shared pipeline and a context-preserving multi-context pipeline under the same task workload.

title: GALaXy-PPI: A Global-Attentive Local Transformer for Multi-Label PPI Typing
authors: Nima Mahmoudi, Mohammad Mehdi Pourhashem Kallehbasti
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Predicting the functional types of protein protein interactions (PPIs) is essential for understanding cellular mechanisms and prioritizing therapeutic hypotheses. We introduce GALaXy-PPI, a dual-branch graph model that unifies global network reasoning with local subgraph understanding. The global branch employs GATv2 to assign content aware attention across the PPI graph, capturing long-range relational context. In parallel, the local branch encodes hop aware ego subgraphs with a lightweight Transformer and learnable gates that balance centroid, neighborhood, and 1-hop context signals. Proteins are represented with ESM-1b embeddings, and training uses Asymmetric Loss to address the strong class imbalance inherent to multi label interaction typing. We evaluate on the SHS27k and SHS148k benchmarks under Random, BFS, and DFS protocols that test generalization to previously unseen proteins. GALaXy-PPI consistently delivers accurate, well calibrated predictions and shows robust performance on both common and rare interaction categories, indicating effective integration of global topology, local structure, and sequence-derived priors. The resulting framework is simple to train and readily applicable to large PPI networks, offering a practical step toward more reliable, fine-grained PPI annotation.

title: Integrating Sustainability Through Multidisciplinary Civil Engineering Curriculum Design
authors: Khaled Shaaban
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Traditional civil engineering education is insufficient to address the complex, interconnected challenges of climate change, resource depletion, equity, and technological disruption. This work suggests that the way to resolve this issue is multidisciplinary integration. Focusing on transportation within civil engineering, different novel undergraduate course concepts that mix transportation engineering with diverse fields such as data science, psychology, law, ecology, and business are presented. These courses are structured into thematic clusters, including technology and data integration, human-centered design and behavior, policy, economics, justice, and resilience and circular systems. The proposed courses aim to produce a generation of engineers who are not only technical experts but also systems thinkers, ethical problem-solvers, and collaborative leaders, equipped to design and manage transportation infrastructure that is sustainable, resilient, and equitable.

title: A Novel Gate Driver Circuit for Crosstalk Suppression of GaN HEMTs in a Phase-Leg Configuration
authors: Zhiliang Wan, Junyuan Chen, Guanfu Liu, Su Meng, Haihong Qin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Although the widespread application of Gallium Nitride High Electron Mobility Transistors (GaN HEMTs) has enhanced converter performance, the severe crosstalk issues in the phase-leg configuration, induced by their extremely high switching speeds, pose a significant threat to system reliability. Conventional crosstalk suppression methods struggle to achieve a balance between suppressing positive and negative crosstalk voltages. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel gate driver circuit. This circuit utilizes an RC network to achieve passive negative voltage generation and realizes adaptive regulation of the gate driver voltage through an auxiliary MOSFET and a diode. During positive crosstalk events, a low-impedance negative voltage loop is provided to prevent false turn-on; conversely, during negative crosstalk events, the gate-source voltage is clamped to a low level to suppress the negative crosstalk voltage spike. Double-pulse simulation results demonstrate that the peak crosstalk voltage is reduced to -0.78V during positive crosstalk. Compared with the traditional constant negative voltage driver, the proposed circuit reduces the negative crosstalk voltage peak from -5.05V to -1.15V. Consequently, the reliability of GaN device operation is effectively improved while maintaining high switching speeds.

title: On the statistical analysis of the response time of some databases modeled as a queueing systems
authors: Dina Buza, Alexei Leahu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: Our paper investigates the statistical analysis of database response time by modeling databases as queueing systems of increasing generality. Starting from the classical M/M/1 model as a reference framework, the study extends the analysis to M/G/1 and GI/G/1 systems in order to better capture the variability and structural complexity of real database operations. The main contribution consists in the development of an algorithm for determining the minimum number of Monte Carlo simulation runs required to ensure, with a prescribed confidence level, that the sample mean of key performance indicators approximates their theoretical values within a given absolute error. The considered performance metrics include traffic intensity and response time, defined as the total time spent by a request in the system. The statistical analysis is based on samples of interarrival and service times, and includes the estimation of mean values, dispersion, and cumulative distribution functions. The proposed approach is validated through simulation experiments, which demonstrate a very good agreement between theoretical characteristics and their statistical estimates, including the empirical cumulative distribution function. The results highlight that the proposed methodology provides a reliable framework for performance evaluation not only for analytically tractable models, but also for more general queueing systems where explicit solutions are unavailable or expressed only in transform form. This makes the approach particularly suitable for the analysis of modern database systems with complex service structures.

title: ReC-HDT Operational Mode and Control Method Applicable for Single-Phase Voltage Sag Compensation
authors: Baichuan Zhu, Chang Yuan, Shaohua Wang, Ran Tao, Ming Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Conventional operating modes of the ReC-HDT are primarily designed for three-phase symmetrical voltage sag compensation. Under single-phase voltage sag conditions, its compensation capability is significantly limited due to insufficient active power support. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel operating mode and corresponding control strategy. This mode enables the ReC-HDT to absorb active power from the healthy phases to support the load in the faulty phase, thereby achieving a dual-series compensation. This approach effectively enhances the compensation capability of the ReC-HDT for single-phase voltage sags. The effectiveness of the proposed control method is validated through digital simulations on the PLECS simulation platform.

title: Teaching Agile Through the Flintstones: A Creative Learning Experience for Young Students
authors: Nuno Pombo, Christina Andersson
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper presents an innovative, hands-on learning activity aimed at introducing secondary school students to core Agile thinking through a narrative-driven and speculative design approach. The activity is embedded in the fictional universe of The Flintstones, where students collaboratively design school-related inventions for the prehistoric town of Bedrock. By combining Agile principles with a playful science fiction context, the intervention seeks to foster creativity, collaboration, and systems thinking in an engaging and age-appropriate manner. The session is structured into four phases: an introductory explanation of Agile concepts using everyday analogies, a short science fiction media stimulus, a team-based ideation and prototyping challenge, and brief group pitches. Empirical data are collected through pre- and post-session questionnaires, observation of group interactions, and analysis of student-generated artifacts. The results indicate that the integration of Agile-inspired practices and storytelling enhances student engagement and supports creative problem-solving and collaborative learning. This work contributes evidence on how narrative-based and experiential pedagogical strategies can effectively support early STEM and software engineering education in secondary school contexts.

title: Spatial Augmented Reality for Collaborative Learning
authors: Eric Peterson, Yasushi Ishida, Nurit Kirshenbaum
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Advanced Human Computer Interaction (HCI) and visualization technologies have the potential to improve learning outcomes for architecture students in difficult subject areas where they have historically underperformed. This paper describes a proposal for an immersive learning system for Structural Design. This is critical for the Architecture, Engineering and Construction (AEC) sector: a shortfall of professionals competent in structural design suggests improvements to education in this area are needed. SpARC Learning proposes a combination of technologies to improve learning: Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR), a type of AR that overlays dynamic graphic information onto objects in the learning environment, Tangible User Interfaces (TUI), objectbased HCIs allowing users to interact with software, and Artificial Intelligence (AI) that supports verbal interaction with the learning system. This immersive learning system facilitates self-guided experiential learning by small groups of students. This project is in the early stages of development; results of initial testing are forthcoming.

title: An approach to determining the set of devices and technologies for a smart home
authors: Bolun Ion, Catruc Adriana, Cojocaru Svetlana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: With limited financial resources available, transforming a home into a smart home may be expedient to be accomplished in several stages. In such a case, the efficient selection of devices and technologies at each stage is important. An approach to this end is proposed in this paper. In the respective optimization problem, defined as a Boolean mathematical programming one, economic and non-economic indices are combined. A conventional example and a Case study with the use of the proposed algorithms for solving the problem is described, which confirm the appropriateness of the approach.

title: Guaranteed Performance Security Control of Nonlinear Systems Under Hybrid Cyber Attacks Via a Memory-Based Event-Triggered Mechanism
authors: Hongyan Yang, Zhifan Zhang, Shen Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: This paper investigates the event-triggered guaranteed-performance security control problem for nonlinear systems subject to hybrid cyber-attacks (denial-of-service (DoS) attacks and deception attacks). First, a fuzzy-model-based description approach is employed to characterize the nonlinear system, effectively capturing its complex dynamic characteristics. Second, to alleviate the communication burden in bandwidth-constrained networks, a history-dependent event-triggering mechanism incorporating historical transmission data is designed, dynamically optimizing information transmission efficiency between sensors and controllers. Third, a security control framework with prescribed performance metrics is constructed. By integrating Lyapunov functional theory and matrix decomposition techniques, sufficient conditions ensuring system stability are rigorously derived. This guarantees that the proposed guaranteed-performance security control scheme can effectively counteract malicious impacts induced by cyber-attacks. Finally, comprehensive simulation case studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed security control method, demonstrating its robustness against hybrid attack patterns while maintaining desired control performance. The results show that, compared to the traditional event-triggered mechanism (ETM) and the dynamic event-triggered mechanism (DETM), the dynamic memory event-triggered mechanism (DMETM) proposed in this paper reduces the number of triggers by approximately 63.87<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>and 18.49<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>, respectively; Compared to the recent Adaptive Memory Event Triggering Mechanism (AMETM), it further reduces communication overhead by approximately 8.12<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, the system maintains exponential mean-square stability under mixed attacks and achieves the specified <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$H\_{\infty }$</tex-math></inline-formula> performance level.

title: Near-Field Beamforming and Port Selection for Fluid Antenna Systems
authors: Sen Chen, Zhentian Zhang, Hao Jiang, Kai-Kit Wong, An Li, Farshad Rostami Ghadi, Hyundong Shin
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This paper studies robust downlink transmission for fluid antenna systems (FAS) in a near-field communication scenario, where a multi-antenna base station (BS) serves a mobile user equipped with a set of reconfigurable candidate ports. To accurately capture the nonlinear phase variations induced by spherical-wave propagation and user mobility, we adopt an exact Euclidean-distance-based near-field channel model. Under imperfect channel state information (CSI), robust transmit beamforming and dynamic port subset selection are jointly optimized, while the receive combiner for each candidate subset is specified by a predefined combining rule. The objective is to maximize the worst-case received signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) under a bounded CSI uncertainty model. To this end, we develop a two-stage framework. For a given candidate port subset, the robust beamforming problem is reformulated as a tractable semidefinite program (SDP) via the S-procedure. Furthermore, to reduce the search complexity associated with densely deployed candidate ports, we propose a coarse-to-fine spatial search strategy that exploits the strong spatial correlation among adjacent FAS ports. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme consistently outperforms fixed-port baselines and non-robust designs, while offering enhanced robustness against CSI uncertainty and significantly reducing the subset-search overhead in near-field environments.

title: A Novel Two-step Approach for Magnetic Resonance Electrical Properties Tomography
authors: Fabiana Capitanio, Rosa Scapaticci, Marta Cavagnaro, Lorenzo Crocco
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this paper, a novel inversion approach to retrieve the electromagnetic properties of human tissues from magnetic resonance imaging data is presented. To face the non-linearity and ill-posedness of the inverse problem, the proposed approach splits the inversion task into two steps. In the first step, the contrast source and the electric field within the region of interest are estimated from the magnetic resonance data. In the second step, the constitutive relationship between the contrast sources and fields distributions is exploited to reconstruct the electromagnetic properties. Notably, the separation in two steps of the solution of the inverse problem allows tackling it as a cascade of linear steps, with a remarkable impact on the problem solution and computational burden. Moreover, in the second step a priori information-such as tissue segmentation-can be easily incorporated for a flexible and reliable reconstruction of the tissue properties. The proposed approach is validated with full 3D realistic simulations, showing that its results positively compare with other integral equation-based state of the art approaches.

title: Toward Secure Operation and Management (O&M) of Satellite Constellations: Efficiency, Resilience, and Reliability in a Network Perspective
authors: Linan Huang, Peilong Liu, Xi Chen, Zhiyuan Lin, Jian Yan, Linling Kuang
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications
abstract: Satellite constellations equipped with Inter-Satellite Links (ISLs) and onboard packet switching enable real-time Operation and Management (O&M) across globally distributed satellites, but at the same time broaden the attack surface and expose the system to unprecedented cybersecurity threats. Existing efforts have primarily focused on optimizing cryptography for single-satellite, point-to-point links, without considering how a broader notion of security should be applied at the constellation level. To bridge this gap, this article advances in two directions: a network perspective—from individual satellites to constellation-wide architectures; and an expanded security perspective—from isolated cryptography to system-level security that embeds efficiency, resilience, and reliability. Together, these two expansions motivate three fundamental questions: (i) how can efficient security mechanisms be designed for dynamic constellation topologies that require adaptive onboard routing; (ii) how can a constellation O&M resiliently recover to an acceptable operational state under worst-case failures of onboard security functions; and (iii) how can the reliability of onboard security functions be enhanced under stringent onboard resource constraints. To address these challenges, we first construct a constellation-wide hybrid security framework that safeguards content field with high semantic sensitivity using End-to-End (E2E) encryption, while protecting routing-related fields through Moving Target Defense (MTD). Next, we introduce a ciphered-mode and safe-mode management mechanism with an M-delayed fallback that balances recovery timeliness against exploitability. Finally, to enhance reliability of onboard security functions, we propose security-aware routers that manage plaintext/ciphered modes and orchestrate access to a shared pool of onboard cipher modules, thereby enabling redundancy to be shared across multiple endpoints and significantly extending the duration of secure operation in ciphered mode. Overall, these solutions comply with existing standards defined by standardization organizations, including Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB) and the Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems (CCSDS), while translating their conceptual security principles into more practical, system-level mechanisms.

title: An Integrated Onboard Charger for Tri-Motor Off-Road Electric Vehicles
authors: Daniel Afriyie, Gerald Brakoh, Ashraf Ali Khan, Mohsin Jamil, Hafiz Furqan Ahmed
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: This paper introduces an efficient integrated onboard charger designed for a three-motor drive electric vehicle. The novelty of this proposed topology lies in the dual use of the motor and inverters to function in both the driving and charging modes. In charging mode, the motors act as coupled inductors for AC-DC conversion, followed by a DC-DC conversion to achieve an acceptable charging voltage. Through this operation, a unity power factor is achieved with an improved total harmonic distortion. This is made possible because of the motor's built-in capabilities and the charger's robust controller. In this paper, the modelling, analysis and operational modes of the proposed topology have been carefully examined to observe its feasibility. The model has also been simulated, and the results have been used to verify the theoretical analysis. The charger's performance, together with its controller, is then evaluated experimentally on a 5kW charger. The research findings show that none of the motors produce torque during charging, and the deployed controller is robust, resulting in 93.4% efficiency and 3.2% THD, making it suitable as a high-performance charger for electric vehicles.

title: Data-Driven Causal Pathway Discovery in Alzheimer’s Disease via Mendelian Randomization
authors: Yi Xia, Cong Li, Shenglan Jin, Liangwen Zhang, Jichun Zhang, Xuexiao Shao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that poses a significant and growing global health challenge due to its multifactorial origin and complex pathophysiological mechanisms. Modern computational biology provides powerful tools to uncover risk factors from large-scale biomedical datasets, yet distinguishing causality from correlation within such observational data remains a fundamental methodological bottleneck. To address this issue, we implement a two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) computational framework, employing genetic variants as instrumental variables to infer causality from complex biological traits. This study focuses on evaluating potential causal relationships among key biological markers, specifically Apolipoprotein E4 (ApoE4), brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), and AD risk. The primary causal inference was conducted using the inverse-variance weighted (IVW) estimator, with a suite of sensitivity analyses, including Mendelian randomization-Egger regression (MR-Egger), weighted median, and Mendelian randomization pleiotropy RESidual sum and outlier (MR-PRESSO) coupled with leave-one-out validation to ensure methodological robustness and mitigate confounding bias. The computational results indicate a statistically significant negative causal effect of genetically instrumented ApoE4 exposure on BDNF levels, suggesting a potential pathway through which ApoE4 influences AD susceptibility. Sensitivity analyses consistently supported the robustness and validity of this causal inference. This work presents a rigorous, data-driven computational pipeline for causal discovery from high-dimensional biomedical data, offering a generalizable methodological framework that can be extended to infer causal networks in other multifactorial disorders.

title: Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Privacy-Preserving HVAC Optimization in Building Clusters
authors: Ying Wang, Hongshun Liu, Junlan Wang, Wei Song, Lei Liu, Boxia Li, Nan Luo, Tianzheng Xu, Zixuan Zou
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study proposes a hierarchical framework for collaborative, privacy-preserving AI-driven Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning systems optimization in building clusters, aiming to enhance energy efficiency and grid stability. Addressing limitations of traditional centralized methods in privacy, communication, and adaptability, our three-tier Cloud-Edge-Device architecture integrates Hierarchical Federated Learning (HFL) as an AI paradigm. HFL enables collaborative model training while keeping sensitive operational data localized.Akey innovation is a personalized Edge Tier aggregation mechanism that leverages machine learning-based clustering to group devices with similar dynamics, effectively managing heterogeneous data. Subsequently, a centralized Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent in the cloud autonomously learns a globally optimal control policy from these aggregated models, without direct data access. Rigorous privacy is further ensured via an Adaptive Differential Privacy (ADP) mechanism that intelligently and dynamically adjusts noise based on system stability. Through extensive experiments, our method achieved final comfort scores of 0.281 on the proposed simulation dataset and 0.479 on the EnergyPlus platform, which validates the effectiveness of the proposed framework. The code will be available at https://github.com/ayoyolover/HFL-HVAC upon the manuscript’s acceptance.

title: Characterization of System-Level SOA with Dynamic Degradation Considering Electro-Thermal Stress Distribution in Power Units
authors: Tongyao Han, Binli Liu, Yifei Luo, Yongle Huang, Ming Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The safe operating area (SOA) of IGBTs is critically influenced by the transient and nonlinear electro-thermal characteristics arising from system-level circuit parameters during operation. Addressing practical challenges, this paper establishes a device-level SOA characterization model based on the physical mechanisms of electro-thermal failure. Then, considering the spatial distribution of electro-thermal stresses resulting from the physical layout of devices in the neutral-point-clamped (NPC) three-level inverter, a system-level SOA characterization method is proposed, which incorporates multi-parameter coupling of main circuit parameters, control loop parameters, and cooling system parameters. Furthermore, an iterative loop model with coupling of degradation parameters and junction temperature evaluation is developed, and then the impact of device fatigue on the system-level SOA is evaluated. Finally, The proposed method is validated on a NPC single-phase three-level inverter. The results confirm the accuracy of the characterized system-level SOA and its dynamic degradation. The presented approach proves the reliability of the devices under extreme conditions and provides a theoretical basis for pushing devices to their operational limits.

title: DAS 2026 Commentary
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)

title: Towards Heterogeneous Multimodal Sentiment Analysis with ASR Errors via Multi-Granularity Contrastive Coordination
authors: Qi Shen, Junchang Xin, Bing Tian Dai, Zhongming Yao, Xinyao Liu, Zhihong Huang, Zhiqiong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Multimodal sentiment analysis improves the understanding of human emotions by integrating multiple modalities. However, the language modality accessed by the actually deployed MSA systems is obtained through automatic speech recognition (ASR), which can induce text errors and lead to MSA performance degradation. Besides, multimodal heterogeneity and multimodal information imbalance further aggravate the negative impact of ASR errors. To this end, we propose a multi-granularity contrastive coordination framework (MGCCF) for achieving robust MSA under ASR errors. Specifically, an error sentiment awareness calibration (ESAC) unit is devised to learn cross-modal word-level correlation and contextual information, enabling the detection and calibration of erroneous sentiment word embedding. Furthermore, a multimodal contrastive coordination (MCC) paradigm is presented to achieve cross-modal multi-granularity alignment from both global semantic and local temporal aspects. Meanwhile, MCC ingeniously leverages non-verbal features to reinforce the ability of ESAC to correct anomalous sentiment information and refine the expression of language features. Eventually, a multimodal information fusion (MIF) approach is developed to deal with multimodal information imbalance, which interpretably quantifies the contributions of different modalities and achieves multimodal dynamic fusion. Comprehensive experiments on seven MSA datasets demonstrate that our MGCCF achieves remarkable performance and surpasses the current state-of-the-art MSA methods.

title: PrivEdge-Loc: Edge-Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Indoor Localization for Multi-Band Networks
authors: Hemant Miranka, Sanjay Kumar Tehariya, Avinash Awasthi, B Prema Mayudu, Subba Rao Peram, Ramesh Babu Batulla, Bharavi Mishra
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this paper, we introduce a new, lightweight, and secure solution, PrivEdge-Loc, which is particularly suitable for multi-band indoor localization in IoT networks. Unlike traditional federated learning, which requires substantial computational power in edge computing, Split Learning greatly reduces this necessity in our solution. Split Learning is a novel machine learning paradigm in which the neural network is split into two parts: one on the edge device and the other. By offloading heavy computation to the server, the edge nodes only need to send low-dimensional compressed data to the server for processing, ensuring that the privacy of raw Channel State Information (CSI) is architecturally protected against eavesdropping. To tackle the statistical heterogeneity in multi-frequency environments, the server leverages a differentiable gating network within a dynamic Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture. This enables the server to dynamically route incoming features to frequency-specific experts without requiring explicit band identification. Additionally, the representational power of the student experts is further enhanced through Hint-Based Knowledge Distillation (HKD), which actively aligns the student experts’ intermediate latent space with that of the pretrained teacher models. Our proposed PrivEdge-Loc is evaluated using a non-IID dataset with 3 frequency bands: 2.4 GHz, 3.5 GHz, and 5.0 GHz. Our framework achieves a highly competitive Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.194 meters. The proposed framework provides a highly scalable, secure, and feasible alternative to computation-intensive federated baselines for data-sensitive, multi-tenant deployments.

title: TAPGuard: A Semantic-Aware Graph Framework for TAP Rule Cascading Threat Detection
authors: Yongheng Xing, You Wang, Xinqi Du, Juncheng Hu, Kun Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: With the rapid advancement of Internet of Things and artificial intelligence, device automation systems have become increasingly integrated with physical environments, introducing new security challenges for Trigger-Action Programming. An improper configuration of TAP rules may lead to severe cascading threats. However, existing methods typically rely on predefined safety properties and fail to capture the underlying semantic dependencies and interactions among rules. To address these limitations, we propose TAPGuard, a semantics-enhanced framework for TAP rule linkage modeling and cascading threat detection. Specifically, we identify two types of cascading threats: explicit threats, which arise from direct device interactions, and implicit threats, which are induced by shared environmental variables and may propagate across semantically related but structurally disconnected rules. TAPGuard leverages large language models to extract structured semantic elements from natural language rule descriptions and incorporates a semantic alignment module to assess the functional similarity between rules. Building on this, we propose a dual-relation context encoder incorporating node-level and semantic-level attention to model heterogeneous dependencies and enable multi-hop relational reasoning in the heterogeneous TAP rule graph. We evaluate TAPGuard on a real-world smart home dataset and demonstrate its effectiveness in detecting cascading threats. Experimental results show that TAPGuard significantly outperforms state-of-the-art graph-based baselines.

title: Agentic Geo-Intelligence-Based Spatio-Temporal Operational Risk Analysis in Sensitive Regions
authors: Sankalp Dwivedi, Chandresh Kumar Maurya, Hemprasad Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The combination of operational processes in sensitive fields leads to the emergence of incident patterns that are characterized by spatial and temporal variability, making proactive and risk-informed decision-making a very important task. While machine learning methods have shown a great ability to describe these patterns, their application is often impeded by a lack of interpretability and insufficient geographic validation. In this paper, an agentic geo-intelligence framework for spatiotemporal operational risk analysis is proposed, combining kernel density estimation of historical patterns, severity-based feature aggregation, probabilistic regression, and geographic validation using GIS within a single analytical process. The data on incidents is discretized into a lattice of spatial grid cells, which converts the task of future risk detection into a predictive classification task. The performance of the model and its geographic validity are assessed by the Global Moran's I, Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA), and Prediction Accuracy Index. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves predictive performance comparable to a Random Forest classifier while offering substantially higher interpretability and spatial coherence. The framework therefore provides a robust foundation for human-in-the-loop operational decision support in sensitive and security-critical environments.

title: Validating a Constraint Programming Solver in Lot Scheduling Under Process Time Variability
authors: Shunsuke Nishinaka, Tsuyoshi Idé
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Constraint Programming (CP) is an established approach for solving lot scheduling problems in semiconductor manufacturing. Due to computational complexity, generating an entire-fab schedule has been difficult. Recently, reinforcement learning (RL) has been studied as a way to overcome this limitation, but improvements in computing power have increased the problem sizes that CP can handle, leading to renewed attention to CP. However, CP is often viewed as a black box, and predicting how the resulting schedules will respond under random disturbances is generally challenging. In this study, we investigate the impact of process time (PT) variability on scheduling outcomes. We estimate process times stochastically and perform scheduling using CP. Our simulation study shows CP’s promising robustness to PT variability.

title: Understanding and Operation Control of Repetitive LVRT Oscillations for Grid-tied CIGs
authors: Leying Li, Youhan Deng, Xiaojun Hua, Yushu Li, Weiwei Yao, Zhen Gao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In the context of the continuous increase in the grid connection rate of new energy sources, the repeated low-voltage ride-through phenomenon of converter interfaced generation(CIG) units is prone to cause voltage oscillations at the grid connection point, seriously threatening the safe and stable operation of the power system. Based on the steady-state power flow calculation method, this paper analyzes the key triggering conditions of voltage oscillations at the grid connection point, reveals the internal mechanism of oscillations caused by the coupling mismatch of the dynamic characteristics of CIG units and the power grid, and clarifies the influence laws of key variables such as low-voltage ride-through control parameters on the oscillation characteristics. The CIG adopts a two-level voltage-source inverter topology with voltage-current dual closed-loop vector control to ensure the accuracy of modeling and simulation. The simulation settings select three-phase symmetrical short-circuit fault and weak AC grid condition, which are in line with actual engineering operating conditions. To address repetitive LVRT oscillations, two targeted suppression strategies are proposed and compared in terms of effectiveness, engineering implementation and applicable scenarios. Considering the potential impact of communication delay and parameter uncertainty, the robustness of the strategies is verified. The research results show that the proposed strategies can effectively suppress voltage fluctuations, avoid continuous low-voltage operation and unit disconnection risks, and improve the stability of CIG grid connection with strong engineering practical value.

title: A Streaming FPGA Architecture for Sparse Matrix Multiplication with Sparsity-Aware Data Reordering
authors: Shuxuan Li, Nikela Papadopoulou, Wim Vanderbauwhede
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Sparse matrix–dense matrix multiplication (SpMM) is a fundamental kernel in graph analytics, graph neural networks, and many sparse deep learning workloads. However, its irregular sparsity patterns make efficient execution on FPGAs challenging. Conventional CSR- or COO-based traversal often leads to irregular control flow, fragmented memory accesses, and limited reuse of dense features, which together reduce pipeline efficiency and make it difficult to sustain high throughput. This work addresses these limitations through a host–FPGA co-design that converts irregular sparse computation into streamable tile-level execution units. By combining sparsity-aware data reordering with an execution-oriented sparse representation, the proposed approach enables more regular data movement and better hardware utilization.

title: The AA–EC Framework: Bridging Authentic Assessment and Accreditation in Engineering Education
authors: Jonathan Loo, Yue Chen, Kok Keong Chai
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The adoption of authentic assessment (AA) in engineering education faces persistent challenges: diverse interpretations of AA qualities, limited alignment with accreditation requirements, and new demands introduced by generative AI. This paper proposes the Authentic Assessment-Engineering Competence (AA-EC) Framework, comprising three components: (i) ten consolidated AA qualities structured within a Task-Process-Outcome (TPO) model, (ii) the AA-EC Alignment Model mapping these qualities to AHEP4, ABET, and CEAB accreditation frameworks, and (iii) the Authenticity Progression Scale (APS), a five-level rubric for evaluating coursework authenticity. Developed through a systematic literature review (PRISMA 2020) and grounded theory coding, the framework equips educators with structured tools for coursework design, enables students to track competence development, and provides institutions with verifiable, accreditation-ready evidence. Although developed for engineering, the framework is adaptable across disciplines by defining discipline-specific and context-specific AA qualities and aligning them with professional and accreditation frameworks.

title: MemWaveNet: a Multi Region Electricity Load Forecasting Model
authors: Xin Liu, Donglan Liu, Han Liu, Fangzhe Zhang, Yuhui Jin, Mengqian Sun
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Holiday electricity load forecasting is of vital importance, since holiday electricity load could be different from that on regular days. However, most studies usually ignore this feature and focus on the results on regular days. electricity load forecasting on holidays requires long-term forecasting, while most work often ignores this. Besides, they usually focus on forecasting for individual locations, while ignoring the relationship among these locations. To address these limitations, we propose MemWaveNet, a framework specifically designed for electricity load forecasting across multiple regions on holidays. The proposed framework has the ability to simultaneously capture the spatial-temporal relationships among different places and apply a memory attention block to incorporate long-term information from historical time series for multi-region load forecasting. Experimental results on two benchmark datasets show that the proposed model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of predictive accuracy.

title: A Dual-Model Nids Architecture Combining Convolutional Neural Networks and Autoencoders for Robust Attack Detection
authors: Robin Jose R, Surya R, Ahila Devi E
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) must accurately identify changing cyber-attacks, including zero-day threats that differ from known patterns. This paper proposes a dual-model NIDS architecture that combines a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with an Autoencoder to achieve strong and flexible attack detection. The CNN component performs supervised classification to capture spatial dependencies in network traffic features. This enables precise detection of known attack classes. At the same time, the Autoencoder learns compact representations of normal traffic and flags anomalies based on reconstruction errors. This approach helps to detect unseen and zero-day attacks effectively. The outputs of both models are combined to create a hybrid decision layer that improves reliability and lowers false positives. Experiments on standard intrusion datasets show that this architecture consistently outperforms traditional single-model NIDS in accuracy, recall, and zero-day detection capability. This dual-model design provides a scalable and reliable framework for modern intrusion detection in today's network environments.

title: Cybernetic Insights on GenAI and Learning in Higher Education
authors: David Ernesto Salinas-Navarro, Eliseo Vilalta-Perdomo
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: The rapid diffusion of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) is reshaping learning conditions, not by introducing new activities but by reorganising existing feedback structures among students, teachers, and learning environments. This study adopts a cybernetic perspective to explore how Gen AI influences the recursive processes of enquiry, collaboration, and reflection. Drawing on the concepts of feedback, adaptation, and regulation, this paper frames GenAI as an active participant in learning systems, redistributing agency, and reshaping the rhythm of interaction. A methodological guide is proposed for constructing case studies across disciplines, combining documentary artefacts and participant accounts to map the cybernetic dynamics in practice. A case study of a Quality Management module illustrates how GenAI reconfigures individual reflection, group collaboration, and assessment practices. This study contributes to cybernetic learning theory by showing how GenAI affects agency and feedback structures, and by providing a framework for designing ethical GenAI learning environments. This study acknowledges the limitations that future studies must address to examine student learning outcomes. The paper concludes by highlighting the research question: How does Gen AI reshape learning as a recursive cybernetic process? Scholars are invited to explore this in their own contexts in future studies.

title: Elimination of Silicon Photonics wafer breakage in barrier metal process
authors: Teo Sin Pang, Eu Eliang, Soon Beng Choo, Chinnadurai Suthakar
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This study introduces a method to eliminate wafer breakage in Silicon Photonics devices during PVD pre-clean processes by extending pump step duration after plasma exposure steps. This additional pump duration allows passive discharge of accumulated static charge, effectively mitigating breakage risk. Experimental validation across multiple production lots confirmed a complete elimination of wafer breakage incidents. The solution requires no hardware modifications, ensuring seamless integration with existing high-volume manufacturing toolsets. As a result, the wafer breakage hit rate for Silicon Photonics was reduced from 13% to 0%.

title: Design of a Heave-Type Wave Energy Converter Based on Magnetic Lead Screw
authors: Jiawei Li, Weimin Wu, Hengyu Wang, Guodong Fan
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: A novel miniaturized wave energy converter (WEC) utilizing a magnetic lead screw (MLS) mechanism is proposed to address the prevalent challenges of conventional WECs, including high maintenance costs and limited reliability. This design provides a compact, corrosion-resistant, and maintenance-free solution, making it particularly suitable for distributed power generation applications. The device employs a non-contact magnetic drive mechanism to efficiently convert the relative heave motion between a surface float and a submerged buoy into electrical energy by a direct-drive power take-off (PTO) system. We evaluated the hydrodynamic performance and the absorbed power of the system under realistic wave conditions using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations. The simulation results demonstrate that the system maintains stable operation under regular wave conditions, achieving an absorbed power of approximately 450W. Under irregular wave conditions, the absorbed power reaches about 250W.

title: Performance Study of a Novel Anti-biofouling "Kathon-Silicone Rubber" Composite Insulator Material
authors: Yating Wei, Dayuan Liu, Changhai Sun, Hongyu Wang, Jiaqi Ni, Ming Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Aiming at the colonization of green algae on conventional composite insulators in high-temperature and high-humidity environments, this paper investigates the performance of a novel algae-resistant insulating material—"Kathon-silicone rubber". The study addresses the issues of reduced hydrophobicity and increased flashover risk caused by algal colonization on insulators. Firstly, Chlorella and Kathon were selected as the test algae and algicide, respectively, and "Kathon–silicone rubber" samples with different compounding concentrations were prepared. Secondly, the algae inhibition performance, mechanical properties, and electrical properties of the samples were tested to analyze the effect of Kathon concentration on their performance. The results show that the sample with 5.0wt% Kathon exhibits the best algae inhibition performance, reaching 75.9%. For samples with 1.0–5.0wt% Kathon, the Shore hardness, elongation at break, breakdown voltage, and dielectric loss factor all meet the industry requirements. However, when the Kathon mass fraction is ⩾ 3.0wt%, the tensile strength of the samples fails to meet the requirement. Therefore, 2.0wt% is identified as the optimal compounding concentration for Kathon. The research findings provide a viable technical pathway for the design of novel anti-algal insulators.

title: HESP-Stream: A Sensor-Direct, Instruction-Driven FPGA System for Real-Time Sparse Event Processing
authors: Jihui Qi, Yongjiang Xue, Qingzeng Song
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: To address the security threats posed by micro-UAVs, this work presents HESP-Stream, a real-time event-based detection system enabled by a sensor-direct, instruction-driven hardware–software co-design. Unlike conventional CPU-assisted pipelines, HESP-Stream eliminates the host-side preprocessing bottleneck by integrating on-chip event voxelization, instruction-driven sparse convolution, and fully streaming FPGA execution into a unified end-to-end datapath. Implemented on a Xilinx Kintex-7 FPGA, HESP-Stream achieves 59.51% mIoU and 85.34% accuracy after quantization. Under typical high-sparsity micro-UAV scenes, the system reaches an end-to-end latency as low as 5.622ms, delivering 2.930× and 2.989× speedups over GPU end-to-end and CPU+GPU pipelines, respectively.

title: The Influence of AI Use Policies on Student Performance and Ethical Awareness in Programming Education
authors: Julio G. Arriaga
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This study examines the impact of institutional policies regulating the ethical use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools in programming education. Two distinct AI use policies were implemented in a five-week intervention with undergraduate engineering students: one emphasizing reflection through written self-assessment and another emphasizing transparency through the submission of full chat logs. Quantitative data from programming exams and survey responses, combined with qualitative analysis of open-ended feedback, revealed that both approaches led to comparable academic performance, suggesting that the reporting format itself has little effect on learning outcomes. However, differences emerged in students' attitudes and perceptions. The reflective policy promoted greater ethical awareness, honesty, and self-regulation, while the chatlog policy encouraged broader adoption of AI tools and stricter compliance with reporting requirements. These findings highlight the importance of clear institutional policies that balance flexibility, transparency, and ethical responsibility, positioning AI as a formative aid rather than a substitute for problem-solving. The study underscores the need to integrate ethical and digital literacy into programming curricula and to design educational frameworks that promote responsible and reflective AI use in higher education.

title: A Quantitative Analysis of Undergraduate Researchers' Intent to Apply to Graduate School
authors: Rachel Burcin, Micah Nye, Vishwas Mruthyunjaya, Isaiah Adu, John M. Dolan
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: Understanding the factors that influence undergraduate students' intent to pursue graduate studies is crucial for developing effective academic pathways and increasing domestic participation in STEM graduate programs. Formal undergraduate research experiences, referred to as REUs, have the potential to enhance student confidence, promote a sense of belonging, and encourage persistence in STEM; however, their impact on intent to apply to graduate studies has not been thoroughly examined. This mixed-methods study investigates whether participation in REUs influences students' intentions to apply to graduate school by comparing national, Computing Research Association (CRA), and local, Carnegie Mellon University's Robotics Institute Summer Scholars (RISS), data. Analyses of CRA national surveys show that students with formal research experience are more than twice as likely to report an intent to apply to graduate school. Formal REU programs are highly effective educational interventions that measurably increase intent to apply to graduate studies.

title: Integrating GenAI with Live Case Studies: Advancing Experiential Learning in Operations Management Education
authors: David Ernesto Salinas-Navarro, Eliseo Vilalta-Perdomo, Jaime Alberto Palma-Mendoza
publish: 2026 IEEE Engineering Education World Conference (EDUNINE)
abstract: This paper examines how Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) can be combined with live case studies to improve experiential learning in operations management education. GenAI can transform education through advanced human-like text generation capabilities, but raises concerns regarding pedagogy, ethics, and academic integrity. This research introduces a framework that combines Kolb's experiential learning theory with live case studies, utilising GenAI as a, agent to learn with to foster student involvement in practical situations. This framework was illustrated using a case study approach in a postgraduate module on operations management. Students used GenAI tools to navigate a live case study to upscale operations for a UK-based ethnic restaurant, illustrating the approach's potential. The findings suggest that students appreciated GenAI's contribution to tackling intricate problems and considered the live case study to be captivating. This study contributes a pedagogical framework for integrating GenAI into experiential learning. However, the limitations include the use of case study research, moderate student participation, and the complexity of live case studies. Future studies should overcome these limitations and explore how GenAI affects learning outcomes.

title: Self-Diagnosis and Self-Recovery Mechanisms for Relay Protection Data Storage in Smart Substations
authors: Zhou Jing, Runyi Wang, Zhiping Shen, Bixiang Dai, Bei Dong, Shaowei Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In response to the severe issue that single event effects (SEE) may cause errors in critical data of relay protection devices, leading to maloperation or failure to operate, this paper proposes a protection method that integrates real-time diagnosis, intelligent decision-making, and active self-recovery. This method constructs a redundant architecture based on dual cores (data processing core and logical instruction core) and dual data areas (operating area and backup area), and incorporates a multi-layer protection mechanism: A CRC32 checksum is appended during the data writing stage to establish a health baseline; periodic state diagnosis and consistency verification are performed on the operating data area (ODA) through logical instruction verification; when data errors are diagnosed, the system does not immediately lockout. Instead, it initiates an intelligent recovery process, which includes multiple re-reads to counteract transient interference, automatic switching to an intact backup area to maintain continuous operation of the device, and the adoption of an efficient binary search positioning and rewriting strategy for rapid online repair of faulty data blocks. This study upgrades the protection strategy from passive lockout to active diagnosis and self-recovery, significantly enhancing the functional resilience and availability of the device when facing storage soft errors. It provides crucial technical support for the construction of intelligent power equipment with inherent self-maintenance capabilities.

title: Hybrid Mixed-Signal Conditioning for Non-Contact Capacitive Sensing of Hand Kinematics for Biomedical Applications
authors: Wanghley Soares Martins, Guillermo Alvarez Bestard, Rhaíra Helena Caetano e Souza, Anelise Costa Dos Santos, Fábio Henrique Monteiro Oliveira
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Non-contact capacitive sensing enables non-invasive measurement of hand kinematics, providing a cost-effective alternative to high-fidelity marker-based motion capture and high-end vision systems. While specialized electrical potential sensors (EPS) involve significant manufacturing costs, the proposed architecture offers a path toward economic accessibility by reducing the hardware complexity and high-speed processing requirements typically associated with clinical-grade motion capture. However, these sensors are highly susceptible to environmental electromagnetic interference, which can obscure critical physiological markers. This work presents a hybrid mixed-signal conditioning architecture specifically optimized to distinguish voluntary reaching movements (0–3 Hz) from pathological tremors (4–18 Hz) in high-interference clinical settings. A fourth-order Butterworth analog front-end with a 25 Hz cutoff was designed as a low-power anti-aliasing stage and evaluated against 101-tap FIR and 4th-order HR digital filters. Results show that while standalone digital filtering achieves a 26 dB SNR, the analog stage enables a 91% reduction in processing power. The proposed cascaded architecture achieved a peak SNR of 29 dB while preserving 74% of CPU headroom for concurrent clinical functions. By integrating physical anti-aliasing with high-precision digital refinement, this co-designed approach provides a robust framework for real-time hand kinematic monitoring.

title: Euclidean SLAM in Dynamic Environments: Two Novel Landmark Estimators for Moving Landmark Detection
authors: Ali Eslamian, Masoud Dorvash, Sayed Mojtaba Mirghaderi
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) is crucial for autonomous robots, enabling them to navigate and map unknown environments. While most SLAM research focuses on visual-based approaches using image processing and deep neural networks, filter-based methods remain popular for non-visual applications. However, these methods often struggle in dynamic environments due to their reliance on stationary landmarks. This paper presents a solution for filter-based SLAM in dynamic environments by introducing two Euclidean-based methods to handle moving landmarks. The proposed methods are evaluated through MATLAB simulations, demonstrating accurate trajectory estimation and comparing their performance against conventional filter-based algorithms, specifically the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and unscented Kalman filter (UKF), in the presence of moving landmarks. Additionally, we introduce a new dataset for evaluating non-visual filter-based SLAM in dynamic environments, providing a benchmark for future research. The proposed methods effectively mitigate the impact of moving landmarks while maintaining accuracy, as validated through real-world scenario simulations.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Passive Detection System: De-Novo Look
authors: Himanshu Chaudhary, Ayush Mudgal, Manoj Kumar Yadav, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Modern warfare increasingly relies on the ability to sense, track and interpret adversary activities without revealing one's own presence. Passive Detection Systems (PDS) have emerged as a niche yet disruptive capability, enabling forces to detect, classify and localize targets by exploiting adversary emissions or environmental disturbances rather than active transmissions. Defence establishments worldwide are investing in passive radar, passive sonar, Electronic Support Measures (ESM), Infrared/ Optical Sensing, Acoustic and Seismic Sensor networks and space-based passive surveillance to counter stealth aircraft, low-observable UAS, cruise missiles and networkcentric operations. These systems offer advantages such as enhanced survivability, resistance to electronic attack and jamming, and covert situational awareness, while also posing challenges related to sensor fusion, data processing, and integration into existing command and control architectures. This paper surveys contemporary developments in passive detection technologies globally and examines India's progress in this domain.

title: Thermal Desorption Spectroscopy with Real-Time Temperature Control by Band Gap Monitoring
authors: Kiyoteru Kobayashi, Kenshi Kimoto, Natsumi Aoki
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: A thermal desorption spectroscopy (TDS) apparatus integrating band-gap-based temperature measurement of silicon specimens with feedback-controlled lamp heating has been developed. Stable and reproducible heating-rate control was achieved over a temperature range of 60–520 °C. For hydrogen-ion-implanted SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/Si specimens, the H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> desorption peak temperature measured at a heating rate of 0.5 °C/s exhibited excellent reproducibility, with a standard deviation of 0.5 °C. Using the developed apparatus, the H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O desorption spectrum from SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> films was clearly resolved into three components with peak temperatures at 168, 315, and 398 °C. This temperature-resolved separation enables clear discrimination between physisorbed water and more strongly bound water-related species. It provides a practical framework for discussing water bonding states on oxide-film surfaces and within the oxide network and for guiding surface-sensitive fabrication processes, including wafer-level hybrid bonding. In addition, H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O desorption from amorphous InGaZnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> thin films was observed at around 110 and 450 °C, together with Zn-related signals (m/z 64 and 66) that became detectable above approximately 360 °C. These results demonstrate that the present apparatus provides a reliable experimental basis for determining desorption temperatures as intrinsic characteristics of SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/Si structures and oxide semiconductor thin films, thereby supporting quantitative evaluation of thermally activated processes and defining thermal process windows in advanced device fabrication.

title: Impact Analysis of Converting 500kV AC Transmission Lines to DC
authors: Bingjie Jin, Zuohong Li, Guangjian Zhang, Shuxin Luo, Chu Jin, Hong Dong, Jindi Luo
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy into the power grid, the limited transmission capacity of existing AC corridors has constrained the efficient accommodation of renewable power. VSC-HVDC technology, leveraging its advantages of high power transmission capacity, fault isolation capability, and rapid power control, provides an effective solution for grid upgrades. This paper addresses a capacity-constrained 500kV critical transmission corridor in a regional grid by proposing its conversion to a ±420kV VSC-HVDC system, thereby enhancing transmission capacity and suppressing short-circuit currents. Simulation analysis reveals that while short-circuit currents at directly connected nodes decrease significantly after conversion, the associated reduction in system short-circuit capacity leads to weakened voltage support capability. To address this issue, a transient control strategy based on voltage dip detection—implementing "active power reduction and reactive power increase"—is proposed. This strategy switches to reactive power-voltage droop control during faults, prioritizing the provision of dynamic reactive power support. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed control strategy effectively improves voltage levels at nearby buses, offering a viable solution to the problem of degraded voltage support capability following AC-to-DC conversion.

title: Analysis and Optimization of Differential-Mode Coupling Mechanism for EMI Filters in Motor Drives
authors: Luo Hu, Sirui Zhou, Guanfu Liu, Haihong Qin, Feifei Bu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: In motor drive systems, electromagnetic interference (EMI) filters suppress conducted emissions; however, non-ideal coupling among internal components may introduce additional differential-mode (DM) noise and degrade filtering performance. To address this issue, this paper investigates the formation mechanism of DM coupling in EMI filters for motor drives. An equivalent model incorporating high-frequency distributed parameters is established to reveal the influence of component layout and magnetic-field coupling on the DM noise spectrum. Based on the analytical results, an optimization method for suppressing DM coupling is proposed by adjusting the relative positions and orientations of filter components without modifying the filter topology. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively reduces DM noise in critical frequency bands and improves the overall suppression performance of the EMI filter.

title: Online Condition Monitoring of Transmission Line Insulators with Distributed Voltage and Leakage Current Measurements
authors: Siwei Zhang, Jinye Zhang, Jian Zhao, Xinhou Liu, Lei Jiang, Yongquan Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Insulators are critical components in transmission line external insulation systems, and their long-term reliability directly affects the secure operation of power grids. Under the combined influence of pollution deposition, humidity, temperature variation, and long-term electromechanical stress, insulators may suffer from surface contamination, internal cracks, low-value insulator defects, zero-value failure, and pollution flashover. To address these issues, this paper proposes a continuous, multimodal condition monitoring method that combines distributed voltage measurement and leakage current detection. The system employs capacitive voltage division to realize real-time sensing of the voltage distribution of insulator strings. Based on sensitive-insulator analysis and single-phase/three-phase judgment logic, deteriorated and zero-value insulators can be accurately identified. Meanwhile, leakage current is employed as the primary online detection method, and a quantitative correction model incorporating multiple environmental parameters is established to suppress environmental interference and precisely evaluate pollution severity. Laboratory experiments and field tests demonstrate that the proposed system enables continuous live-line monitoring with good accuracy. It effectively detects early defects and excessive pollution, providing reliable data support for condition-based maintenance of smart grids.

title: Comparative Analysis of Electric Current Generated During Irreversible Electroporation in Prostate, Pancreas, Brain and Liver Tumors
authors: Adrian Jordan-Diaz, A. Vera-Hernández, C.J. Trujillo-Romero, M. I. Gutierrez
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Irreversible electroporation (IRE) is a non-thermal ablation technique used for the treatment of soft tissue tumors when thermal ablation is not feasible. Treatment outcome depends on tissue electrical properties, electrode configuration, and the electric current generated during pulse delivery. In this work, a finite element method (FEM) model was developed to evaluate electric field distribution, electric current, and electroporation volumes under a single 100 μs IRE pulse using dynamic, electric-field-dependent conductivity. Prostate, pancreas, liver, and brain tissues were analyzed using four needle electrodes with inter-electrode spacings of 10, 12, and 15 mm. The results show that prostate tissue generates the highest electric current, reaching 27.93 A, 26.69 A, and 24.61 A for spacings of 10, 12, and 15 mm, respectively, while pancreas, liver, and brain reached 53%,50%, and 46% of the prostate peak current, respectively. Pancreas tissue exhibited the largest IRE volume in the entire model, reaching 14.0 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at 15 mm spacing, followed by prostate tissue with 12.71 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Analysis of the tumoral region showed that the highest IRE coverage was achieved with a 10 mm inter-electrode spacing, with coverage values up to 97% for pancreas and 96% for prostate tissue. These results highlight the strong influence of tissue-specific electrical properties and electrode geometry on current levels and electroporation coverage, providing useful insight for pulse generator design and IRE treatment planning.

title: NeuroMesh Sentinel: Decentralized Swarm Intelligence for Autonomous Multi-Cloud Security
authors: Krishnaveni K, Jeevitha K, J.T Anita Rose
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: The Multi-cloud strategies are being embraced by cloud infrastructures, yet centralized security systems pose a single point of failure thus creating lagging breach detection, lateral movement, and log tampering vulnerabilities. This paper presents NeuroMesh Sentinel, a decentralized security architecture that generates cloud resources into autonomous agents and creates a self-organizing mesh network. The system integrates indications of the biological immune system mimicry, swarm intelligence, stigmergic communication, hyperdimensional computing, and Byzantine fundamental fault-tolerant consensus to obtain quick, adaptive discover and reaction to threats. The agents constantly acquire identifying behavioral baselines, identify anomalies, authenticate threats collectively, and automatically apply countermeasures, and blockchain provides cryptographically unalterable audit trails. Prototyping The prototyping phase in the design of this system proved sub-second detection, significant level of false positive reduction, autonomous threat neutralization and resistance to compromised nodes. Also, through the framework, there is also sharing of federated their threat intelligence between organizations without compromising on privacy. NeuroMesh Sentinel is a radical innovation in the field of multi-cloud security providing a scalable, self-evolving and demonstrably resilient architecture that can overcome some of the most inherent disadvantages of the traditional centralized monitoring system.
